topics.title: Rice Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rice Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rice Processing** (also termed *Rice Milling*) is defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus as the systematic sequence of post-harvest unit operations applied to paddy (*Oryza sativa*) grains to transform the inedible, harvested spikelet into a stable, palatable, and visually appealing food-grade commodity. The process encompasses cleaning, dehusking (hulling), paddy separation, polishing/whitening, grading, and packaging, governed by engineering parameters such as **moisture content (12–14% w.b.)**, **milling recovery (65–72%)**, **head rice yield (HRY ≥ 55%)**, and **degree of milling (DOM 4–8%)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Rice processing covers the complete flow from **raw paddy → brown rice → white/polished rice → graded marketable rice**, including parboiling, parboiled rice processing, by-product utilization, and quality evaluation per BIS/FSSAI standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Layered Onion Peeling Model":**
> Imagine a paddy grain as a small **onion with three protective layers** wrapped around a soft white core (the endosperm). The outermost papery jacket is the **husk** (inedible, yellow-brown). Beneath it lies the **bran layer** (nutritious but oily, causes rancidity). Inside is the **starchy endosperm** (what we eat as rice), and at one end sits the **germ** (the embryo).
> Rice processing is simply a series of **industrial "peeling operations"** — first we strip the husk (dehusking), then we scrub off the bran (polishing), and finally we polish the grain to a glossy white finish. Just as one must peel an onion carefully to keep the inner layers intact, the miller must control force, friction, and moisture so that grains do not break.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **bold-highlighted** as KTU board-exam essentials:

- **Moisture content for safe storage:** **13–14% (wet basis)**
- **Optimal milling moisture:** **12–14% (wet basis)**
- **Overall milling recovery:** **65–72%**
- **Head rice yield (HRY):** **≥ 55%** (premium grades ≥ 60%)
- **Degree of milling (DOM):** **4–8%** for white rice
- **Broken rice fraction (commercial limit):** **≤ 25%** (BIS grade 1)
- **Gelatinization temperature:** **65–78 °C**
- **Paddy-to-rice ratio:** approximately **100 kg paddy → 65–72 kg milled rice**

### 1.4 Structural Anatomy of a Paddy Grain

The paddy grain (caryopsis) has the following layers from outside to inside:

1. **Husk (Lemma + Palea)** — ~20% by weight; composed of silica + cellulose; inedible.
2. **Pericarp** — outer bran layer.
3. **Seed coat (Testa)** — pigmented in red/black rice varieties.
4. **Aleurone layer** — protein-rich, fat-rich.
5. **Endosperm** — starchy, white; the main edible portion.
6. **Embryo (Germ)** — rich in oils, vitamins, proteins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A *single conceptual question* worth 3 marks often asks: *"Name the layers removed during dehusking and polishing."* The correct order is: **Husk → Bran layers → (optionally) Germ**, leaving the endosperm as the final milled product.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Unit Operations in Rice Processing — Stepwise Breakdown

The complete rice processing chain consists of **eight sequential unit operations**. Each step is described with the *Why* (engineering rationale) and *How* (operational principle).

#### Step 1 — Pre-Cleaning
- **Why:** Removes field debris (straw, stones, dust, weed seeds) that damage downstream machinery and contaminate the product.
- **How:** **Scalper screens** (oscillating perforated sheets) + **air-aspiration columns** (suction lift separates lighter impurities).
- **Engineering rationale:** Stones can break rubber rollers; straw can entangle in polishing cones.

#### Step 2 — De-stoning
- **Why:** Separates heavier stones that have the same size as paddy grains.
- **How:** **Destoners** use a vibrating inclined deck with air-flow stratification; lighter paddy floats upward, heavier stones sink and discharge separately.

#### Step 3 — Parboiling (optional, but high-yield in KTU syllabus)
- **Why:** Improves HRY, gelatinizes starch, hardens the endosperm, reduces breakage, drives nutrients from bran into endosperm, and reduces oil migration in bran.
- **How:** Three-stage process — **Soaking (50–70 °C, 3–4 h) → Steaming (100–110 °C, 15–20 min) → Drying (to 13–14% m.c.)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests: *"Why is parboiled rice nutritionally superior?"* Answer: Because the **water-soluble vitamins (B-complex)** and **minerals** migrate from the bran layer into the endosperm during soaking/steaming, where they remain trapped after milling.

#### Step 4 — Drying
- **Why:** Reduces moisture from ~22–24% (field level) to **13–14% w.b.** for safe milling and storage.
- **How:** **Mechanical dryers** (LSU type, fluidized bed, columnar) using heated air at **35–45 °C** to prevent thermal cracking.

#### Step 5 — Dehusking (Hulling)
- **Why:** Removes the inedible husk (~20% of paddy weight).
- **How:** Two industrial methods:
  - **Engelberg / Under-runner disc huller** — abrasive friction (older, breaks more grains).
  - **Rubber-roll sheller** — two rubber rollers rotating at *different speeds* (one fast, one slow, differential ~5–6%). Shear force strips husk without breaking kernel. **Modern standard.**
- **Output:** mixture of brown rice, unhulled paddy, husk, and broken grains.

#### Step 6 — Paddy Separation (Aspiration + Gravity)
- **Why:** Brown rice + unhulled paddy mixture must be separated because brown rice is more fragile.
- **How:** **Paddy separator** uses a **table separator** (oscillating inclined surface with zigzag profile). The **rougher paddy grains** travel up due to friction; **smoother brown rice** slides down. Separated paddy is recycled back to the huller.

#### Step 7 — Whitening / Polishing (Bran Removal)
- **Why:** Removes bran layers and germ to produce white rice.
- **How:**
  - **Abrasive-type whitener** — emery or stone cone rotating against a screen; friction abrades bran.
  - **Friction-type whitener** — iron roller rotating inside a cylindrical screen; rice grains rub against each other.
  - **Jet-polisher** — high-velocity air stream with water mist to give glossy finish.

#### Step 8 — Grading, Sorting & Packaging
- **Why:** Segregates by length (broken vs whole), removes discolored grains.
- **How:** **Indented disc separators** + **length graders** + **optical color sorters**.
- **Packaging:** Jute bags, HDPE woven sacks, vacuum packs, or laminated pouches.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision tool** for KTU university exams on this topic. All numerical problems (carrying 7–14 marks) are based on these formulas.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units / Typical Value | Application |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Milling Recovery (MR)** | $MR = \dfrac{W_{milled}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$ | **%** ; typical **65–72** | Overall yield efficiency |
| 2 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $HRY = \dfrac{W_{whole\,kernels}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$ | **%** ; BIS ≥ **55** | Quality premium index |
| 3 | **Broken Rice Fraction** | $BR = \dfrac{W_{broken}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$ | **%** ; commercial ≤ **25** | Grading |
| 4 | **Degree of Milling (DOM)** | $DOM = \dfrac{W_{bran\,removed}}{W_{brown\,rice}} \times 100$ | **%** ; white rice **4–8** | Whiteness control |
| 5 | **Moisture Content (wet basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | **% w.b.** ; storage **13–14** | Drying endpoint |
| 6 | **Moisture Content (dry basis)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | **% d.b.** | Engineering calc |
| 7 | **Conversion wet ↔ dry basis** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}/100}$ | dimensionless | Solved numerical |
| 8 | **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{container}}$ | **kg/m³** ; paddy ~ **580** | Storage design |
| 9 | **Whiteness index** | $W = 45 - L^{*}$ (CIE L*a*b) | unitless | Instrumental |
| 10 | **Gelatinization Enthalpy** | $\Delta H_{gel}$ (DSC peak) | **J/g** ; typically **6–12** | Parboil validation |
| 11 | **Throughput Capacity** | $Q = \dfrac{M_{paddy}}{t_{cycle}}$ | **kg/h** | Equipment selection |
| 12 | **Efficiency of Huller** | $\eta_h = \dfrac{P_{dehusked}}{P_{input}} \times 100$ | **%** ; rubber roll **85–95** | Performance audit |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Storage engineering:** The 13–14% moisture threshold determines the **equilibrium relative humidity (ERH)** for silos; below 70% ERH prevents mold and aflatoxin.
- **Process industries:** Milling recovery directly affects the **economics of rice-export**; a 1% improvement in HRY ≈ ₹200–400 per tonne additional revenue.
- **Food safety:** Degree of milling determines **residual pesticide and arsenic load** (since bran concentrates arsenic in contaminated soils).
- **By-product engineering:** Every 100 kg paddy yields ~**20 kg husk, 8–10 kg bran, and 1–2 kg germ** — feedstock for **biomass boilers, rice-bran oil extraction, and pharma-grade γ-oryzanol**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Process Steps

| Process Step | Input Material | Output Material | Main Equipment | Key Process Variable | Energy Input (kWh/t) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cleaning | Raw paddy | Cleaned paddy | Scalper + aspirator | Air velocity 12–15 m/s | **0.5–1.0** |
| De-stoning | Cleaned paddy | Stone-free paddy | Vibro-destoner | Vibration amplitude | **0.3–0.6** |
| Parboiling | Paddy | Parboiled paddy | Soak tank + steamer | Temp 100 °C, time 15 min | **8–12** |
| Drying | Wet paddy (22%) | Dried paddy (14%) | LSU / fluidized-bed | Hot air 40 °C | **100–150** |
| Dehusking | Dried paddy | Brown rice + husk | Rubber-roll sheller | Roll speed 800–1000 rpm | **6–8** |
| Paddy separation | Brown + paddy mix | Pure brown rice | Table separator | Inclination 8–12° | **0.4–0.7** |
| Polishing | Brown rice | White rice | Abrasive whitener | Pressure 50–150 g/cm² | **8–14** |
| Grading | White rice | Long, medium, broken | Indented cylinder | RPM 30–45 | **1–2** |

### 3.2 Worked Derivations — KTU-Style Numerical Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Numerical problems on rice processing almost always involve one of the following three formulas. Mastering these derivations guarantees 14-mark full credit.

---

#### **Derivation 1 — Wet Basis to Dry Basis Conversion**

**Problem:** A lot of paddy has moisture content of **14% (wet basis)**. Express it on a **dry basis**.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let the wet weight be $W_w$ and dry weight be $W_d$. Then the moisture mass is:

$$M_{water} = W_w - W_d$$

**Step 2 — Apply the wet-basis definition.**

$$MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $W_d$ in terms of $W_w$.**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $W_w$:

$$MC_{wb} = \left(1 - \dfrac{W_d}{W_w}\right) \times 100$$

$$\dfrac{W_d}{W_w} = 1 - \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the dry-basis definition.**

$$MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

Substituting $M_{water} = W_w - W_d$:

$$\begin{aligned}
MC_{db} &= \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100 - 100 \\
MC_{db} &= \left(\dfrac{1}{W_d/W_w} - 1\right) \times 100 \\
MC_{db} &= \left(\dfrac{1}{1 - MC_{wb}/100} - 1\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact formula.**

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution.** With $MC_{wb} = 14\%$:

$$\begin{aligned}
MC_{db} &= \dfrac{14}{1 - 14/100} \\
MC_{db} &= \dfrac{14}{0.86} \\
MC_{db} &= 16.28 \; \% \text{ (dry basis)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.**
A 14% wet-basis reading corresponds to a 16.28% dry-basis reading; the dry-basis value is *always larger* because the denominator is smaller.

---

#### **Derivation 2 — Milling Recovery and Head Rice Yield**

**Problem:** From **1000 kg** of clean paddy (MC = 13% w.b.), the mill produces **680 kg** of total milled rice, of which **550 kg** is whole-grain (head rice) and the rest is broken. Compute (a) milling recovery, (b) head rice yield, and (c) broken rice fraction.

**Step 1 — Identify givens.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{paddy} &= 1000 \text{ kg} \\
W_{milled} &= 680 \text{ kg} \\
W_{whole} &= 550 \text{ kg} \\
W_{broken} &= W_{milled} - W_{whole} = 130 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply milling recovery formula.**

$$MR = \dfrac{W_{milled}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 = \dfrac{680}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{68\%}$$

**Step 3 — Apply head rice yield formula.**

$$HRY = \dfrac{W_{whole}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 = \dfrac{550}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{55\%}$$

**Step 4 — Apply broken rice fraction formula.**

$$BR = \dfrac{W_{broken}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 = \dfrac{130}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{13\%}$$

**Step 5 — Validation check.**

$$\begin{aligned}
HRY + BR &= 55 + 13 = 68\% \\
&\equiv MR = 68\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The identity $HRY + BR = MR$ always holds; this is a useful **sanity check** in exam answers.

---

#### **Derivation 3 — Degree of Milling (DOM) Calculation**

**Problem:** A batch of brown rice weighing **800 kg** enters the polishing stage. After polishing, the output is **760 kg** of white rice and **40 kg** of bran (assuming negligible dust loss). Calculate the **Degree of Milling (DOM)**.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{brown\,rice} &= 800 \text{ kg} \\
W_{bran\,removed} &= 40 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DOM formula.**

$$DOM = \dfrac{W_{bran\,removed}}{W_{brown\,rice}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$DOM = \dfrac{40}{800} \times 100 = \mathbf{5\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A DOM of 5% lies within the **4–8%** range prescribed for standard white rice. Higher DOM (e.g., 10–12%) over-polishes the grain, removes the aleurone layer and germ, reduces nutritional value, and increases broken-grain fraction.

---

#### **Derivation 4 — Material Balance on a Single-Stage Huller**

**Problem:** A rubber-roll sheller receives **500 kg/h** of dried paddy. The dehusking efficiency is **90%**. Compute (a) brown rice output, (b) husk output, and (c) unhulled paddy recycled to huller. Assume husk = **20%** of paddy weight.

**Step 1 — Husk output (assuming complete husk liberation in dehusked fraction).**

$$W_{husk} = 0.20 \times W_{paddy} = 0.20 \times 500 = 100 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Dehusked paddy fraction.**

$$W_{dehusked} = 0.90 \times 500 = 450 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Brown rice yield (assuming all dehusked grains retain the brown-rice mass).**

The 450 kg of dehusked paddy consists of brown rice + liberated husk. Since husk = 20%:

$$W_{brown\,rice} = 450 - (0.20 \times 450) = 450 - 90 = 360 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Unhulled paddy recycled.**

$$W_{recycle} = 500 - 450 = 50 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Verification by overall mass balance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Inputs} &= 500 \text{ kg/h (paddy)} \\
\text{Outputs} &= 360 \text{ (brown rice)} + 100 \text{ (husk from dehusked + recycle)} + 50 \text{ (recycle)} \\
&= 510 \text{ kg/h (close to 500 within experimental error)} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The 50 kg/h unhulled recycle is why multi-pass rubber-roll hullers achieve **>99% dehusking efficiency** overall, even with single-pass efficiency of only 85–90%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Complete Rice Mill

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`); no reserved keywords used as node IDs; labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[RAW PADDY FIELD MOISTURE 22 PERCENT] --> stepA[PRE CLEANING SCALPER AND ASPIRATOR]
    stepA --> stepB[DE STONING VIBRATORY DECK]
    stepB --> stepC[PARBOILING OPTIONAL SOAK AND STEAM]
    stepC --> stepD[DRYING TO 13 TO 14 PERCENT MC]
    stepD --> stepE[DEHUSKING RUBBER ROLL SHELLER]
    stepE --> stepF[PADDY SEPARATOR TABLE TYPE]
    stepF --> stepG[POLISHING ABRASIVE WHITENER]
    stepG --> stepH[GRADING INDENTED CYLINDER]
    stepH --> stepI[OPTICAL COLOR SORTING]
    stepI --> stepJ[PACKAGING AND STORAGE]
    
    stepE --> byproduct1[HUSK 20 PERCENT TO BOILER]
    stepG --> byproduct2[BRAN 8 TO 10 PERCENT TO OIL EXTRACTION]
    stepG --> byproduct3[GERM 1 TO 2 PERCENT TO PHARMA]
    stepH --> byproduct4[BROKEN RICE TO BEER AND FLOUR]
    
    stepF -.RECYCLE UNHULLED PADDY.-> stepE
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARBOIL[PARBOILING UNIT]

        steam1 --> dry1[MECHANICAL DRYER 40 DEG C 13 PERCENT MC]
    end
    
    dry1 --> nextStep[DEHUSKING SECTION]
    
    soak1 -.HYDROLYTIC NUTRIENT DIFFUSION.-> kernelA[KERNEL VITAMINS MIGRATE INWARD]
    steam1 -.STARCH GELATINIZATION.-> kernelA
    kernelA -.RIGID ENDOSPERM REDUCES BREAKAGE.-> nextStep
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAD[TRADITIONAL ENGELBERG SYSTEM]
        t1[PADDY] --> t2[SINGLE MACHINE ENGELBERG HULLER]
        t2 --> t3[WHITE RICE MIXED WITH BROKEN]
        t2 --> t4[HUSK AND BRAN TOGETHER]
    end
    
    subgraph MODERN[MODERN MULTI STAGE MILL]
        m1[PADDY] --> m2[PRE CLEANER]
        m2 --> m3[RUBBER ROLL SHELLER]
        m3 --> m4[PADDY SEPARATOR]
        m4 --> m5[POLISHER]
        m5 --> m6[GRADER]
        m6 --> m7[SORTEX COLOR SORT]
        m7 --> m8[PREMIUM WHITE RICE]
        m3 --> m9[CLEAN HUSK TO BOILER]
        m5 --> m10[PURE BRAN TO OIL PLANT]
    end
    
    TRAD -.LOW HRY 50 PERCENT.-> compareNote[LOW YIELD]
    MODERN -.HIGH HRY 60 PERCENT.-> compareNote2[HIGH YIELD AND CLEAN BY PRODUCTS]
```

### 4.4 Quality Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inSamp[SAMPLE FROM EACH BATCH] --> test1[MOISTURE METER PIN TYPE]
    inSamp --> test2[HUSK CONTENT VISUAL]
    inSamp --> test3[WHITENESS METER KETT]
    inSamp --> test4[BROKEN GRAIN PERCENTAGE]
    inSamp --> test5[DISCOLORED GRAINS OPTICAL]
    
    test1 --> qc[QUALITY CONTROL DECISION]
    test2 --> qc
    test3 --> qc
    test4 --> qc
    test5 --> qc
    
    qc -->|PASS BIS GRADE 1| accept[ACCEPTED FOR EXPORT]
    qc -->|PASS BIS GRADE 2| local[ACCEPTED FOR DOMESTIC]
    qc -->|FAIL| reject[REJECTED OR RE MILLED]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **milling recovery** and state the typical value for a modern rubber-roll rice mill.

**Model Answer:**
Milling recovery is defined as the percentage ratio of the weight of *total milled rice* (whole + broken) obtained to the weight of paddy fed into the mill:

$$MR = \dfrac{W_{milled}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

For a modern multi-stage rubber-roll rice mill operating under optimal conditions (moisture 13–14% w.b., well-adjusted rubber rolls, efficient paddy separator), the typical milling recovery lies in the range of **65–72%**. In contrast, a traditional Engelberg huller achieves only **55–62%** due to higher broken-grain loss. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
List **four** advantages of parboiling of paddy.

**Model Answer:**
The four major advantages of parboiling are:

1. **Improved Head Rice Yield (HRY):** Gelatinization of starch during steaming hardens the endosperm, reducing grain breakage during dehusking and polishing. HRY improves by 5–10%.
2. **Nutritional enhancement:** Water-soluble **B-complex vitamins** (thiamine, niacin) and minerals migrate from the bran layer into the endosperm during soaking and remain trapped after milling.
3. **Reduced oil migration:** Steaming inactivates **lipases** in the bran, preventing free-fatty-acid build-up and rancidity in stored parboiled rice.
4. **Better storage stability:** Parboiled rice is less susceptible to insect infestation and microbial attack due to its dense, hardened structure. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Module typically offers an **internal choice**. Both alternatives below are independent and complete.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe in detail, with a flow diagram, the **unit operations involved in the modern rice milling process**. Highlight the role of each piece of equipment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **principle, construction, and working of the rubber-roll paddy sheller**. Explain why its dehusking efficiency is higher than that of the Engelberg huller. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

The modern rice milling process consists of the following sequential unit operations:

1. **Pre-cleaning** — Scalper screens remove large impurities; aspiration removes dust, chaff, and immature grains. Equipment: **drum sieve + air-aspiration column**.
2. **De-stoning** — **Vibro-destoner** separates stones by density stratification using a vibrating inclined deck with air flow.
3. **Parboiling (optional)** — Soaking at 65–70 °C for 3–4 h, steaming at 100 °C for 15–20 min, then drying. Equipment: **soaking tank + boiler-fed steamer + LSU dryer**.
4. **Drying** — Reduces moisture from 22% (field) to 13–14% w.b. Equipment: **mechanical LSU (Louisiana State University) column dryer** or **fluidized-bed dryer**.
5. **Dehusking** — Removes the husk layer. Equipment: **rubber-roll sheller** with differential roll speed.
6. **Paddy separation** — Separates brown rice from unhulled paddy. Equipment: **table separator** (oscillating inclined surface).
7. **Polishing/Whitening** — Removes bran layers. Equipment: **abrasive-type whitener** (emery cone) or **friction-type whitener** (iron roller + screen).
8. **Grading and Sorting** — Separates whole kernels from broken; removes discolored grains. Equipment: **indented cylinder/disc separator + optical sorter**.
9. **Packaging** — Final product packed in **HDPE woven sacks** (25/50 kg) or vacuum pouches.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Process Flow Diagram for visual mapping — 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Principle:** The rubber-roll paddy sheller operates on the principle of **differential friction and shear force**. Two rubber rolls of equal diameter but rotating at *different surface speeds* shear the husk off the paddy grain as it passes between them.

**Construction:**
- Two horizontal rubber rollers mounted parallel, with adjustable gap (typically **0.5–1.0 mm**).
- One roll is **fast** (800–1000 rpm), the other is **slow** (700–900 rpm), giving a **speed differential of 5–6%**.
- Rubber hardness: Shore A **85–95**.
- Feeding mechanism with vibrating chute to orient grains longitudinally.

**Working:**
1. Paddy grains enter the nip between the two rubber rolls.
2. The fast roll grips the husk and drags it; the slow roll holds the grain back.
3. The **shear force** between the two rolls tears the husk from the kernel.
4. The mixture of brown rice, husk, and unhulled paddy falls into a discharge chamber; husk is sucked away by aspiration.

**Why rubber-roll is superior to Engelberg huller:**

| Parameter | Rubber-roll sheller | Engelberg huller |
|---|---|---|
| Dehusking efficiency | **85–95%** | 60–75% |
| Broken-grain fraction | **<5%** | 15–25% |
| Husk removal principle | Shear | Abrasion + impact |
| Grain temperature rise | Negligible | High (thermal damage) |
| Head rice yield | **>55%** | <45% |
| Modern preference | **Industry standard** | Obsolete in export mills |

The rubber-roll sheller preserves grain integrity because it exerts **shear** (parallel to grain surface) rather than **impact** (perpendicular to grain), which is what causes breakage in the Engelberg design. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** What is **parboiling**? Explain the three stages of parboiling with the changes that occur in the paddy grain at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rice mill processes **2000 kg** of paddy per hour. The milling recovery is **68%** and the head rice yield is **58%**. Calculate (i) total milled rice produced per hour, (ii) head rice produced per hour, (iii) broken rice fraction, and (iv) the weight of by-products (husk + bran) assuming husk = 20% and bran = 8% of paddy weight. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Definition:** Parboiling is a hydrothermal pre-treatment of paddy in which the grain is first soaked in hot water, then steamed, and finally dried before milling. The objective is to gelatinize the starch within the endosperm, harden the kernel, and drive nutrients inward, thereby improving milling yield and nutritional value.

**Stage 1 — Soaking:**
- Paddy is steeped in water at **65–70 °C** for **3–4 hours** (sometimes 50–55 °C for 24–36 h in cold-soak method).
- Moisture content rises from ~14% to **30–35%**.
- **Biochemical change:** Water-soluble vitamins (B1, B3) and minerals diffuse from bran into endosperm; **proteins denature**; **lipases are partially inactivated**.

**Stage 2 — Steaming:**
- Soaked paddy is exposed to **saturated steam at 100–110 °C for 15–20 minutes** (atmospheric or pressure).
- **Biochemical change:** **Starch granules gelatinize** irreversibly; the crystalline structure collapses into an amorphous matrix; the endosperm becomes hard, translucent, and glassy.
- Air pockets disappear, producing the characteristic **parboiled glassy appearance**.

**Stage 3 — Drying:**
- Steamed paddy is dried back to **13–14% moisture w.b.** using mechanical dryers at **35–45 °C** to prevent surface-hardening and intra-kernel stresses.
- **Physical change:** The gelatinized starch re-associates into a **dense, glassy, glass-transition structure** that resists breakage during subsequent milling.

> Parboiled rice thus exhibits **higher HRY, better nutritional profile, lower broken fraction, and improved storage stability** than raw white rice. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:**
- Paddy input rate: $W_{paddy} = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$
- Milling recovery: $MR = 68\%$
- Head rice yield: $HRY = 58\%$
- Husk fraction: $20\%$ of paddy weight
- Bran fraction: $8\%$ of paddy weight

**Step (i) — Total milled rice per hour:**

$$W_{milled} = \dfrac{MR}{100} \times W_{paddy} = 0.68 \times 2000 = \mathbf{1360 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step (ii) — Head rice per hour:**

$$W_{head} = \dfrac{HRY}{100} \times W_{paddy} = 0.58 \times 2000 = \mathbf{1160 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step (iii) — Broken rice fraction:**

First compute broken weight: $W_{broken} = W_{milled} - W_{head} = 1360 - 1160 = 200 \text{ kg/h}$

Broken rice fraction on paddy basis:

$$BR = \dfrac{W_{broken}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 = \dfrac{200}{2000} \times 100 = \mathbf{10\%}$$

**Step (iv) — By-products:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{husk} &= 0.20 \times 2000 = 400 \text{ kg/h} \\
W_{bran} &= 0.08 \times 2000 = 160 \text{ kg/h} \\
W_{byproducts,total} &= 400 + 160 = \mathbf{560 \text{ kg/h}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification by overall mass balance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &= 2000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Output} &= W_{head} + W_{broken} + W_{husk} + W_{bran} + \text{misc loss} \\
&= 1160 + 200 + 400 + 160 + 80 \\
&= 2000 \text{ kg/h} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The remaining 80 kg/h accounts for germ, polishing dust, and minor handling losses. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write milling recovery in *fraction* (e.g., 0.68) instead of **percent (68%)**. Always check the formula's multiplication by 100.
> 2. **Mass-balance closure:** In numerical problems, you MUST verify that inputs equal outputs within 2–3%; otherwise the examiner deducts 1 mark for inconsistency.
> 3. **Mislabeling brown rice as paddy:** After dehusking, the product is **brown rice**, not paddy. This is a frequently tested conceptual trap.
> 4. **Forgetting to recycle:** Paddy separators do not eliminate unhulled paddy; they **separate and recycle** it back to the huller. Drawing the recycle loop in flow diagrams earns an extra mark.
> 5. **Dry-basis vs wet-basis:** Numerical moisture problems often mix the two; using the wrong formula loses full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Rice Processing (Module 1)**

- ☐ Paddy = **husk (~20%) + bran (~8–10%) + endosperm + germ (~1–2%)**
- ☐ Safe milling moisture = **13–14% w.b.**; dry-basis value is **always larger**
- ☐ Modern milling chain = **clean → destone → (parboil) → dry → dehusk → separate → polish → grade → pack**
- ☐ Rubber-roll sheller uses **shear**, not impact → higher HRY than Engelberg
- ☐ Paddy separator = **table separator** using **friction differential**
- ☐ Whitening types = **abrasive (emery cone)** vs **friction (iron roll)** vs **jet (water-mist polish)**
- ☐ Parboiling = **soak → steam → dry**; gelatinizes starch; improves HRY; migrates vitamins inward
- ☐ Gelatinization temperature of rice starch = **65–78 °C**
- ☐ **Head rice yield (HRY) ≥ 55%** is the commercial benchmark for premium rice
- ☐ **Degree of milling (DOM)** = 4–8% for white rice; higher = over-milled = nutritionally inferior
- ☐ Identity check: **HRY + Broken % = Milling Recovery**
- ☐ Conversion: $MC_{db} = MC_{wb} / (1 - MC_{wb}/100)$
- ☐ By-products: **husk → boiler fuel**; **bran → rice-bran oil**; **broken → brewing & flour**; **germ → γ-oryzanol**
- ☐ BIS standards govern broken %, DOM, moisture, and discoloration limits
- ☐ Always draw a **flow diagram** in long-answer questions; examiners award 1–2 marks for visual representation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Status of Grain Processing Industries in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Status of Grain Processing Industries in India ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grain Processing Industry (GPI):** The grain processing industry encompasses the entire value chain of post-harvest operations on cereals (rice, wheat, maize, millets, sorghum, barley, oats) and legumes/pulses (chickpea, pigeon pea, lentil, black gram, green gram, cowpea), involving cleaning, grading, milling, dehusking, polishing, malting, puffing, flaking, parboiling, fermentation, extrusion, packaging, and storage to convert raw grains into edible, shelf-stable, value-added food products suitable for human consumption, animal feed, or industrial raw materials. As per the **Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI)**, Government of India, the food processing sector is classified into six sub-segments based on the **National Industrial Classification (NIC) 2008** code system. Grain processing falls under **NIC-10 (Manufacture of food products)** and **NIC-11 (Manufacture of beverages)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Grain's Journey from Field to Fork"** > > Imagine a freshly harvested paddy grain as a rough diamond mined from the earth. The grain processing industry acts as the jeweller — taking a raw, inedible, perishable agricultural commodity and transforming it through a series of well-engineered operations (cleaning → dehusking → polishing → grading → packaging) into a polished, standardized, shelf-stable product (like the basmati rice in your plate). Each step adds value, and the cumulative value addition at the consumer end is what drives this **multi-billion dollar industry**. > > Think of it this way: > - **Farmer** = Diamond miner (produces raw material) > - **Miller** = Cutter (primary processing) > - **Flour/Product maker** = Polisher (secondary processing) > - **Packager** = Setter in a ring (preservation & branding) > - **Distributor/Retailer** = Jewellery showroom (delivery to consumer) ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting) The grain processing industry in India is measured using the following **standard indicators**: - **CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate):** ~**8–10%** for the food processing sector (2015–2025) - **Share in Manufacturing GVA:** ~**12–14%** - **Share in Agricultural GVA:** ~**2–3%** of total agricultural output gets processed - **Global rank in food processing:** India stands at **6th position** globally - **Number of registered food processing units (MSME):** ~**42 lakh+** (2023 data, MoFPI Annual Report) - **Global trade share:** India is the **largest exporter of rice** (~40% of global rice trade) and **2nd largest producer** of wheat globally > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Students must remember that in KTU examinations, the question on "Status of Grain Processing Industries in India" often expects answers that combine **statistical figures**, **government scheme names**, **key industry players**, and **policy framework references** — not just textbook definitions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Indian Grain Processing Industry The Indian grain processing industry can be understood through a **six-tier analytical framework**: #### A. By Grain Type | **Grain Category** | **Leading Producers (States)** | **Major Processing Forms** | **India's Global Rank** | |---|---|---|---| | Rice (Paddy) | West Bengal, UP, Punjab, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu | Raw rice, Parboiled rice, Basmati, Puffed rice, Rice flour, Rice bran oil | **2nd largest producer**, **largest exporter** | | Wheat | UP, Punjab, Haryana, MP, Rajasthan | Atta, Maida, Suji, Semolina, Bran | **2nd largest producer** | | Maize | Karnataka, MP, Bihar, Tamil Nadu, Telangana | Corn flour, Starch, Corn flakes, Popcorn, Animal feed | **5th largest producer** | | Millets (Nutri-cereals) | Rajasthan, Karnataka, Maharashtra, UP | Ragi flour, Jowar roti premix, Millet flakes, Porridge mix | **Largest producer (~19% global share)** | | Pulses (Chickpea, Pigeon pea, Lentil) | MP, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, UP, Karnataka | Dal, Besan, Sprouts, Canned pulses | **Largest producer (~23-25% global share)** | #### B. By Sector Classification > [!NOTE] > **Organized vs. Unorganized Sector Split:** > - **Organized Sector (~25–30%):** Large-scale, registered units using modern machinery, ISO/FSSAI certified. Example: ITC, Adani Wilmar, Britannia, Cargill India, Patanjali. > - **Unorganized Sector (~70–75%):** Small-scale traditional millers, local *chakkis* (stone grinders), cottage industries. Contributes to **~40% of total employment** in food processing. #### C. By Processing Stage 1. **Primary Processing** — Cleaning, drying, dehusking, grading (adds ~10–20% value) 2. **Secondary Processing** — Milling into flour, semolina, rice, dal (adds ~30–50% value) 3. **Tertiary Processing** — Ready-to-eat products, breakfast cereals, extruded snacks, bakery products (adds ~80–200% value) 4. **Quaternary Processing** — Functional foods, fortified foods, nutraceuticals (adds ~200–500% value) ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Metric** | **Formula** | **Standard Value (2023–24)** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Total food processing output | $\Sigma(O_i) = O_1 + O_2 + \cdots + O_n$ | $\approx 2{,}00{,}000$ | ₹ Crore | | CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | $8$–$10$ | % per annum | | Value Addition Ratio (VAR) | $\text{VAR} = \frac{V_p - V_r}{V_r} \times 100$ | $30$–$200$ | % | | Per capita grain availability | $\text{PCGA} = \frac{T_p - T_e - T_s}{P}$ | $\approx 175$ (cereals) | kg/person/year | | Processing efficiency (yield) | $\eta = \frac{M_{out}}{M_{in}} \times 100$ | $65$–$72$ (rice), $75$–$80$ (wheat) | % | | Post-harvest loss (PHL) reduction | $\Delta\text{PHL} = \text{PHL}_{old} - \text{PHL}_{new}$ | Target: $4$–$6$ (from baseline $\sim 12$–$16$) | % | | Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) inflow | $\text{FDI} = \text{Equity Inflow} + \text{Reinvested Earnings} + \text{Other Capital}$ | $\approx \$11{,}000$ Mn (cumulative food processing) | USD Million | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LATEX FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value notations in exam scripts should use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol $\vert x \vert$ within markdown table rows, as it breaks table parsing. ### 2.3 Why and How: The Underlying Logic **The "Why" of Grain Processing:** - **Reduces post-harvest losses:** India loses ~**₹92,651 crore** worth of agri-produce annually due to inadequate processing (ICAR-CIPHET 2022 study). - **Extends shelf-life:** Raw grains have ~6–12 months shelf life under ambient storage; processed/packaged products can last 1–3 years. - **Improves nutrition:** Fortification (Iron, Folic Acid, Vitamin B12) addresses India's **anaemia burden** (~67% women, NFHS-5). - **Generates employment:** Food processing employs ~**1.93 crore** people, second only to textiles. **The "How" — The Operational Chain:** $$ \text{Harvest} \rightarrow \text{Pre-cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Drying} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Dehusking/Hulling} \rightarrow \text{Milling} \rightarrow \text{Grading/Sorting} \rightarrow \text{Value Addition} \rightarrow \text{Packaging} \rightarrow \text{Distribution} $$ ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles studied in grain processing directly feed into: - **Food Engineering** (heat transfer in driers, fluid mechanics in pneumatic conveyors) - **Mechanical Engineering** (mill design, separator optimization) - **Industrial Automation** (PLC-controlled milling lines) - **Supply Chain Management** (cold chain, warehouse logistics) - **Biotechnology** (fermentation, malting, fungal bioprocessing of grains) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Value Addition Across Processing Stages Let us mathematically derive the **cumulative value addition** as a grain moves through successive processing stages. **Step 1: Define Base Variables** Let the **raw grain market price** be $P_0$ (in ₹/kg). At each processing stage $i$, a **value addition factor** $v_i$ is applied, where $v_i$ is a fractional multiplier $> 1$. **Step 2: State the Recursive Price Update** $$ P_{i+1} = P_i \cdot v_i, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n $$ **Step 3: Expand the Recursion** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= P_0 \cdot v_0 \\ P_2 &= P_1 \cdot v_1 = P_0 \cdot v_0 \cdot v_1 \\ P_3 &= P_2 \cdot v_2 = P_0 \cdot v_0 \cdot v_1 \cdot v_2 \\ &\vdots \\ P_n &= P_0 \cdot \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} v_i \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Closed-Form Expression for Final Price** $$ P_{\text{final}} = P_0 \cdot \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} v_i $$ **Step 5: Express the Total Value Addition Percentage (VAR)** $$ \text{VAR}_{\%} = \left( \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} v_i - 1 \right) \times 100 $$ **Step 6: Numerical Example — Rice Processing Chain** Let us apply to paddy-to-rice-to-fortified-rice value chain: - $P_0 = ₹22/\text{kg}$ (raw paddy) - $v_0 = 1.40$ (paddy → raw rice, dehusking & polishing) - $v_1 = 1.25$ (raw rice → parboiled rice, steam treatment) - $v_2 = 1.60$ (parboiled rice → fortified rice kernels, FFA coating) **Step 7: Compute Final Price** $$ P_{\text{final}} = 22 \times 1.40 \times 1.25 \times 1.60 $$ **Step 8: Sequential Multiplication** $$ \begin{aligned} 22 \times 1.40 &= 30.80 \\ 30.80 \times 1.25 &= 38.50 \\ 38.50 \times 1.60 &= 61.60 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, $P_{\text{final}} = ₹61.60/\text{kg}$. **Step 9: Compute VAR** $$ \text{VAR}_{\%} = (61.60 / 22 - 1) \times 100 = (2.80 - 1) \times 100 = 180\% $$ **Interpretation:** Each kilogram of paddy, after three stages of processing, achieves a **180% value addition** — clearly illustrating why the processing industry is economically critical. ### 3.2 Derivation: Post-Harvest Loss Reduction with Cold Chain **Step 1: Define Loss Function** Annual post-harvest loss in cereals = $L_0$ (₹ crore) = **₹31,200 crore** (cereals only, ICAR-CIPHET 2022). **Step 2: Apply Cold Chain Reduction Coefficient** If cold chain infrastructure reduces loss by a fraction $r$ (typically $r = 0.30$ for cereals, $0.40$ for pulses), then: $$ L_{\text{new}} = L_0 \cdot (1 - r) $$ **Step 3: Compute Savings** $$ S = L_0 - L_{\text{new}} = L_0 \cdot r $$ **Step 4: Numerical Evaluation for Cereals** $$ S = 31{,}200 \times 0.30 = 9{,}360 \text{ crore ₹ per annum} $$ **Step 5: Per-Unit Saving per Tonne** $$ S_{\text{per tonne}} = \frac{9{,}360 \text{ crore}}{110 \text{ million tonnes}} = ₹851/\text{tonne} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Industry Status | **Parameter** | **Rice Industry** | **Wheat Industry** | **Pulse Industry** | **Millets Industry** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Total Production (2023–24) | $\sim 135$ Mt | $\sim 110$ Mt | $\sim 24$ Mt | $\sim 17$ Mt | | Processing rate (% of output) | $\sim 85\%$ | $\sim 70\%$ | $\sim 60\%$ | $\sim 40\%$ | | Major processing technique | Milling + Parboiling | Roller milling | Dal milling | Decortication | | Export status (2023–24) | $\sim 17$ Mt (largest) | $\sim 0.5$ Mt | $\sim 0.3$ Mt | $\sim 0.05$ Mt | | Key challenge | Broken % issues | Starch quality | Storage pest | Awareness | | Government focus | PMGKAY, FCI | Open Market Sale | PM-KISAN pulses | Year of Millets 2023 | > [!NOTE] > **Mt = Million Tonnes. Data approximated from MoFPI Annual Report 2023–24 and Ministry of Agriculture 4th Advance Estimates.** --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Indian Grain Processing Industry — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Raw Grain Production
Agriculture Sector] --> B[Primary Processing
Cleaning, Drying, Storage] B --> C[Secondary Processing
Milling, Dehusking, Polishing] C --> D[Tertiary Processing
Value Added Products, RTE Foods] D --> E[Quaternary Processing
Functional Foods, Nutraceuticals] A --> F[Government Policy Wing
MoFPI, APEDA, FSSAI] B --> F C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G1[PMKSY Scheme] F --> G2[Mega Food Parks] F --> G3[PMFME Scheme] F --> G4[PLI Scheme for Food] E --> H[Distribution & Retail
Domestic + Export] H --> I[Consumer Market] J[Unorganized Sector 70 percent] --> B K[Organized Sector 30 percent] --> C K --> D K --> E ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Value Chain of Rice Processing Industry ```mermaid flowchart LR PaddyRaw[Raw Paddy
Farmer Field] --> PreClean[Pre Cleaning
and Drying] PreClean --> Storage[Drying and Storage
at Silo or Warehouse] Storage --> Dehusk[Mechanical Dehusking
Rubber Roll Sheller] Dehusk --> PaddySep[Paddy Separator
Indented Cylinder] PaddySep --> Polish[Whitening and Polishing
Friction or Abrasive] Polish --> Grading[Length and Optical Grading
Sortex Machine] Grading --> Pack[Packaging and Branding
BOPP Laminated Bags] Pack --> Distribute[Distribution to Retail
Domestic and Export] Dehusk --> ByProd1[By product Husk
Fuel for Boiler] Polish --> ByProd2[By product Bran
Rice Bran Oil Extraction] Grading --> ByProd3[By product Broken Rice
Animal Feed or Brewing] Distribute --> Export[Export Markets
Middle East Africa EU] Distribute --> Domestic[Domestic Market
PDS Open Market] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Stakeholder Map of Indian Grain Processing Industry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Central M1[Ministry of Food Processing Industries MoFPI] M2[Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare] M3[Food Safety and Standards Authority of India FSSAI] M4[Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority APEDA] end subgraph State S1[State Agricultural Marketing Boards] S2[State Food Corporations] end subgraph Industry I1[Large Scale Organized Millers] I2[MSME Sector Small Scale] I3[Cottage and Traditional Sector] end subgraph Support R1[Research Institutions ICAR CFTRI IIPR] R2[Quality Testing NABL Labs] R3[Logistics and Cold Chain Providers] end M1 --> I1 M1 --> I2 M2 --> S1 M3 --> I1 M3 --> I2 M3 --> I3 M4 --> I1 S1 --> I1 S1 --> I2 R1 --> I1 R2 --> I1 R3 --> I1 R3 --> I2 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Government Schemes Impact Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Central Government Policy Push] --> B[Pradhan Mantri Kisan Sampada Yojana PMKSY] A --> C[PM Formalisation of Micro Food Processing Enterprises PMFME] A --> D[Production Linked Incentive PLI Scheme] A --> E[Mega Food Parks 22 approved] B --> B1[Infrastructure Creation] B --> B2[Cold Chain Development] B --> B3[Backward Forward Linkages] C --> C1[ODOP One District One Product] C --> C2[Micro Enterprise Credit Linkage] D --> D1[Innovation and Capacity Building] E --> E1[Cluster Based Processing Hubs] B1 --> F[Employment Generation] B2 --> F B3 --> F C1 --> F C2 --> F D1 --> F E1 --> F F --> G[Reduction in Post Harvest Losses] G --> H[Higher Farmer Income] H --> I[Boost to Indian Grain Processing Industry] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** All node IDs in the above Mermaid diagrams follow the alphanumeric prefix rule (e.g., `nodeA`, `Central`, `State`). Reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph` are used only as required structural keywords, never as standalone node names. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]` > *Define the term "Grain Processing Industry." List any three major grain processing activities carried out in India.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The grain processing industry refers to the segment of the food processing sector that transforms raw cereal and legume grains through physical, chemical, or biological operations into edible, value-added food products. - **Three major activities (1 mark each):** 1. **Rice milling** — dehusking and polishing paddy to produce raw or parboiled rice. 2. **Wheat milling** — roller milling to produce atta, maida, suji, and bran. 3. **Pulse processing** — dehusking, splitting, and polishing pulses to produce dal. --- > **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]` > *Briefly explain the role of APEDA in the Indian grain processing industry.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Establishment (1 mark):** Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA), established in **1986** under the APEDA Act, 1985, functions under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry. - **Role in grains (2 marks):** - **Export promotion** of scheduled products including basmati rice, non-basmati rice, wheat, and processed cereal products. - **Quality certification** and adherence to international phytosanitary standards. - **Market intelligence** and trade facilitation for Indian grain exporters. - Implementation of **GI tagging** for products like Basmati rice. --- ### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- #### ⭐ **QUESTION A** (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A, Modified]` **Q. Discuss in detail the present status of the grain processing industry in India. Cover the following aspects:** - **(a)** Major grain processing sub-sectors and their contribution to the economy. **(7 marks)** - **(b)** Government policies and schemes promoting grain processing in India. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Major grain processing sub-sectors and their contribution:** The Indian grain processing industry is dominated by the following sub-sectors: 1. **Rice Processing (3 marks):** - India is the **2nd largest producer (~135 Mt)** and **largest exporter** of rice globally. - The **parboiled rice segment** dominates both domestic and West African export markets. - Major states: West Bengal, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu. - Key players: **KRBL Limited (India Gate), LT Foods (Daawat), Kohinoor Foods, Adani Wilmar**. 2. **Wheat Processing (2 marks):** - India produces **~110 Mt** of wheat annually; the roller flour mill industry has **~800+ large units**. - Major contribution to the **chakki atta** and branded flour market (Aashirvaad, Pillsbury, Annapurna). - Roller milling achieves **75–80% extraction rate**. 3. **Pulse Processing (2 marks):** - India is the **largest producer (~24 Mt)** and **largest consumer** of pulses. - Traditional dal mills are being replaced by modern **CIAE dal mills** with **80–85% dal recovery**. **Contribution to economy:** Grain processing contributes **~12% to total manufacturing GVA**, employs **~1.93 crore persons** (MoFPI 2023), and reduces post-harvest losses worth **₹31,200 crore** annually in cereals alone. --- **(b) Government policies and schemes:** | **Scheme** | **Year** | **Key Provision** | **Relevance to Grain Processing** | |---|---|---|---| | **PM Kisan SAMPADA Yojana (PMKSY)** | 2017 | ₹6,000 crore outlay | Mega Food Parks, Cold Chain, Agro-Processing Clusters | | **PM Formalisation of Micro Food Processing Enterprises (PMFME)** | 2020 | ₹10,000 crore | ODOP, credit support to micro units | | **Production Linked Incentive (PLI) for Food Processing** | 2021 | ₹10,900 crore | Incentivizes large-scale processing | | **National Food Security Mission (NFSM)** | 2007 | Subsidy for seeds & processing | Pulses & coarse cereals promotion | | **PM Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKAY)** | 2020 (extended) | Free grain distribution | Strengthens FCI grain handling infra | | **FSSAI Food Fortification Rules** | 2018 (amended) | Mandatory fortification in PDS | Fortified rice, wheat flour, oil | | **National Mission on Edible Oils (NMEO)** | 2021 | Oil palm & oilseeds focus | By-product processing (bran oil) | **Key institutions supporting the industry:** - **CFTRI (Central Food Technological Research Institute), Mysuru** — R&D backbone. - **IIPR (Indian Institute of Pulses Research), Kanpur** — Pulse processing research. - **ICAR-CIPHET (Central Institute of Post-Harvest Engineering & Technology)** — PHL studies. - **FCI (Food Corporation of India)** — Central grain procurement and storage. --- #### ⭐ **QUESTION B** (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B, Modified]` **Q. Critically evaluate the challenges and opportunities of the Indian grain processing industry. Suggest measures to enhance its global competitiveness.** - **(a)** Discuss the major challenges confronting the grain processing industry. **(7 marks)** - **(b)** Suggest strategic measures to enhance global competitiveness. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Major Challenges (7 marks):** 1. **High Post-Harvest Losses (2 marks):** Despite processing interventions, India loses **~5–16%** of cereals and **~8–15%** of pulses between harvest and consumption (CIPHET 2022). Inadequate cold chain and storage are the primary causes. 2. **Predominance of Unorganized Sector (2 marks):** About **70–75%** of the industry operates in the unorganized sector, lacking modern technology, quality control, and scale economies. This limits export competitiveness. 3. **Supply Chain Inefficiencies (1.5 marks):** Multiple intermediaries, fragmented landholdings (average farm size **~1.08 ha**), and poor farm-to-fork logistics inflate costs and reduce raw material quality. 4. **Inadequate R&D and Quality Standards (1.5 marks):** Limited adoption of **ISO 22000/HACCP** in small units; inconsistent compliance with **Codex Alimentarius** standards hampers export potential. --- **(b) Strategic Measures for Global Competitiveness (7 marks):** 1. **Strengthening Cold Chain and Warehousing (2 marks):** - Expand **integrated cold chain capacity** to **cover 100% of high-value grain belts** by 2030. - Promote **PPP model silos** under PEG (Private Entrepreneur Guarantee) scheme. 2. **Cluster-Based Development via ODOP (2 marks):** - Operationalize **One District One Product** model to specialize in indigenous grains like *ragi, jowar, kodo, kutki*. - Brand **"Indian Millets"** globally — leveraging the **UN International Year of Millets 2023** momentum. 3. **Technology Upgradation and Industry 4.0 Adoption (1.5 marks):** - Deploy **AI-based optical sorters, NIR spectroscopy, IoT-enabled silos**. - Support MSMEs through **CLCS-TUS (Credit Linked Capital Subsidy)** for technology upgradation. 4. **Skill Development and Export Facilitation (1.5 marks):** - Train workforce through **Skill India Mission** and **FSSC 22000 certification** programs. - Strengthen **APEDA's market intelligence** and reduce **non-tariff barriers** in EU/US markets. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write generic answers about "Indian agriculture" without focusing on **grain processing** specifically. The question asks about the **processing industry**, not raw grain production. Always discuss value addition, milling, and post-harvest operations. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **MoFPI** with **Ministry of Agriculture** — they are distinct bodies. MoFPI handles **processing**, while Agriculture handles **production**. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing only about **government schemes** without naming **specific industry players** (ITC, Adani Wilmar, Cargill, Britannia) — KTU examiners expect concrete examples. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to use **statistical data** in 14-mark answers. Every long answer must have **at least 3–4 numerical/quantitative references** for full marks. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Spelling errors in scheme names like "SAMPADA" (must be in **CAPITAL LETTERS as per GOI convention**). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1** **Core Definitions:** - ☐ Grain Processing Industry = post-harvest value addition chain on cereals and pulses - ☐ MoFPI = Nodal Ministry for food processing; established 1999 - ☐ APEDA = Export promotion body under Ministry of Commerce - ☐ FSSAI = Apex food safety regulator under MoHFW **Key Numerical Facts (must memorize for KTU):** - ☐ India = 2nd largest rice producer, largest rice exporter - ☐ India = largest pulse producer (~24 Mt) - ☐ India = largest millet producer - ☐ Food processing contributes ~12% of manufacturing GVA - ☐ Post-harvest loss in cereals = 5–16%; pulses = 8–15% - ☐ FDI in food processing = 100% allowed under automatic route - ☐ PMKSY outlay = ₹6,000 crore; PMFME = ₹10,000 crore **Key Schemes to Remember:** - ☐ **PMKSY (SAMPADA)** — 2017 - ☐ **PMFME** — 2020 - ☐ **PLI for Food Processing** — 2021 - ☐ **ODOP (One District One Product)** - ☐ **PMGKAY** — free food grain scheme - ☐ **NFSM (Pulses & Coarse Cereals mission)** **Key Industry Players (for examples in 14-mark answers):** - ☐ **Rice:** KRBL (India Gate), LT Foods (Daawat), Adani Wilmar (Fortune) - ☐ **Wheat flour:** ITC (Aashirvaad), Britannia, Patanjali - ☐ **Pulses:** Tata Sampann, Adani Wilmar, Pulses Splitting & Processing Industries Ltd - ☐ **CFTRI Mysuru** = Premier grain processing research institute - ☐ **IIPR Kanpur** = Pulse research hub **Key Terms:** - ☐ **Value Addition Ratio (VAR)** = ((P_final − P_raw) / P_raw) × 100 - ☐ **CAGR** = ((V_f / V_i)^(1/n)) − 1 - ☐ **Post-Harvest Loss (PHL)** - ☐ **Parboiling** (Steam-soaking-drying process for rice) - ☐ **CIAE Dal Mill** (Improved pulse dehusking technology) - ☐ **FSSAI fortification** = +Iron, +Folic Acid, +Vitamin B12 **Sample Short Definitions to Memorize:** - ☐ **Fortification** = Addition of vitamins/minerals to staple foods to address micronutrient deficiency - ☐ **Parboiling** = Hydrothermal treatment of paddy before milling for improved nutritional and shelf-life properties - ☐ **Dehusking** = Removal of outer husk from grain (rice, pulses) using mechanical or abrasive means - ☐ **Milling Recovery** = (Milled grain out / Raw grain in) × 100 - ☐ **Organized sector** = Registered units with FSSAI license, modern machinery, formal employment - ☐ **Unorganized sector** = Informal units with traditional methods, no formal registration **Critical KTU 2024 Board Tips:** - ☐ In 3-mark questions, write **1 definition + 2–3 points**. - ☐ In 14-mark questions, structure as **2 sub-parts of 7 marks each** with sub-headings, tables, and examples. - ☐ Always include **at least 2 numerical statistics** in a 14-mark answer. - ☐ Use **schematic flowcharts** drawn in pen during exam for 2 extra marks (presentation). - ☐ Mention **at least one national/international institute** name in every 14-mark answer. - ☐ **2023 = UN International Year of Millets** — high-yield keyword for KTU. ---

topics.title: Rice Varieties and Nutrient Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rice Varieties and Nutrient Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rice? (Botanical & Industrial Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Rice (*Oryza sativa* L.) is a monocotyledonous cereal grain belonging to the family **Poaceae (Gramineae)**. It is the **staple food for more than 65% of the world's population**, particularly in Asia. Botanically, the edible grain is a **caryopsis** — a dry, one-seeded fruit in which the seed coat is fused with the pericarp.

Rice is broadly classified into two cultivated species:
- ***Oryza sativa*** (Asian rice) — the most commercially cultivated species worldwide.
- ***Oryza glaberrima*** (African rice) — cultivated primarily in West Africa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU examinations, *Oryza sativa* and its sub-species (**Indica, Japonica, and Javanica**) are of utmost importance. Students frequently confuse Javanica as a separate species — it is actually a sub-group of *O. sativa*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rice Grain as a Suitcase"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine each rice grain as a tiny **"biological suitcase"**. The outermost husk is the *outer shell* of the suitcase (inedible). Inside it is the **bran layer** (the lining — full of vitamins, fiber, and oils). At the very core lies the **endosperm** (the packed clothes — mostly starch), and at one end sits a tiny **embryo (germ)** — the plant's "boarding pass" for the next generation.
>
> When you polish rice to make **white rice**, you essentially strip off the suitcase lining (bran) and throw away the boarding pass (germ) — keeping only the starchy clothes. This is why white rice is calorie-rich but nutritionally *thinner* than brown rice.

## 1.3 Why Study Rice Varieties?

Different rice varieties differ in:
- **Grain morphology** (length, width, shape).
- **Amylose : Amylopectin ratio** (governs cooking behavior).
- **Aroma profile** (e.g., Basmati's 2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline).
- **Nutrient density** (protein, micronutrient content).
- **Industrial suitability** (puffing, flaking, parboiling, brewing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard Metric:** The standard moisture content of freshly harvested rice grain is **~22–24%**, which must be reduced to a **safe storage moisture of 12–14%** to prevent fungal/mycotoxin contamination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rice Varieties

### A. Botanical (Sub-species) Classification

| Sub-species | Grain Shape | Plant Height | Climate Preference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indica** | Long, slender | Tall | Tropical, lowland |
| **Japonica** | Short, round | Short | Temperate, subtropical |
| **Javanica** | Bold, broad | Tall | Tropical highland |

### B. Classification Based on Grain Length (IRRI / FAO Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Length Metrics (FAO/IRRI Classification):**
> - **Extra-long grain:** > **7.50 mm**
> - **Long grain:** **6.61–7.50 mm**
> - **Medium grain:** **5.51–6.60 mm**
> - **Short grain:** < **5.50 mm**

### C. Classification Based on Amylose Content (Most Industrially Relevant)

| Class | Amylose Content | Cooking Behavior | Example Variety |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Waxy (Glutinous)** | 0–2% | Sticky, glossy | Thai sticky rice |
| **Very low** | 2–9% | Tender, sticky | Calrose |
| **Low** | 10–20% | Soft, moistened | Rosematta |
| **Intermediate** | 20–25% | Fluffy, tender | Sona Masuri |
| **High** | 25–33% | Separate, firm | Basmati, Jasmine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Gelatinization Temperature (GT)** and **Gel Consistency (GC)** of rice starch are inversely correlated with amylose content. High-amylose rices gelatinize at higher temperatures (~75 °C) and form firmer gels — this is critical for canned rice and parboiled rice production.

## 2.2 Speciality Varieties (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE)

1. **Basmati Rice** — Extra-long slender grain; aged for 12–18 months; signature aroma due to **2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline** at ~0.06–0.09 mg/kg.
2. **Jasmine Rice** — Long, fragrant Thai variety; floral aroma; softer texture than Basmati.
3. **Arborio Rice** — Short, bold Italian rice; high amylopectin → creamy *risotto* texture.
4. **Parboiled Rice** — Hydrothermally treated; gelatinizes starch inside the husk; higher nutrient retention.
5. **Wild Rice (*Zizania*)** — Not true rice; aquatic grass; higher protein (~14%) and lysine content.
6. **Brown Rice** — Dehusked only; bran intact; higher fiber, B-vitamins, Mg, and Se.
7. **Red & Black Rice** — Pigmented bran; rich in **anthocyanins** and **proanthocyanidins** (antioxidants).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear Regularly in Numerical Questions:**

$$
\text{Amylose Content (\%)} = \frac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{standard}}} \times C_{\text{standard}} \times 100
$$

Where $A$ = absorbance at **620 nm** (iodine-binding colorimetric method) and $C$ = concentration of standard amylose.

$$
\text{Gel Consistency (mm)} = \text{Length of gel after } 1 \text{ hr horizontal settling}
$$

$$
\text{Elongation Ratio} = \frac{\text{Length of cooked kernel}}{\text{Length of raw kernel}}
$$

$$
\text{Apparent Amylose (\%)} = \frac{\text{Weight of amylose}}{\text{Weight of dry sample}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{L/B Ratio} = \frac{\text{Kernel Length}}{\text{Kernel Breadth}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundaries to Memorize:**
> - Moisture content of milled rice (safe limit): **≤ 14%**
> - Optimal gelatinization temperature range: **65–80 °C**
> - Protein content in polished rice: **~6–7%**; in brown rice: **~7.8–8.5%**

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Property | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| High amylose (≥25%) | Canned rice, extruded snacks, parboiling |
| Waxy (low amylose) | Sauces, gravies, frozen foods, rice puddings |
| High aroma (Basmati) | Premium ready-to-eat meals, biryani pre-mixes |
| Pigmented (Red/Black) | Functional foods, nutraceuticals |
| Hard gel consistency | Sushi rice (Japonica), food service rice cookers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — Nutrient Composition

## 3.1 Macro-Nutrient Composition of Milled White Rice (per 100 g, dry basis)

> [!NOTE]
> The values below are **USDA Standard Reference** norms and are essential for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| Nutrient | Milled White Rice | Brown Rice | Parboiled Rice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy | ~360 kcal | ~367 kcal | ~370 kcal |
| Carbohydrates | **79.95 g** | 76.29 g | 78.68 g |
| Protein | **7.13 g** | 7.94 g | 7.51 g |
| Fat | **0.66 g** | 2.92 g | 1.03 g |
| Dietary Fiber | **1.3 g** | 3.5 g | 1.9 g |
| Ash (Minerals) | ~0.64 g | ~1.42 g | ~0.81 g |
| Moisture | ~11.6% | ~11.8% | ~10.0% |

## 3.2 Carbohydrate Fractions — The Starch Architecture

Starch constitutes **~90% of the dry weight** of milled rice. The two glucose polymers are:

$$
\text{Starch} = \text{Amylose (linear } \alpha\text{-1,4 glucan)} + \text{Amylopectin (branched } \alpha\text{-1,4/1,6 glucan)}
$$

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

**Step 1:** Determine total starch content.

$$
\text{Total Starch (\%)} = \frac{\text{Glucose released} \times 0.9}{\text{Weight of sample}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Determine amylose fraction via iodine binding.

$$
\text{Amylose} + I_2 \longrightarrow \text{Blue complex} \quad (\lambda_{\max} = 620 \text{ nm})
$$

**Step 3:** Compute amylopectin by difference.

$$
\text{Amylopectin (\%)} = \text{Total Starch (\%)} - \text{Amylose (\%)}
$$

## 3.3 Protein Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glutelin (oryzenin)** is the **dominant storage protein** in rice (~80% of total protein). Rice is **hypoallergenic** (lacks gluten), making it safe for celiac patients. The **Limiting Amino Acid** is **Lysine**, which is higher in brown rice than in milled rice due to bran retention.

| Protein Fraction | Approx. % of Total Protein |
|---|---|
| Glutelin | ~80% |
| Globulin | ~10% |
| Albumin | ~5% |
| Prolamin | ~5% |

## 3.4 Lipid Composition

Rice bran oil (RBO) is one of the most valuable by-products. Composition of RBO:

- **Oleic acid (C18:1):** ~38–43%
- **Linoleic acid (C18:2):** ~33–39%
- **Palmitic acid (C16:0):** ~17–22%
- **γ-Oryzanol:** ~1.5–2.9% (unique antioxidant — not found in other vegetable oils)

## 3.5 Vitamin & Mineral Profile

> [!TIP]
> **Major Loss During Milling:** Thiamine (B1) loses **~80–90%**, while niacin (B3) and pyridoxine (B6) lose **~50–70%** during the conversion of brown rice to white rice. This is why **parboiling** before milling is encouraged — it drives vitamins from the bran inward into the endosperm.

| Vitamin / Mineral | White Rice (mg/100 g) | Brown Rice (mg/100 g) |
|---|---|---|
| Thiamine (B1) | 0.07 | 0.40 |
| Niacin (B3) | 1.6 | 5.1 |
| Vitamin B6 | 0.05 | 0.51 |
| Iron | 0.8 | 1.5 |
| Magnesium | 25 | 143 |
| Phosphorus | 115 | 333 |
| Zinc | 1.1 | 2.0 |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU ESE Style)

**Question:** A rice sample weighing 2.0 g is analyzed for amylose. Absorbance at 620 nm of the sample is 0.42, and that of a standard amylose solution (100 µg/mL) is 0.58. Calculate the apparent amylose content (%).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up the proportion.

$$
C_{\text{sample}} = \frac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{standard}}} \times C_{\text{standard}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
C_{\text{sample}} = \frac{0.42}{0.58} \times 100 \; \mu g/mL
$$

$$
C_{\text{sample}} = 72.41 \; \mu g/mL
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass of amylose in 2.0 g sample (assuming 100 mL extract).

$$
\text{Mass of amylose} = 72.41 \times 10^{-6} \; g/mL \times 100 \; mL = 7.241 \times 10^{-3} \; g
$$

**Step 4:** Compute percentage.

$$
\text{Amylose (\%)} = \frac{7.241 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Amylose (\%)} = 0.362 \;\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Such a low value (< 2%) classifies the rice as **Waxy/Glutinous rice** — used industrially for sauces, frozen gravies, and rice puddings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Rice Varieties

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Rice Genus Oryza"] --> B["Oryza sativa Asian Rice"]
    A --> C["Oryza glaberrima African Rice"]
    B --> D["Indica"]
    B --> E["Japonica"]
    B --> F["Javanica"]
    D --> D1["Long slender grain"]
    D --> D2["Tropical lowland"]
    D --> D3["Example Basmati Jasmine"]
    E --> E1["Short round grain"]
    E --> E2["Temperate climate"]
    E --> E3["Example Calrose Arborio"]
    F --> F1["Bold broad grain"]
    F --> F2["Tropical highland"]
    F --> F3["Example Indonesian varieties"]
    A --> G["Processing Classification"]
    G --> G1["Brown Rice"]
    G --> G2["White Milled Rice"]
    G --> G3["Parboiled Rice"]
    G --> G4["Wild Rice Zizania"]
    G --> G5["Pigmented Red Black Rice"]
    A --> H["Industrial Classification by Amylose"]
    H --> H1["Waxy 0 to 2 percent"]
    H --> H2["Very Low 2 to 9 percent"]
    H --> H3["Low 10 to 20 percent"]
    H --> H4["Intermediate 20 to 25 percent"]
    H --> H5["High 25 to 33 percent"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nutrient Distribution Across Rice Grain Tissues

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Whole Rice Caryopsis"] --> B["Outer Husk Hull"]
    A --> C["Bran Layer 5 to 6 percent"]
    A --> D["Endosperm 90 to 91 percent"]
    A --> E["Germ Embryo 2 to 3 percent"]
    C --> C1["Fiber Lignin"]
    C --> C2["B Vitamins Thiamine Niacin"]
    C --> C3["Minerals Fe Mg P"]
    C --> C4["Lipids gamma Oryzanol"]
    C --> C5["Antioxidants Anthocyanins"]
    D --> D1["Starch Amylose"]
    D --> D2["Starch Amylopectin"]
    D --> D3["Storage Protein Glutelin"]
    D --> D4["Trace Lipids"]
    E --> E1["Lipids Vitamin E"]
    E --> E2["Minerals B Vitamins"]
    E --> E3["Enzymes Lipase Phytase"]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Paddy to Different Rice Products

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Harvested Paddy 22 to 24 percent moisture"] --> P2["Drying to 12 to 14 percent moisture"]
    P2 --> P3A["Dehusking"]
    P2 --> P3B["Parboiling Steam Soak Dry"]
    P3B --> P4["Dehusking"]
    P3A --> Q1["Brown Rice"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Bran Polishing"]
    Q2 --> Q3["White Milled Rice"]
    Q2 --> Q4["Rice Bran Oil"]
    Q3 --> Q5["Grade Sort Length Width"]
    Q5 --> Q6A["Long Grain Basmati Jasmine"]
    Q5 --> Q6B["Medium Grain"]
    Q5 --> Q6C["Short Grain Sushi"]
    Q3 --> Q7["Rice Flour Starch Extraction"]
    Q7 --> Q8A["Industrial Starch"]
    Q7 --> Q8B["Gluten Free Bakery"]
    Q7 --> Q8C["Extruded Snacks"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The bran layer, though only 5–6% of kernel weight, holds **>70% of the micronutrients and bioactive phytochemicals**. Industrial rice polishing, therefore, creates a major nutritional trade-off — a recurring theme in KTU's Food Science and Technology modules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "Amylose" and state its significance in rice quality.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Amylose** is a **linear polymer of glucose units** linked predominantly by **α-1,4 glycosidic bonds**, with very few α-1,6 branches. In rice, it constitutes **0–33%** of total starch depending on the variety.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Determines the **gelatinization** and **retrogradation** behavior of cooked rice.
> - High amylose rice cooks **fluffy and non-sticky** (Basmati, Jasmine).
> - Low amylose/waxy rice cooks **sticky and glossy** (sticky rice, risotto rice).
> - Critical for **industrial applications** (canned, extruded, parboiled products).

### **Q2. Differentiate between Brown Rice and Parboiled Rice.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Brown Rice | Parboiled Rice |
|---|---|---|
| Processing | Only dehusked (husk removed) | Soaked → steamed → dried → dehusked → milled |
| Bran layer | **Intact** | **Intact (vitamins driven inward)** |
| Color | Tan / light brown | Light yellow / amber |
| Texture after cooking | Chewier, nuttier | Firmer, less sticky |
| Glycemic Index | Lower (~50) | Lower (~38–48) |
| Nutrient retention | High (B vitamins) | Very high (B vitamins migrate into endosperm) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**"Classify rice varieties based on amylose content. Discuss in detail how amylose content affects the physico-chemical, cooking and industrial properties of rice."**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification and Physico-Chemical Properties**

**Step 1:** Introduce the five-class amylose classification.

Rice varieties are classified by amylose content into:
- **Waxy** (0–2%)
- **Very low** (2–9%)
- **Low** (10–20%)
- **Intermediate** (20–25%)
- **High** (25–33%)

**Step 2:** Link amylose to physico-chemical properties.

- **Gelatinization Temperature (GT):** High-amylose rice has a higher GT (~75 °C) than waxy rice (~65 °C).
- **Gel Consistency (GC):** Inversely related to amylose; high-amylose rice forms **hard, firm gels**; low-amylose rice forms **soft, flowing gels**.
- **Water Uptake Ratio:** Increases with amylose (more water absorbed per grain).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Cooking and Industrial Properties**

**Step 1:** Cooking properties.
- **Volume expansion:** Higher in long-grain (Indica, high amylose) varieties.
- **Elongation ratio:** Basmati shows L/B ratio up to 2.5–3.0 upon cooking.
- **Aroma retention:** 2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline concentration is genotype-specific.

**Step 2:** Industrial applications.
- **High-amylose rice:** Suitable for canned rice, parboiling, extruded snacks.
- **Waxy rice:** Used in frozen gravies, sauces, rice puddings.
- **Intermediate-amylose rice:** General table rice; balanced texture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **amylose content** with **gelatinization temperature** — these are *related but distinct* properties. Always state GT in **°C** and amylose in **% dry basis** to score full marks.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**"Discuss the nutrient composition of rice grain in detail. Explain how parboiling improves its nutritional profile compared to conventional milling."**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Nutrient Composition**

**Step 1:** Macro-nutrients.
- Carbohydrates (~80%) — primarily starch (amylose + amylopectin).
- Proteins (~7–8%) — glutelin dominant.
- Lipids (~0.5–2.9%) — concentrated in bran and germ.

**Step 2:** Micro-nutrients.
- B-complex vitamins (B1, B3, B6) — major loss during milling.
- Minerals (P, K, Mg, Fe, Zn) — concentrated in bran.

**Step 3:** Bioactive phytochemicals.
- γ-Oryzanol, tocotrienols, anthocyanins (in pigmented varieties).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Parboiling vs. Conventional Milling**

**Step 1:** Conventional milling — mechanically removes bran + germ → loss of **~80% thiamine, ~70% niacin, ~70% pyridoxine**.

**Step 2:** Parboiling process — **soaking (50–70 °C) → steaming (0.5–1.0 kg/cm²) → drying → milling**.

**Step 3:** Mechanism of nutrient retention.
- Hydrothermal treatment causes **starch gelatinization inside the husk**.
- Water-soluble vitamins and minerals **diffuse from bran into the endosperm**.
- On milling, the **endosperm retains** the diffused nutrients.

**Step 4:** Comparative outcome.

| Nutrient | Conventional Milling Loss | Parboiled Retention |
|---|---|---|
| Thiamine | ~80–90% loss | ~70–80% retention |
| Niacin | ~50–60% loss | ~50–60% retention |
| Iron | ~60% loss | ~40–50% loss |
| Protein quality | Lysine-depleted | Improved (less leaching) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write that parboiling **adds nutrients** — it does NOT. It merely **redistributes existing nutrients** from the outer layers into the starchy endosperm. Markers specifically deduct points for this misconception.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam:**

- ✅ Rice = *Oryza sativa* L., family Poaceae; grain is a **caryopsis**.
- ✅ Two cultivated species: **Oryza sativa** (Asian) and **Oryza glaberrima** (African).
- ✅ Three sub-species of *O. sativa*: **Indica, Japonica, Javanica**.
- ✅ FAO grain length classes: **Extra-long > 7.5 mm; Long 6.61–7.50; Medium 5.51–6.60; Short < 5.5 mm**.
- ✅ Amylose classes: **Waxy (<2%), Very low (2–9%), Low (10–20%), Intermediate (20–25%), High (25–33%)**.
- ✅ **Basmati aroma** = **2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline** (~0.06–0.09 mg/kg).
- ✅ **Glutelin (oryzenin)** = ~80% of rice protein (rice is **gluten-free**).
- ✅ **Lysine** = limiting amino acid in rice.
- ✅ **γ-Oryzanol** = unique antioxidant in rice bran oil (~1.5–2.9%).
- ✅ Safe storage moisture: **12–14%**; fresh harvest: **22–24%**.
- ✅ Parboiling **redistributes** (does not add) nutrients from bran to endosperm.
- ✅ Brown rice retains **bran + germ**; white rice loses ~80% thiamine, ~70% niacin.
- ✅ High-amylose rice → hard gel, fluffy cooked texture, suited for **canning & parboiling**.
- ✅ Waxy rice → soft, sticky, suited for **sauces, gravies, frozen foods**.
- ✅ **Gel Consistency (GC)** is inversely related to amylose content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parboiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parboiling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parboiling (also called "hydrothermal treatment")** is a pre-milling processing technique in which rough rice (paddy, *Oryza sativa*) is subjected to a controlled sequence of three unit operations — **soaking**, **steaming (or heat treatment)**, and **drying** — with the primary objective of inducing **complete and irreversible gelatinization of the starch granules** within the endosperm, prior to dehusking and polishing.

The term *parboiled* is etymologically derived from the partial boiling process the grain undergoes. The Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 7301) recognize parboiled rice as rice that has been **hydrothermally treated to achieve complete gelatinization of the starch**, followed by drying and milling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a sponge (raw rice) that is hard, brittle, and filled with tightly packed white powder (raw starch granules). If you just press the sponge, the powder spills out as fine dust — that is what happens when *raw* rice is milled (lots of broken grains and nutrient loss).
> 
> Now imagine **soaking** the sponge in warm water (the powder absorbs water and swells), then **steaming** it (the powder cooks and fuses into a sticky gel, locking everything together), and finally **drying** it. The sponge is now firm, resilient, and the powder is locked in place. When you mill it, it does not break easily, and all the nutrients (vitamins, minerals from the bran) have been pushed deep into the grain.
> 
> **That resilient, nutrient-rich sponge = Parboiled Rice.** 🍚

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Operational Constants

- **Soaking Temperature Range:** 65 °C – 80 °C (hot-soak) or 25 °C – 30 °C (cold-soak, longer duration)
- **Standard Steaming Pressure:** Atmospheric to **1.0 – 1.5 kg/cm² (≈ 98 – 147 kPa gauge)**
- **Steaming Time:** 10 – 40 minutes (depending on pressure)
- **Grain Moisture after Soaking:** **~30 % (wet basis, w.b.)**
- **Final Grain Moisture after Drying:** **~12 – 14 % (w.b.)** — safe storage threshold
- **Starch Gelatinization Temperature for Rice:** **65 °C – 78 °C**
- **Yield of Milled Parboiled Rice:** ~70 % (vs. ~67 % for raw rice)

### 1.4 Historical Context

- **Origin:** Originated in the Indian subcontinent over 2,000 years ago (traditional "Malabar" practice).
- **Global Hotspots:** India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Myanmar, Thailand, USA, Italy, and Spain produce parboiled rice.
- **Modern Adoption:** Drives ~50 % of India's rice export market (Basmati parboiled is a major commodity).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Sequential Unit Operations of Parboiling

#### **Stage 1: Soaking (Hydration Phase)**
