The paddy is immersed in water to raise its moisture content to a level where starch granules are fully plasticized.

**Sub-steps:**
1. **Cleaning & Grading:** Removal of foreign matter, immature grains, and stones.
2. **Pre-heating (Optional):** Initial warm water dip (~40 °C) to prevent grain cracking from thermal shock.
3. **Hot Soaking:** Paddy held at **65–80 °C** for 3–4 hours, OR
4. **Cold Soaking:** Paddy held at ambient temperature (**25–30 °C**) for 24–48 hours.
5. **Drainage & Holding:** Excess water drained; grains equilibrate internally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Soaking End-Point (Critical Control Point, CCP):** Moisture content must reach **~30 % (w.b.)** to ensure complete starch gelatinization in the subsequent steaming step. Below 25 %, gelatinization is incomplete (chalky grains). Above 35 %, grains may burst during steaming.

**Engineering Reasons (The "Why"):**
- Water acts as a **plasticizer**, lowering the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the endosperm.
- It enables uniform heat transfer in the steaming step.
- It facilitates **diffusion-controlled moisture migration** into the kernel interior.

#### **Stage 2: Steaming (Gelatinization Phase)**

The hydrated paddy is exposed to saturated steam, raising the grain temperature above the **starch gelatinization temperature (65–78 °C)**.

**Sub-steps:**
1. **Loading:** Hydrated paddy loaded into a steam-jacketed vessel or batch steamer.
2. **Steam Injection:** Saturated steam at **1.0–1.5 kg/cm²** (or atmospheric) introduced.
3. **Hold Time:** Maintained for 10–40 minutes.
4. **Discharge:** Steam cut off; grains discharged to the drying yard.

**The "How" — Mechanism of Gelatinization:**
- Steam heat + bound water → **hydrogen bonds in crystalline starch are broken**.
- Amylose leaches out of the granule matrix.
- Starch granules **swell irreversibly** and form a **continuous gel network**.
- The kernel becomes translucent (loss of chalkiness).
- **Vitamins and minerals** from the bran (aleurone layer) **diffuse inward** into the endosperm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Pass vs. Pressure Parboiling:**
> - **Single-Pass (Atmospheric):** Steaming at 100 °C; longer time (20–40 min); less energy-intensive.
> - **Pressure Parboiling:** Steam at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² (≈ 120–127 °C); 10–20 min; better gelatinization uniformity.

#### **Stage 3: Drying (Stabilization Phase)**

The gelatinized grains must be dried to a safe moisture content (**~12–14 % w.b.**) to prevent:
- Microbial spoilage (fungi, bacteria)
- Insect infestation
- Re-association (retrogradation) of starch chains

**Common Drying Methods:**
- **Sun Drying:** Traditional, low cost, 2–3 days (weather-dependent).
- **Mechanical Drying (LSD — LSU, Fluidized Bed):** Faster, uniform, industrial scale.
- **Tempering Step:** After first pass drying to ~18 %, grains are held (tempered) for 4–8 hours to equalize internal moisture, then a second drying pass to 12–14 %.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for numerical problems in ESE.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Soaking end-point, drying final moisture |
| 2 | Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Engineering drying calculations |
| 3 | Wet to Dry Basis Conversion | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | % | Inter-conversion |
| 4 | Dry to Wet Basis Conversion | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}} \times 100$ | % | Inter-conversion |
| 5 | Weight of Water to be Removed | $W_{water} = W_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{100 - M_f}$ | kg | Drying load calculation |
| 6 | Head Rice Yield (HRY) | $HRY = \dfrac{\text{Mass of head (whole) grains}}{\text{Total mass of paddy sample}} \times 100$ | % | Quality indicator |
| 7 | Degree of Gelatinization (DG) | $DG = \dfrac{V_{gel}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Measured by alkali spreading score or DSC |
| 8 | Thermal Efficiency of Steamer | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed\ by\ grain}}{Q_{supplied\ by\ steam}} \times 100$ | % | Energy audit |

> **Notation:** $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight, $W_i$ = initial weight, $M_i$ = initial moisture (w.b.), $M_f$ = final moisture (w.b.)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Production of stable, nutrient-dense rice for export and military rations (long shelf life, ~2 years).
- **Nutrition Security:** Parboiled rice retains **~80 % of B-vitamins (thiamine)** vs. only ~20 % in raw milled rice.
- **Energy Sector:** Rice husk from parboiling plants is used as **biomass fuel** for steam generation (circular economy).
- **Process Engineering:** Forms the basis of starch modification techniques (pre-gelatinized starch, modified rice flour).

### 2.4 Classification of Parboiling Methods

| Method | Soaking | Steaming | Characteristics |
|--------|---------|----------|-----------------|
| **Traditional (Single-Boiling)** | 48–72 h, cold | Open pan, 20–30 min | Uneven gelatinization, high breakage |
| **CFTRI Method (India)** | 3–4 h, hot (70 °C) | Atmospheric steam, 15–20 min | Uniform, industrial scale |
| **Pressure Parboiling** | Hot soak | Pressure steam (1.0–1.5 kg/cm²) | Faster, best HRY |
| **Dry-Heat Parboiling** | Steam directly (no pre-soak) | High pressure, longer time | Energy-efficient variant |
| **CIBA (Switzerland) Method** | Vacuum soak | Steam under vacuum | Reduced effluent |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Calculation of Water Removal During Drying (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A batch of parboiled paddy has an initial moisture content of **30 % (w.b.)** after soaking and steaming. The paddy is to be dried to a safe storage moisture of **12 % (w.b.)**. If the initial wet weight of the batch is **1000 kg**, calculate the weight of water to be evaporated.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
- Initial moisture, $M_i = 30\ \%\ (w.b.)$
- Final moisture, $M_f = 12\ \%\ (w.b.)$
- Initial total weight, $W_i = 1000\ kg$

**Step 2 — Calculate the dry solid matter (DSM) mass in the batch:**

Since moisture is reported on wet basis, the dry solid mass is constant during drying. The fraction of dry solids in the initial wet grain is:

$$
f_{ds} = \dfrac{100 - M_i}{100} = \dfrac{100 - 30}{100} = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of dry solids (this is invariant during drying):**

$$
W_d = f_{ds} \times W_i = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700\ \text{kg of dry solids}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the initial mass of water in the batch:**

$$
W_{w,i} = W_i - W_d = 1000 - 700 = 300\ \text{kg of water}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the final mass of the batch (W_f) using the dry basis formula:**

By definition of moisture content (w.b.):

$$
M_f = \dfrac{W_{w,f}}{W_f} \times 100
$$

Since $W_f = W_d + W_{w,f}$, we substitute:

$$
M_f = \dfrac{W_{w,f}}{W_d + W_{w,f}} \times 100
$$

Solving for $W_{w,f}$:

$$
0.12 = \dfrac{W_{w,f}}{700 + W_{w,f}}
$$

$$
0.12(700 + W_{w,f}) = W_{w,f}
$$

$$
84 + 0.12 \cdot W_{w,f} = W_{w,f}
$$

$$
84 = W_{w,f} - 0.12 \cdot W_{w,f} = 0.88 \cdot W_{w,f}
$$

$$
W_{w,f} = \dfrac{84}{0.88} = 95.45\ \text{kg of water}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the mass of water to be removed:**

$$
\boxed{W_{water\ removed} = W_{w,i} - W_{w,f} = 300 - 95.45 = 204.55\ \text{kg}}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification using the direct engineering formula:**

$$
W_{water} = W_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{100 - M_f}
$$

$$
W_{water} = 1000 \times \dfrac{30 - 12}{100 - 12} = 1000 \times \dfrac{18}{88} = 204.55\ \text{kg}
$$

✅ **Results match. Final answer: ~204.55 kg of water must be evaporated.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Property Changes During Parboiling

| Grain Property | Raw Milled Rice | Parboiled Rice | Mechanism |
|----------------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Color** | White | Amber/Yellow | Maillard reaction (soaking+steaming), bran pigment diffusion |
| **Hardness** | Soft, fragile | Hard, glassy | Starch gel matrix; cell wall fusion |
| **Translucency** | Opaque (chalky) | Translucent (glassy) | Loss of air spaces during gelatinization |
| **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | 50–60 % | 65–75 % | Fewer broken grains due to toughened endosperm |
| **Nutrient Retention** | Low (B-vitamins lost in polishing) | High (vitamins diffuse inward) | Concentration gradient drives inward diffusion |
| **Cooking Time** | 15–20 min | 20–25 min | Starch already partly gelatinized |
| **Storage Stability** | 6–12 months | 18–24 months | Low moisture, enzyme inactivation |
| **Glycemic Index (GI)** | Medium-High (73) | Medium-Low (68) | Resistant starch formation |

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Required for Drying (Engineering Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Estimate the energy required to dry the 1000 kg batch from the previous example, given an initial grain temperature of 80 °C and final temperature of 35 °C, assuming water evaporates at ~95 °C average.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to raise water from 80 °C to 95 °C:**

$$
Q_1 = m_{water} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T = 204.55 \times 4.186 \times (95 - 80)
$$

$$
Q_1 = 204.55 \times 4.186 \times 15 = 12{,}841.5\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of vaporization (at 95 °C, $L_v \approx 2266\ \text{kJ/kg}$):**

$$
Q_2 = m_{water} \cdot L_v = 204.55 \times 2266
$$

$$
Q_2 = 463{,}510.3\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy required:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 12{,}841.5 + 463{,}510.3
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 476{,}351.8\ \text{kJ} \approx 132.3\ \text{kWh}}
$$

**Real-world implication:** This justifies the use of **rice husk-fired boilers** in parboiling plants — the husk from 1 ton of paddy (~220 kg) can supply ~150 kWh of thermal energy, nearly self-sufficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow Diagram: Industrial Parboiling Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Paddy Intake] --> B[Pre-Cleaning and Grading]
    B --> C[Soaking Tank with Hot Water 70-80 deg C]
    C --> D[Soaking Hold 3-4 hours, moisture 30 percent w.b.]
    D --> E[Drainage and Surface Drying]
    E --> F[Steam Jacketed Vessel or Pressure Steamer]
    F --> G[Steam Treatment 1.0 to 1.5 kg per cm2, 10-20 min]
    G --> H[Starch Gelatinization Achieved]
    H --> I[First Stage Drying to 18 percent moisture]
    I --> J[Tempering Bin 4 to 8 hours for moisture equilibration]
    J --> K[Second Stage Drying to 12 to 14 percent moisture]
    K --> L[Ambient Cooling and Conditioning]
    L --> M[Storage Silo for Parboiled Paddy]
    M --> N[Dehusking and Polishing]
    N --> O[Final Grading and Sorting]
    O --> P[Packaged Parboiled Rice]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: Functional Interactions in a Parboiling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[Input Stream]
        P1[Raw Paddy 12 percent moisture]
    end

    subgraph SOAK[Soaking Subsystem]
        S1[Hot Water Reservoir]
        S2[Soak Tank 1]
        S3[Soak Tank 2]
        S4[Water Recirculation Pump]
    end

    subgraph STEAM[Steaming Subsystem]
        V1[Boiler Unit]
        V2[Steam Header]
        V3[Batch Steamer Vessel]
        V4[Pressure Control Valve]
    end

    subgraph DRY[Drying Subsystem]
        D1[First Pass Dryer]
        D2[Tempering Silo]
        D3[Second Pass Dryer]
    end

    subgraph OUT1[Output Stream]
        Q1[Parboiled Paddy 13 percent]
        Q2[Effluent Water]
        Q3[Rice Husk Biomass]
    end

    P1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> V3
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V4 --> V3
    V3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> Q1
    S2 --> Q2
    V1 --> Q3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Matrix: Selection of Parboiling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Parboiling Method] --> Q1{Scale of Operation}
    Q1 -->|Small Scale Traditional| M1[Single Boiling Method]
    Q1 -->|Medium Scale Industrial| M2[CFTRI Hot Soak Method]
    Q1 -->|Large Scale Export Quality| M3[Pressure Parboiling Method]
    Q1 -->|Premium Specialty| M4[Vacuum or Dry Heat Method]

    M1 --> C1[Pros: Low Cost, Simple Equipment]
    M1 --> C2[Cons: Low HRY, Long Soak, Uneven Quality]

    M2 --> C3[Pros: Uniform, Good HRY, Moderate Cost]
    M2 --> C4[Cons: Moderate Energy Use]

    M3 --> C5[Pros: Best Quality, High HRY, Faster]
    M3 --> C6[Cons: High Capital, Skilled Operation]

    M4 --> C7[Pros: Energy Efficient, Low Effluent]
    M4 --> C8[Cons: High Tech, Limited Adoption]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Explanation

- **Soaking Tank Geometry:** Typically a **rectangular concrete tank (3 m × 1.5 m × 1.2 m)** with perforated false bottom for water circulation.
- **Steam Vessel:** A **vertical cylindrical pressure vessel (SS 304 grade)** with steam inlet at top and discharge valve at bottom.
- **Dryers:** **LSU-type columnar dryers** (4–6 m tall) for continuous flow; **flat-bed dryers** for batch operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define parboiling. List any two advantages of parboiled rice over raw milled rice.*

**Model Answer:**
Parboiling is a hydrothermal treatment of paddy involving soaking, steaming, and drying to achieve irreversible gelatinization of starch before milling.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Higher Head Rice Yield (65–75 %)** because the gelatinized endosperm resists breakage during dehusking and polishing.
2. **Better nutrient retention** — vitamins (especially thiamine) and minerals from the bran layer diffuse into the endosperm during steaming, reducing polishing losses.
3. *Any one more valid point: longer shelf life, lower glycemic index, reduced insect infestation during storage.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the optimum soaking and steaming conditions for industrial parboiling of paddy.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Soaking:** Hot water at **65–80 °C for 3–4 hours** (or cold water at 25–30 °C for 24–48 hours) until grain moisture reaches **~30 % (w.b.)**.
- **Steaming:** Saturated steam at **1.0–1.5 kg/cm² pressure for 10–20 minutes** (atmospheric: 20–40 min at 100 °C), to ensure complete starch gelatinization.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Module 1 → Choose **ANY ONE** of the following two questions. Each carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** Describe the three main unit operations involved in the parboiling of paddy, with the underlying physico-chemical changes at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parboiling unit receives 5 tonnes of paddy at 14 % moisture (w.b.). After soaking, moisture rises to 30 % (w.b.). The paddy is then steamed and dried to 13 % (w.b.) for storage. Calculate: (i) the mass of water absorbed during soaking, and (ii) the mass of water removed during drying. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Unit Operations and Physico-Chemical Changes:**

| Stage | Process | Physico-Chemical Change |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|
| **1. Soaking** | Paddy immersed in hot water (65–80 °C) for 3–4 h | **Hydration** — water diffuses into endosperm; moisture rises from ~14 % to ~30 % (w.b.); starch granules absorb water and swell |
| **2. Steaming** | Saturated steam at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–20 min | **Starch Gelatinization** — at 65–78 °C, hydrogen bonds in crystalline starch break; granules swell irreversibly; amylose leaches out; **vitamins and minerals from bran diffuse inward**; **Maillard reaction** occurs (color change to amber) |
| **3. Drying** | Sun or mechanical drying in two stages with tempering | **Moisture reduction to 12–14 % (w.b.)**; **retrogradation** of starch; **enzyme inactivation**; improved storage stability |

> Additional points: (i) loss of chalky appearance (translucency develops), (ii) hardening of kernel, (iii) prevention of kernel breakage during milling.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Initial paddy weight, $W_1 = 5000\ kg$ at $M_1 = 14\ \%\ (w.b.)$
- After soaking: $M_2 = 30\ \%\ (w.b.)$
- After drying: $M_3 = 13\ \%\ (w.b.)$

**Step 1: Dry solid mass (invariant throughout):**

$$
W_d = W_1 \times \dfrac{100 - M_1}{100} = 5000 \times 0.86 = 4300\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2: Weight after soaking ($W_2$):**

Using $M_2 = \dfrac{W_2 - W_d}{W_2} \times 100$:

$$
0.30 = \dfrac{W_2 - 4300}{W_2} \implies 0.30 \cdot W_2 = W_2 - 4300
$$

$$
W_2 = \dfrac{4300}{0.70} = 6142.86\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3: Water absorbed during soaking:**

$$
\boxed{W_{abs} = W_2 - W_1 = 6142.86 - 5000 = 1142.86\ \text{kg}}
$$

**Step 4: Weight after drying ($W_3$):**

$$
0.13 = \dfrac{W_3 - 4300}{W_3} \implies 0.13 \cdot W_3 = W_3 - 4300
$$

$$
W_3 = \dfrac{4300}{0.87} = 4942.53\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5: Water removed during drying:**

$$
\boxed{W_{removed} = W_2 - W_3 = 6142.86 - 4942.53 = 1200.33\ \text{kg}}
$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$
W_{removed} = W_2 \times \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{100 - M_3} = 6142.86 \times \dfrac{30 - 13}{100 - 13} = 6142.86 \times \dfrac{17}{87} = 1200.33\ \text{kg} \checkmark
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Compare the traditional (single-boiling) method of parboiling with the modern CFTRI and pressure parboiling methods, highlighting the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the physico-chemical changes that occur in paddy during steaming, with reference to starch gelatinization, vitamin migration, and color development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Parboiling Methods:**

| Parameter | Traditional (Single-Boiling) | CFTRI Method | Pressure Parboiling |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Soaking** | Cold water, 48–72 h | Hot water (70 °C), 3–4 h | Hot water (70–80 °C), 2–3 h |
| **Steaming** | Open pan, 20–30 min at 100 °C | Atmospheric steam, 15–20 min | Pressure steam 1.0–1.5 kg/cm², 10–20 min |
| **HRY** | 50–60 % | 65–70 % | 70–75 % |
| **Gelatinization Uniformity** | Poor (chalky centers) | Good | Excellent |
| **Energy Use** | Low (sun drying) | Moderate | High (boiler-driven) |
| **Capital Cost** | Very Low | Moderate | High |
| **Scale** | Cottage industry | Medium industrial | Large industrial/export |
| **Limitations** | Long time, inconsistent quality | Moderate capital | Skilled operators needed |

> Additional: Pressure parboiling produces the darkest amber color but highest vitamin retention; CFTRI is the most balanced method for medium-scale units in India.

**(b) Physico-Chemical Changes During Steaming:**

1. **Starch Gelatinization:**
   - At ~65–78 °C, thermal energy breaks hydrogen bonds in crystalline starch.
   - Water molecules (already present from soaking) penetrate the granule matrix.
   - Starch granules **swell irreversibly** (up to 10× their original volume).
   - **Amylose leaches out** of the granule and forms an inter-granular gel network.
   - The endosperm becomes a **continuous, semi-crystalline gel** — translucent in appearance.
   - Crystallinity is lost; the X-ray diffraction pattern changes from A-type (rice) to a more amorphous signature.

2. **Vitamin Migration (Nutrient Diffusion):**
   - During soaking, water-soluble vitamins (B-complex: thiamine B1, niacin B3, pyridoxine B6) are partially leached out into the soak water.
   - During steaming, the temperature gradient (high inside, low at surface) and moisture gradient create a **concentration-driven inward diffusion** of these vitamins from the aleurone/pericarp layers into the starchy endosperm.
   - Result: parboiled rice retains **~80 % of thiamine** vs. only ~20 % in raw milled rice.

3. **Color Development (Maillard Reaction):**
   - Reducing sugars (glucose) from the aleurone layer react with **free amino groups** of proteins at high steaming temperatures.
   - This non-enzymatic **Maillard browning** produces melanoidin pigments, giving parboiled rice its characteristic **amber/yellow-brown color**.
   - Longer steaming time and higher temperature intensify the color (pressure parboiling > CFTRI > traditional).

4. **Other Changes:**
   - **Lipid complexing** with amylose (reduces rancidity).
   - **Cell wall fusion** (improves milling resistance).
   - **Enzyme inactivation** (lipase, protease — extends shelf life).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake Trap:** Most students confuse **wet basis (w.b.)** and **dry basis (d.b.)** moisture contents in numerical problems. In parboiling, all industry-standard values are quoted in **w.b.** — read the question carefully and label your final answer with the correct basis!
> 2. **Marks Deduction for Skipped Steps:** In 7-mark numerical problems, examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if the formula is not stated before substitution. Always write: *"Let $M_i$ = initial moisture (w.b.); $W_i$ = initial weight; $W_d$ = dry solid mass (constant)..."*
> 3. **Theory Trap:** Students often write "parboiling = partial boiling of rice" without mentioning **starch gelatinization** as the core objective. Always include the phrase *"irreversible gelatinization of starch granules"* — this is the KTU mark-grabbing phrase.
> 4. **Diagram Mistake:** If you draw a flowchart, label every box with the operating condition (temperature, time, pressure) — unlabeled boxes fetch only partial credit.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Parboiling (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Hydrothermal treatment (soaking + steaming + drying) of paddy to achieve **irreversible starch gelatinization** before milling.
- ☐ **Starch Gelatinization Temperature (Rice):** **65–78 °C** (key constant).
- ☐ **Three Stages:** Soaking → Steaming → Drying (with tempering in modern processes).
- ☐ **Soaking End-Point Moisture:** **~30 % (w.b.)** [CCP].
- ☐ **Steaming Parameters:** Atmospheric (100 °C, 20–40 min) OR Pressure (**1.0–1.5 kg/cm², 10–20 min**).
- ☐ **Final Storage Moisture:** **12–14 % (w.b.)**.
- ☐ **Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$
  - $W_{water} = W_i \times \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{100 - M_f}$
  - $W_d = W_i \times \dfrac{100 - M_i}{100}$ (dry solids, invariant)
- ☐ **Key Quality Parameter:** **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** — parboiled rice has 65–75 % HRY vs. 50–60 % for raw rice.
- ☐ **Nutritional Advantage:** Retention of **~80 % thiamine (B1)**, **~70 % niacin (B3)**; loss of only ~20 % in raw milling.
- ☐ **Color Cause:** **Maillard reaction** (reducing sugars + amino groups) → amber/yellow color.
- ☐ **Translucency Cause:** Loss of air spaces + starch gel matrix.
- ☐ **Methods to Know:** Traditional single-boiling, CFTRI, Pressure parboiling, Dry-heat, Vacuum (CIBA).
- ☐ **Self-Sufficiency:** Rice husk from parboiling can fuel the boiler (circular economy model).
- ☐ **Advantages:** Higher HRY, better nutrition, longer shelf life, lower GI, less breakage, resistant starch.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Longer processing time, color change (may be undesirable for some markets), higher energy than raw milling, possible off-odor (if not dried properly).
- ☐ **Industrial Equipment to Know:** Soaking tank (concrete, perforated base), steam-jacketed batch steamer (SS 304), LSU columnar dryer, tempering silo.
- ☐ **Examiners love:** Process flow diagrams, numerical moisture calculations, mechanism of gelatinization, comparison tables of methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physicochemical Changes During Parboiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physicochemical Changes During Parboiling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parboiling** is a controlled **hydrothermal treatment** of paddy (rough rice, *Oryza sativa*) involving three sequential unit operations — **soaking**, **steaming/heat treatment**, and **drying** — performed prior to dehusking and milling. During this process, the endosperm undergoes irreversible **starch gelatinization**, **protein denaturation**, **nutrient migration**, and **enzyme inactivation**, collectively termed the *physicochemical changes of parboiling*.

The official **KTU 2024 Scheme** defines the phenomenon as the *"moisture-thermal conditioning of intact paddy grains so as to bring about gelatinization of the starch within the endosperm, thereby improving milling yield, nutritional profile, and storage stability of the resulting milled rice."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Sponge-Boiling Metaphor":**
>
> Imagine a dry kitchen sponge made of tightly packed tiny balloons (starch granules) glued together by a thin rubber mesh (protein matrix). If you soak the sponge in warm water, water slowly seeps into the spaces between balloons. Now, if you **boil** this wet sponge, two things happen:
>
> 1. The tiny balloons **absorb water, swell, and burst open**, releasing their contents which then re-solidify into a continuous gel mass (this is **starch gelatinization**).
> 2. The thin rubber mesh **shrinks and hardens** because its proteins cook (this is **protein denaturation**).
>
> The result is a single, hardened, yellowish, translucent cake — exactly what happens inside a parboiled rice kernel. This is why parboiled rice grains are **glassier, harder, and more resistant to breakage during milling** than raw rice.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

The following physical constants and standard ranges are universally accepted in rice parboiling literature:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Gelatinization temperature range | **65 – 78 °C** (typical for rice starch) |
| Pasting temperature | **68 – 75 °C** |
| Soaking moisture target | **30 – 35 % (w.b.)** |
| Steaming temperature | **100 – 120 °C** (saturated steam) |
| Steaming pressure (pressure parboiling) | **1.0 – 2.5 kg/cm²** |
| Final dried moisture content | **12 – 14 % (w.b.)** |
| Glass transition temperature (dry) | **~150 °C** |
| Glass transition temperature (wet, ~30% mc) | **~30 – 50 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic is directly mapped to **CO1 (Module 1)** — *"Understand the composition, structure, and processing principles of cereals and legumes."* The physicochemical changes link the *raw material properties* to the *finished product quality*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequential Mechanism of Physicochemical Changes

Parboiling is not a single event but a **cascade of overlapping physicochemical transitions** triggered by heat and moisture. The grain passes through four distinct thermal regimes, each driving specific changes.

#### Stage A — Water Absorption (Soaking, 50 – 70 °C)

- Water enters the grain **primarily through the micropyle** and secondary cracks in the hull.
- Hydration follows **Fickian diffusion** through the bran layers into the endosperm.
- The moisture content rises from **~12 % to ~30 – 35 % (w.b.)**.
- No starch gelatinization occurs yet (temperature is below gelatinization onset).
- Soluble vitamins (especially **thiamine / Vitamin B₁**) and minerals begin to **diffuse inward**.

#### Stage B — Starch Gelatinization (Steaming, > 70 °C)

- Once grain temperature crosses the **gelatinization temperature (T_gel ~ 65 – 78 °C)**, starch granules absorb water rapidly, swell irreversibly, and lose their **birefringence** (loss of "Maltese cross" under polarized light).
- Amylose **leaches out** of the granules into the surrounding matrix.
- Upon cooling, amylose **retrogrades** and forms a firm, glassy network — responsible for the characteristic **translucency** of parboiled rice.

#### Stage C — Protein Denaturation & Crosslinking (70 – 100 °C)

- Endosperm storage proteins (mainly **glutelins**, ~80 % of rice protein) denature.
- **Disulfide (–S–S–) bonds** reform between protein molecules.
- **Maillard reaction** between protein amine groups and reducing sugars produces melanoidins → responsible for the **yellowish-brown color** of parboiled rice.

#### Stage D — Nutrient Migration & Enzyme Inactivation (> 80 °C)

- **Thiamine (B₁)**, **niacin (B₃)**, **minerals (Ca, Mg, P, Fe)**, and **lipids** migrate **from bran → endosperm**.
- **Lipases, peroxidases, and catalases are inactivated** → dramatically improves **storage life** and reduces rancidity.
- **Pectic substances** in cell walls soften and partially dissolve.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and form the high-yield set for numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius Rate Equation (gelatinization kinetics) | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law (1-D moisture diffusion) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | $D$ = moisture diffusivity (m²/s) |
| 3 | Crank's Solution for Slab Hydration | $\dfrac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} = 1 - \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{8}{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{D(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}t}{4L^{2}}\right)$ | $L$ = half-thickness of grain (m) |
| 4 | Gordon-Taylor Equation (Glass Transition) | $T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k\,w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\,w_2}$ | $w$ = mass fractions, $k$ = Gordon-Taylor constant |
| 5 | Starch Swelling Power | $SP = \dfrac{W_{swollen} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % basis |
| 6 | Water Absorption Index (WAI) | $WAI = \dfrac{W_{sediment}}{W_{dry\,sample}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | Le Chatelier Relation (Pressure-Temp.) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | For steam pressure parboiling |
| 8 | Maillard Reaction Rate Proxy | $\text{Browning Index} \propto \int_{0}^{t}\!\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT(\tau)}\right)d\tau$ | Used to predict color development |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These physicochemical principles are the foundation of the entire **parboiled rice industry** in Kerala (Kuttanad), Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu (Tanjore), and globally in Bangladesh and Thailand. Mastery of these kinetics allows engineers to:

- **Design optimized soaking tanks** with correct L:D ratio to ensure uniform hydration.
- **Set autoclave parameters** (pressure × time) for **single-step pressure parboiling** vs traditional open-pan methods.
- **Predict shelf life** based on residual lipase activity (which controls free fatty acid buildup and rancidity).
- **Engineer micronutrient retention** — the migration of thiamine from bran to endosperm is one of parboiling's most important nutritional benefits, particularly relevant to **beriberi prevention** historically documented in South-East Asia.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Uptake During Soaking (Fickian Diffusion Model)

We model the rice grain as an **isothermal sphere of radius R**, where water diffuses radially inward.

**Starting Point — Fick's Second Law in spherical coordinates:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left[\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial r^{2}} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the new variable $u = C \cdot r$ to eliminate the spherical term:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial r^{2}}$$

This reduces the spherical PDE to a 1-D Cartesian form.

**Step 2 — Apply the following initial and boundary conditions:**

$$C(r,\,0) = C_0, \quad C(R,\,t) = C_s \; (\text{surface saturation}), \quad \left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Separation of variables yields the Crank series solution for a sphere:**

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} = 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{D\,n^{2}\pi^{2}\,t}{R^{2}}\right)$$

where $M_t$ is the moisture content at time $t$, $M_\infty$ is the equilibrium moisture, and $R$ is the grain radius (~3 mm for paddy).

**Step 4 — Engineering simplification for short times (valid for $M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$):**

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} \approx \frac{6}{R}\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi}}$$

This **square-root-of-time law** is the most commonly used form in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example:**

> *Given: $D = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, $R = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $M_\infty = 0.35$, $M_0 = 0.12$, find $M_t$ at $t = 4$ h = 14,400 s.*

Calculate the diffusion penetration parameter:

$$\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-10})(14{,}400)}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{5.50 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.42 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$$

Compute the dimensionless moisture ratio:

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} \approx \frac{6}{3 \times 10^{-3}} \times 7.42 \times 10^{-4} = 1.484 \;(\text{capped at } 1.0)$$

Since the ratio exceeds unity, the short-time approximation is **invalid**; we must use the full series, which would give $M_t \approx 0.32$ at 4 hours — consistent with industrial soaking practice (**30 – 35 % w.b.**).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gordon-Taylor Glass Transition Equation for Paddy

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical observation** that glass transition temperature $T_g$ of a biopolymer–water mixture varies linearly with composition when plotted versus mass fraction.

**Step 2 — Apply the rule-of-mixtures for free volume:**

$$T_g = w_1\,T_{g1} + w_2\,T_{g2}$$

where subscript 1 = dry biopolymer, 2 = water.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Gordon-Taylor correction constant $k$ to account for non-ideal mixing of free volumes:**

$$T_g = \frac{w_1\,T_{g1} + k\,w_2\,T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\,w_2}$$

**Step 4 — Standard values for rice:**

$$T_{g,\text{dry starch}} \approx 150\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_{g,\text{water}} = -135\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad k = 0.22 \text{ (rice)}$$

**Step 5 — Worked example at $w_{\text{water}} = 0.30$ (typical soaked moisture):**

$$T_g = \frac{(0.70)(150) + (0.22)(0.30)(-135)}{0.70 + (0.22)(0.30)}$$

$$T_g = \frac{105 + (-8.91)}{0.766} = \frac{96.09}{0.766} \approx 125.4\,^\circ\text{C}$$

This is the dry-basis $T_g$. On a wet-basis at the same moisture, considering the plasticizing effect of water, the **effective $T_g$ drops to ~30 – 50 °C** — explaining why parboiled paddy must be dried carefully to avoid **case-hardening** and **stress cracking**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect Summary of Physicochemical Changes

| # | Physical/Chemical Change | Driving Cause | Location in Grain | Effect on Final Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Starch gelatinization | Heat + water above 65–78 °C | Endosperm | Hardness ↑, translucency ↑ |
| 2 | Protein denaturation | Heat > 70 °C | Endosperm (glutelin matrix) | Texture firming, Maillard browning |
| 3 | Amylose retrogradation | Cooling after steaming | Endosperm | Glassy appearance, lower stickiness |
| 4 | Thiamine (B₁) migration | Concentration gradient + heat | Bran → endosperm | **Nutritional benefit** (anti-beriberi) |
| 5 | Lipid migration | Heat-induced membrane fluidity | Bran → endosperm | Better mouthfeel, richer flavor |
| 6 | Pigment migration | Solubility in hot water | Bran → endosperm | Yellowish color |
| 7 | Enzyme inactivation | Heat > 80 °C | Throughout grain | **Longer shelf life**, no rancidity |
| 8 | Cell wall pectin dissolution | Hydrolysis | Aleurone layer | Improved nutrient access |
| 9 | Volume expansion | Swelling pressure of starch | Whole grain | Visible kernel enlargement (~5–10 %) |
| 10 | Reduction in chalkiness | Filling of air voids by gelatinized starch | Endosperm | Higher head rice yield |
| 11 | Glass transition shift | Moisture plasticization | Whole grain | Affects drying behavior |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Parboiling Process & Associated Physicochemical Changes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Paddy Intake Moisture ~12 percent wb] --> B[Cleaning and Grading]
    B --> C[Soaking Stage 50 to 70 degC, 3 to 4 hours]
    C --> C1[Fickian Moisture Diffusion]
    C --> C2[Vitamin B1 begins inward migration]
    C --> C3[Target moisture 30 to 35 percent]
    C --> D[Steaming Stage 100 to 120 degC, 15 to 30 min]
    D --> D1[Starch Gelatinization above 65 degC]
    D --> D2[Protein Denaturation and S-S crosslinks]
    D --> D3[Maillard Browning Reaction]
    D --> D4[Lipase and Peroxidase Inactivation]
    D --> D5[Lipid and Pigment Migration to Endosperm]
    D --> E[Drying Stage 12 to 14 percent final moisture]
    E --> E1[Amylose Retrogradation]
    E --> E2[Glass Transition and Case Hardening Avoidance]
    E --> F[Milling Dehusking and Polishing]
    F --> G[Parboiled Rice Kernels Translucent Yellow Hard]
    G --> G1[High Head Rice Yield]
    G --> G2[Improved Nutritional Profile]
    G --> G3[Extended Shelf Life]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Hierarchical Mapping of Physicochemical Phenomena

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Conditions]
        I1[Raw Paddy 12 percent mc]
        I2[Soak Water Temp 50 to 70 degC]
        I3[Steam Pressure 1.0 to 2.5 kg per sq cm]
    end
    subgraph MECHANISMS[Physicochemical Mechanisms]
        M1[Heat Transfer Conduction and Convection]
        M2[Mass Transfer Water Diffusion Fickian]
        M3[Phase Transition Starch Gelatinization]
        M4[Chemical Reaction Protein Denaturation Maillard]
        M5[Transport Migration of Vitamins Lipids Pigments]
        M6[Enzyme Inactivation Thermal]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Quality Attributes]
        O1[Hardness and Translucency]
        O2[Yellowish Color]
        O3[Nutrient Density B1 Ca Fe]
        O4[Head Rice Yield Greater than 70 percent]
        O5[Shelf Life Greater than 12 months]
    end
    I1 --> M1
    I1 --> M2
    I2 --> M2
    I3 --> M1
    I3 --> M3
    M1 --> M3
    M1 --> M4
    M2 --> M3
    M2 --> M5
    M3 --> M4
    M3 --> O1
    M4 --> O1
    M4 --> O2
    M5 --> O3
    M6 --> O5
    M3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Microstructural View of a Parboiled Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL[Outer Pericarp and Bran Layers]
        H1[Hull Husk]
        H2[Pericarp Seed Coat]
        H3[Aleurone Layer Rich in Vitamins and Minerals]
    end
    subgraph ENDO[Endosperm Region After Parboiling]
        E1[Gelatinized Starch Matrix Amorphous Retrograded]
        E2[Denatured Protein Network Disulfide Bridges]
        E3[Migrated Lipid Droplets]
        E4[Migrated Thiamine and Minerals]
        E5[Residual Air Voids Largely Filled]
    end
    subgraph GERM[Embryo Germ]
        G1[Inactivated Enzymes Lipase Peroxidase]
    end
    H3 --> E4
    H3 --> E3
    E1 --> E5
    E2 --> E5
    G1 --> OQ[Overall Quality Hard Translucent Nutritious]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams intentionally avoid physical-drawing limitations of Mermaid and instead use **block-level functional architecture** to convey how each physicochemical change at the microstructural level contributes to the macroscopic quality of the parboiled kernel. This is the recommended approach for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 viva voce questions where students are asked to *"diagram the internal changes during parboiling."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> *Define parboiling. List the three major physicochemical changes that occur during the parboiling of paddy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Parboiling is a hydrothermal pre-milling treatment of paddy in which the grain is soaked, steamed, and dried so as to cause gelatinization of the starch within the endosperm.

**Three major physicochemical changes (2 marks — 1 mark each for any two, 0.5 mark each for naming all three):**

1. **Starch gelatinization** — starch granules absorb water, swell, and lose crystallinity above 65–78 °C.
2. **Protein denaturation** — endosperm glutelins unfold and re-crosslink via disulfide bonds.
3. **Nutrient migration** — thiamine (B₁), niacin, lipids, and minerals diffuse from bran into the endosperm, enhancing nutritional value.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
> *Why does parboiled rice have a longer shelf life than raw milled rice?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Parboiled rice has longer shelf life because the **steaming step (>80 °C)** in parboiling **irreversibly inactivates the hydrolytic and oxidative enzymes** — specifically **lipase, lipoxygenase, peroxidase, and catalase** — present in the bran and embryo (1 mark). In raw rice, these enzymes remain active and catalyze **lipid hydrolysis** → free fatty acids → rancidity and off-flavors during storage (1 mark). Additionally, the gelatinized starch and denatured protein matrix create a **physical barrier** that reduces oxygen permeability and microbial access (1 mark). The combined effect is a shelf life of **12–18 months** versus **3–6 months** for raw milled rice.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Exam]**
>
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **physicochemical changes that occur during the soaking and steaming stages of rice parboiling**. Support your answer with the underlying mechanisms and the **gelatinization temperature range** of rice starch. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Fickian diffusion equation** for moisture uptake during soaking and apply it to compute the equilibrium soaking time for paddy with $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s and grain radius $R = 3.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, assuming the short-time approximation is valid up to $M_t/M_\infty = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**1. Soaking Stage (2 marks):**
- Water enters primarily through the **micropyle** and **hull cracks**.
- Hydration is governed by **Fickian diffusion** through bran into endosperm.
- Moisture rises from **12 % to ~30 – 35 % (w.b.)**.
- **No starch gelatinization** occurs as temperature is sub-gelatinization (50–70 °C).
- Water-soluble vitamins (especially **thiamine**) begin inward migration.

**2. Steaming Stage (3 marks):**
- Grain temperature crosses the **gelatinization range of 65 – 78 °C** (rice starch).
- **Starch granules** absorb water, swell irreversibly, and lose birefringence.
- **Amylose leaches** out and on cooling, **retrogrades** → glassy endosperm.
- **Glutelin proteins denature** and form new **disulfide (–S–S–) crosslinks**.
- **Maillard reaction** between reducing sugars and amine groups → yellow-brown color.
- **Lipases, peroxidases, catalases inactivated** → shelf life extension.
- **Lipids and pigments migrate** from bran to endosperm.

**3. Why Gelatinization Temperature Matters (2 marks):**
The gelatinization temperature defines the **minimum thermal energy threshold** below which parboiling is incomplete. Insufficient steaming yields a "half-parboiled" grain with poor milling quality and high brokens. Industrial autoclaves are operated at **100 – 120 °C** with steam pressure **1.0 – 2.5 kg/cm²** to ensure complete gelatinization throughout the kernel cross-section.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start from Fick's second law in spherical coordinates for radial diffusion:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\left[\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial r^{2}} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply substitution $u = C \cdot r$ to obtain the 1-D Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Boundary conditions: $C(r,0) = C_0$, $C(R,t) = C_s$, $\partial C/\partial r \vert_{r=0} = 0$. Crank's solution for the sphere gives:

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} = 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{Dn^{2}\pi^{2}t}{R^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Short-time approximation valid for $M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$:

$$\frac{M_t - M_0}{M_\infty - M_0} \approx \frac{6}{R}\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Set $M_t/M_\infty = 0.5$ and solve for $t$:

$$0.5 = \frac{6}{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}\sqrt{\frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-10})\,t}{3.1416}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10}\,t}{3.1416}} = \frac{0.5 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 2.667 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$$

Square both sides:

$$\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-10}\,t}{3.1416} = (2.667 \times 10^{-4})^{2} = 7.111 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$t = \frac{7.111 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.1416}{1.5 \times 10^{-10}} = 1489\,\text{s} \approx 24.8\,\text{min}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Engineering conclusion: A soaking time of **~25 minutes** in the short-time regime is sufficient to reach half-saturation. Industrial practice extends this to **3–4 hours** to reach near-equilibrium (**30–35 % w.b.**) for uniform gelatinization during the subsequent steaming step.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main Exam — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **gelatinization of rice starch** during parboiling using a **first-order kinetic model**. Derive the rate expression $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ and discuss how steaming temperature affects the degree of gelatinization. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **migration of nutrients (especially thiamine, lipids, and minerals)** from the bran to the endosperm during parboiling. How does this improve the **nutritional value** and **milling quality** of parboiled rice compared to raw milled rice? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Gelatinization is modeled as a **first-order phase transition**. Let $\alpha$ be the fraction of starch gelatinized at time $t$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T)(1 - \alpha)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Integration with $\alpha(0) = 0$:

$$\alpha(t) = 1 - \exp[-k(T)\,t]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The rate constant $k$ follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where $E_a$ for rice starch gelatinization ≈ **40 – 80 kJ/mol**, $A$ ≈ $10^{20}$ to $10^{30}$ s⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting back:

$$\alpha(t,\,T) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-A\,t\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Effect of temperature — a **10 °C rise** in steaming temperature roughly **doubles** the rate constant $k$ (typical $Q_{10} \approx 2$). For example, increasing steaming from 100 °C to 110 °C shortens the time to reach 99 % gelatinization from ~30 min to ~15 min.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The degree of gelatinization $DG$ is measured experimentally via **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** as:

$$DG(\%) = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{raw}} - \Delta H_{\text{parboiled}}}{\Delta H_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$$

Complete parboiling corresponds to **DG > 90 %**.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Industrial significance — this kinetic model allows engineers to **design autoclave residence time** as a function of temperature, optimizing energy input while ensuring DG > 90 % throughout the batch.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** During steaming, the cell membranes of the **aleurone layer and bran** become permeable due to thermal disruption. Water-soluble nutrients (thiamine, niacin) and fat-soluble compounds (lipids, carotenoid pigments) are driven **down the concentration gradient** from bran → endosperm via diffusion and **convective mass transfer** through the softened cell walls.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** **Thiamine (Vitamin B₁)** content in parboiled rice is **~2 – 3 times higher** than in raw milled rice (because thiamine normally resides in the bran, which is removed in raw rice polishing but is retained in the endosperm in parboiled rice). Historically, this has prevented **beriberi** in populations consuming parboiled rice as a staple (notably in Kerala and Tamil Nadu).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Lipid migration** (~0.5 – 1.0 % lipid in polished parboiled rice versus ~0.2 – 0.4 % in raw polished rice) improves **mouthfeel, flavor, and satiety**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Minerals** (Ca, Mg, P, Fe) concentrate in the endosperm, improving **mineral density** per unit mass of cooked rice.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Milling quality improvement** — the gelatinized starch matrix cements the endosperm, making the kernel **harder and more resistant to breakage**. Head rice yield (HRY) rises from **~55 – 60 %** in raw rice to **~70 – 75 %** in properly parboiled rice.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Reduction in chalkiness** — the gelatinized starch fills the air voids in the endosperm, producing **translucent, vitreous kernels** that command a premium market price.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Parboiling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing gelatinization with melting.** Gelatinization is **irreversible swelling in the presence of water**, not melting. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Quoting wrong gelatinization temperature range.** Rice starch gelatinizes at **65 – 78 °C**, NOT 100 °C (which is the boiling point of water and the steaming temperature). These are frequently confused.
> 3. **Forgetting enzyme inactivation.** Many students only mention starch changes and forget **lipase inactivation** — which is the *single most important reason* parboiled rice stores longer.
> 4. **Mixing up "head rice yield" with "total rice yield."** Head rice yield refers to **whole unbroken kernels** after milling, which is the metric directly improved by parboiling.
> 5. **Not mentioning the Maillard reaction** when explaining color formation — color is **not** purely from pigment migration; non-enzymatic browning contributes significantly.
> 6. **In numerical problems**, failing to convert units (e.g., $D$ in m²/s but $R$ in cm) is a **1-mark deduction** as per KTU valuation key.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Physicochemical Changes During Parboiling**
>
> ☐ **Parboiling = Soaking + Steaming + Drying** (hydrothermal pre-treatment of paddy).
>
> ☐ **Primary change = Starch gelatinization** at **65 – 78 °C**, irreversible, loss of birefringence.
>
> ☐ **Amylose retrogradation** on cooling → glassy, translucent kernel.
>
> ☐ **Protein denaturation** (mainly glutelin) + new **disulfide bonds** → firm texture.
>
> ☐ **Maillard reaction** → characteristic **yellow-brown color**.
>
> ☐ **Nutrient migration inward:** thiamine (B₁), niacin, Ca, Mg, P, Fe, lipids, pigments.
>
> ☐ **Enzyme inactivation:** lipase, peroxidase, catalase → **shelf life ↑ from 3–6 months to 12–18 months**.
>
> ☐ **Soaking moisture target = 30 – 35 % w.b.**; **steaming 100 – 120 °C**.
>
> ☐ **Glass transition (T_g)** drops with moisture — drives drying strategy.
>
> ☐ **Key benefits:** ↑ Head Rice Yield (70–75 %), ↑ Nutrient retention, ↑ Shelf life, ↓ Chalkiness.
>
> ☐ **Fick's second law** governs soaking; **Arrhenius kinetics** governs gelatinization.
>
> ☐ **CO1 mapping (KTU 2024):** Understands cereal processing principles and their physicochemical basis.
>
> ☐ **Common exam trap:** never equate gelatinization temperature with steaming temperature — they are different physical quantities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soaking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Soaking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soaking** is a foundational **primary unit operation** in cereals and legume processing in which cleaned, dehulled (or whole) grains are immersed in a liquid medium (water, salt solution, or an alkaline solution) for a defined period at a controlled temperature to drive **moisture absorption** by the seed matrix, thereby inducing **plasticization** of the cellular structure, **reactivation of endogenous enzymes**, and **partial leaching of soluble anti-nutritional factors**.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme context, soaking is positioned as the **first hydration step** in the cascade of primary processing operations (cleaning → **soaking** → dehulling/dehusking → milling/grinding → cooking/extrusion). It is governed by the thermodynamic principle of *water activity differential* ($a_w$ gradient) between the grain interior and the bulk soaking medium, driving diffusive mass transfer until near-equilibrium hydration is reached.

**Standard Process Metrics (KTU Reference Values):**

| Parameter | Typical Range (Cereals) | Typical Range (Legumes) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Soaking Duration | **8 to 16 hours** | **12 to 24 hours** |
| Soaking Temperature | **25 °C to 50 °C** | **25 °C to 100 °C** (with pressure) |
| Grain-to-Water Ratio | **1:1.5 to 1:3 (w/v)** | **1:2 to 1:5 (w/v)** |
| Final Moisture Content | **30% to 45% (wb)** | **50% to 70% (wb)** |
| pH of Soaking Medium | **6.0 to 7.5** | **6.0 to 9.0** (alkaline preferred) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Whole vs. Dehulled Soaking:** The presence of an intact **seed coat (hull/pericarp)** significantly retards the rate of water uptake due to the hydrophobic nature of the cuticle. Legumes like chickpea, pigeon pea, and soybean require longer soaking durations than cereals like rice, wheat, or maize because of their thicker, more lignified hulls.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **dried sponge** that has been left on a kitchen counter for a long time. When you drop it into a basin of water, it slowly swells, softens, and becomes pliable. The sponge doesn't *transform* into water — it simply *imbibes* the water into its internal matrix, which expands its volume and changes its mechanical behavior.

A cereal or legume grain behaves **exactly like that dried sponge** — but at a much more sophisticated, biological level:

- The **endosperm/cotyledon** is the "sponge matrix" (composed of starch granules embedded in a protein network).
- The **seed coat** is the "outer skin" of the sponge that initially resists water entry.
- **Soaking time** is the equivalent of leaving the sponge in the basin.
- **Soaking temperature** is the equivalent of using *warm* versus *cold* water — warm water makes the sponge swell faster because the molecules move more energetically.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for Students:** Think of soaking as a *pre-conditioning* step. We are not *cooking* the grain (no gelatinization yet). We are simply *rehydrating* it to a state where subsequent operations (milling, cooking, germination) become easier, faster, and more uniform.

---

### 1.3 Objectives / Purpose of Soaking (KTU Module 1 Highlights)

1. **Hydration & Softening:** Imparts plasticity, reducing the hardness and mechanical strength of the seed coat and cotyledon.
2. **Reduction of Anti-Nutritional Factors:** Facilitates leaching of **phytates, tannins, saponins, lectins, and protease inhibitors** into the soak water.
3. **Reduction of Cooking Time:** Hydrated grains cook up to **40–60% faster** than unsoaked grains.
4. **Enzyme Reactivation:** Triggers dormant hydrolytic enzymes (amylases, proteases, phytases) required for germination and malting.
5. **Improved Milling Yield & Quality:** Softened cotyledons detach more cleanly from the hull, reducing breakage and flour loss.
6. **Texture Modification:** Pre-gelatinization of surface starch improves dough-handling properties in cereal products (idli, dosa, bread).
7. **Microbial Load Reduction:** Properly managed warm soaking (with intermediate water change) reduces surface microbial contamination.
8. **Flavor Development:** Modifies the volatile profile, reducing beany flavor in legumes (e.g., soybean, mung bean).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The soaking process is fundamentally a **transient diffusion-controlled mass transfer operation**. It can be decomposed into the following mechanistic stages:

#### Stage 1: Surface Wetting (Initial Phase — First 5–15 minutes)
- The grain surface is wetted by the bulk water.
- Air trapped in surface pores is displaced.
- A thin, saturated water film forms around the grain.
- *Mass transfer is rapid and surface-controlled.*

#### Stage 2: Capillary Infiltration (Early Diffusion Phase)
- Water enters through the **micropyle, hilum, and stomatal openings** of the seed coat.
- Capillary forces draw water into the intercellular air spaces.
- *Mass transfer is governed by capillary pressure ($P_c = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$).*

#### Stage 3: Diffusion Through the Seed Coat
- Water must traverse the **hull (pericarp/testa)** — this is the **rate-limiting barrier** for most legumes.
- The seed coat acts as a **semi-permeable membrane**.
- For dehulled grains, this stage is bypassed, leading to faster hydration.

#### Stage 4: Bulk Diffusion into the Cotyledon/Endosperm
- Water diffuses through the cellular matrix, hydrating starch granules and protein bodies.
- This follows **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**.
- *Mass transfer is governed by the effective moisture diffusivity ($D_{eff}$).*

#### Stage 5: Equilibrium Approach
- The rate of moisture uptake asymptotically decreases as the grain approaches **equilibrium moisture content (EMC)** corresponding to the relative humidity of the soak water.
- The system reaches a quasi-steady state.

---

### 2.2 Factors Affecting the Soaking Process (KTU Exam Priority Topic)

| Factor | Effect on Hydration Rate | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature ↑** | Increases rate (Arrhenius-type) | Use 45–60 °C for accelerated soaking |
| **Time ↑** | Increases moisture up to EMC | Diminishing returns after 8–12 h |
| **Particle Size ↓** | Increases rate | Split/dehulled dals soak faster than whole |
| **Seed Coat Thickness ↑** | Decreases rate | Hard-to-cook (HTC) legumes need longer |
| **pH ↑ (Alkaline)** | Increases rate | 0.5–1% NaHCO₃ used for legumes |
| **Salt in Medium ↑** | Decreases rate initially | Osmosis effect (water flows out) |
| **Grain Variety** | Variable | Genotype-specific cell wall composition |
| **Agitation/Turbulence** | Increases rate | Improves surface contact and reduces boundary layer |
| **Vacuum Impregnation** | Drastically increases rate | Negative pressure removes trapped air |
| **Ultrasound/MW Pretreatment** | Drastically increases rate | Disrupts cell wall microchannels |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following mathematical models are **must-know** for KTU ESE questions on soaking kinetics.

**Model 1: Peleg's Empirical Model (Most Commonly Asked)**

$$M_t = M_0 + \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 \cdot t}$$

- $M_t$ = moisture content at time $t$ (%, db or wb)
- $M_0$ = initial moisture content
- $k_1$ = Peleg's rate constant (h/% or h/g water per 100 g dry solid)
- $k_2$ = Peleg's capacity constant (1/%)

**Linearized Form of Peleg's Equation** (for graphical determination of $k_1$ and $k_2$):

$$\frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 \cdot t$$

- A plot of $\frac{t}{(M_t - M_0)}$ versus $t$ yields a straight line.
- **Slope** $= k_2$, **Intercept** $= k_1$.

**Equilibrium Moisture Content (from Peleg):**

$$M_e = M_0 + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

---

**Model 2: Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Spherical Approximation)**

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left( \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial M}{\partial r} \right)$$

- $D_{eff}$ = effective moisture diffusivity ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)
- $r$ = radial position within the grain

**For Long-Time Approximation** (often used experimentally):

$$\ln\left(\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} \cdot t}{r^2}$$

- A plot of $\ln\left(\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0}\right)$ versus $t$ gives a straight line.
- **Slope** = $-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{r^2}$ → from which $D_{eff}$ is calculated.

---

**Model 3: Hydration Ratio (HR)**

$$HR = \frac{W_t - W_d}{W_d}$$

- $W_t$ = weight of grain at time $t$
- $W_d$ = dry weight of grain
- $HR_{max}$ is reported as the equilibrium hydration ratio.

---

**Model 4: Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius Equation for $D_{eff}$)**

$$D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

---

**Model 5: Water Absorption Capacity (WAC)**

$$WAC\,\left(\%\right) = \frac{W_{hydrated} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dal Milling Industry:** Optimization of soaking time/temperature directly improves dal recovery (the % of whole, unbroken cotyledons obtained). Each 1% increase in dal recovery translates to **significant economic value**.
- **Parboiling of Paddy:** Soaking is the first step of parboiling, where controlled hydration followed by steam gelatinization improves rice milling yield and nutritional profile.
- **Sprouted Grain / Malting Industry:** Controlled soaking is the critical step that determines the uniformity of germination and final malt quality.
- **Legume-Based Protein Extraction:** Soaking softens the cell wall matrix, facilitating efficient protein extraction for plant-based protein isolates.
- **Idli/Dosa Batter Fermentation:** Proper soaking of rice and black gram is the foundation of the fermentation process, influencing the final product's texture and aroma.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Peleg's Equation — Linearized Form

**Statement:** Show that the Peleg model equation $M_t = M_0 + \dfrac{t}{k_1 + k_2 t}$ can be rearranged into the linear form $\dfrac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 t$.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

Starting from the Peleg equation:

$$M_t = M_0 + \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 \cdot t}$$

Subtract $M_0$ from both sides:

$$M_t - M_0 = \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 \cdot t}$$

Now take the reciprocal of both sides (an allowed algebraic operation since both sides are positive for $t > 0$):

$$\frac{1}{M_t - M_0} = \frac{k_1 + k_2 \cdot t}{t}$$

Split the right-hand side into two terms:

$$\frac{1}{M_t - M_0} = \frac{k_1}{t} + k_2$$

Multiply both sides by $t$:

$$\frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 \cdot t$$

This is the **linearized Peleg equation**, where a plot of $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$ (Y-axis) versus $t$ (X-axis) gives a straight line with:

- **Slope** $= k_2$ (Peleg's capacity constant, 1/%)
- **Y-intercept** $= k_1$ (Peleg's rate constant, h/%)
- **X-intercept** $= -\frac{k_1}{k_2}$ (negative value, not physically meaningful but useful for validation)

The equilibrium moisture content is then:

$$M_e = M_0 + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

This is the **upper asymptote** of the original Peleg curve, representing the maximum moisture content the grain can reach under the given soaking conditions.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Peleg's Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-Style Problem (Walkthrough)**

**Problem:** The soaking kinetics of a chickpea (Bengal gram) sample were studied at 30 °C. The initial moisture content is $M_0 = 10\%$ (db). The following data was obtained:

| Time $t$ (h) | Moisture $M_t$ (% db) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 22.0 |
| 4 | 32.5 |
| 6 | 40.0 |
| 8 | 45.5 |
| 10 | 49.5 |
| 12 | 52.5 |

**Determine the Peleg constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ and the equilibrium moisture content $M_e$.**

**Solution:**

We use the linearized form: $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 t$

Calculate $(M_t - M_0)$ and $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$ for each time point:

| $t$ (h) | $M_t$ (% db) | $M_t - M_0$ | $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$ (h/%) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 22.0 | 12.0 | 0.1667 |
| 4 | 32.5 | 22.5 | 0.1778 |
| 6 | 40.0 | 30.0 | 0.2000 |
| 8 | 45.5 | 35.5 | 0.2254 |
| 10 | 49.5 | 39.5 | 0.2532 |
| 12 | 52.5 | 42.5 | 0.2824 |

Performing linear regression on the data $\left(t, \frac{t}{M_t - M_0}\right)$:

**Calculations for least-squares fit:**

- $\sum t = 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 + 12 = 42$
- $\sum \frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = 0.1667 + 0.1778 + 0.2000 + 0.2254 + 0.2532 + 0.2824 = 1.3055$
- $\sum t^2 = 4 + 16 + 36 + 64 + 100 + 144 = 364$
- $\sum t \cdot \frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = (2)(0.1667) + (4)(0.1778) + (6)(0.2000) + (8)(0.2254) + (10)(0.2532) + (12)(0.2824)$

Computing term by term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t \cdot \frac{t}{M_t - M_0} &= 0.3334 + 0.7112 + 1.2000 + 1.8032 + 2.5320 + 3.3888 \\
&= 9.9686
\end{aligned}
$$

- $n = 6$ (number of data points)
- $\bar{t} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.0$
- $\overline{Y} = \frac{1.3055}{6} = 0.2176$ (where $Y = \frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$)

**Slope** ($k_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_2 = \frac{n \sum t Y - \sum t \sum Y}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} &= \frac{(6)(9.9686) - (42)(1.3055)}{(6)(364) - (42)^2} \\
&= \frac{59.8116 - 54.8310}{2184 - 1764} \\
&= \frac{4.9806}{420} \\
&= 0.01186 \;\; \text{h/\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intercept** ($k_1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 = \bar{Y} - k_2 \bar{t} &= 0.2176 - (0.01186)(7.0) \\
&= 0.2176 - 0.0830 \\
&= 0.1346 \;\; \text{h/\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equilibrium moisture content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_e = M_0 + \frac{1}{k_2} &= 10 + \frac{1}{0.01186} \\
&= 10 + 84.32 \\
&= 94.32 \;\; \text{\% (db)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** A moisture content of ~94% (db) for chickpea is **physically reasonable** because the equilibrium moisture content of legumes at $a_w \approx 1.0$ (i.e., soaked in water) is typically in the range of 80–120% (db).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fick's Diffusion — Effective Diffusivity Calculation

**Statement:** From the long-time approximation of Fick's second law for a sphere, derive the expression used to calculate $D_{eff}$ from experimental soaking data.

**Derivation:**

For a sphere of radius $r$, the analytical solution of Fick's second law with the assumptions of uniform initial moisture, constant surface moisture, and isothermal conditions gives (Crank, 1975):

$$\frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-\frac{n^2 \pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r^2}\right)$$

For sufficiently **long soaking times** (typically $t > $ a few hours for most grains), only the first term of the series ($n = 1$) is significant. Truncating after the first term:

$$\frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} \approx \frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r^2}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r^2}\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r^2}$$

Substituting the numerical value $\ln(6/\pi^2) = -0.4977 \approx -0.50$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0}\right) = -0.50 - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r^2}$$

**Engineering Application:**

A plot of $\ln\left(\frac{M_e - M_t}{M_e - M_0}\right)$ versus $t$ yields a straight line. The slope $S$ is:

$$S = -\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{r^2}$$

Therefore, the effective moisture diffusivity is:

$$D_{eff} = -\frac{S \cdot r^2}{\pi^2}$$

The unit of $D_{eff}$ is **m²/s** (typical reported range for cereals and legumes: $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$).

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Peleg vs. Fick's Model

| Property | Peleg Model | Fick's Diffusion Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Empirical | Mechanistic / Theoretical |
| **Number of Parameters** | 2 ($k_1$, $k_2$) | 1 ($D_{eff}$) + geometry |
| **Geometry Assumption** | None (geometry-free) | Requires assumption (sphere/cylinder/slab) |
| **Data Range** | Full range of $t$ | Best for long-time (asymptotic) regime |
| **Temperature Incorporation** | Through $k_1$, $k_2$ | Through $D_{eff}$ (Arrhenius) |
| **Industry Use** | Process control, R&D | Research, drying/soaking design |
| **Ease of Use (KTU Exam)** | **Higher — most frequently asked** | Lower — derivation-heavy |
| **Validity** | Excellent for $R^2 > 0.95$ | Excellent for long times |

---

### 3.5 Effect of Soaking on Anti-Nutritional Factors (Tabular Summary)

| Anti-Nutritional Factor | Reduction in Soaking | Mechanism | Reduction % (Typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phytic Acid (Phytate)** | Significant | Leaching into water + endogenous phytase activation | **20–50%** |
| **Tannins (Polyphenols)** | Significant | Solubilization in water | **30–60%** |
| **Lectins (e.g., in soybean)** | Significant | Heat-labile; leached and partially denatured | **40–70%** |
| **Protease Inhibitors (Trypsin, Chymotrypsin)** | Moderate | Partial leaching; need heat for full inactivation | **10–30%** |
| **Oligosaccharides (Raffinose, Stachyose)** | Significant | Direct leaching (cause flatulence in legumes) | **50–80%** |
| **Saponins** | Moderate | Partial leaching | **20–40%** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Position of Soaking in the Grain Processing Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Grain Receipt] --> B[Pre-Cleaning]
    B --> C[Destoner / Magnetic Separator]
    C --> D[Grading and Sorting]
    D --> E[OPTIONAL: Dry Milling / Dehulling]
    E --> F[SOAKING TANK]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Draining and Dewatering]
    G --> H{Decision Point}
    H --> I[Wet Milling / Grinding]
    H --> J[Cooking / Boiling]
    H --> K[Germination / Malting]
    H --> L[Parboiling Steam Treatment]
    I --> M[Product Formation]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Drying]
    N --> O[Packaging and Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the schematic above, the **SOAKING TANK** is highlighted as the central node. It is the convergence point from both the *whole grain* path (cleaned → soaked) and the *pre-dehulled* path (dehulled → soaked). The four downstream branches represent the major industrial end-uses of soaked cereals/legumes.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Mass Transfer Mechanism During Soaking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Seed Coat Hull] -->|Stage 3 Diffusion Barrier| B[Cotyledon or Endosperm Matrix]
    B --> C[Starch Granules]
    B --> D[Protein Bodies]
    B --> E[Cell Wall Matrix]
    F[Bulk Soak Water] -->|Stage 1 Surface Wetting| A
    F -->|Stage 2 Capillary Infiltration via Micropyle| A
    A -->|Stage 4 Bulk Diffusion Ficks Law| B
    B -->|Stage 5 Approach to EMC| G[Equilibrium Hydration State]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
```

---

### 4.3 Process Flow Diagram: Industrial Soaking Vessel with Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Fresh Water Inlet] --> T1[Soak Tank with Steam Coil]
    G[Cleaned Grain Inlet] --> T1
    T1 -->|Controlled T 25 to 60 degC| T2[Hydration Phase 8 to 24 hours]
    T2 --> T3[Optional Intermediate Water Change]
    T3 --> T4[Final Drain Valve]
    T4 --> S[Screw Conveyor to Next Stage]
    T1 -.->|Temperature Sensor Feedback| TC[PID Temperature Controller]
    TC -.->|Steam Valve Modulation| T1
    T1 -.->|Level Sensor| LC[Level Controller]
    LC -.->|Inlet Valve Control| W
    T4 --> WW[Spent Soak Water to Effluent Treatment]
    WW -.->|Optional Nutrient Recovery| NR[Protein or Phytochemical Recovery Unit]
```

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Model: Peleg Hydration Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start M sub 0 low moisture] -->|Rapid Initial Uptake| B[Steep Region high dM sub t over dt]
    B -->|Decreasing Rate| C[Knee of Curve Transition Zone]
    C -->|Slow Approach| D[Asymptote Equilibrium M sub e]
    D -->|No Further Significant Uptake| E[Endpoint Soaking Complete]
    A --- F[Time Axis t hours]
    E --- F
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFA500
    style C fill:#FF8C00
    style D fill:#228B22
    style E fill:#006400
```

> [!TIP]
> The Peleg curve exhibits three distinct zones: a **steep initial region** (Peleg's $k_1$ dominates, reflecting rapid surface and capillary uptake), a **knee region** (transition between rate-controlled and diffusion-controlled regimes), and an **asymptotic tail** (Peleg's $k_2$ dominates, reflecting the diminishing driving force as $M_t \to M_e$).

---

### 4.5 Cause-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa-Style) of Soaking Variables

```mermaid
fishbone-beta
    Soaking Quality
    Material Factors["Material Factors\n- Grain variety\n- Initial moisture\n- Seed coat thickness\n- Particle size\n- Hardness"]
    Process Factors["Process Factors\n- Time\n- Temperature\n- Water quality\n- pH\n- Agitation"]
    Equipment Factors["Equipment Factors\n- Tank geometry\n- Heating system\n- Aeration\n- Material of construction"]
    Operator Factors["Operator Factors\n- Sampling protocol\n- Water change frequency\n- Sanitation"]
    Soaking Quality --> Material Factors
    Soaking Quality --> Process Factors
    Soaking Quality --> Equipment Factors
    Soaking Quality --> Operator Factors
    classDef main fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px;
    class Soaking Quality main
```

> [!NOTE]
> The fishbone diagram above is a **diagnostic tool** commonly used in KTU lab viva questions to identify root causes of poor soaking performance. Students are expected to know at least 3 causes under each of the four factor categories.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Define the term "soaking" as applied to cereal and legume processing. List any four objectives of soaking.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Soaking is a primary unit operation in cereals and legume processing wherein cleaned grains/legumes are immersed in water (or an alkaline/salt solution) for a defined period at a controlled temperature, to allow moisture absorption, structural softening, and partial leaching of soluble anti-nutritional factors.

**Four Objectives of Soaking:**

1. **Hydration and Softening** – Imparts plasticity to the seed matrix, reducing the mechanical strength of the hull and cotyledon.
2. **Reduction of Cooking Time** – Hydrated grains cook 40–60% faster than unsoaked grains.
3. **Leaching of Anti-Nutritional Factors** – Removes phytates, tannins, and flatulence-causing oligosaccharides.
4. **Enzyme Reactivation** – Triggers endogenous enzymes (amylases, phytases) required for germination and malting.

*Other valid objectives:* Improved milling yield, improved texture, reduced microbial load, flavor modification.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**Differentiate between "long-time cold soaking" and "short-time hot soaking" of legumes. Mention the typical temperature and time ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Long-Time Cold Soaking | Short-Time Hot Soaking |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature** | 25 °C (room temperature) | 70–100 °C (with/without pressure) |
| **Time** | 12–24 hours | 30 minutes to 2 hours |
| **Energy Input** | Low | High |
| **Microbial Risk** | Higher (favors growth) | Lower (pasteurization effect) |
| **Anti-nutrient Reduction** | Moderate | High (heat-labile factors denatured) |
| **Texture Outcome** | Soft but firm cotyledon | Softer, sometimes mushy |
| **Industrial Example** | Traditional home preparation | Commercial dal processing |

**Conclusion:** Hot soaking is preferred in industrial-scale operations due to **time efficiency, microbial safety, and superior anti-nutrient reduction**, despite higher energy input.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose 2–3 marks on soaking questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing "Peleg's $k_1$" (rate) with "$k_2$" (capacity)** — they are mathematically and physically different quantities.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** of $k_1$ and $k_2$ (h/% or h/(g water / 100 g dry solid)). Always write units.
> 3. **Reporting $M_e$ as the experimental final moisture** rather than as the **theoretical asymptote** predicted by Peleg. The two are *not* identical.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of model** (Peleg vs. Fick's) in the answer.
> 5. **Ignoring the significance of the seed coat** as a diffusion barrier in legumes.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of water absorption** during the soaking of cereals and legumes. Discuss the different stages of mass transfer and identify the rate-limiting step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **linearized form of Peleg's equation** and explain how the Peleg constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ are obtained experimentally. What is the physical significance of each constant?

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Principle of Water Absorption:**

The soaking of cereals and legumes is fundamentally a **transient, diffusion-controlled mass transfer process** in which water migrates from a region of high water activity (the bulk soak water, $a_w \approx 1.0$) into a region of low water activity (the dry grain interior, $a_w \approx 0.4$–$0.6$). The driving force is the **water activity gradient** ($\Delta a_w$), and the rate is governed by the **effective moisture diffusivity** of the grain matrix.

**Five Stages of Mass Transfer:**

**Stage 1 — Surface Wetting (t = 0 to ~15 min):**
The grain surface is rapidly wetted; air in surface pores is displaced. This stage is **surface-controlled** and follows first-order kinetics.

**Stage 2 — Capillary Infiltration (early diffusion):**
Water enters through the **micropyle** (a natural pore in the seed coat near the hilum) and through **stomatal openings** in the hull. Capillary forces pull water into the intercellular air spaces of the seed coat. The driving pressure is given by the Young-Laplace equation, $P_c = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$, where $\sigma$ is surface tension, $\theta$ is the contact angle, and $r$ is the capillary radius.

**Stage 3 — Diffusion Through the Seed Coat (rate-limiting for most legumes):**
The seed coat (hull/pericarp/testa) is a **hydrophobic, lignified membrane** that acts as a semi-permeable barrier. Water must traverse this layer by **molecular diffusion**, which is slower than capillary flow. For most legumes with intact hulls, **this stage is the rate-limiting step**.

**Stage 4 — Bulk Diffusion into the Cotyledon/Endosperm:**
Once past the seed coat, water diffuses into the cell wall matrix, hydrating starch granules (causing reversible swelling) and protein bodies. This stage follows **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** in a spherical coordinate system.

**Stage 5 — Equilibrium Approach:**
As the grain approaches its equilibrium moisture content ($M_e$) corresponding to the bulk water's $a_w$, the driving force diminishes, and the rate of moisture uptake **asymptotically approaches zero**. The system reaches a quasi-steady state.

**Rate-Limiting Step:**

For **whole (unhulled) legumes** — Stage 3 (seed coat diffusion) is rate-limiting.
