For **dehulled grains** (e.g., dehusked rice, split dal) — Stage 4 (bulk diffusion in the matrix) becomes the rate-limiting step because Stage 3 is bypassed.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Derivation of Linearized Peleg Equation:**

Starting from the standard Peleg equation:

$$M_t = M_0 + \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 t}$$

Rearranging:

$$M_t - M_0 = \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 t}$$

Reciprocating:

$$\frac{1}{M_t - M_0} = \frac{k_1 + k_2 t}{t} = \frac{k_1}{t} + k_2$$

Multiplying both sides by $t$:

$$\frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 t \quad \textbf{(Linearized Form)}$$

**Experimental Determination:**

1. Conduct a soaking experiment and record $(t, M_t)$ pairs.
2. Calculate $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$ for each time point.
3. Plot $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0}$ (Y-axis) versus $t$ (X-axis).
4. Perform linear regression to obtain the best-fit straight line.
5. **Slope** $= k_2$ (Peleg's capacity constant)
6. **Y-intercept** $= k_1$ (Peleg's rate constant)

**Physical Significance:**

- **$k_1$ (rate constant, h/%):** Represents the **inverse of the initial water absorption rate**. A *smaller* $k_1$ means *faster* initial water uptake. $k_1$ is large when the seed coat is thick or hydrophobic.

- **$k_2$ (capacity constant, 1/%):** Represents the **inverse of the maximum moisture uptake capacity** above the initial moisture. A *smaller* $k_2$ means the grain can absorb *more* water (i.e., higher $M_e$). $k_2$ is related to the grain's hygroscopicity and matrix porosity.

- **Equilibrium moisture content:** $M_e = M_0 + \dfrac{1}{k_2}$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the **anti-nutritional factors** present in cereals and legumes? Explain how the soaking process helps in their reduction, with a suitable tabulated comparison.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **methods employed to enhance the soaking efficiency** of cereals and legumes. Include at least two recent/non-conventional methods in your answer.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Anti-Nutritional Factors (ANFs):**

Anti-nutritional factors are **naturally occurring compounds** in plant foods that interfere with the absorption or utilization of nutrients. Cereals and legumes contain several ANFs, with legumes being particularly rich in them.

**Common ANFs in Cereals & Legumes:**

| ANF | Common Source | Health Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phytic Acid (Phytate)** | All cereals & legumes, esp. bran | Binds minerals (Ca, Fe, Zn) — reduces bioavailability |
| **Tannins (Polyphenols)** | Sorghum, millet, red kidney bean, cowpea | Bind proteins — reduce digestibility; astringent taste |
| **Lectins** | Soybean, kidney bean, lentil | Agglutinate red blood cells; cause GI distress |
| **Trypsin/Chymotrypsin Inhibitors** | All legumes, esp. soybean | Inhibit protein-digesting enzymes — reduce protein utilization |
| **Saponins** | Quinoa, soybean, chickpea | Bitter taste; can lyse RBCs at high doses |
| **Oligosaccharides (Raffinose, Stachyose)** | All legumes | Cause flatulence and abdominal discomfort |
| **Cyanogenic Glycosides** | Cassava, sorghum (limited) | Release HCN upon hydrolysis — toxic |

**Mechanism of Reduction by Soaking:**

1. **Direct Leaching:** Water-soluble ANFs (oligosaccharides, tannins, saponins, some phytates) dissolve into the soak water and are removed when the water is drained.
2. **Endogenous Enzyme Activation:** Soaking activates **endogenous phytase**, which hydrolyzes phytic acid into inositol and free phosphate, thereby reducing the chelation of minerals.
3. **pH-Mediated Solubilization:** Soaking in slightly alkaline solutions (e.g., 0.5% NaHCO₃) increases the solubility and leachability of phytates and tannins.
4. **Microbial Fermentation Side-Effects:** During long soaks at room temperature, native microflora can partially ferment oligosaccharides, further reducing flatulence-causing sugars.
5. **Swelling and Cell Wall Disruption:** Hydration softens the cell wall, exposing bound ANFs to subsequent processing (e.g., cooking, germination) more effectively.

**Tabular Summary of ANF Reduction by Soaking:**

| Anti-Nutritional Factor | Reduction by Soaking Alone | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phytic Acid | 20–50% | Leaching + Phytase activation |
| Tannins | 30–60% | Solubilization in water |
| Lectins | 40–70% | Leaching + partial denaturation |
| Protease Inhibitors | 10–30% | Partial leaching (full inactivation requires heat) |
| Oligosaccharides (Raffinose, Stachyose) | 50–80% | Direct leaching |
| Saponins | 20–40% | Partial leaching |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Conventional Methods of Enhancing Soaking Efficiency:**

1. **Increase in Temperature (Warm Soaking, 45–60 °C):** Increases molecular kinetic energy and reduces water viscosity, accelerating diffusion. The Arrhenius relationship applies: $D_{eff} = D_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$.

2. **Alkaline Soaking (pH 8–9):** Use of dilute NaHCO₃ or Na₂CO₃ softens the seed coat, increases the solubility of phytates/tannins, and improves hydration rate.

3. **Mechanical Pre-Treatment (Dehulling, Scarification, Cracking):** Removes or damages the seed coat, eliminating the rate-limiting barrier. Dehulled legumes soak 30–50% faster than whole ones.

4. **Agitation and Aeration:** Mechanical stirring reduces the external boundary layer thickness, increasing the surface mass transfer coefficient.

5. **Use of Salt Solutions (e.g., 0.5–1% NaCl):** Surprisingly, salt can *initially slow* soaking due to osmotic outflow, but post-soak rinsing gives a softened, more uniformly hydrated grain with improved cooking quality.

6. **Intermediate Water Change:** Discarding soak water at the mid-point and refilling with fresh water maintains a steep concentration gradient for leached ANFs, enhancing their removal.

**Non-Conventional / Emerging Methods:**

**1. Vacuum Impregnation (VI) Soaking:**
- The grains are first placed under **vacuum (typically 50–100 mbar)** for 10–30 minutes.
- Vacuum removes trapped air from the intercellular spaces and pores.
- When the vacuum is released and water is introduced, the **pressure differential** forces water rapidly into the evacuated spaces.
- Result: **Soaking time reduced by 50–70%** and more uniform moisture distribution.

**2. Ultrasound-Assisted Soaking:**
- Application of **high-frequency ultrasound (20–100 kHz)** in the soak water.
- Acoustic cavitation creates micro-bubbles that **collapse violently near the seed surface**, generating micro-jets and shock waves.
- These phenomena **disrupt the seed coat micro-structure**, creating micro-channels for water entry.
- Result: **25–40% reduction in soaking time** and improved phytate reduction.

**3. Microwave-Assisted Soaking:**
- Brief, intermittent **microwave exposure (2450 MHz, 100–500 W)** rapidly heats the internal moisture, causing **internal steam generation** that physically disrupts the seed coat.
- The grain is hydrated by both thermal and pressure-driven mechanisms.
- Result: **Up to 60% faster soaking** with comparable textural quality.

**4. High-Pressure Processing (HPP) Pre-Treatment:**
- Grains are first subjected to **200–600 MPa pressure** for 1–10 minutes.
- The pressure temporarily disrupts cell membranes and cell walls (without cooking), creating new water pathways.
- Subsequent atmospheric soaking is then much faster.
- Result: Dramatically improved hydration kinetics and improved protein extractability.

**5. Enzyme-Assisted Soaking:**
- Addition of **cellulase or pectinase** (typically 0.1–0.5% w/w) to the soak water.
- These enzymes partially **hydrolyze the cell wall polysaccharides** of the seed coat.
- Result: **30–50% reduction in soaking time** with the bonus of improved digestibility.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Know Before Exam):**

- Soaking is a **diffusion-controlled, primary unit operation** that softens grains and reduces cooking time by 40–60%.
- The **rate-limiting step** for whole legumes is **diffusion through the seed coat** (Stage 3).
- The **driving force** is the **water activity gradient** ($\Delta a_w$) between the bulk soak water and the dry grain.
- **Peleg's model** ($M_t = M_0 + \frac{t}{k_1 + k_2 t}$) is the **most exam-relevant empirical model** — memorize the linearized form: $\frac{t}{M_t - M_0} = k_1 + k_2 t$.
- **$k_1$** = rate constant (smaller = faster initial absorption); **$k_2$** = capacity constant (smaller = higher EMC).
- **$M_e$** = $M_0 + \frac{1}{k_2}$ — the **theoretical asymptote** (not the experimental final moisture).
- **Fick's long-time approximation** gives a linear $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ plot with slope $-\pi^2 D_{eff}/r^2$.
- **$D_{eff}$** for cereals/legumes typically lies in the range $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Temperature ↑** → **Soaking rate ↑** (Arrhenius dependence).
- **Alkaline soaking** improves phytate and tannin removal.
- **ANF reduction by soaking:** Oligosaccharides 50–80%, Tannins 30–60%, Phytates 20–50%, Protease inhibitors 10–30%.
- **Vacuum impregnation, ultrasound, and microwave** are emerging non-conventional methods that can reduce soaking time by **25–70%**.
- **Softening ratio** / **Hydration ratio** are the two key engineering metrics used to quantify soaking effectiveness.
- **Hard-to-cook (HTC)** defect in legumes is a major industrial problem that **extends soaking time** by 2–3×.
- Typical **industrial parameters:** 1:2 to 1:5 (grain:water), 25–60 °C, 8–24 hours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steaming** is a hydrothermal processing unit operation in which whole, dehulled, crushed, or flaked **cereals and legumes** are exposed to saturated or superheated water vapor (typically at atmospheric pressure or under low-pressure conditions, ranging from **100 °C to 120 °C**) for a controlled contact time. The operation transfers latent heat of vaporization (≈ **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C) to the substrate without direct water immersion, triggering simultaneous physicochemical, structural, and biochemical changes within the grain matrix.

In the strict context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OEFTT836)**, steaming is classified as a **primary pre-conditioning unit operation** that precedes downstream processes such as flaking, puffing, roasting, roller drying, and extrusion in cereals, and de-hulling, de-toxification, and flour preparation in legumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Steaming is positioned at the intersection of *thermal processing* and *moisture conditioning*. It is functionally distinct from *boiling* (direct water contact) and *autoclaving* (saturated steam under pressure > 1 atm).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing **idiyappam (nool puttu)** in a traditional Kerala kitchen. The rice flour is not soaked in boiling water, but is exposed to steam rising from the cooker. The flour grains soften, become pliable, and bind together — yet they remain relatively dry to the touch. That is steaming in action.

In another familiar example, when you steam **par-boiled rice (Unda rice / Red parboiled rice)**, the paddy is soaked in hot water, then drained and exposed to steam in a metal drum. The steam penetrates the husk, gelatinizes the starch within the endosperm, and locks the nutrients inside the grain. The result is a firmer, more nutritious rice grain compared to raw rice.

In essence:
- **Boiling** = grain sits *in* water → leaching losses are high.
- **Steaming** = grain sits *under* vapor → minimal leaching, maximum internal modification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Steaming delivers heat *and* moisture *simultaneously* to the grain interior, triggering **starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and enzyme inactivation** — three pillars of cereal/legume transformation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Steam temperature (atmospheric) | **100 °C** |
| Steam temperature (low-pressure) | **105 – 120 °C** |
| Latent heat of vaporization | **2257 kJ/kg** |
| Specific heat of dry grain | **1.3 – 1.6 kJ/kg·K** |
| Steaming time (cereals) | **5 – 30 minutes** |
| Steaming time (legumes) | **15 – 60 minutes** |
| Target moisture gain | **2 – 6 % (w.b.)** |
| Steam pressure (low-pressure cooker) | **0.05 – 0.3 MPa** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Steaming — Objectives

Steaming is rarely an end in itself; it is a **gateway process**. The objectives that drive its application in cereals and legumes are:

1. **Starch Gelatinization:** At ~**60 – 75 °C** (depending on the starch source), starch granules absorb water, swell irreversibly, and lose crystallinity. Steaming supplies both the temperature and the moisture for this transition.
2. **Protein Denaturation:** Legume proteins (globulins such as *vicilin* and *legumin*) denature between **80 – 95 °C**, improving digestibility and reducing anti-nutritional factors.
3. **Enzyme Inactivation:** Lipase, lipoxygenase, peroxidase, and protease enzymes are inactivated, arresting rancidity and off-flavor development during storage.
4. **Anti-Nutritional Factor (ANF) Reduction:** In legumes, steaming reduces **trypsin inhibitors, lectins, phytic acid**, and **oligosaccharides (raffinose, stachyose)** responsible for flatulence.
5. **Microbial Load Reduction:** Surface microflora is reduced by **1 – 3 log cycles**.
6. **Improved Dehulling:** In legumes like *pigeon pea (toor dal)* and *black gram (urad dal)*, steaming loosens the seed coat, facilitating subsequent milling.

## 2.2 The 'How' — Process Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

When steam contacts the grain, the following chain of events occurs:

- **Step 1: Condensation Heat Transfer.** Steam condenses on the cooler grain surface, releasing its latent heat (**2257 kJ/kg**) instantly. This is a highly efficient mode of heat transfer (heat transfer coefficient **h ≈ 6000 W/m²·K** for steam vs. ~**200 W/m²·K** for hot air).
- **Step 2: Surface Heating.** The outer layers rapidly rise to **~100 °C**.
- **Step 3: Moisture Penetration.** Condensed water migrates inward by capillary action and diffusion, increasing grain moisture by **2 – 6 %**.
- **Step 4: Internal Starch Gelatinization.** Once interior temperature exceeds the gelatinization temperature, starch granules swell and amylose leaches out.
- **Step 5: Structural Reorganization.** On cooling and drying, the gelatinized starch retrogrades partially, forming a **rigid, glassy matrix** that confers hardness (e.g., parboiled rice).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = m_{steam} \cdot L$ | Heat transferred by steam (L = latent heat) | kJ |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{gel} = T_{process} - T_{gel}$ | Driving force for gelatinization | °C or K |
| 3 | $M_f = M_i + \Delta M$ | Final moisture = Initial moisture + Moisture absorbed | % w.b. |
| 4 | $t_{process} = \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot V}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_{steam} - T_{grain})}$ | Approx. time to reach core temperature (1st-order lumped model) | s |
| 5 | $E_a^{gel} \approx 60 \text{ to } 90$ | Activation energy for starch gelatinization | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$ | Effective moisture diffusivity (Arrhenius form) | m²/s |
| 7 | $W_{gel} = W_0 \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-k \cdot t)\right]$ | Fractional gelatinization kinetics (1st-order) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{process} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency of steamer | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation has been avoided inside table rows to preserve Markdown table integrity; absolute values and norms are implicit in the physical context (e.g., all temperatures in Kelvin are positive).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Parboiled Rice Industry (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Punjab):** Steaming is the *core* unit operation. Roughly **20 % of global rice** is parboiled — that is, soaked → steamed → dried.
- **Instant Noodle Manufacturing:** Wheat flour is steamed before noodle formation to pre-gelatinize starch, reducing cooking time.
- **Breakfast Cereal Flakes:** Oats and maize are steamed at **110 – 120 °C** before roller flaking to soften the kernel.
- **Legume Dehulling Plants (Tur, Moong, Urad):** Short steaming (15 – 20 min) conditions the seed coat for clean milling, reducing broken kernel fraction from ~**15 %** down to **3 – 5 %**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Heat and Mass Balance of a Batch Steamer

Consider a batch steamer of capacity **M (kg of dry grain)** at initial moisture **Mᵢ (% w.b.)** and initial temperature **Tᵢ (°C)**. Saturated steam at **Tₛ = 100 °C** contacts the grain for time **t (s)**.

### 3.1.1 Energy Balance

The heat required to (a) raise grain temperature from $T_i$ to $T_f$ and (b) supply latent heat for the moisture absorbed is:

$$
Q_{required} = M \cdot c_{p,grain} \cdot (T_f - T_i) + \Delta M \cdot L
$$

where:
- $c_{p,grain}$ = specific heat of grain ≈ **1.5 kJ/kg·K**
- $\Delta M$ = mass of steam condensed on the grain (kg)
- $L$ = latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**

The heat supplied by the steam is:

$$
Q_{supplied} = m_{steam} \cdot L
$$

Equating (assuming 100 % condensation efficiency):

$$
m_{steam} = \frac{M \cdot c_{p,grain} \cdot (T_f - T_i)}{L}
$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $M = 1000$ kg paddy
- $T_i = 30$ °C
- $T_f = 100$ °C (assume full equilibration)
- $c_{p,grain} = 1.5$ kJ/kg·K
- $L = 2257$ kJ/kg

**Step 1:** Compute sensible heat rise.

$$
Q = 1000 \times 1.5 \times (100 - 30) = 1000 \times 1.5 \times 70 = 105{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute steam requirement.

$$
m_{steam} = \frac{105{,}000}{2257} \approx 46.52 \text{ kg of steam}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret. To raise **1 tonne** of paddy from 30 °C to 100 °C, approximately **46.5 kg** of saturated steam must condense on the grain. This corresponds to a moisture pickup of about **4.65 %** on a wet basis — which is consistent with the typical **2 – 6 %** moisture gain observed in parboiling operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step Logic Recap:** The numerical 4.65 % moisture gain is a *lower bound*. In practice, additional steam condenses as the grain equilibrates with the humid microenvironment, raising total pickup to **5 – 8 %** before drainage.

## 3.2 Kinetics of Starch Gelatinization During Steaming

Starch gelatinization follows a **pseudo-first-order kinetic model** under isothermal conditions:

$$
W(t) = W_\infty \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-k \cdot t)\right]
$$

where:
- $W(t)$ = degree of gelatinization at time $t$ (dimensionless, 0 → 1)
- $W_\infty$ = maximum achievable gelatinization (≈ 1.0 for full gelatinization)
- $k$ = rate constant (s⁻¹), strongly temperature-dependent
- $t$ = steaming time (s)

The rate constant obeys the **Arrhenius relation**:

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

### 3.2.1 Derivational Bridge

Starting from the differential form of first-order kinetics:

$$
\frac{dW}{dt} = k \cdot (W_\infty - W)
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_0^W \frac{dW'}{W_\infty - W'} = \int_0^t k \, d\tau
$$

$$
-\ln(W_\infty - W')\Big|_0^W = k \cdot t
$$

$$
-\ln(W_\infty - W) + \ln(W_\infty) = k \cdot t
$$

Solving for $W(t)$:

$$
W(t) = W_\infty \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-k \cdot t)\right]
$$

This confirms the boxed formula above.

### 3.2.2 Numerical Illustration

For rice starch at $T = 70$ °C, $k \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹, $E_a \approx 75$ kJ/mol.

After **$t = 10$ min = 600 s**:

$$
W(600) = 1.0 \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 600)\right]
$$

$$
W(600) = 1 - \exp(-0.72) = 1 - 0.487 = 0.513
$$

So roughly **51 %** of starch granules are gelatinized after 10 minutes at 70 °C — a slow process. Raising temperature to **95 °C** (well above gelatinization onset) increases $k$ by orders of magnitude, achieving near-complete gelatinization in **2 – 5 minutes**.

## 3.3 Anti-Nutritional Factor (ANF) Reduction in Legumes

Trypsin inhibitor activity (TIA) reduction follows similar first-order decay:

$$
TIA(t) = TIA_0 \cdot \exp(-k_{TIA} \cdot t)
$$

For chickpea steamed at **100 °C**, $k_{TIA} \approx 0.04$ min⁻¹. After **30 min**:

$$
TIA(30) = TIA_0 \cdot \exp(-0.04 \times 30) = TIA_0 \cdot \exp(-1.2) \approx 0.301 \cdot TIA_0
$$

This corresponds to a **~70 % reduction** in trypsin inhibitor activity — a major nutritional gain.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Steaming on Different Grains

| Grain | Steaming Temp (°C) | Time (min) | Moisture Gain (%) | Primary Purpose |
|-------|---------------------|------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Paddy (rice) | 100 – 105 | 15 – 25 | 4 – 6 | Parboiling |
| Wheat | 100 – 110 | 10 – 20 | 3 – 5 | Pre-conditioning for milling / noodle making |
| Maize | 105 – 120 | 15 – 30 | 5 – 7 | Flaking / breakfast cereal |
| Oats | 100 – 110 | 15 – 25 | 5 – 8 | Kilning + flake formation |
| Pigeon pea (Tur) | 100 | 15 – 20 | 3 – 5 | Dehulling aid |
| Black gram (Urad) | 100 | 15 – 20 | 3 – 5 | Dehulling aid |
| Chickpea (Chana) | 100 | 20 – 30 | 4 – 6 | ANF reduction |
| Soybean | 100 – 105 | 20 – 40 | 5 – 8 | Trypsin inhibitor destruction |

---

## 3.5 Types of Steamers Used in Industry

1. **Open Atmospheric Batch Steamer:** Simplest design; grain rests on a perforated tray above a boiling water reservoir. Used in cottage-scale parboiling.
2. **Pressure Steamer (Autoclave-type):** Operates at **0.1 – 0.3 MPa**, achieving temperatures up to **143 °C**. Used in modern parboiling plants.
3. **Continuous Steam Cooker (e.g., 'Crystal' or 'Jain' systems):** Grain moves through a horizontal trough while steam is injected from above. Throughput up to **10 – 50 tonnes/hour**.
4. **Rotary Drum Steamer:** Cylindrical drum rotates slowly, ensuring uniform steam contact. Common in legume processing.
5. **Fluidized-Bed Steam Cooker:** Steam jets fluidize the grain layer, maximizing heat transfer coefficient.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Batch Steaming Operation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the interactions and unit-operation flow of a typical batch steaming line used in cereal/legume processing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Grain Intake] --> B[Pre-cleaning & Grading]
    B --> C[Optional: Hot Water Soaking]
    C --> D[Drainage & Surface Drying]
    D --> E[Batch Steamer Vessel]
    E -- Saturated Steam @ 100-120 °C --> F[Steaming Zone]
    F --> G[Temperature-Hold Section]
    G --> H[Steam Release & Vapor Recovery]
    H --> I[Conditioned Grain Discharge]
    I --> J{Downstream Routing}
    J --> K[Mechanical Drying]
    J --> L[Roller Flaking]
    J --> M[Dehulling Mill]
    J --> N[Extrusion Line]
    K --> O[Storage / Packing]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    E --- P[Steam Generator Boiler]
    P -- Steam Supply Line --> E
    P -- Condensate Return --> Q[Boiler Feed Tank]
    Q --> P
```

## 4.2 Process Decision Tree — Parameter Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Is grain a cereal or legume?}
    S1 -- Cereal --> S2{Target product?}
    S1 -- Legume --> S3{Target product?}
    S2 -- Parboiled rice --> S4[100 °C, 15-25 min, 4-6% moisture gain]
    S2 -- Flaked cereal --> S5[110-120 °C, 15-30 min, 5-7% moisture gain]
    S2 -- Noodle wheat --> S6[100 °C, 10-20 min, 3-5% moisture gain]
    S3 -- Dehulled dal --> S7[100 °C, 15-20 min, 3-5% moisture gain]
    S3 -- ANF-reduced flour --> S8[100 °C, 30-60 min, 5-8% moisture gain]
    S4 --> R1[Verify: Gelatinization > 80%, Moisture within target]
    S5 --> R1
    S6 --> R1
    S7 --> R2[Verify: Seed coat peels cleanly, < 5% brokens]
    S8 --> R3[Verify: TIA reduction > 70%, color acceptable]
    R1 --> END[Release to Next Unit Operation]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Heat and Mass Transfer Pathways During Steaming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Steam Phase]
        V[Saturated Water Vapor at 100 °C]
    end
    subgraph G[Grain Matrix]
        O[Outer Husk / Pericarp]
        M[Outer Endosperm]
        C[Core Endosperm]
        E[Embryo]
    end
    V -- Condensation --> O
    O -- Conduction --> M
    M -- Conduction + Diffusion --> C
    O -- Capillary Migration --> M
    V -- Convective Heat --> O
    C -. Retrograde On Cooling .-> M
    E -. Moisture Gain .-> O
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define steaming in the context of cereal and legume processing. Mention two advantages of steaming over direct boiling. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Steaming is a hydrothermal unit operation in which cereals or legumes are exposed to saturated water vapor (typically at **100 – 120 °C**) without direct immersion in water. The process transfers latent heat of condensation (≈ **2257 kJ/kg**) to the grain, causing starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and enzyme inactivation.

*Advantages over direct boiling:*
1. **Minimal leaching losses** — water-soluble vitamins, sugars, and salts remain inside the grain (critical in parboiling rice).
2. **Uniform heat transfer** — condensation delivers a heat transfer coefficient roughly **30× higher** than hot air, ensuring rapid and even heating.

---

### Q2. What is the typical range of moisture gain during steaming of cereals? Why is moisture gain important? `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The typical moisture gain is **2 – 6 % (w.b.)** for cereals and up to **8 %** for legumes. Moisture gain is essential because:
- It supplies the **water** required for starch granule swelling and gelatinization.
- It conditions the grain for **subsequent operations** such as dehulling, flaking, or extrusion.
- Insufficient moisture leads to **incomplete gelatinization**; excessive moisture causes **kernel bursting** and downstream drying inefficiencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Equivalent]`

**(a)** Explain the objectives of steaming in cereals and legumes with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Steaming is a versatile pre-conditioning operation with the following objectives:

1. **Starch Gelatinization (Cereals):** In parboiled rice, steaming at **100 – 105 °C** for 15 – 25 min gelatinizes the starch, locking nutrients inside the grain and producing a firmer, less sticky cooked rice.

2. **Protein Denaturation (Legumes):** Steaming soybean at **100 °C** for 20 – 40 min denatures trypsin inhibitors and lectins, improving protein digestibility from ~**60 %** to over **85 %**.

3. **Enzyme Inactivation:** Lipase and lipoxygenase in oats are inactivated by steaming at **110 °C**, extending shelf life from weeks to months.

4. **Dehulling Aid:** Steaming pigeon pea at **100 °C** for 15 min loosens the gum layer binding the seed coat, reducing broken kernels during milling from **15 %** to **3 – 5 %**.

5. **Microbial Reduction:** Surface microbial load is reduced by **1 – 3 log cycles**, enhancing food safety.

6. **Pre-gelatinization for Noodle/Flake Production:** Steamed wheat flour hydrates uniformly, producing noodles with smooth texture and reduced cooking loss.

**(b)** Derive the first-order kinetic expression for starch gelatinization during steaming and compute the degree of gelatinization achieved after 15 minutes at 80 °C, given $k = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the differential form of first-order kinetics:

$$
\frac{dW}{dt} = k \cdot (W_\infty - W)
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dW}{W_\infty - W} = k \, dt
$$

Integrating from 0 to $t$ and from 0 to $W$:

$$
\int_0^W \frac{dW'}{W_\infty - W'} = \int_0^t k \, d\tau
$$

$$
-\ln(W_\infty - W) + \ln(W_\infty) = k \cdot t
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{W_\infty}{W_\infty - W}\right) = k \cdot t
$$

Solving:

$$
W(t) = W_\infty \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-k \cdot t)\right]
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- $t = 15$ min = $900$ s
- $k = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹
- $W_\infty = 1.0$

$$
k \cdot t = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 900 = 4.5
$$

$$
W(900) = 1.0 \cdot \left[1 - \exp(-4.5)\right] = 1 - 0.0111 = 0.9889
$$

**Result:** Approximately **98.9 %** starch gelatinization is achieved — virtually complete. This explains why industrial parboiling uses relatively short steaming times at high temperatures.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Equivalent]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe a continuous steaming system used in parboiled rice processing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous steaming system (e.g., the 'Improved CFTRI parboiling system') consists of the following sequential units:

1. **Pre-cleaner & Soaking Tank:** Paddy is cleaned and soaked in hot water (**65 – 70 °C**) for 3 – 4 h until moisture reaches ~**30 % w.b.**
2. **Drainage Section:** Excess water is removed by vibrating screens.
3. **Steam Cooker / Steaming Column:** Soaked paddy enters at the top and moves downward through a vertical or horizontal column. Saturated steam at **100 – 105 °C** is injected countercurrently from the bottom. Residence time: **15 – 25 min**.
4. **Tempering / Holding Section:** Steamed paddy is held for **10 – 15 min** to allow moisture and temperature equilibration within the kernel.
5. **Mechanical Dryer:** Two-stage dryer reduces moisture from ~**35 %** to **14 % w.b.**
6. **Polisher & Grader:** Final product is polished and graded.

The flow is:

```
Soaking Tank → Drainage → Steam Cooker → Tempering → Dryer → Polisher → Storage
```

**(b)** Calculate the steam required (in kg) to raise 2 tonnes of soaked paddy from 70 °C to 100 °C during steaming. Given: specific heat of soaked paddy = 2.5 kJ/kg·K, latent heat of steam = 2257 kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mass of soaked paddy, $M = 2000$ kg
- Initial temperature, $T_i = 70$ °C
- Final temperature, $T_f = 100$ °C
- Specific heat, $c_p = 2.5$ kJ/kg·K
- Latent heat, $L = 2257$ kJ/kg

**Step 1:** Heat required for sensible temperature rise.

$$
Q = M \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_i)
$$

$$
Q = 2000 \times 2.5 \times (100 - 70)
$$

$$
Q = 2000 \times 2.5 \times 30 = 150{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2:** Steam mass required (assuming 100 % condensation efficiency).

$$
m_{steam} = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{150{,}000}{2257}
$$

$$
m_{steam} \approx 66.46 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Moisture pickup estimate (on wet basis).

$$
\Delta M_{wb} = \frac{66.46}{2000 + 66.46} \times 100 \approx 3.22 \text{ \% w.b.}
$$

**Result:** Approximately **66.5 kg** of saturated steam is required, corresponding to a moisture pickup of **~3.2 %**, well within the target 2 – 6 % range for parboiling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Steaming Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing boiling with steaming** — students often write that grains are "cooked in water," losing 1–2 marks on conceptual clarity.
> 2. **Forgetting the Arrhenius temperature dependence** of gelatinization rate constant $k$ — at least 1 mark is reserved for recognizing that $k$ increases sharply with temperature.
> 3. **Wrong unit for latent heat** — using $L$ in J/kg instead of kJ/kg causes a 1000× numerical error.
> 4. **Ignoring moisture correction** in heat balance — soaked paddy has $c_p \approx 2.5$ kJ/kg·K, NOT 1.5 kJ/kg·K (which is for dry grain). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the steam quality assumption** — always state "assume 100 % saturated steam, 100 % condensation efficiency" to avoid ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Steaming** is exposure of grains to saturated vapor (typically **100 – 120 °C**), NOT immersion in water.
- Heat transfer occurs primarily via **condensation** of steam (latent heat **≈ 2257 kJ/kg**), which is ~30× faster than convective hot-air heating.
- Three core physicochemical changes: **starch gelatinization (60 – 75 °C), protein denaturation (80 – 95 °C), enzyme inactivation (> 80 °C)**.
- Typical moisture gain: **2 – 6 %** for cereals; up to **8 %** for legumes.
- Gelatinization follows **first-order kinetics**: $W(t) = W_\infty [1 - \exp(-k \cdot t)]$.
- Rate constant obeys **Arrhenius**: $k = k_0 \exp(-E_a / R T)$.
- Specific heat of **dry** grain ≈ **1.5 kJ/kg·K**; of **soaked** grain ≈ **2.5 kJ/kg·K**.
- Trypsin inhibitor reduction follows first-order decay: $TIA(t) = TIA_0 \exp(-k \cdot t)$.
- Key industrial applications: **parboiled rice, noodle wheat, oat kilning, legume dehulling, breakfast cereal flaking, ANF reduction in soybean**.
- Steamer types: **atmospheric batch, pressure autoclave, continuous column, rotary drum, fluidized bed**.
- **Always** distinguish steaming from boiling, autoclaving, and extrusion in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Drying # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Drying** is a unit operation involving the simultaneous transfer of **heat** and **mass**, in which the moisture content of a wet solid (cereal grain or legume) is reduced by evaporation of the liquid (water) into a surrounding gaseous medium (typically hot air) until the desired final moisture content is achieved for safe storage and processing. In the context of cereals and legumes, drying is the **most critical post-harvest operation** because freshly harvested grains often have moisture contents in the range of **18–30% (w.b.)** while safe storage moisture is typically **12–14% (w.b.)** for cereals and **10–12% (w.b.)** for legumes. Drying aims to lower this moisture to a level that inhibits microbial, enzymatic, and insect activity. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology:** > - **Moisture Content (MC)**: Mass of water per unit mass of wet material (wet basis) or per unit mass of dry solid (dry basis). > - **Wet Basis (w.b.)**: $M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ > - **Dry Basis (d.b.)**: $M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ > - **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)**: The moisture content at which the grain neither gains nor loses moisture when exposed to air of given temperature and relative humidity. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **wet towel hanging on a clothesline on a sunny day**. The sun provides heat (energy), the wind carries away the moisture-laden air (mass transfer), and the towel gradually becomes dry. This is essentially what happens in grain drying, but in a precisely controlled industrial manner. Now picture thousands of rice or wheat grains inside a tall cylindrical bin, with hot air being forced upward through them. Each grain acts like a tiny sponge releasing water vapor into the air. The drying air absorbs moisture and exits at the top, while grains move downward (or stay in place) and progressively become drier. **Why is drying so crucial for cereals and legumes?** - 🌾 **Prevents spoilage** by reducing water activity ($a_w$) below 0.70 - 🦠 **Stops mold growth** (e.g., *Aspergillus flavus* producing aflatoxins in peanuts and maize) - 🐛 **Reduces insect infestation** (insects need $a_w > 0.55$ to thrive) - ⏱️ **Enables safe long-term storage** (months to years) - 💪 **Preserves nutritional quality** and germination capability (for seed grains) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Drying Metrics for Cereals (Industry Standards):** > - **Safe Storage Moisture**: **12–14% w.b.** for cereals; **10–12% w.b.** for legumes > - **Recommended Drying Air Temperature**: **40–60°C** for seed grains; up to **100°C** for commercial/feed grain > - **Maximum Drying Temperature to Preserve Viability**: **≤ 43°C** for rice seed, **≤ 45°C** for wheat seed # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principles of Drying (The Science Behind It) Drying is governed by **two simultaneous transport phenomena**: ### A. Heat Transfer (Energy Balance) Heat flows **from the hot drying air → into the grain kernel**, where it provides the latent heat of vaporization required to convert liquid water into vapor. $$ Q = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) $$ Where: - $Q$ = heat transferred (kJ) - $m_a$ = mass of dry air (kg) - $c_p$ = specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - $T_{in}, T_{out}$ = inlet and outlet air temperatures (°C) ### B. Mass Transfer (Moisture Removal) Water vapor diffuses **from the interior of the grain → through the pores → into the boundary layer of air → carried away by the drying air**. $$ N_A = k_y \cdot (Y_{sat} - Y) $$ Where: - $N_A$ = mass flux of water (kg/m²·s) - $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s) - $Y_{sat}$ = humidity ratio at saturation - $Y$ = humidity ratio of bulk air ## 2.2 Psychrometrics — The Behavior of Moist Air > [!NOTE] > **Psychrometrics** is the study of the thermodynamic properties of moist air. In drying, the key properties are: > - **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)**: Actual air temperature > - **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**: Temperature measured with a wet wick (adiabatic saturation temperature) > - **Relative Humidity (RH)**: Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor to saturation pressure > - **Humidity Ratio (Y)**: kg of water vapor per kg of dry air $$ Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} $$ ## 2.3 Drying Rate Curve — The "Heart" of Drying Theory The drying rate curve plots **drying rate vs. moisture content**. It typically shows three distinct periods: | Period | Name | Mechanism | Rate Controlling Step | |--------|------|-----------|----------------------| | **A–B** | Initial Adjustment (Warm-up) | Grain temperature rises to wet bulb temperature | Heat transfer dominates | | **B–C** | Constant Rate Period | Surface water evaporates freely (like free water) | External mass transfer (air boundary layer) | | **C–D** | First Falling Rate Period | Surface dries out; water moves from interior by capillary action | Internal diffusion begins | | **D–E** | Second Falling Rate Period | Only bound water remains; movement by molecular diffusion | Internal moisture diffusion (fully rate-limiting) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight for Cereals**: Most of the drying of grains (like rice, wheat, corn) occurs in the **falling rate periods (C–D and D–E)**, NOT the constant rate period. This is because grains have very small particles with mostly **bound/internal moisture** and very little free surface water. ## 2.4 Thin-Layer vs. Deep-Bed Drying | Feature | Thin-Layer Drying | Deep-Bed Drying | |---------|-------------------|------------------| | **Grain Depth** | ≤ 1 grain thick (≤ 2 cm) | 0.5 m to several meters | | **Air Velocity** | High (1–2 m/s) | Low to medium (0.1–0.5 m/s) | | **Drying Time** | Short (minutes to few hours) | Long (hours to days) | | **Modeling** | Page model, Newton model, Henderson-Pabis | Logarithmic, exponential drying fronts | | **Use** | Laboratory studies, kernel-level analysis | Industrial bin/silo dryers | ## 2.5 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) — The Fundamental Drying Limit > [!IMPORTANT] > **The grain will never dry below the EMC** corresponding to the air's temperature and relative humidity. EMC is the **thermodynamic lower limit** of drying. ### Common EMC Models: **Henderson Equation (1952):** $$ EMC = \left[ \frac{-\ln(1-RH)}{A \cdot (T+C)} \right]^{1/B} $$ **Chung-Pfost Equation:** $$ EMC = E - F \cdot \ln[-(T+C) \cdot \ln(RH)] $$ Where $A, B, C, E, F$ are empirical constants for each grain, $T$ is temperature (°C), and $RH$ is decimal relative humidity. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — these formulas are the **most tested** in KTU exams on Drying.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % w.b. | | 2 | $M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % d.b. | | 3 | $M_d = \frac{M_w}{100 - M_w} \times 100$ | Conversion: w.b. → d.b. | % d.b. | | 4 | $M_w = \frac{M_d}{100 + M_d} \times 100$ | Conversion: d.b. → w.b. | % w.b. | | 5 | $m_w = \frac{M_w}{100} \cdot (m_w + m_s)$ | Mass of water in sample | kg | | 6 | $Q = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Heat transferred to air | kJ | | 7 | $Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | Humidity ratio | kg water/kg dry air | | 8 | $RH = \frac{P_v}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100$ | Relative humidity | % | | 9 | $\dot{m}_{water} = m_a \cdot (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$ | Moisture removed per unit dry air | kg/s | | 10 | $Drying\ Rate = -\frac{dM}{dt}$ | Definition of drying rate | %/h or kg/kg·h | | 11 | $\frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | Newton's thin-layer model | dimensionless | | 12 | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$ | Page's thin-layer model | dimensionless | | 13 | $t_{total} = \frac{m_s \cdot (M_{d1} - M_{d2})}{\dot{m}_{evap}}$ | Total drying time | s or h | | 14 | $H_{air} = c_p \cdot T + Y \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$ | Enthalpy of moist air | kJ/kg dry air | | 15 | $E_{th} = \frac{m_{water} \cdot h_{fg}}{Q_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency of dryer | dimensionless | Where $h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization) ≈ **2501 kJ/kg at 0°C**, ≈ **2406 kJ/kg at 40°C**, ≈ **2257 kJ/kg at 100°C**. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Drying calculations and principles are applied in: - **Industrial rice and paddy dryers** (flatbed, LSU, column, fluidized bed) - **Maize/corn drying** in the Corn Belt (USA) and agricultural hubs in India (Bihar, Karnataka) - **Pulse milling industries** for pigeon pea, chickpea, mung bean - **Seed processing plants** for germination preservation - **Storage silo design** with aeration systems - **Energy optimization** of drying operations (cost reduction) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solved Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture Content **Starting Point Definitions:** $$ M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100 \quad \text{...(i)} $$ $$ M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100 \quad \text{...(ii)} $$ **Step 1: Express $m_w$ in terms of $M_d$** From (ii): $$ m_w = \frac{M_d \cdot m_s}{100} $$ **Step 2: Substitute into (i)** $$ M_w = \frac{\frac{M_d \cdot m_s}{100}}{\frac{M_d \cdot m_s}{100} + m_s} \times 100 $$ **Step 3: Factor out $m_s$ from the denominator** $$ M_w = \frac{\frac{M_d \cdot m_s}{100}}{m_s \left( \frac{M_d}{100} + 1 \right)} \times 100 $$ **Step 4: Cancel $m_s$ and simplify** $$ M_w = \frac{\frac{M_d}{100}}{\frac{M_d + 100}{100}} \times 100 = \frac{M_d}{M_d + 100} \times 100 $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{M_w = \frac{M_d}{100 + M_d} \times 100} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is d.b. preferred in drying calculations?** Because d.b. moisture content is **independent of the total mass** of the sample, which decreases as water evaporates. Using d.b. simplifies mass balance equations significantly. ## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of Water to be Removed in a Drying Operation **Problem Setup:** A batch of paddy has initial mass $m_1$ at moisture $M_{d1}$ (d.b.). It must be dried to $M_{d2}$ (d.b.). Find the mass of water to be removed and final mass $m_2$. **Step 1: Mass balance on dry solid** Since the dry solid mass is conserved: $$ m_{s1} = m_{s2} = m_s $$ $$ m_1 = m_s + m_{w1} = m_s \left( 1 + \frac{M_{d1}}{100} \right) $$ $$ m_s = \frac{m_1}{1 + \frac{M_{d1}}{100}} = \frac{100 \cdot m_1}{100 + M_{d1}} $$ **Step 2: Mass of water initially present** $$ m_{w1} = m_s \cdot \frac{M_{d1}}{100} = \frac{100 \cdot m_1}{100 + M_{d1}} \cdot \frac{M_{d1}}{100} = \frac{m_1 \cdot M_{d1}}{100 + M_{d1}} $$ **Step 3: Final mass after drying** $$ m_2 = m_s \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{M_{d2}}{100} \right) = \frac{100 \cdot m_1}{100 + M_{d1}} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{M_{d2}}{100} \right) $$ $$ m_2 = m_1 \cdot \frac{100 + M_{d2}}{100 + M_{d1}} $$ **Step 4: Mass of water removed** $$ \boxed{m_{water\ removed} = m_1 - m_2 = m_1 \left[ 1 - \frac{100 + M_{d2}}{100 + M_{d1}} \right] = \frac{m_1 \cdot (M_{d1} - M_{d2})}{100 + M_{d1}}} $$ ## 3.3 Numerical Example 1: Moisture Content Conversion (Model 3-Mark Question) > **Question (KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style):** > A sample of wheat has moisture content of **22% (w.b.)**. Calculate: (a) moisture content on dry basis, (b) the mass of water in 500 kg of wheat, and (c) the final mass if it is dried to **14% w.b.** **Solution:** **Part (a): Convert w.b. to d.b.** $$ M_d = \frac{M_w}{100 - M_w} \times 100 = \frac{22}{100 - 22} \times 100 $$ $$ M_d = \frac{22}{78} \times 100 = 28.21\% \text{ d.b.} $$ **Part (b): Mass of water in 500 kg of wheat** $$ m_w = \frac{M_w}{100} \cdot (m_w + m_s) = \frac{22}{100} \cdot 500 = 110 \text{ kg} $$ **Part (c): Final mass when dried to 14% w.b.** Method 1: Using d.b. (more robust) $$ m_2 = m_1 \cdot \frac{100 + M_{d2}}{100 + M_{d1}} $$ Where $M_{d2} = \frac{14}{86} \times 100 = 16.28\% $ d.b., and $M_{d1} = 28.21\%$ d.b. $$ m_2 = 500 \cdot \frac{100 + 16.28}{100 + 28.21} = 500 \cdot \frac{116.28}{128.21} $$ $$ m_2 = 500 \cdot 0.9069 = 453.47 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{m_2 \approx 453.5 \text{ kg}} $$ So **46.53 kg of water** must be removed. ## 3.4 Numerical Example 2: Thin-Layer Drying Calculation (Page Model) > **Question (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style):** > Paddy is dried in a thin-layer dryer. The initial moisture content is $M_0 = 25\%$ d.b., and EMC is $M_e = 6\%$ d.b. The Page model constants are $k = 0.025 /min$ and $n = 1.4$. Calculate the time required to reach moisture content of **15% d.b.** and the moisture ratio at that time. **Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Moisture Ratio (MR)** $$ MR = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{15 - 6}{25 - 6} = \frac{9}{19} = 0.4737 $$ **Step 2: Apply Page's Model** $$ MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n) $$ $$ 0.4737 = \exp(-0.025 \cdot t^{1.4}) $$ **Step 3: Take natural logarithm on both sides** $$ \ln(0.4737) = -0.025 \cdot t^{1.4} $$ $$ -0.7470 = -0.025 \cdot t^{1.4} $$ $$ t^{1.4} = \frac{0.7470}{0.025} = 29.88 $$ **Step 4: Solve for $t$** $$ t = (29.88)^{1/1.4} = (29.88)^{0.7143} $$ $$ \ln(t) = 0.7143 \cdot \ln(29.88) = 0.7143 \cdot 3.3967 = 2.4265 $$ $$ t = e^{2.4265} = 11.32 \text{ minutes} $$ $$ \boxed{t \approx 11.3 \text{ minutes}} $$ ## 3.5 Numerical Example 3: Heat and Mass Balance on a Continuous Dryer > **Question (KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style):** > A continuous-flow paddy dryer processes **2 tonnes/hour** of paddy from **24% w.b. to 14% w.b.** Hot air enters at **80°C** with humidity ratio $Y_1 = 0.012$ kg/kg and leaves at **45°C** with $Y_2 = 0.028$ kg/kg. Calculate: (a) the moisture removed per hour, (b) the dry air flow rate required, and (c) the heat required per hour. **Solution:** **Part (a): Moisture removed per hour** Mass of paddy fed = 2000 kg/h $m_{w1} = 0.24 \times 2000 = 480$ kg/h $m_{s} = 2000 - 480 = 1520$ kg/h $m_{w2} = \frac{14}{86} \times 1520 = 247.4$ kg/h $$ \text{Water removed} = 480 - 247.4 = 232.6 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Part (b): Dry air flow rate** Moisture picked up per kg of dry air: $$ Y_2 - Y_1 = 0.028 - 0.012 = 0.016 \text{ kg water/kg dry air} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{a, dry} = \frac{232.6}{0.016} = 14,537.5 \text{ kg dry air/h} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{a, dry} \approx 14,538 \text{ kg/h} \approx 14.54 \text{ tonnes/h}} $$ **Part (c): Heat required per hour** The heat is used to: 1. Evaporate water: $232.6 \times 2406 = 559,635.6$ kJ/h (using $h_{fg}$ at ~40°C) 2. Heat the grain (assume grain enters at 30°C, leaves at 40°C, $c_{grain} = 1.5$ kJ/kg·K): $2000 \times 1.5 \times 10 = 30,000$ kJ/h 3. Heat losses (assume 15% of useful heat): $\approx 88,645$ kJ/h Total: $Q \approx 678,280$ kJ/h ≈ 188 kW # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Drying Process (Mass & Heat Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Wet Grain Input
M0 high MC] --> B[Heater
Burner or Heat Exchanger] B --> C[Heated Air
T1 high, Y1 low] C --> D[Drying Chamber
Grain Bed] D --> E[Dried Grain Output
M2 low MC] D --> F[Moist Exhaust Air
T2 lower, Y2 higher] F --> G[Fan + Cyclone
Dust Removal] G --> H[Atmosphere Discharge] D --> I[Grain Cooling Section] I --> E E --> J[Storage Silo or Bagging] ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Stages of Drying in a Grain Kernel ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Wet Grain Kernel
MC 20-30 percent] --> B[Warming Up Period
Grain temp rises to WBT] B --> C[Constant Rate Period
Surface water evaporates] C --> D[First Falling Rate
Internal capillary flow] D --> E[Second Falling Rate
Bound water diffusion] E --> F[Equilibrium Reached
MC equals EMC] F --> G[Safe Storage State
MC 12-14 percent w.b.] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Thin-Layer Drying vs Deep-Bed Drying ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TL[Thin Layer Drying] TL1[Single grain layer] --> TL2[High air velocity 1 to 2 m/s] TL2 --> TL3[Uniform drying] TL3 --> TL4[Minutes to hours] end subgraph DL[Deep Bed Drying] DL1[Thick grain bed 0.5 to 5 m] --> DL2[Low air velocity 0.1 to 0.5 m/s] DL2 --> DL3[Drying front moves upward] DL3 --> DL4[Hours to days] end ``` ## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: Classification of Grain Dryers ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Grain Dryers] --> B[Sun Drying] A --> C[Mechanical Dryers] B --> B1[Yard drying] B --> B2[Shelf drying] C --> D[Batch Type] C --> E[Continuous Type] D --> D1[Flatbed Dryer] D --> D2[Bin Dryer] D --> D3[Sack Dryer] E --> E1[Column Dryer LSU] E --> E2[Fluidized Bed Dryer] E --> E3[Rotary Dryer] E --> E4[Belt Conveyor Dryer] ``` ## 4.5 System Diagram: Psychrometric Chart Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Air at DBT T1] --> B[Sensible Heating
T1 increases, Y constant] B --> C[Air at DBT Th
Lower RH] C --> D[Air Enters Dryer Bed] D --> E[Evaporative Cooling
T decreases, Y increases] E --> F[Exhaust Air
Higher RH, lower T] F --> G[Released to Atmosphere] D --> H[Grain loses moisture
MC drops over time] H --> I[Output: Dried Grain] ``` ## 4.6 Block Diagram: Quality Changes During Drying ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Drying Conditions] --> B[Temperature Control] A --> C[Time Control] A --> D[Moisture Removal Rate] B --> E[Over-drying: Cracks, breakage] B --> F[Under-drying: Spoilage risk] C --> G[Heat damage at long exposure] D --> H[Stress cracks in rice] E --> I[Quality Loss
Milling yield down] F --> J[Storage Loss
Mold and insects] G --> K[Nutritional Loss
Protein denaturation] H --> L[Head rice reduction] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define moisture content. Distinguish between wet basis and dry basis moisture content. A grain sample weighs 250 g before drying and 200 g after complete oven drying. Calculate both the wet basis and dry basis moisture content.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Moisture content is the amount of water present in a material, expressed as a percentage of either the wet mass (wet basis) or the dry mass (dry basis). | Basis | Definition | Formula | |-------|------------|---------| | **Wet Basis (w.b.)** | Water mass per unit total mass | $M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | | **Dry Basis (d.b.)** | Water mass per unit dry solid mass | $M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | **Calculation:** - Initial mass: 250 g; Final dry mass: 200 g - Mass of water removed: $m_w = 250 - 200 = 50$ g - Dry solid mass: $m_s = 200$ g $$ M_w = \frac{50}{250} \times 100 = 20\% \text{ w.b.} $$ $$ M_d = \frac{50}{200} \times 100 = 25\% \text{ d.b.} $$ **Answer:** $M_w = 20\%$ w.b. and $M_d = 25\%$ d.b. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: What is Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)? Explain its significance in grain drying with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** EMC is the moisture content at which the grain is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air, i.e., it neither gains nor loses moisture. The grain's vapor pressure equals the partial pressure of water vapor in air. **Significance:** 1. **Theoretical drying limit:** Grain can never be dried below the EMC of the air used. 2. **Drying strategy:** Selecting drier air (higher temperature, lower RH) gives lower EMC, enabling deeper drying. 3. **Storage stability:** Grains stored at MC > EMC will continue to release moisture; grains at MC < EMC will absorb moisture from humid air. 4. **Example:** Paddy with EMC ≈ 14% d.b. in air at 30°C and 80% RH cannot be dried below this value using that air. To achieve the desired 12% MC for storage, either temperature must be raised or RH must be reduced. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse EMC with **Critical Moisture Content (CMC)**. EMC is a property of the air-grain system at equilibrium; CMC is the moisture content at which the drying rate transitions from constant-rate to falling-rate period. **Do not interchange them in answers.** --- ## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern] **Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **mechanical column-type (LSU) paddy dryer**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A batch of 1000 kg of maize is to be dried from **25% w.b. to 13% w.b.** Calculate: (i) the initial and final moisture content on dry basis, (ii) the total mass of water to be removed, and (iii) the volume of water removed in litres. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) LSU Column Dryer:** The **Louisiana State University (LSU) column dryer** is a continuous-flow, mixed-flow grain dryer widely used for paddy and maize. **Construction:** - Tall vertical rectangular chamber (column) with alternating rows of **inlet air ducts** and **exhaust air ducts** - **V-shaped baffles** between rows to create zig-zag grain flow - **Top section**: Wet grain inlet with holding bin - **Bottom section**: Cooling section + dried grain discharge auger - **External**: Centrifugal fan, air heater (burner or steam coil), control panel **Working Principle:** 1. Wet paddy enters from the top. 2. Hot air is forced horizontally through the grain column (perpendicular to grain flow) from inlet ducts. 3. Grain zig-zags downward under gravity between the V-baffles. 4. Hot air picks up moisture and exits through exhaust ducts. 5. The cooled dried grain is discharged at the bottom by an auger. **Advantages:** - Continuous operation, high capacity - Uniform drying due to mixing at baffles - Compact footprint for large capacity - Suitable for paddy, maize, wheat **Limitations:** - High initial capital cost - Risk of over-drying in some zones - Requires skilled operation - Energy-intensive **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m_1 = 1000$ kg, $M_{w1} = 25\%$ w.b., $M_{w2} = 13\%$ w.b. **(i) Moisture content on dry basis:** Initial: $$ M_{d1} = \frac{25}{100-25} \times 100 = \frac{25}{75} \times 100 = 33.33\% \text{ d.b.} $$ Final: $$ M_{d2} = \frac{13}{100-13} \times 100 = \frac{13}{87} \times 100 = 14.94\% \text{ d.b.} $$ **(ii) Mass of water removed:** Mass of dry solid: $$ m_s = m_1 \cdot \frac{100 - M_{w1}}{100} = 1000 \cdot \frac{75}{100} = 750 \text{ kg} $$ Initial water mass: $m_{w1} = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250$ kg Final water mass: $m_{w2} = 0.13 \cdot m_2$ Final grain mass: $$ m_2 = m_s + m_{w2} = 750 + m_{w2} $$ Also: $m_{w2} = m_s \cdot \frac{M_{d2}}{100} = 750 \cdot 0.1494 = 112.07$ kg $$ m_2 = 750 + 112.07 = 862.07 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \text{Water removed} = 1000 - 862.07 = 137.93 \text{ kg} \approx 137.93 \text{ kg} $$ **(iii) Volume of water (density = 1 kg/L):** $$ V = 137.93 \text{ L} $$ **Final Answers:** $M_{d1} = 33.33\%$ d.b., $M_{d2} = 14.94\%$ d.b., water removed = **137.93 kg = 137.93 L** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Internal Choice] **Q: (a)** Explain the **thin-layer drying theory** with the **Page model** and **Newton model**. State their mathematical forms and explain the significance of drying constants $k$ and $n$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A batch dryer handles **500 kg of wheat** per batch. The initial MC is **20% (d.b.)** and final MC is **12% (d.b.)** Hot air is supplied at **60°C, RH = 20%**. The exhaust air leaves at **35°C** with RH = 75%. Calculate: (i) the humidity ratio of inlet and outlet air, (ii) the dry air required per hour if drying takes 4 hours, and (iii) the thermal efficiency (assume no heat losses and grain is heated from 28°C to 40°C). Use saturation pressures: $P_{sat}(60°C) = 19.9$ kPa, $P_{sat}(35°C) = 5.63$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Thin-Layer Drying Theory:** In **thin-layer drying**, the grain is exposed in a layer one or a few grains thick to a continuous stream of uniform hot air. Mathematical models predict the moisture content vs. time relationship. **Newton's Model (Lewis, 1921):** $$ \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k \cdot t) $$ - Simple exponential decay - Assumes $n = 1$ - $k$ = drying constant (1/time), depends on T, RH, air velocity **Page's Model (1949):** $$ \frac{M - M_0}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k \cdot t^n) $$ $$ MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n) $$ - Empirical modification of Newton model - $n$ is a shape factor; for grains, $n$ typically ranges from **0.5 to 1.5** - Better fit to experimental data for cereals and legumes **Significance of $k$ and $n$:** - $k$ reflects the **rate of moisture removal** — higher $k$ means faster drying - $n$ reflects the **drying mechanism** — values of $n > 1$ indicate an induction period; $n < 1$ indicate rapid initial drying - For paddy at 40–60°C, typical values: $k = 0.01$–$0.05$ /min, $n = 1.0$–$1.5$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m_{batch} = 500$ kg wheat, $M_{d1} = 20\%$ d.b., $M_{d2} = 12\%$ d.b., $t = 4$ h **(i) Humidity ratio of inlet and outlet air:** **Inlet:** $T_1 = 60°C$, $RH_1 = 20\%$ $$ P_{v1} = 0.20 \times 19.9 = 3.98 \text{ kPa} $$ $$ Y_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.98}{101.325 - 3.98} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.98}{97.345} = 0.0254 \text{ kg/kg} $$ **Outlet:** $T_2 = 35°C$, $RH_2 = 75\%$ $$ P_{v2} = 0.75 \times 5.63 = 4.2225 \text{ kPa} $$ $$ Y_2 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{4.2225}{101.325 - 4.2225} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{4.2225}{97.1025} = 0.0270 \text{ kg/kg} $$ **Answers:** $Y_1 = 0.0254$ kg/kg, $Y_2 = 0.0270$ kg/kg **(ii) Dry air required per hour:** Mass of dry solid: $m_s = \frac{500}{1 + 0.20} = 416.67$ kg Water to remove: $m_w = 416.67 \times (0.20 - 0.12) = 33.33$ kg per batch Rate of water removal: $\dot{m}_w = 33.33/4 = 8.33$ kg/h Moisture pick-up by air per kg dry air: $Y_2 - Y_1 = 0.0270 - 0.0254 = 0.0016$ kg/kg $$ \dot{m}_{a,dry} = \frac{8.33}{0.0016} = 5,208 \text{ kg dry air/h} $$ **Answer:** $\dot{m}_{a,dry} \approx 5,208$ kg/h **(iii) Thermal efficiency:** Heat input by air (per kg dry air, cooling from 60°C to 35°C): $$ \Delta Q_{air} = c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1.005 \cdot (60 - 35) = 25.125 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Total heat input: $Q_{in} = 5,208 \times 25.125 = 130,851$ kJ/h Useful heat (evaporation at 35°C, $h_{fg} \approx 2406$ kJ/kg, plus heating grain): - Evaporation: $8.33 \times 2406 = 20,042$ kJ/h - Heating grain: $500/4 \times 1.5 \times (40-28) = 125 \times 1.5 \times 12 = 2,250$ kJ/h - Total useful: $22,292$ kJ/h $$ \eta_{th} = \frac{22,292}{130,851} \times 100 = 17.04\% $$ **Answer:** $\eta_{th} \approx 17\%$ — typical for low-temperature batch dryers. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert w.b. ↔ d.b.** — most common mistake. Always check which basis the question uses. > 2. **Using $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg (at 100°C)** instead of the temperature-appropriate value. For low-temperature drying, use $h_{fg}$ at the air's average temperature. > 3. **Confusing $m_{w1}$ and $m_s$** in final mass calculations — remember $m_2 = m_s(1 + M_{d2}/100)$. > 4. **Writing $\ln$ for $\log_{10}$** — use $\ln$ in all Page and Newton model calculations. > 5. **Ignoring EMC** in thin-layer problems — if the question gives EMC, you MUST include it in the moisture ratio. > 6. **Not stating units** in final answers (e.g., "kg", "% d.b.", "kJ/h") — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Drying Module 1** ### 🔹 Core Concepts - [ ] **Drying** = simultaneous heat + mass transfer; reduces MC to safe storage level. - [ ] **Safe MC for cereals**: 12–14% w.b.; **for legumes**: 10–12% w.b. - [ ] **Max drying temp for seed grains**: ≤ 43°C (rice), ≤ 45°C (wheat) to preserve germination. ### 🔹 Key Formulas (must memorize) - [ ] $M_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w+m_s} \times 100$ ; $M_d = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ - [ ] Conversion: $M_d = \frac{M_w}{100-M_w} \times 100$ - [ ] Newton's model: $MR = e^{-kt}$ - [ ] Page's model: $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ - [ ] Mass of water removed: $m_w = m_1 \cdot \frac{M_{d1} - M_{d2}}{100 + M_{d1}}$ (when d.b. used) - [ ] Humidity ratio: $Y = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ ### 🔹 Psychrometrics - [ ] DBT ↑ with heating (sensible); T ↓ & Y ↑ through drying bed (evaporative cooling). - [ ] RH of hot dry air is **lower** even if Y is constant. - [ ] EMC is the **drying limit**; lower for hot, dry air. ### 🔹 Drying Rate Curve - [ ] **4 periods**: A–B (warm-up), B–C (constant rate), C–D (1st falling), D–E (2nd falling). - [ ] For grains, drying is **predominantly in the falling rate periods** (internal diffusion-controlled). ### 🔹 Dryer Types - [ ] **Sun drying**: cheap but slow, weather-dependent, contamination risk. - [ ] **LSU column dryer**: continuous, mixed-flow, common for paddy. - [ ] **Flatbed batch dryer**: small-scale, used in seed processing. - [ ] **Fluidized bed**: fast drying for parboiled rice, high thermal efficiency. ### 🔹 Quality Effects - [ ] High drying temperature → **stress cracks** in rice → head rice yield ↓. - [ ] Over-drying → weight loss, brittleness, breakage during milling. - [ ] Under-drying → mold, aflatoxin (in maize, peanuts), sprouting. ### 🔹 Common Exam Traps - [ ] Always **show mass balance**: $m_1 = m_2 + m_{w,removed}$. - [ ] **State assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "neglecting heat losses", "EMC = 0"). - [ ] **Convert all MCs to the same basis** before any calculation. - [ ] In Page's model, **$\ln$ means natural log** (base $e$). > [!TIP] > **🎯 KTU Topper's Tip:** Practice at least **5 numerical problems** combining moisture conversion + drying time + heat balance. These are the **most repeated question types** in Module 1 of OEFTT836 across the last 5 KTU university exam papers. --- > **END OF NOTES — Module 1, Topic: Drying | OEFTT836 Cereals and Legume Technology | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Modern Methods of Parboiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modern Methods of Parboiling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parboiling** is a controlled hydrothermal (combined heat + moisture) pre-milling treatment applied to paddy (rough rice, *Oryza sativa* L.), consisting of three sequential unit operations: (1) **soaking** the paddy in water (cold, warm, or under pressure), (2) **gelatinization** of the endosperm starch through steaming or boiling, and (3) **drying** to a safe moisture content (~**12–14 % w.b.**) for milling. The process is defined by the FAO Codex Alimentarius as "*the hydrothermic treatment of paddy to produce a parboiled grain in which the starch is fully gelatinized*."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The modern methods section specifically emphasizes the technological improvements over the traditional "soak-boil-dry" pit method (which takes **48–72 hours**). Modern methods target **gelatinization completeness**, **color uniformity**, **reduced cracking**, and **milling yield enhancement** (typically **+5–8 %** head rice recovery over raw rice).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a packet of dry popcorn kernels versus a packet that has been pre-steamed:

- A **raw rice kernel** is like a glass marble — hard, translucent, brittle. When the mill removes the husk and bran, it cracks easily, and valuable nutrients (Vitamin B1/thiamine, niacin) inside get lost with the bran.
- A **parboiled rice kernel** is like a piece of hardened clay that was once soft and was then re-hardened. The starch inside has "melted and re-solidified" (gelatinization), sealing the nutrients inside the grain. It now resists breakage during milling.

> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of parboiling as **"pressure-cooking the rice inside its own husk"**. Just as pressure-cooking lentils softens them inside out without breaking the outer layer, parboiling cooks the starch *inside* the husk so the rice becomes stronger, more golden, and more nutritious.

### 1.3 Fundamental Principles

The science rests on three physical/chemical phenomena:

| Phenomenon | Temperature Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Absorption** | 25 – 80 °C | Moisture migrates into the kernel via capillary action and diffusion |
| **Starch Gelatinization** | **68 – 78 °C** | Starch granules swell irreversibly; crystalline structure collapses |
| **Protein Denaturation** | 60 – 90 °C | Matrix protein sets, locking nutrients into the endosperm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants (KTU Board Examinable):**
> - **Gelatinization Temperature of Rice Starch = 68 – 78 °C**
> - **Moisture after soaking = ~30 % (w.b.)**
> - **Safe storage moisture = 12 – 14 % (w.b.)**
> - **Recommended steaming pressure = 0.5 – 5.0 kg/cm² (for pressure methods)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Modern Parboiling

The shift from traditional to modern parboiling is driven by three engineering objectives:
1. **Reduction of soaking time** (from days to hours).
2. **Uniform gelatinization** without over-cooking the outer layers.
3. **Energy efficiency** through heat recovery and pressure utilization.

The general mass and heat balance framework for any parboiling method follows:

$$ M_1 \, X_1 + M_w \, X_w = M_2 \, X_2 $$

Where:
- $M_1$ = initial mass of paddy (kg)
- $X_1$ = initial moisture content of paddy (decimal, w.b.)
- $M_w$ = mass of water absorbed (kg)
- $X_w$ = moisture of absorbed water (≈ 1.0)
- $M_2$ = final mass after soaking (kg)
- $X_2$ = target moisture (~0.30, i.e., 30 %)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Three Stages

**Stage 1 — Soaking (Hydration):**
- Cold soaking: ambient, 24–72 h (traditional)
- Warm soaking: 50–70 °C, 3–6 h (modern)
- Pressure soaking: 1–3 kg/cm², 60–80 °C, 2–4 h (advanced)

**Stage 2 — Steaming/Gelatinization:**
- The wet paddy is exposed to saturated steam. Heat transfer occurs via condensation on the cool grain surface, releasing latent heat ($h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg at 100 °C).
- Completion is judged by the **"glass-cutting test"** — fully gelatinized grains show no opaque white core when cut transversely.

**Stage 3 — Drying:**
- Moisture is reduced from ~30 % to **12–14 % w.b.** to arrest microbial and enzymatic activity.
- Two-stage drying is preferred: (a) tempering at high moisture, (b) final drying to safe moisture.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Equation / Value | Unit | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial moisture (raw paddy) | $X_1$ | $0.11$ to $0.14$ | decimal w.b. | At harvest |
| Soaking moisture | $X_2$ | $\approx 0.30$ | decimal w.b. | Before steaming |
| Final moisture (safe storage) | $X_3$ | $0.12$ to $0.14$ | decimal w.b. | Before milling |
| Water absorption (mass) | $M_w$ | $M_2 - M_1$ | kg | Mass balance |
| Latent heat of steam | $h_{fg}$ | $\mathbf{2257}$ | kJ/kg | At 100 °C |
| Sensible heat of water | $c_p$ | $\mathbf{4.186}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | 20–80 °C |
| Gelatinization onset | $T_g$ | $\mathbf{68}$ | °C | Rice starch |
| Gelatinization completion | $T_{gc}$ | $\mathbf{78}$ | °C | Rice starch |
| Head rice yield (parboiled) | $HRY_p$ | $65$ to $72$ | % | Modern method |
| Head rice yield (raw) | $HRY_r$ | $55$ to $60$ | % | Untreated |
| Degree of gelatinization | $DG$ | $\geq 90$ | % | Properly parboiled |
| Color (Hunter L value) | $L$ | $45$ to $55$ | – | Light parboil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The gelatinization temperature is the *thermodynamic bottleneck* of the entire process. Any modern parboiling method is essentially a *strategy to deliver heat across the 68–78 °C window as quickly and uniformly as possible* without scorching the bran layer (which turns dark due to Maillard browning above **110 °C**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Soaking Time (Fick's Second Law of Diffusion)

The penetration of water into the rice kernel is modeled as one-dimensional diffusion through a spherical/stubby cylinder. The unsteady-state moisture diffusion is governed by Fick's second law:

$$ \frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left( \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial X}{\partial r} \right) $$

For long soaking times (where the kernel approaches equilibrium moisture $X_\infty$), Crank's solution simplifies to:

$$ \frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-n^2 \pi^2 \frac{D_{eff} \, t}{r^2}\right) $$

For sufficiently large $t$, only the first term ($n=1$) is significant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\left(\frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty}\right) &= \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \pi^2 \frac{D_{eff}}{r^2} t \\
&= -0.498 - \pi^2 \frac{D_{eff}}{r^2} t
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the soaking time $t$ required to reach moisture $X_t$:

$$
t = \frac{r^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty}\right) \right]
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**
- $r$ = effective radius of kernel = **1.7 × 10⁻³ m** (1.7 mm)
- $D_{eff}$ at 65 °C ≈ **1.2 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s**
- $X_0$ = 0.12, $X_\infty$ = 0.30, $X_t$ = 0.28 (target near saturation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{(1.7 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-11}} \left[\ln(0.6079) - \ln\left(\frac{0.28 - 0.30}{0.12 - 0.30}\right)\right] \\
&= \frac{2.89 \times 10^{-6}}{1.184 \times 10^{-10}} \left[-0.498 - \ln(0.111)\right] \\
&= 24408 \times [-0.498 - (-2.197)] \\
&= 24408 \times 1.699 \\
&\approx 41470 \, \text{s} \approx 11.5 \, \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms why **warm soaking (65 °C)** reduces the traditional 48–72 h soaking to a few hours — diffusion coefficient $D_{eff}$ roughly doubles for every 10 °C rise in temperature (Arrhenius behavior).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Modern Parboiling Methods

| Method | Soaking Temp | Soaking Time | Steaming Condition | Total Cycle | Product Color | Investment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Traditional (Pit)** | Ambient (25–30 °C) | 48–72 h | Open boiling, 1–2 h | ~3 days | Dark yellow-brown | Very Low |
| **CFTRI Method** | 65–70 °C | 3–4 h | Steam at 3–5 psi, 10–20 min | ~5–6 h | Golden yellow | Medium |
| **Pressure Parboil** | 70–80 °C (under press.) | 2–3 h | Steam at 1–3 kg/cm², 15 min | ~4 h | Light golden | High |
| **Single-Stage Steam** | Pre-steam soak | 1–2 h | Single steaming pass | ~3 h | Pale yellow | Medium |
| **Double-Stage Steam (DRR)** | 70–75 °C | 2 h | Steam → temper → steam | ~4 h | Uniform golden | Medium–High |
| **Jadhav Method** | 65 °C | 4–5 h | Boiling water + steam | ~6 h | Golden yellow | Medium |
| **Dry Heat / Sand-roasting** | None (hot sand) | – | 150–200 °C contact | 30–60 min | Dark brown | Low |

### 3.3 Process Logic of the CFTRI Method (Step-by-Step)

This is the most exam-relevant modern method. The Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore, India, developed this process — it is **directly referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus**.

**Step 1 — Cleaning & Grading:** Paddy is cleaned of stones, chaff, and immature grains. Pre-cleaning is essential because foreign matter insulates grains and prevents uniform hydration.

**Step 2 — Soaking:** Paddy is immersed in water pre-heated to **65–70 °C** in a stainless-steel tank. Soaking continues until moisture reaches **~30 % w.b.**, typically **3–4 hours**.

**Step 3 — Drainage:** Excess water is drained. The wet paddy is held for **15–30 minutes** to allow surface water to drip off — this prevents waterlogging of the steaming chamber.

**Step 4 — Steaming (Gelatinization):** Wet paddy is conveyed to a steam-jacketed vessel. Saturated steam at **3–5 psi (≈ 110–115 °C)** is applied for **10–20 minutes**. Completion is verified by the **glass-cut test**.

**Step 5 — First Drying (Tempering):** Steamed paddy is dried in a mechanical dryer (e.g., LSU dryer) from ~30 % down to **~18–20 % w.b.** over 1–2 hours.

**Step 6 — Tempering:** Paddy is held in bins for **4–6 hours** to allow internal moisture redistribution — this prevents fissuring (stress cracks).

**Step 7 — Second Drying:** Final drying reduces moisture to **12–14 % w.b.**, after which the parboiled paddy is ready for milling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Yield Improvement Math:**
> Traditional: HRY ≈ 58 % → Modern CFTRI: HRY ≈ 70 %
> Gain = 70 − 58 = **+12 percentage points**, which directly translates to commercial value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CFTRI Modern Parboiling Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Paddy Input, 12-14 percent MC] --> B[Pre-Cleaning Unit]
    B --> C[Grading and Destoning]
    C --> D[Hot Water Soaking Tank, 65-70 deg C]
    D --> E{Drainage and Surface Drip, 15-30 min}
    E --> F[Steam Jacketed Vessel, 3-5 psi]
    F --> G{Gelatinization Check, Glass Cut Test}
    G -->|Pass| H[First Stage Drying, to 18-20 percent MC]
    G -->|Fail| F
    H --> I[Tempering Bin, 4-6 hours]
    I --> J[Second Stage Drying, to 12-14 percent MC]
    J --> K[Cooling and Conditioning]
    K --> L[Parboiled Paddy Output, Ready for Milling]
    L --> M[Milling Machine]
    M --> N[Parboiled Rice, HRY 65-72 percent]
```

```mermaid
    CCP4 --> CCP5[CCP5: DG greater than 90 percent via glass cut test]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Diagrams:**
> - Rectangular boxes represent process steps or unit operations.

> - Arrows indicate mass flow direction.
