<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define parboiling. List any **three advantages** of modern parboiling methods over the traditional pit method.

**Model Answer:**
Parboiling is the hydrothermal treatment of paddy (rough rice) involving soaking, gelatinization by steaming, and drying prior to milling. Modern methods overcome the limitations of the traditional pit method.
Three advantages:
1. **Time reduction**: Cycle time reduced from 72 h to 4–6 h.
2. **Improved head rice yield**: From ~58 % to 65–72 % (an increase of ~7–14 %).
3. **Better hygiene and uniformity**: Closed-system steaming eliminates contamination and produces uniform color and degree of gelatinization.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
State the **gelatinization temperature range** of rice starch. Why is this range critical for designing any parboiling method?

**Model Answer:**
The gelatinization temperature of rice starch is **68–78 °C**. This range is critical because:
- Below 68 °C, starch granules remain crystalline and gelatinization is incomplete, leading to **under-parboiled** grains with low nutritional retention.
- Above 78 °C, starch fully gelatinizes; further heating causes **Maillard browning** and discoloration of the bran.
- Therefore, every modern parboiling method is engineered to bring the kernel interior to **at least 78 °C** uniformly without exceeding ~110 °C at the surface.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **CFTRI method** of parboiling in detail with a process flow diagram. Mention the operating conditions at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **traditional pit method** with the **modern CFTRI method** based on time, yield, color, hygiene, and energy consumption. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — CFTRI Method Description (7 marks)**

The Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore, developed this modern parboiling process to address the time, hygiene, and yield issues of traditional methods. The unit operations are:

| Stage | Operation | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-cleaning | Removal of stones, chaff, immature grains | Aspirator + destoner |
| 2. Hot soaking | Stainless steel tank | 65–70 °C, 3–4 h |
| 3. Draining | Surface drip | 15–30 min |
| 4. Steaming | Steam-jacketed vessel | 3–5 psi (≈110–115 °C), 10–20 min |
| 5. First drying | LSU/mechanical dryer | 30 % → 18–20 % MC |
| 6. Tempering | Holding bin | 4–6 h |
| 7. Second drying | Continuous dryer | 18–20 % → 12–14 % MC |

**Process Flow:** Raw paddy → Cleaning → Hot soaking (65–70 °C) → Draining → Steaming (3–5 psi, 15 min) → First drying → Tempering → Second drying → Parboiled paddy (12–14 % MC) → Milling.

The **glass-cut test** is performed after steaming: a fully gelatinized grain shows a translucent, glassy cross-section with **no opaque white core**.

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Traditional Pit Method | Modern CFTRI Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Total cycle time** | 3–4 days | 5–6 hours |
| **Soaking temp** | Ambient (25–30 °C) | 65–70 °C |
| **Soaking duration** | 48–72 h | 3–4 h |
| **Drying** | Open sun drying (2–3 days) | Mechanical (2–4 h) |
| **Head rice yield** | 55–60 % | 65–72 % |
| **Color uniformity** | Uneven, dark patches | Uniform golden yellow |
| **Hygiene** | Open, dust/contamination risk | Closed system, hygienic |
| **Energy efficiency** | Low (solar drying) | Higher but controllable |
| **Labor** | High (manual turning) | Low (mechanized) |

**Inference:** Modern CFTRI method provides superior quality, higher yield, hygiene, and reproducibility, justifying the capital investment for commercial rice mills.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles of pressure parboiling** and **double-stage steam parboiling**. State their operating conditions and advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Fick's diffusion equation**, derive the relationship between soaking time and effective moisture diffusivity for rice parboiling. Show how temperature affects the diffusion coefficient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Pressure & Double-Stage Methods (7 marks)**

**Pressure Parboiling Method:**
- Paddy is soaked in water under **elevated pressure (1–3 kg/cm²)** at **70–80 °C**.
- The higher pressure forces water into the kernel rapidly via enhanced capillary action, reducing soaking time to **2–3 hours**.
- Steaming is also done under pressure (3–5 kg/cm²) at **120–130 °C** for **15–20 min**, achieving **complete gelatinization in a single pass**.
- **Advantages:** Fastest cycle, uniform gelatinization, ideal for high-capacity industrial plants.

**Double-Stage Steam Parboiling (DRR Method, developed by Directorate of Rice Research, Hyderabad):**
- Paddy is **partially steamed** first (5–10 min) to seal the surface pores, then soaked in **hot water at 70–75 °C for 2 h**.
- A **second steaming** pass (10–15 min) completes gelatinization.
- The intermediate tempering minimizes grain cracking and produces a **lighter, more uniform color**.
- **Advantages:** Reduced Maillard browning, better head rice recovery, and lower fuel consumption than single-stage high-pressure methods.

**Comparison Summary:**
| Feature | Pressure Parboil | Double-Stage Steam |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking time | 2–3 h | 2 h |
| Steaming temp | 120–130 °C | 100–110 °C |
| Capital cost | High | Medium |
| Color | Slightly darker | Light, uniform |
| Energy use | High (steam pressure) | Moderate |

**Part (b) — Diffusion Derivation (7 marks)**

The water absorption by a rice kernel is modeled as transient diffusion through a sphere of effective radius $r$. Fick's second law applies:

$$ \frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left( \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial X}{\partial r} \right) $$

**Boundary and initial conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(r, 0) &= X_0 \quad \text{(initial moisture)} \\
X(R, t) &= X_\infty \quad \text{(surface moisture = soak water MC)} \\
\frac{\partial X}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=0} &= 0 \quad \text{(symmetry at center)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Crank's series solution (for sphere):**

$$
\frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-n^2 \pi^2 \frac{D_{eff} t}{R^2}\right)
$$

**For large $t$ (only first term matters):**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{6}{\pi^2}\right) - \pi^2 \frac{D_{eff}}{R^2} t
$$

**Solving for soaking time $t$:**

$$
t = \frac{R^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} \left[ -0.498 - \ln\left(\frac{X_t - X_\infty}{X_0 - X_\infty}\right) \right]
$$

**Temperature dependence of $D_{eff}$ (Arrhenius equation):**

$$
D_{eff} = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

where $E_a$ ≈ **25–35 kJ/mol** for rice, $D_0$ ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s. From this it follows that:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**Example:** Going from 30 °C (303 K) to 70 °C (343 K):

$$
\ln\left(\frac{D_{70}}{D_{30}}\right) = \frac{30000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{343}\right) = 3608 \times (0.00330 - 0.00292) = 1.37
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{D_{70}}{D_{30}} = e^{1.37} \approx 3.94
$$

So raising soaking temperature from 30 °C to 70 °C **quadruples the diffusion coefficient**, which is the theoretical justification for modern hot-soaking methods reducing soaking time from 48–72 h to 3–4 h.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse gelatinization (68–78 °C) with Maillard browning (>110 °C).** Examiners deduct 1 mark every time a student uses these interchangeably.
> - In diffusion derivations, students often forget the **negative sign in the exponent** or **drop the boundary condition $\partial X/\partial r = 0$ at the center**. Always state all three boundary/initial conditions explicitly.
> - The **CFTRI method is NOT the same as the Jadhav method.** Jadhav uses open boiling; CFTRI uses closed pressurized steaming. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> - When stating HRY (Head Rice Yield), always express it as a **percentage**, not a decimal. Write **70 %**, not 0.70.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parboiling** = Soaking + Gelatinization + Drying of paddy before milling.
- **Gelatinization temperature** of rice starch = **68–78 °C** (memorize this range).
- **Target soaking moisture** ≈ **30 % w.b.**; **final safe moisture** = **12–14 % w.b.**
- **Modern methods** reduce total cycle from ~3 days (traditional) to **4–6 hours**.
- **CFTRI method**: hot soak at 65–70 °C → steam at 3–5 psi → two-stage drying.
- **Pressure parboiling**: 1–3 kg/cm² soaking, 120–130 °C steaming — fastest method.
- **Double-stage (DRR) steam**: partial steam → hot soak → final steam → uniform light color.
- **Jadhav method**: hot soak + boiling water treatment (medium tech).
- **Dry heat method**: hot sand/contact roasting (produces dark parboiled rice, instant-cooking).
- **HRY improvement**: Modern methods raise head rice yield from **~58 % to 65–72 %**.
- **Quality check**: **Glass-cut test** verifies complete gelatinization (no white opaque core).
- **Maillard browning** must be avoided by keeping surface temperature **below 110 °C**.
- **Fick's second law** governs soaking; $D_{eff}$ roughly **quadruples** per 40 °C rise (Arrhenius).
- **Energy principle**: Modern methods target the **68–78 °C window** as efficiently as possible.
- **Examinable equations**: Fickian diffusion, Arrhenius $D_{eff}$, mass balance $M_1X_1 + M_wX_w = M_2X_2$.
- **Key process checkpoints (CCPs)**: Soak temp, soak MC, steam pressure/time, degree of gelatinization (DG ≥ 90 %), final MC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Rice Milling

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rice Milling** is defined as the **post-harvest mechanical processing of paddy (rough rice)** into edible white rice, involving a sequential series of unit operations such as **cleaning, de-stoning, husking (de-hulling), paddy separation, whitening/polishing, grading, and packaging**, while preserving maximum head rice yield (HRY) and nutritional integrity.

The kernel of paddy is a caryopsis consisting of four major structural components:
1. **Husk (Hull)** — outer fibrous chaff, ~20% of paddy weight.
2. **Bran layer (Pericarp + Aleurone + Embryo/Germ)** — oily outer coat of brown rice, ~8–10%.
3. **Endosperm (Starchy kernel)** — edible portion after milling, ~70%.
4. **Embryo (Germ)** — reproductive part, lipid-rich.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Milling Recovery (MR)** is the ratio of total milled rice obtained to the paddy input, expressed as a percentage. The **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** is the mass percentage of whole kernels (≥ ¾ grain length) retained after milling — the **most critical quality indicator** assessed by KTU examiners.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Layered Chocolate Truffle"

Think of a paddy grain like a **layered chocolate truffle** wrapped in a stiff brown paper:

- The **outer brown paper** = **Husk** (inedible, must be peeled off → *Husking*).
- The **thin chocolate coating** = **Bran + Germ** (nutritive but causes rancidity in storage → *Polishing/Whitening*).
- The **inner nougat** = **Endosperm** (the edible white rice we eat).

Just as you cannot eat a truffle without unwrapping it, you cannot eat rice without first **removing the husk, then scraping the bran**. The trick is to remove ONLY the unwanted layers without breaking the soft nougat inside. In rice milling, **breaking a grain means downgrading it from "head rice" to "broken rice"** — and broken rice fetches only **50–60%** the price of head rice in the open market.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Benchmarks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Key Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Paddy → Rice Conversion Ratio:** 100 kg paddy ≈ **65–70 kg** milled rice (with **5–6 kg** broken).
> - **Husk Recovery:** ~**20%** of paddy weight (used as fuel/biomass).
> - **Bran Recovery:** ~**8–10%** (rice bran oil source).
> - **Head Rice Yield (HRY) Standard:** ≥ **70%** for premium grade.
> - **Degree of Milling (DOM):** Typically **6–9%** for white rice; **0%** for brown rice.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequential Unit Operations in Modern Rice Milling

Rice milling is NOT a single operation — it is a **chain of 7–8 unit operations**, each with its own machinery and quality impact. Understanding the **order and dependency** between them is critical for KTU exams.

### Stage 1 — Pre-Cleaning (Raw Paddy Cleaner)
- **Purpose:** Remove **dust, straw, immature grains, stones, foreign seeds, and metal particles**.
- **Equipment:** Vibratory sieves, aspirators (air-classifier), magnetic separators.
- **Why it matters:** Foreign matter accelerates wear of rubber rolls in the husker.

### Stage 2 — De-Stoning (Specific Gravity Separator)
- **Purpose:** Remove **stones, mud clods, and heavy impurities** using density difference.
- **Principle:** Paddy density ≈ **1.18 g/cm³** vs. stone density ≈ **2.5 g/cm³**.
- **Equipment:** De-stoner (Stratifier with vibration + airflow).

### Stage 3 — Husking / De-hulling (The Core Operation)
Three major technologies exist:

| Method | Principle | Efficiency | Broken % |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Engleberg Huller** (Under-runner disc) | Shear + friction between two emery discs | Low (~65%) | High (~20–30%) |
| **Rubber Roll Sheller** | Shear between two rubber rolls rotating at differential speeds | High (~85–90%) | Low (~2–5%) |
| **Centrifugal / Impact Sheller** | Centrifugal impact against rubber lining | Medium (~75%) | Medium (~5–10%) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Rubber Roll Sheller** is the **gold standard** in modern KTU curriculum — it produces the **least broken rice** because rubber is elastic and "grips" the husk without crushing the endosperm.

### Stage 4 — Paddy Separation (Brown Rice vs. Un-husked Paddy)
- **Output of husker is a MIX** of brown rice (~80%) + un-husked paddy (~20%).
- **Equipment Types:**
  - **Compartment Separator** (Trieur / Slot-type)
  - **Indented Cylinder Separator**
  - **Tray Separator** (most common in India)
- **Principle:** Brown rice (smooth) slides; paddy (rough hull) tumbles/slips.

### Stage 5 — Whitening / Polishing (Removing Bran Layer)
- **Abrasive Type Whitener:** Uses emery/carborundum roll — grinds off bran.
- **Friction Type Whitener:** Uses friction between grains under pressure — peels bran.
- **Jet Polisher (Mist Polisher):** Uses water mist + friction for a glossy surface.

### Stage 6 — Grading & Sorting
- **Indented Cylinders / Length Graders:** Separate broken from head rice.
- **Optical Sorters (Color Sorters):** Remove **discolored, chalky, yellow, or black grains** using CCD cameras + air jets.

### Stage 7 — Packaging & Storage
- Moisture reduced to **12–14%** before packaging.
- Packed in **PP woven bags, jute bags, or vacuum pouches**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.

| Parameter | Formula | Variables | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Milling Recovery (MR)** | $\displaystyle MR(\%) = \frac{M_{rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | $M_{rice}$ = mass of total milled rice; $M_{paddy}$ = input paddy mass | 65 – 70% |
| **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $\displaystyle HRY(\%) = \frac{M_{head\,rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | $M_{head\,rice}$ = mass of kernels $\geq$ ¾ grain length | 50 – 70% |
| **Broken Percentage** | $\displaystyle B(\%) = \frac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\,rice}} \times 100$ | $M_{broken}$ = mass of broken kernels | 5 – 15% |
| **Degree of Milling (DOM)** | $\displaystyle DOM(\%) = \frac{M_{brown} - M_{white}}{M_{brown}} \times 100$ | Mass loss from brown to white rice | 6 – 9% |
| **Husking Efficiency (HE)** | $\displaystyle HE(\%) = \frac{M_{husked}}{M_{paddy\,fed}} \times 100$ | $M_{husked}$ = brown rice out | 80 – 90% |
| **Throughput** | $\displaystyle Q = \frac{M_{paddy}}{t}$ | kg processed per hour | Depends on machine |
| **Specific Energy Consumption** | $\displaystyle SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{M_{rice}} \bigg[\frac{kWh}{ton}\bigg]$ | $E_{total}$ = total electrical energy | 35 – 55 kWh/ton |
| **Whitening Efficiency** | $\displaystyle WE(\%) = \frac{DOM_{actual}}{DOM_{required}} \times 100$ | Actual vs. desired bran removal | $>90\%$ target |

**Note:** In the table above, the absolute-value / ratio-bar delimiters have been rendered as $\bigg[$ and $\bigg]$ to avoid breaking Markdown table pipe syntax.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

- **Biomass Energy Recovery:** Rice husk (~20% of paddy) is fed into **biomass boilers** to generate steam for parboiling plants — making rice mills **net-zero waste** facilities.
- **Rice Bran Oil (RBO) Industry:** Bran (~8–10%) is stabilized and solvent-extracted to yield **RBO**, rich in **Oryzanol** (a cholesterol-lowering antioxidant).
- **Broken Rice Utilization:** Converted to **rice flour, rice starch, brewing adjuncts, infant food, and pet feed**.
- **Export Quality Compliance:** HRY and DOM must meet **Codex Alimentarius / FSSAI standards** for international export.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style Problem

### **Sample Problem:**
> A modern rice mill processes **10,000 kg of paddy per shift (8 hours)**. The milling recovery is **68%** and the broken percentage within the milled rice is **12%**. Calculate:
> (a) Total milled rice obtained
> (b) Head rice (whole kernels) obtained
> (c) Quantity of broken rice
> (d) Quantity of husk produced (assuming husk = 22% of paddy)
> (e) Quantity of bran produced (assume bran = 8% of paddy)

---

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given Data (Extracted & Tabulated):**
- $M_{paddy} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $Milling\ Recovery\ (MR) = 68\%$
- $Broken\ \%\ within\ milled\ rice = 12\%$
- $Husk = 22\%$ of paddy
- $Bran = 8\%$ of paddy

---

#### **(a) Total Milled Rice Obtained**

$$M_{rice} = M_{paddy} \times \frac{MR}{100}$$

$$M_{rice} = 10{,}000 \times \frac{68}{100}$$

$$M_{rice} = 6{,}800 \text{ kg}$$

**Interpretation:** From 10,000 kg of paddy, the mill produces **6,800 kg** of edible milled rice per shift.

---

#### **(b) Head Rice (Whole Kernels) Obtained**

Head rice is the portion of milled rice that is **NOT broken**. Since broken = 12%, head rice fraction = **88%**.

$$M_{head\,rice} = M_{rice} \times \frac{(100 - B)}{100}$$

$$M_{head\,rice} = 6{,}800 \times \frac{88}{100}$$

$$M_{head\,rice} = 5{,}984 \text{ kg}$$

---

#### **(c) Quantity of Broken Rice**

$$M_{broken} = M_{rice} \times \frac{B}{100}$$

$$M_{broken} = 6{,}800 \times \frac{12}{100}$$

$$M_{broken} = 816 \text{ kg}$$

**Verification (Mass Balance Check):**

$$M_{head\,rice} + M_{broken} = 5{,}984 + 816 = 6{,}800 \text{ kg} \checkmark$$

This equals the total milled rice — confirming the algebraic correctness.

---

#### **(d) Quantity of Husk Produced**

$$M_{husk} = M_{paddy} \times \frac{22}{100}$$

$$M_{husk} = 10{,}000 \times 0.22$$

$$M_{husk} = 2{,}200 \text{ kg}$$

---

#### **(e) Quantity of Bran Produced**

$$M_{bran} = M_{paddy} \times \frac{8}{100}$$

$$M_{bran} = 10{,}000 \times 0.08$$

$$M_{bran} = 800 \text{ kg}$$

---

### **Full Mass Balance Table (Engineering Output)**

| Component | Mass (kg) | % of Paddy | Engineering Disposition |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Head Rice | 5,984 | 59.84% | Premium market / Export |
| Broken Rice | 816 | 8.16% | Flour, brewing, feed |
| Husk | 2,200 | 22.00% | Boiler fuel / Biomass power |
| Bran | 800 | 8.00% | Rice bran oil extraction |
| **Moisture Loss + Dust** | 200 | 2.00% | Evaporation during processing |
| **TOTAL** | **10,000** | **100%** | Mass balance verified |

> [!NOTE]
> The mass balance MUST close to **100% ± 0.5%**. If your numbers don't add up, recheck — KTU examiners specifically award marks for **mass balance verification**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Husking Methods

| Attribute | Engleberg Huller | Rubber Roll Sheller | Centrifugal Sheller |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Working Principle** | Shear friction on emery discs | Differential speed rubber rolls | Centrifugal impact on rubber liner |
| **Husking Efficiency** | 60–70% | 85–95% | 75–85% |
| **Broken Rice Generation** | 20–30% | 2–5% | 5–10% |
| **Capital Cost** | Very Low (₹) | Moderate (₹₹₹) | Moderate (₹₹) |
| **Throughput** | Low (50–200 kg/h) | High (2–10 tons/h) | Medium (1–3 tons/h) |
| **Endosperm Damage** | High | Very Low | Medium |
| **Suitability** | Rural cottage units | **Industrial KTU recommendation** | Medium-scale plants |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Operator Skill Needed** | Low | High (roll adjustment) | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Modern Multi-Stage Rice Milling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Paddy Intake] --> A2[Pre-Cleaner]
    A2 --> A3[De-Stoner]
    A3 --> A4[Moisture Conditioner]
    A4 --> A5[Rubber Roll Husker]
    A5 --> A6[Paddy Separator]
    A6 --> A7a[Un-husked Paddy]
    A6 --> A7b[Brown Rice]
    A7a --> A5
    A7b --> A8[Whitener Stage 1]
    A8 --> A9[Whitener Stage 2]
    A9 --> A10[Mist Polisher]
    A10 --> A11[Length Grader]
    A11 --> A12a[Head Rice to Packing]
    A11 --> A12b[Broken Rice Collection]
    A12a --> A13[Optical Color Sorter]
    A13 --> A14[Weighing and Packaging]
    A14 --> A15[Finished White Rice Output]
    A5 -.-> BY1[Husk to Boiler]
    A8 -.-> BY2[First Stage Bran to Extraction]
    A9 -.-> BY3[Second Stage Bran to Extraction]
    A2 -.-> BY4[Dust and Light Impurities Rejected]
    A3 -.-> BY5[Stones Rejected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows ( → ) represent the **main material flow** of rice kernels. Dotted arrows ( -.-> ) represent **by-product streams** (husk, bran, impurities). The loop from paddy separator back to husker represents **re-circulation of un-husked paddy** for a second pass.

## 4.2 Process Hierarchy — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[INPUT: Raw Paddy] --> B2[BLOCK 1: CLEANING UNIT]
    B2 --> B3[BLOCK 2: HUSKING UNIT]
    B3 --> B4[BLOCK 3: SEPARATION UNIT]
    B4 --> B5[BLOCK 4: WHITENING UNIT]
    B5 --> B6[BLOCK 5: GRADING UNIT]
    B6 --> B7[BLOCK 6: SORTING UNIT]
    B7 --> B8[OUTPUT: Polished White Rice]

    B2 -.by1.-> BB1[Husk 22%]
    B3 -.by2.-> BB2[Brown Rice 78%]
    B5 -.by3.-> BB3[Polishing Bran 8%]
    B6 -.by4.-> BB4[Broken Kernels 12%]
    B7 -.by5.-> BB5[Discolored Grains 1 to 2%]
```

## 4.3 Husker (Rubber Roll Sheller) — Functional Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Hopper] --> C2[Feed Roll]
    C2 --> C3[Nip Region]
    C3 --> C4[Slow Rubber Roll]
    C3 --> C5[Fast Rubber Roll]
    C4 --> C6[Brown Rice Outlet]
    C5 --> C6
    C3 --> C7[Husk Aspirator]
    C7 --> C8[Husk Collection]
    C6 --> C9[Paddy Separator Inlet]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept Highlighted:** The **nip region** between the **slow roll** (lower peripheral speed, $\approx 9\text{ m/s}$) and the **fast roll** ($\approx 15\text{ m/s}$) creates a **differential speed of $\approx 6\text{ m/s}$**. This differential SHEARS the husk off without crushing the endosperm. This is the **exact mechanism** that makes rubber roll shellers superior to Engleberg hullers, and KTU examiners **frequently test this concept**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1:**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> Define **Head Rice Yield (HRY)**. Why is HRY considered more important than total milling recovery in commercial rice trade?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Head Rice Yield is the percentage of milled rice kernels that are **whole and unbroken**, i.e., having a length **≥ ¾ of the average grain length**, expressed as a percentage of the original paddy mass.
- **Formula (1 Mark):** $HRY(\%) = \dfrac{M_{head\,rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$
- **Why more important than MR (1 Mark):** Because commercial rice is priced based on **whole-grain appearance, cooking uniformity, and consumer preference**. Broken rice fetches only 40–60% of the price of head rice. Hence, two mills may have equal MR but vastly different profitability based on HRY.

---

### **Question A2:**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> List any **three advantages** of a **rubber roll sheller** over the **traditional Engleberg huller**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Higher Husking Efficiency** (85–95% vs. 60–70%) → less re-circulation load. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Lower Broken Rice Generation** (2–5% vs. 20–30%) → higher HRY and economic value. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Lower Power Consumption per ton** + ability to handle **long, slender, and basmati-type paddy** without breakage → superior versatility. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives below are model questions for full exam preparation.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain with a **neat block diagram** the **sequence of unit operations** in a modern multi-stage rice milling plant. Describe the function of **any three major machines** in detail.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rice mill processes **25 tonnes of paddy per day**. If the milling recovery is **70%** and broken rice within the milled rice is **8%**, calculate:
> - (i) Total milled rice produced per day.
> - (ii) Head rice produced per day.
> - (iii) Quantity of husk generated per day (assume husk = 22% of paddy).
> - (iv) Prepare a complete **mass balance table**.
> **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram + 3 Machine Descriptions (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (already covered in SECTION 4.1 — refer to the Mermaid flowchart).** The student should redraw in pen-and-paper exam as:

```
Paddy → Pre-Cleaner → De-Stoner → Husker → Paddy Separator
       → Whitener → Polisher → Grader → Color Sorter → Packing
```

**Machine Descriptions (select any three, 2 Marks each + 1 Mark for diagram):**

1. **Rubber Roll Sheller:** Removes husk using **differential-speed rotating rubber rolls**. Slow roll at ~9 m/s and fast roll at ~15 m/s. The grain is gripped and husk is sheared off in the nip region. Produces brown rice with minimal breakage.

2. **Paddy Separator (Tray Type):** Uses **reciprocating inclined trays** with hemispherical indentations. **Brown rice** (smooth surface) slides off the trays; **un-husked paddy** (rough hull) is retained and re-circulated back to the husker.

3. **Abrasive Whitener:** A rotating **emery-coated roll** within a cylindrical sieve. Bran layer is abraded off by the friction between the grain and emery surface. Used in first whitening pass.

4. **Optical Color Sorter:** Uses **CCD cameras + pneumatic ejectors** to detect and reject **chalky, yellow, black, or discolored grains** at high speed (5,000–10,000 kernels/sec).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $M_{paddy} = 25{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$
- $MR = 70\%$
- Broken fraction within milled rice $B = 8\%$
- Husk = 22%

**(i) Total Milled Rice:**
$$M_{rice} = 25{,}000 \times \frac{70}{100} = 17{,}500 \text{ kg/day} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Head Rice:**
$$M_{head\,rice} = 17{,}500 \times \frac{(100-8)}{100} = 17{,}500 \times 0.92 = 16{,}100 \text{ kg/day} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Husk:**
$$M_{husk} = 25{,}000 \times \frac{22}{100} = 5{,}500 \text{ kg/day} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**(iv) Mass Balance Table (2 Marks):**

| Component | Mass (kg) | % of Paddy |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Head Rice | 16,100 | 64.40% |
| Broken Rice | 1,400 | 5.60% |
| Husk | 5,500 | 22.00% |
| Bran (assumed 8%) | 2,000 | 8.00% |
| Loss/Moisture | 0 | 0.00% |
| **TOTAL** | **25,000** | **100%** |

**Verification:** $16{,}100 + 1{,}400 + 5{,}500 + 2{,}000 = 25{,}000 \checkmark$ Mass balance closed.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Differentiate between the **Engleberg huller** and the **rubber roll sheller** in terms of working principle, husking efficiency, broken rice generation, and suitability for basmati rice. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **parboiling**? Describe the **traditional and modern parboiling methods** with their advantages and disadvantages. How does parboiling improve **milling yield and nutritional quality**? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Engleberg Huller | Rubber Roll Sheller | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Principle** | Shear + friction between two emery discs | Shear between rubber rolls at differential speed | 1.5 |
| **Husking Efficiency** | 60–70% | 85–95% | 1.5 |
| **Broken %** | 20–30% | 2–5% | 1.5 |
| **Suitability for Basmati** | Unsuitable (excessive breakage of long slender grains) | **Highly suitable** (gentle husking preserves length) | 1.5 |
| **Cost / Operator Skill** | Low / Low | Higher / Higher | 1.0 |

**Part (b) — Parboiling Description (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Parboiling is a **hydrothermal treatment** of paddy wherein the grain is **soaked in water, steamed, and dried** before milling, causing **gelatinization of the starch** within the endosperm.

**Traditional Method (1.5 Marks):**
- **Soaking:** Paddy is soaked in water at ambient temperature for **24–72 hours** in concrete tanks.
- **Steaming:** Drained paddy is steamed in batches (1–2 hours) until husk splits open.
- **Drying:** Sun-dried to 12–14% moisture.

**Modern Method (1.5 Marks):**
- **Hot Soaking:** Water at 60–70°C for 4–6 hours.
- **Pressure Parboiling:** Paddy steamed under **1–3 kg/cm² pressure** for 15–20 minutes (CFTRI process).
- **Mechanized Drying:** Using **fluidized bed / column dryers**.

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
- **Milling Yield:** Improved HRY by **15–20%** because starch hardening makes kernels **resistant to breakage**.
- **Nutrition:** Vitamin B-complex (especially **thiamine**) migrates from bran INTO the endosperm, hence parboiled rice is **more nutritious** than raw white rice.
- **Storage:** Gelatinized starch is **less susceptible to insect attack**, longer shelf life.
- **Cooking:** Produces **firmer, less sticky rice** with reduced cooking loss.

**Disadvantages (1 Mark):**
- Longer processing time, color darkens to amber/yellow, longer cooking time required.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Confusing MR with HRY:** MR includes broken rice; HRY does NOT. Examiners award **ZERO** if these are interchanged. Always write full forms first, then use abbreviations.
> 2. **Forgetting Mass Balance Verification:** Always show a final **"Total = Input"** check row. KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** exclusively for mass balance closure.
> 3. **Wrong Units in Energy Calculations:** SEC must be in **kWh/ton**, NOT kWh/kg.
> 4. **Calling Engleberg a "modern" huller:** It is **OBSOLETE / traditional**. Examiners specifically test awareness of technological obsolescence.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** In any 14-mark question on processing, **at least 3–4 marks** are reserved for a properly labelled block diagram. A textual answer without a diagram caps your score.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Month Quick Glance Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Paddy structure = **Hull (20%) + Bran (8–10%) + Endosperm (70%) + Germ**
- ✅ **Milling Recovery (MR)** = $\dfrac{M_{rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ → typical **65–70%**
- ✅ **HRY** is **MORE important** than MR commercially
- ✅ **Rubber Roll Sheller > Engleberg Huller** in efficiency and gentleness
- ✅ **Differential roll speed** ($\approx 6\text{ m/s}$) is the key to low-breakage husking
- ✅ **Paddy separator** separates by **surface friction difference**
- ✅ **Whitening** removes bran; **Polishing** improves gloss
- ✅ **Optical sorter** uses **CCD cameras + air jets**
- ✅ **Husk = 22%** of paddy → biomass boiler fuel
- ✅ **Bran = 8–10%** of paddy → rice bran oil extraction
- ✅ **Broken rice** uses: flour, brewing, infant food, pet feed
- ✅ **Parboiling** = soaking + steaming + drying → improves HRY by **15–20%**
- ✅ **Degree of Milling (DOM)** = **6–9%** for white rice, **0%** for brown rice
- ✅ **Always close the mass balance** in numericals (100% ± 0.5%)
- ✅ **SEC benchmark:** **35–55 kWh/ton** of rice
- ✅ **Basmati rice → MUST use rubber roll sheller** (not Engleberg)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipments Involved in Rice Milling Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Equipments Involved in Rice Milling Processing # 🏭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal Academic Definition **Rice Milling Processing** refers to the systematic sequence of mechanical, physical, and sometimes physico-chemical operations that transform harvested paddy (*Oryza sativa*) into edible white rice suitable for human consumption. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEFFT836, this involves a chain of specialized **equipments** arranged in a defined flow, each designed to progressively remove impurities, the husk, the bran layers, and to grade the final product. The primary unit operations and their associated machinery are: 1. **Pre-Cleaning** → Air-Screen Cleaners, Magnetic Separators 2. **De-Stoning** → Gravity Separators / Destoners 3. **Husking (De-hulling)** → Rubber Roll Shellers / Paddy Hullers 4. **Paddy Separation** → Paddy Separators (Compartment / Tray Type) 5. **Whitening & Polishing** → Abrasive Whitener, Friction Whitener, Mist Polisher 6. **Grading & Sorting** → Length Graders (Indented Cylinders), Thickness Graders, Color Sorters 7. **Weighing & Packaging** → Automatic Bagging Scales > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on **identifying each piece of equipment, its operating principle, its function in the process flow, and its effect on milling recovery and head rice yield.** Equipment selection directly affects the commercial **milling recovery (typically 66–70%)** of the plant. --- ## 💡 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) Imagine paddy grains as a **box of chocolates**, where each grain is wrapped in multiple layers: - 🟫 The outermost **rough shell** = the **husk** (inedible, hard cellulose-silica layer) - 🟠 The middle **brown film** = the **bran layer** (rich in oil, fiber — removed for white rice) - ⚪ The **white pearl inside** = the **endosperm** (the edible polished rice) Now, imagine you have a **production line in a factory** where millions of such wrapped chocolates must be unwrapped, sorted, cleaned, polished, and graded — *without crushing the pearl inside*. That entire factory assembly line, with each station having a specific purpose, **IS the rice milling processing system**. | Real-World Equivalent | Rice Milling Operation | |---|---| | Wiping muddy boxes clean | Pre-cleaning & destoning | | Removing the outer cardboard box | Husking (rubber roll sheller) | | Sorting out chocolates that still have wrappers | Paddy separator | | Peeling off the inner foil | Whitening / Polishing | | Sorting big vs. small pearls | Length grading | | Removing discoloured pieces | Color sorting | > [!NOTE] > **Did you know?** The rice husk is one of the hardest natural materials — it contains ~20% silica. This is why **rubber rolls** (not metal) are used in husking, because metal-on-metal contact would shatter the brittle grain into broken pieces, ruining **head rice yield**. --- ## 🎯 Why Each Equipment Stage Matters (Big Picture) - **Without cleaning** → stones damage the rubber rolls in the husker, increasing downtime. - **Without husking** → no brown rice to polish. - **Without paddy separation** → unhulled grains pass to whitening, causing incomplete polishing and wastage. - **Without whitening** → brown rice (rich in lipids) goes rancid within weeks → short shelf life. - **Without grading** → a mixed bag of whole + broken grains fetches lower market price. - **Without mist polishing** → rice surface looks dull, less appealing to consumers. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember the flow:** **"C–S–H–P–W–G–B"** → **C**leaning → **S**toning → **H**usking → **P**addy separation → **W**hitening → **G**rading → **B**agging. # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧩 Equipment-Wise Operational Breakdown ### 1️⃣ AIR-SCREEN CLEANER (Pre-Cleaner) - **Operating Principle:** Combines **aspiration (air suction)** with **vibratory sieves** (2–3 sieves of different perforation sizes). - **Sieves used:** - Top sieve: Removes large impurities (straw, string, leaves) - Bottom sieve: Removes small impurities (sand, dust, small seeds) - **Air stream:** Lifts light impurities (dust, empty grains, chaff) via aspiration ducts. - **Why needed:** Protects downstream rubber rolls from abrasive wear. > [!NOTE] > Standard pre-cleaner handles up to **8–10 tonnes/hour** of paddy in modern plants. --- ### 2️⃣ MAGNETIC SEPARATOR (Inline Protection) - **Principle:** Permanent magnets or electromagnets capture ferrous metal pieces. - **Location:** Installed at the **receiving conveyor** before the paddy enters the cleaner. - **Why:** Prevents metal sparks/fire in downstream dust-handling equipment. --- ### 3️⃣ DESTONER (Gravity Separator) - **Principle:** Exploits **density difference** between paddy (~0.75 g/cm³) and stones (~2.4 g/cm³). - **Mechanism:** Vibrating inclined deck with **perforated mesh** + upward air flow fluidizes the grain bed. Heavier stones migrate to the top layer and travel upward against vibration to a discharge spout; lighter grains stay on the deck and flow downhill. - **Performance:** Removes stones of size close to paddy grains that sieves cannot differentiate. --- ### 4️⃣ PADDY HUSKER / RUBBER ROLL SHELLER - **Most critical equipment in rice milling.** - **Construction:** Two rubber rolls (typically polyurethane, **Shore hardness 80–95**, diameter **200–250 mm**, width **100–250 mm**) rotating at **different speeds**. - **Speed ratio:** Typically **1 : 1.25 to 1 : 1.40** (fast roll : slow roll). - **Linear speed differential:** ~**8–12 m/s** at the nip. - **Mechanism:** Grain caught between the two rolls is sheared → husk splits and is torn off. - **Paddy husk separation:** **Aspirator column** below the husker uses suction to lift the light husk while heavier brown rice falls. - **Husking efficiency:** Designed for **75–85%** per pass (single pass); recirculated through paddy separator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why rubber rolls and not steel?** Rubber deforms slightly, grips the grain, applies gradual shear, and absorbs shock — minimizing **broken kernel formation**. Steel would crack the brittle endosperm → drop in **head rice yield**. --- ### 5️⃣ PADDY SEPARATOR - **Purpose:** Separate unhulled paddy (heavier, longer with husk intact) from brown rice (lighter, shorter). - **Two major types:** - **a) Compartment Type (Tray Separator)** - Inclined reciprocating compartments. - Separation based on **bulk density difference** + **shape (length)** difference. - Compartment has an indented pocket that scoops up brown rice and discharges it sideways. - **b) Flat-Bed (Indented Cylinder) Separator** - Slowly rotating cylinder with small hemispherical indents on inner surface. - Indents lift the smaller brown rice grains and drop them into an internal screw conveyor. - Larger unhulled paddy grains stay at the bottom and are conveyed back to husker. - **Typical separation efficiency:** **95–98%**. --- ### 6️⃣ WHITENER (Abrasive Type) - **Function:** Removes the **bran layer** (~6–8% of brown rice weight). - **Construction:** Horizontal cylindrical sieve cage; rotating abrasive roller made of **silicon carbide stones or Carborundum** grits. - **Mechanism:** Friction + abrasion abrades the bran off the grain surface. - **Bran removal:** Pneumatic suction lifts bran particles through the perforated cage. - **Pressure:** Adjustable via counter-weight mechanism on discharge gate. --- ### 7️⃣ FRICTION-TYPE WHITENER (Cone Polisher) - **Construction:** Conical rotor inside a cylindrical screen. - **Principle:** Grains are rubbed against each other (**grain-on-grain friction**) under pressure — gentler than abrasive type. - **Use:** Often the **second stage** after abrasive whitener for finer polishing. --- ### 8️⃣ MIST POLISHER (Water Mist Polisher) - **Function:** Applies a controlled mist of water (~**0.2–0.5%** of rice weight) during final polishing. - **Purpose:** Water dissolves residual bran, creates **glossy surface**, reduces **broken fraction**, increases **head rice yield** by ~**2–3%**. - **Also:** Heat from friction + moisture softens the bran layer for cleaner removal. --- ### 9️⃣ LENGTH GRADER (Indented Cylinder Separator) - **Principle:** Cylinder with hemispherical indents (size ~**4.5–6 mm** for rice). - **Action:** Short grains (brokens) fit into indents and are lifted; long whole grains (head rice) are discharged at the end. - **Used for:** Separating brokens from head rice, also pre-grading before color sorting. --- ### 🔟 THICKNESS GRADER / TRIEUR DRUM - **Principle:** Cylindrical drum with concentric perforations of graded sizes. - **Action:** Thicker grains retained first; thinner grains (brokens) fall through. --- ### 1️⃣1️⃣ COLOR SORTER (Optical Sorter) - **Principle:** High-resolution **CCD cameras / optical sensors** scan every grain; discoloured, chalky, or defective grains are ejected by a precise **air-jet nozzle**. - **Rejection rate:** Adjustable; typically **1–5%**. - **Use:** Removes yellow, chalky, immature, and discoloured kernels. --- ### 1️⃣2️⃣ AUTOMATIC BAGGING SCALE - **Principle:** Load-cell based weighing with automated filling and stitching. - **Throughput:** 200–400 bags/hour (25/50 kg bags). --- ## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table summarizes every key formula you must memorize for rice milling numerical problems.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Milling Recovery** | $MR = \dfrac{W_{rice}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$ | $W_{rice}$ = weight of total milled rice; $W_{paddy}$ = weight of raw paddy | **66 – 70 %** | | 2 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $HRY = \dfrac{W_{head}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100$ | $W_{head}$ = weight of whole unbroken kernels | **50 – 60 %** | | 3 | **Brokens Yield** | $BY = MR - HRY$ | Brokens as % of paddy | **10 – 20 %** | | 4 | **Husking Efficiency** | $\eta_H = \dfrac{W_{husked}}{W_{input\ paddy}} \times 100$ | % paddy husked in single pass | **75 – 85 %** | | 5 | **Whitening Degree** | $W_D = \dfrac{W_{brown} - W_{white}}{W_{brown}} \times 100$ | % bran removed | **6 – 10 %** | | 6 | **Degree of Polish** | $DOP = \dfrac{W_{after\ polish}}{W_{before\ polish}} \times 100$ | % mass retained | **88 – 94 %** | | 7 | **Throughput of rubber roll** | $Q = \pi \cdot L \cdot D \cdot n \cdot k$ | $L$=roll length; $D$=diameter; $n$=RPM; $k$=empirical factor | tonnes/hr | | 8 | **Roll speed differential** | $\Delta v = \pi D (n_{fast} - n_{slow})$ | Linear speed difference at nip | **8 – 12 m/s** | | 9 | **Slip ratio** | $SR = \dfrac{n_{fast} - n_{slow}}{n_{fast}} \times 100$ | Relative speed ratio in % | **20 – 30 %** | | 10 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{E\ (kWh)}{W_{paddy}\ (tonnes)}$ | Energy per tonne processed | **40 – 70 kWh/tonne** | > [!WARNING] > **Do not confuse "Milling Recovery" with "Head Rice Yield".** Milling Recovery is *all* rice (head + brokens), while Head Rice Yield is *only* whole grains. In exam problems, always check which one is being asked. --- ## 🌾 Engineering Utility The above equations are not merely theoretical — they directly determine: - **Profitability:** A 1% increase in HRY on a 100 TPD plant = **₹15–25 lakh additional revenue/year**. - **Equipment selection:** Whitener throughput equations help specify motor power. - **Energy audit:** SEC values help compare old vs. modern milling plants. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📐 Derivation 1: Total Rice Output from Paddy **Problem Statement:** A rice mill processes **1000 kg of cleaned paddy**. After milling, the operator records: - Weight of head rice = 580 kg - Weight of brokens = 90 kg - Weight of husk = 230 kg - Weight of bran = 80 kg - Weight of dust/waste = 20 kg Compute the **Milling Recovery (MR)**, **Head Rice Yield (HRY)**, and **Broken Percentage**. ### Step 1: Compute Total Milled Rice $$ \begin{aligned} W_{rice} &= W_{head} + W_{brokens} \\ W_{rice} &= 580 + 90 \\ W_{rice} &= 670\ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Total rice output includes both whole kernels and broken pieces, since both are commercially saleable (at different prices). --- ### Step 2: Compute Milling Recovery (MR) $$ \begin{aligned} MR &= \frac{W_{rice}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 \\ MR &= \frac{670}{1000} \times 100 \\ MR &= 67\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* This percentage tells the miller how much of the input paddy was converted to finished rice — a key KPI. --- ### Step 3: Compute Head Rice Yield (HRY) $$ \begin{aligned} HRY &= \frac{W_{head}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 \\ HRY &= \frac{580}{1000} \times 100 \\ HRY &= 58\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* HRY is the most valued metric because broken rice fetches only 50–60% of the price of head rice. --- ### Step 4: Compute Broken Percentage (relative to total rice) $$ \begin{aligned} BP &= \frac{W_{brokens}}{W_{rice}} \times 100 \\ BP &= \frac{90}{670} \times 100 \\ BP &= 13.43\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* This percentage indicates the **mechanical breakage rate** of the milling system. Industry standard is **≤ 15%**. --- ### Step 5: Mass Balance Verification $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input} &= \text{Output} \\ W_{paddy} &= W_{rice} + W_{husk} + W_{bran} + W_{dust} \\ 1000 &= 670 + 230 + 80 + 20 \\ 1000 &= 1000\ \text{kg} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Conservation of mass — the sum of all outputs must equal the input. --- ## 📐 Derivation 2: Whitening Degree & Degree of Polish **Problem Statement:** Brown rice weighs **1000 kg** entering a whitener. After abrasive whitening, the rice weighs **920 kg**. After mist polishing, it weighs **915 kg**. Compute: - (a) Whitening Degree $W_D$ - (b) Degree of Polish $DOP$ after mist polishing - (c) Compare with industry standard. ### Step 1: Whitening Degree $$ \begin{aligned} W_D &= \frac{W_{brown} - W_{white}}{W_{brown}} \times 100 \\ W_D &= \frac{1000 - 920}{1000} \times 100 \\ W_D &= \frac{80}{1000} \times 100 \\ W_D &= 8\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* This means **8% of brown rice mass was removed as bran**. The typical bran layer is 6–8% — so this whitener is operating in the optimal range. --- ### Step 2: Degree of Polish after Mist Polishing $$ \begin{aligned} DOP &= \frac{W_{after\ polish}}{W_{before\ polish}} \times 100 \\ DOP &= \frac{915}{920} \times 100 \\ DOP &= 99.46\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Mist polisher removed only **5 kg** (≈ 0.5%) of additional mass — its purpose is **surface gloss** not mass removal. --- ### Step 3: Total Bran Removed (Cumulative) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total bran} &= W_{brown} - W_{final} \\ &= 1000 - 915 \\ &= 85\ \text{kg} \\ \%\text{removed} &= \frac{85}{1000} \times 100 = 8.5\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Industry-standard for fully polished rice is **8–10%** bran removal; values above 10% mean **over-polishing** (loss of nutrients, increased broken formation). --- ## 📐 Derivation 3: Rubber Roll Husker Speed Differential **Problem Statement:** A rubber roll husker has rolls of diameter **250 mm**. The fast roll rotates at **900 RPM** and the slow roll at **720 RPM**. Compute: - (a) Linear speed of fast roll (m/s) - (b) Linear speed of slow roll (m/s) - (c) Linear speed differential (m/s) - (d) Slip ratio (%) ### Step 1: Convert RPM to Linear Speed $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \pi \cdot D \cdot n \\ \text{where } n &= \text{RPS} = \frac{RPM}{60} \end{aligned} $$ For fast roll: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{fast} &= \frac{900}{60} = 15\ \text{RPS} \\ v_{fast} &= \pi \times 0.25 \times 15 \\ v_{fast} &= 3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 15 \\ v_{fast} &= 11.78\ \text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ For slow roll: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{slow} &= \frac{720}{60} = 12\ \text{RPS} \\ v_{slow} &= \pi \times 0.25 \times 12 \\ v_{slow} &= 3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 12 \\ v_{slow} &= 9.42\ \text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Linear Speed Differential $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta v &= v_{fast} - v_{slow} \\ \Delta v &= 11.78 - 9.42 \\ \Delta v &= 2.36\ \text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Wait — this is **NOT** the industrial differential. The differential should be calculated differently. The **peripheral speed** is: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{fast} &= \pi \times 0.25 \times 15 = 11.78\ \text{m/s} \\ v_{slow} &= \pi \times 0.25 \times 12 = 9.42\ \text{m/s} \\ \text{Speed ratio} &= \frac{900}{720} = 1.25 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* In industry, **speed ratio of 1 : 1.25** is standard. The peripheral speeds (~10–12 m/s) provide the kinetic energy needed for husk shear. The **actual shearing differential** depends on roll surface grip. ### Step 3: Slip Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} SR &= \frac{n_{fast} - n_{slow}}{n_{fast}} \times 100 \\ SR &= \frac{900 - 720}{900} \times 100 \\ SR &= 20\ \% \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* A slip ratio of **20–30%** ensures firm husk grip with minimum kernel breakage. Lower slip → husk not removed; higher slip → excessive breakage. --- ## 📐 Derivation 4: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) **Problem Statement:** A mill uses **44 kWh** to process **1 tonne** of paddy. Compute the SEC and comment. ### Step 1: Direct Calculation $$ \begin{aligned} SEC &= \frac{E\ (\text{kWh})}{W\ (\text{tonne})} \\ SEC &= \frac{44}{1} \\ SEC &= 44\ \text{kWh/tonne} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Comparison with Benchmarks | Plant Type | SEC (kWh/tonne) | |---|---| | Traditional huller mill | **60 – 80** | | Modern multi-stage mill | **40 – 55** | | Best-in-class modern plant | **≤ 40** | *Conclusion:* 44 kWh/tonne falls in the **modern multi-stage range** — operationally efficient. --- ## 📈 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Equipment Parameters on Yield | Equipment | Key Variable | Increase Effect on HRY | Increase Effect on Brokens | Industry Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Rubber Roll** | Roll differential | ↓ initially, then ↓ sharply | ↑ sharply if too high | 1.20 – 1.40 ratio | | **Rubber Roll** | Shore hardness | ↑ (cleaner husk removal) | ↑ if too hard | 80 – 95 Shore A | | **Whitener** | Abrasive pressure | ↑ bran removal | ↑ brokens sharply | 50 – 200 g/cm² | | **Whitener** | Retention time | ↑ whiteness | ↑ brokens | 30 – 60 s | | **Mist Polisher** | Water mist rate | ↑ gloss | Neutral to ↑ | 0.2 – 0.5 % of rice | | **Paddy Separator** | Inclination angle | Better separation | Neutral | 10° – 18° | | **Color Sorter** | Air-jet pressure | ↑ rejection accuracy | Neutral | 0.3 – 0.6 MPa | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🌾 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Rice Mill > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid flowchart represents the **complete flow of paddy through all equipment stages** in a modern multi-stage rice milling plant.
