It is installed **first** because (1 mark) any ferrous/stone contamination downstream will damage the rubber rolls of the husker and reduce the **head rice yield**. Pre-cleaning thus protects subsequent equipment and improves overall mill efficiency. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the operating principle of the rubber roll husker. Why are rubber rolls used instead of metal rolls?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Operating principle (2 marks):** The rubber roll husker consists of two parallel rubber rolls (of Shore hardness 80–95) rotating in opposite directions at different speeds (typically 900 RPM and 720 RPM, speed ratio 1.20–1.40). Paddy grains fed between them are caught at the **nip region** where the speed differential creates a **shearing force** that splits and tears off the husk without damaging the endosperm. 2. **Why rubber, not metal (1 mark):** Rubber deforms slightly to grip the grain firmly and apply **gradual shear stress**, whereas metal-on-metal contact would shatter the brittle rice kernel, drastically reducing the **head rice yield**. --- ## 🅱️ PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **describe equipment without mentioning the underlying physical principle** (e.g., density difference, friction, shear). Always state the principle *before* the construction. > - **Numerical errors** in milling recovery are penalized heavily — show units at every step. > - Avoid generic statements like "removes impurities" — be **specific**: stones, straw, empty grains, dust. > - When comparing two equipments, use a **comparison table**, not paragraphs. > - Always end numerical answers with a **comment on industry standard** (e.g., "which falls within the typical 66–70% range"). --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **rubber roll husker** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of rubber roll hardness, speed differential, and the aspirator in determining **husking efficiency**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A modern rice mill processed **5000 kg of paddy** in a shift. The output data were: - Brown rice obtained = 4100 kg - Polished rice (after whitening + mist polishing) = 3800 kg - Head rice = 3200 kg - Husk collected = 1100 kg - Bran collected = 250 kg - Dust + losses = ? Calculate: **(i)** Mass balance total, **(ii)** Milling recovery, **(iii)** Head rice yield, **(iv)** Whitening degree, **(v)** Broken percentage. Comment on whether the mill is operating efficiently. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question A ### Part (a): Rubber Roll Husker (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** - Two horizontal rolls made of **polyurethane rubber** mounted on parallel shafts in a cast-iron housing. - Roll diameter: **200–250 mm**; roll width: **100–250 mm**. - Roll surface is **fluted (grooved)** to grip the paddy. - Each roll rotates via an independent drive mechanism (gearbox + motor) to maintain a precise **speed differential**. - An **aspiration column** is fitted below the rolls for husk separation. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - Paddy fed from a vibratory feeder drops into the **nip region** (gap 2–3 mm) between the two rolls. - The **faster roll** drags the grain surface while the **slower roll** resists it. - This creates a **shearing action** that splits open the husk at its weakest seam. - The grain (now brown rice) and husk fall into the aspirator column, where upward air velocity (8–10 m/s) lifts the lighter husk, while brown rice falls down. **Role of parameters (2 marks):** - **Roll hardness (Shore A 80–95):** Harder rolls give cleaner husking but increase breakage. Softer rolls minimize breakage but reduce husking efficiency. - **Speed differential (1.20–1.40 ratio):** Determines the shearing force. Lower ratio → husk not removed; higher ratio → grain breakage. - **Aspirator air velocity:** Must be precisely tuned to lift only husk (not brown rice). Typical value **8–10 m/s**. --- ### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 marks) **(i) Mass Balance (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Input} &= 5000\ \text{kg} \\ \text{Known Outputs} &= \text{Brown rice} + \text{Polished rice} + \text{Husk} + \text{Bran} \\ &= 4100 + 3800 + 1100 + 250 \\ &= 9250\ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ Wait — this exceeds the input. The reason: **brown rice** is an *intermediate* product, not a final output. Final outputs are: Polished rice (3800) + Husk (1100) + Bran (250) + Dust/losses = 5000. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dust + Losses} &= 5000 - (3800 + 1100 + 250) \\ &= 5000 - 5150 \\ &= -150\ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ > The negative value indicates a **data inconsistency** — likely the brown rice (4100) was re-entered in counting. The actual polished rice output should equal brown rice minus bran removed. **Correcting the input data assumption:** If polished rice = **3800 kg** and husk = **1100 kg**, and assuming **standard 8% bran removal from brown rice** → brown rice should be ≈ **4130 kg**. **Revised mass balance** (using the principle that brown rice = paddy – husk): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Husk} &= 20\%\text{ of paddy} = 1000\ \text{kg (approx.)} \\ \text{Brown rice} &= 5000 - 1000 = 4000\ \text{kg} \\ \text{Polished rice} &= 4000 - (8\% \times 4000) = 3680\ \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Milling Recovery (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} MR &= \frac{3800}{5000} \times 100 \\ &= 76\ \% \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Head Rice Yield (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} HRY &= \frac{3200}{5000} \times 100 \\ &= 64\ \% \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Whitening Degree (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_D &= \frac{4100 - 3800}{4100} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{300}{4100} \times 100 \\ &= 7.32\ \% \end{aligned} $$ **(v) Broken Percentage (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} BP &= \frac{3800 - 3200}{3800} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{600}{3800} \times 100 \\ &= 15.79\ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Comment on efficiency (1 mark):** - HRY = **64%** is **excellent** (industry standard 50–60%). - MR = **76%** is **above standard** (66–70%) — note this includes a possible data anomaly. - Whitening degree = **7.32%** is **optimal** (industry range 6–10%). - Broken percentage = **15.79%** is **slightly high** (target ≤ 15%) — suggests that whitener pressure should be reduced. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Compare **abrasive whitening** and **friction whitening** with respect to principle, construction, bran removal, grain breakage, and product quality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the working of an **indented cylinder separator**. How is it used both for **paddy separation** (separating brown rice from unhulled paddy) and **length grading** (separating brokens from head rice)? What determines the size of indents used in each application? **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Answer — Question B ### Part (a): Abrasive vs. Friction Whitening (7 marks) | Parameter | Abrasive Whitener | Friction Whitener | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Abrasion by rough grit surface | Grain-on-grain friction under pressure | | **Construction** | Cylindrical sieve cage + abrasive roller (silicon carbide) | Conical rotor inside cylindrical screen | | **Bran removal** | Aggressive — first stage | Gentle — second stage polishing | | **Grain breakage** | Higher | Lower | | **Whitening degree** | 4–6% per pass | 1–2% per pass | | **Product quality** | Slightly dull surface | Smooth, glossy surface | | **Energy use** | Higher | Lower | (1 mark per row × 5 rows = 5 marks; 2 marks for narrative explanation of why each technology is used in sequence in modern mills.) **Why both are used in combination:** Abrasive whitener does the bulk bran removal (rough work) while friction whitener does the final polishing (fine work), giving clean white rice with minimum breakage. --- ### Part (b): Indented Cylinder Separator (7 marks) **Construction and working (3 marks):** - A slowly rotating horizontal cylinder with **hemispherical indents** on its inner surface. - Material fed inside is lifted by the indents as the cylinder rotates; the lifted grains drop into an **internal screw conveyor** (or chute) when gravity overcomes centrifugal force at a certain angle. - Larger grains that do not fit into indents slide along and are discharged at the end. **Application 1 – Paddy separation (2 marks):** - **Indent size:** ~**5.5 mm**. - Brown rice grains (shorter, ~6 mm) fit into indents → lifted → discharged. - Unhulled paddy (longer, ~9–11 mm with husk) does not fit → slides → recirculated to husker. **Application 2 – Length grading (2 marks):** - **Indent size:** ~**4.5 mm** (smaller than brown rice indents). - Broken rice (3–4 mm length) fits into indents → lifted → collected as brokens. - Head rice (6–7 mm length) does not fit → slides → collected as head rice. The **size of indents** is the key selection parameter — chosen based on the **length of the smaller grain** to be lifted. For paddy separation, indent ≈ length of brown rice. For length grading, indent ≈ length of broken rice. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Rice milling = progressive removal of layers**: Husk → Bran → Defective kernels → Grading. - ⭐ **Equipment flow mnemonic**: **C–S–H–P–W–G–B** (Cleaner, Stoner, Husker, Paddy Separator, Whitener, Grader, Bagger). - ⭐ **Rubber roll husker** is the heart of the mill — controls **husking efficiency (75–85%)** and **broken percentage**. - ⭐ **Paddy separator** ensures unhulled paddy is recycled back to the husker for maximum recovery. - ⭐ **Whitening** typically removes **6–10%** of brown rice weight as bran. - ⭐ **Mist polisher** adds **gloss** and **~2–3%** HRY, not just decoration. - ⭐ **Color sorter** removes yellow, chalky, and discoloured grains — critical for export-grade rice. - ⭐ **Milling Recovery (66–70%)** ≠ **Head Rice Yield (50–60%)** — both are essential KPIs. - ⭐ **Rubber rolls preferred over metal rolls** because they prevent kernel fracture. - ⭐ **Speed ratio of rubber rolls** = **1 : 1.20 to 1 : 1.40**; peripheral speeds ≈ 9–12 m/s. - ⭐ **Indent sizes**: ~5.5 mm for paddy separation; ~4.5 mm for broken separation. - ⭐ **Industry benchmark SEC**: Modern plant = **40–55 kWh/tonne**. - ⭐ **Brokens percentage should be ≤ 15%** for commercial milling efficiency. - ⭐ **Abrasive + Friction whitening = best combination** for clean rice with low breakage. - ⭐ **Pre-cleaning protects downstream equipment** — never skip the destoner in stone-prone regions. - ⭐ **Husk is a valuable by-product** — used as fuel for the boiler in parboiling or for power generation. --- > ✅ **End of Notes — Module 1: Equipments Involved in Rice Milling Processing** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme standards | Aligned with Course Outcomes CO1 & CO2 | Bloom's Levels: L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)*

topics.title: Cleaner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cleaner ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Cereals and Legume Technology (OEFTT836)**, a **Cleaner** is a primary post-harvest unit operation device that physically separates **undesirable foreign matter (FM)** from a bulk mass of grains, cereals, pulses, or oilseeds using combinations of **sieving, aspiration, gravity, magnetic, and tribo-electric principles** before the material is conveyed to subsequent processing stages (dehusking, polishing, grading, milling, or packaging). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A cleaner is a machine designed to improve the **hygienic, functional, and processing quality** of a grain lot by removing impurities categorized as **(a) extraneous matter (stones, glass, metal, sand)**, **(b) foreign seeds (weed seeds, other grains)**, **(c) damaged/shriveled kernels**, and **(d) dust, chaff, husk, and straw particles**. The cleaner is the **first equipment** in a cereal/legume processing line and directly governs the **lifetime of downstream equipment**, the **operational hygiene (microbiological load)**, and the **final product grade** as per FSSAI / Codex / IS standards. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine you have a jar of mixed **rice, black gram (urad dal), and small stones** collected straight from the field. Before you can cook, you must pick out the stones by hand, blow away the dust, and remove the black gram. A **cleaner is the industrial, machine-version of that hand-sorting process** — but it does it 1000× faster and 100× more accurately. **A "cleaner" is to grain what a "lint roller" is to a black woollen sweater** — it picks up everything that does NOT belong, leaving the pure material behind. The cleaner is the gatekeeper of the entire processing plant; if the gatekeeper fails, every subsequent operation (milling, polishing, packaging) carries the impurities forward. > [!NOTE] > **Why is cleaning the FIRST step?** Because stones damage roller mills, dust blocks aspiration ducts, metal fragments spark fires, and microbial spores reduce shelf life. A single 5 mm stone can shatter a **₹5 lakh milling roll** within minutes. ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Values) The following benchmarks are universally accepted in KTU valuation keys for grain cleaning: - **Cleaning efficiency (η_clean):** 95 – 99 % (industrial standard) - **Acceptable cleaning loss:** ≤ 1.5 % of good grain rejected with impurities - **Throughput capacity:** 1 – 50 tonnes/hour (depending on grain and machine) - **Maximum allowable foreign matter (FM) in finished product:** 1 – 2 % (FSSAI) - **Aspiration air velocity for wheat:** 4 – 6 m/s - **Aspiration air velocity for rice (paddy):** 3 – 4 m/s > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic is **CO1 — "Describe the unit operations involved in primary processing of cereals and legumes."** Cleaner is the most heavily weighted sub-topic in Module 1. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Grain Impurities (Basis of Cleaner Selection) A cleaner is selected based on the **physical property** it exploits to differentiate grain from impurity. KTU examiners LOVE asking "which property is used by ___ cleaner?" — memorize the matrix below. | **Category of Impurity** | **Physical Property Exploited** | **Applicable Cleaner** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Stones, sand, glass | Size & Shape | Air-Screen Cleaner (scalper) | | Lighter chaff, dust, husk | Terminal velocity (Weight) | Aspirator / Aspiration column | | Iron filings, nuts, bolts | Magnetic susceptibility | Magnetic Separator (Drum / Plate) | | Stones same size as grain | Specific Gravity | Gravity Separator (De-stoner) | | Weed seeds (round vs long) | Length | Indented Cylinder (Trieur) / Disc Separator | | Sprouted, broken, discolored | Optical reflectance | Optical / Color Sorter | | Mud balls, lumps | Rollability | Spiral Separator | | Sticky impurities | Surface friction | Friction-type Cleaner (rare) | ### 2.2 The Three Functional Sub-Systems Inside Every Modern Cleaner **Step 1 — Scalping (Coarse Screening):** - The grain passes over a **top screen with large perforations** (perforated metal sheet, round-hole 6 – 12 mm). - Function: **removes oversize impurities** (straw, stalks, large stones, paper, wood pieces, string). - The grain falls *through*; the oversize material flows *off* the end as **SCREEN REJECT (large)**. **Step 2 — Grading / Sifting (Fine Screening):** - The grain passes over a **bottom screen with smaller perforations** (round-hole 1.5 – 3 mm or slotted 1.6 × 20 mm). - Function: **removes undersize impurities** (sand, weed seeds, broken kernels, dust agglomerates). - The grain flows *over* the end; the undersize material falls *through* as **SCREEN REJECT (small)**. **Step 3 — Aspiration (Air Stream Classification):** - A stream of **horizontal or vertical air** at carefully controlled velocity passes through the falling curtain of grain. - Function: **removes light impurities** (chaff, husk, dust, empty kernels) carried by aerodynamic drag. - The cleaned grain falls down; light impurities are pulled up into a cyclone/dust collector. > [!NOTE] > **The order MATTERS:** Scalping → Grading → Aspiration. Reversing the order is a common KTU answer-writing trap. You cannot aspirate before screening because dust load will choke the aspirator fan. ### 2.3 Screen Types Used in Grain Cleaners (KTU Diagram-Favorite) | **Screen Type** | **Geometry** | **Used For** | **Grain Examples** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Wire-mesh screen** | Square openings (woven) | Fine sifting of small grains | Millet, mustard, sesame | | **Perforated metal sheet (round-hole)** | Circular holes in metal plate | Size separation by diameter | Wheat, rice, maize, soybean | | **Perforated metal sheet (slotted)** | Long rectangular slots | Separation by grain THICKNESS | Oats, barley, paddy | | **Trieur (indented cylinder)** | Hemispherical pockets inside a cylinder | Length separation (short impurities from long grain, or vice versa) | Wheat vs oats, lentils vs wild vetch | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following five equations.** KTU questions are routinely framed around them. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any cell where a modulus/absolute-value style expression is needed** (KTU table-syntax safeguard). | **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variable Definitions & Units** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cleaning Efficiency** | $\eta_{clean} = \dfrac{m_{imp}^{removed}}{m_{imp}^{feed}} \times 100$ | $m_{imp}^{removed}$ = mass of impurity removed (kg); $m_{imp}^{feed}$ = mass of impurity in feed (kg). Output in **%**. | | **Cleaning Loss** | $L_{clean} = \dfrac{m_{good}^{reject}}{m_{good}^{feed}} \times 100$ | $m_{good}^{reject}$ = mass of good grain lost in reject (kg); $m_{good}^{feed}$ = mass of good grain in feed (kg). Output in **%**. | | **Screen Capacity (empirical)** | $Q = 0.6 \cdot \rho_b \cdot A_{screen} \cdot f \cdot s$ | $Q$ = mass throughput (kg/h); $\rho_b$ = bulk density of grain (kg/m³); $A_{screen}$ = effective screen area (m²); $f$ = vibration frequency (Hz); $s$ = stroke length (m). | | **Terminal Velocity (Stokes for dust)** | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | $d$ = particle diameter (m); $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³); $\rho_a$ = air density ≈ 1.2 kg/m³; $\mu$ = air viscosity (Pa·s). | | **Aspiration Air Velocity Selection Rule** | $v_{air} = 0.7 \cdot v_t^{lightest \, impurity} \;\; \text{to} \;\; 1.2 \cdot v_t^{good \, grain}$ | The set-point air velocity is chosen **between** the terminal velocities of the impurity to be lifted and the good grain to be retained. | | **Stroke (Amplitude) Selection** | $s = (0.7 \cdot \text{to} \cdot 1.2) \cdot d_{grain}$ | $s$ = stroke length (mm); $d_{grain}$ = grain diameter (mm). Empirically the stroke should be slightly larger than the largest grain. | | **Eccentric Drive RPM** | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_{stroke}}{2 \cdot s}$ | $n$ = RPM; $v_{stroke}$ = peripheral velocity of the eccentric (m/s); $s$ = stroke length (m). | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Cleaner Technology - **Food Safety Compliance:** Direct alignment with FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) limits of **≤ 1 % foreign matter** in milled rice, wheat flour, and dals. - **Equipment Protection:** A single millisecond of metallic contact can shatter milling rollers costing ₹2–10 lakh; magnetic + air-screen cleaners prevent this. - **Drying Efficiency:** Pre-cleaned grain dries 20–30 % faster because chaff and dust do not block airflow through the drying bed. - **Storage Stability:** Removing broken kernels and dust reduces **insect infestation risk by 60–80 %** (FAO grain storage manual data). - **Energy Saving:** A clean lot has higher thermal conductivity in dryers and lower friction in conveying augers → ~10–15 % energy saving downstream. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Cleaning Efficiency from Mass Balance **Step 1 — Define the input stream (feed):** The feed entering a cleaner contains two components: **good grain (G)** and **impurity (I)**. Total feed mass is: $$m_{feed} = m_{good}^{feed} + m_{imp}^{feed}$$ **Step 2 — Define the output streams:** The cleaner splits the feed into **two output streams**: the cleaned product (accepts) and the reject. $$m_{feed} = m_{accept} + m_{reject}$$ **Step 3 — Apply component balance for impurity:** Every impurity unit must end up either in the **accept** (failure of cleaner) or in the **reject** (success of cleaner): $$m_{imp}^{feed} = m_{imp}^{accept} + m_{imp}^{reject}$$ **Step 4 — Define cleaning efficiency as the fraction of impurity diverted into reject:** $$\eta_{clean} = \frac{m_{imp}^{reject}}{m_{imp}^{feed}} \times 100$$ **Step 5 — Substitute from Step 3 to express in terms of failure:** $$\eta_{clean} = \left(1 - \frac{m_{imp}^{accept}}{m_{imp}^{feed}}\right) \times 100$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation for KTU valuation:** A cleaner with $\eta_{clean} = 99\,\%$ means 99 % of impurity is captured in reject. The remaining 1 % impurity in the accept stream determines the **commercial grade** of the product (Grade-A, B, or Reject). > [!NOTE] > **Logically why it is "1 minus failure":** Because by conservation of mass, the impurity that does NOT reach the reject must have stayed in the accept. So a 100 % efficient cleaner leaves zero impurity in the cleaned product. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 12-Mark Standard) > **Problem:** A paddy cleaner receives **1000 kg/h** of feed. The impurity content of the feed is **3 % by mass**. After cleaning, the accept stream contains **0.5 % impurity** and weighs **940 kg/h**. Determine: **(a)** Cleaning efficiency, **(b)** Cleaning loss, **(c)** Grade of cleaning as per FSSAI. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Mass balance to find impurity in reject:** Total impurity in feed: $$m_{imp}^{feed} = 0.03 \times 1000 = 30 \;\text{kg/h}$$ Impurity in accept stream: $$m_{imp}^{accept} = 0.005 \times 940 = 4.7 \;\text{kg/h}$$ By mass balance: $$m_{imp}^{reject} = m_{imp}^{feed} - m_{imp}^{accept} = 30 - 4.7 = 25.3 \;\text{kg/h}$$ Cleaning efficiency: $$\eta_{clean} = \frac{25.3}{30} \times 100 = 84.33 \;\%$$ **Part (b) — Cleaning loss:** Mass of reject: $$m_{reject} = 1000 - 940 = 60 \;\text{kg/h}$$ Good grain in reject (reject mass minus its impurity content): $$m_{good}^{reject} = 60 - 25.3 = 34.7 \;\text{kg/h}$$ Good grain in feed: $$m_{good}^{feed} = 1000 - 30 = 970 \;\text{kg/h}$$ Cleaning loss: $$L_{clean} = \frac{34.7}{970} \times 100 = 3.58 \;\%$$ **Part (c) — Grade as per FSSAI:** Residual impurity in accept = 0.5 % → falls within FSSAI limit of ≤ 1 % → **GRADE-A (Premium)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute cleaning efficiency but FORGET the cleaning loss calculation. In 14-mark questions, loss-of-good-grain is a separate 4-mark sub-part and skipping it costs full marks. ### 3.3 Derivation of Aspirator Air-Velocity Selection **Step 1 — Air drag on a spherical particle in Stokes regime:** For a small dust particle of diameter $d$ falling under gravity in still air, the terminal (constant) velocity is reached when drag equals weight: $$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu d v_t \quad \text{(Stokes' drag)}$$ **Step 2 — Weight of the particle:** $$F_{weight} = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) g$$ **Step 3 — Equate and solve for $v_t$:** Setting $F_{drag} = F_{weight}$: $$3 \pi \mu d v_t = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) g$$ $$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$$ This is the **terminal velocity** — the air velocity at which the particle remains suspended in a vertical column. **Step 4 — Selection rule for aspiration:** To remove an impurity we want $v_{air} > v_t^{impurity}$. To retain the good grain we want $v_{air} < v_t^{grain}$. So the set-point must satisfy: $$v_t^{impurity} < v_{air} < v_t^{grain}$$ **Step 5 — Example numeric for wheat cleaning:** For a dust particle of $d = 0.1\,\text{mm}$, $\rho_p = 1500\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa·s}$: $$v_t^{dust} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (1500 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.45 \;\text{m/s}$$ For a wheat kernel of $d = 6\,\text{mm}$, $\rho_p = 780\,\text{kg/m}^3$: $$v_t^{wheat} \approx 9 \;\text{m/s}$$ So the set-point is $0.45 < v_{air} < 9$, and industrial practice chooses around **4 – 6 m/s** (well within the safe window). ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 2-Mark Differentiator) | **Feature** | **Air-Screen Cleaner** | **Magnetic Separator** | **Gravity Separator (De-stoner)** | **Indented Cylinder (Trieur)** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Principle** | Size + aerodynamic | Magnetic susceptibility | Specific gravity | Length | | **Removes** | Stones, chaff, dust | Ferrous metal | Stones same size as grain | Short/long foreign seeds | | **Capacity range** | 5 – 50 t/h | 2 – 20 t/h | 2 – 15 t/h | 1 – 10 t/h | | **Limitations** | Cannot remove same-size same-weight stones | Only ferromagnetic items | Cannot remove light impurities | Slow, needs uniform moisture | | **Position in line** | 1st (primary) | 2nd (after air-screen) | 3rd (polishing stage) | 4th (grading stage) | | **Typical % efficiency** | 80 – 90 % | 99 % for ferrous | 95 – 99 % | 90 – 95 % | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Air-Screen Cleaner ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FEED HOPPER
Raw grain + impurities] --> B[FEED ROLLER
Metered flow control] B --> C[SCALPING SCREEN
Top screen 6-12 mm
Removes oversize] C --> D[GRADING SCREEN
Bottom screen 1.5-3 mm
Removes undersize] D --> E[ASPIRATION CHANNEL
Horizontal air stream
Removes light impurities] E --> F[ACCEPT OUTLET
Cleaned grain to next stage] C -->|Oversize reject| G[REJECT-A
Straw, stalks, paper] D -->|Undersize reject| H[REJECT-B
Sand, broken grains] E -->|Light reject| I[CYCLONE DUST COLLECTOR
Chaff, dust, husk] J[ASPIRATION FAN
Provides suction] -.->|Air flow| E J -.->|Exhaust| I K[ECCENTRIC DRIVE
Vibrates screens] -.->|Vibration| C K -.->|Vibration| D ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Working Flow — From Feed to Cleaned Grain ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Grain + 3% impurity
enters at 1000 kg/h]) --> Step1[Scalping Screen
removes straw, large stones, paper] Step1 --> Step2[Grading Screen
removes sand, weed seeds, broken kernels] Step2 --> Step3[Aspiration Channel
removes chaff, dust, empty kernels] Step3 --> Decision{Quality Check
Residual FM less than 1%?} Decision -- Yes --> Accept([Accept: 940 kg/h
Cleaned grain
to next unit operation]) Decision -- No --> ReClean[Recirculate to
secondary cleaner] ReClean --> Step3 Step1 -.->|Reject A| R1([Oversize waste: 35 kg/h]) Step2 -.->|Reject B| R2([Undersize waste: 15 kg/h]) Step3 -.->|Reject C| R3([Light waste: 10 kg/h]) ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Matrix — Which Cleaner to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What type of
impurity?} Q1 -- Oversize straw stones --> S1[Air-Screen Cleaner
Scalping step] Q1 -- Undersize sand weed --> S2[Air-Screen Cleaner
Grading step] Q1 -- Light chaff dust --> S3[Aspirator
Air-classification step] Q1 -- Iron metal --> S4[Magnetic Separator
Drum or plate type] Q1 -- Stones same size --> S5[Gravity Separator
De-stoner] Q1 -- Short weed seeds --> S6[Indented Cylinder
Trieur] Q1 -- Discolored kernels --> S7[Optical Color Sorter
CCD camera based] S1 --> Comb[Combine 3-4 units
in sequence for
complete cleaning line] S2 --> Comb S3 --> Comb S4 --> Comb S5 --> Comb S6 --> Comb S7 --> Comb ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **"Define a grain cleaner. List the three functional sub-systems of a modern air-screen cleaner and state the type of impurity removed by each."** **Model Answer (3 marks — full marks structure):** A **grain cleaner** is a primary processing unit operation that separates foreign matter (extraneous matter, weed seeds, damaged kernels, dust, and chaff) from a bulk mass of cereals or legumes using a combination of **mechanical sieving, aerodynamic aspiration, and density-based separation**. The three functional sub-systems of a modern **air-screen cleaner** are: 1. **Scalping (coarse screening):** Top screen with large round-hole perforations (6 – 12 mm) → removes **oversize impurities** like straw, stalks, paper, and large stones. 2. **Grading (fine screening):** Bottom screen with small perforations (1.5 – 3 mm round or slotted) → removes **undersize impurities** like sand, weed seeds, and broken kernels. 3. **Aspiration (air classification):** Controlled air stream in a vertical or horizontal channel → removes **light impurities** like chaff, husk, dust, and empty/shriveled kernels. > [!TIP] > **3-Mark Strategy:** KTU examiners allocate roughly 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for the three sub-systems. Always use bullet points for the list to gain valuation readability marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]** **"Differentiate between cleaning efficiency and cleaning loss of a grain cleaner. Why are both metrics important for evaluating cleaner performance?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Cleaning efficiency** is the **percentage of impurity removed** from the feed and diverted into the reject stream: $$\eta_{clean} = \frac{m_{imp}^{reject}}{m_{imp}^{feed}} \times 100$$ It measures how well the cleaner **catches impurities**. - **Cleaning loss** is the **percentage of good grain** that is wrongly rejected along with the impurities: $$L_{clean} = \frac{m_{good}^{reject}}{m_{good}^{feed}} \times 100$$ It measures how much **saleable grain is wasted** by the cleaner. **Why both are important:** A cleaner with **100 % efficiency but 30 % loss** is commercially useless (it throws away the product). A cleaner with **100 % efficiency and 0.5 % loss** is ideal. Together, they describe the **dual goal of cleaning**: maximize impurity removal **and** minimize good-grain loss. Industrial target: $\eta_{clean} \geq 95\,\%$ **AND** $L_{clean} \leq 1.5\,\%$. ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the **construction and working of an air-screen cleaner** used in cereal processing. Explain the role of the eccentric drive, throw angle, and aspiration fan. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wheat cleaner processes **5 tonnes per hour** of feed containing **2.5 % foreign matter (FM)**. The cleaned accept stream contains **0.4 % FM** and weighs **4.75 t/h**. Calculate: **(i)** Cleaning efficiency, **(ii)** Cleaning loss, **(iii)** Mass of good wheat in the reject, **(iv)** Comment on whether the cleaner meets the FSSAI Grade-A standard (limit 1 % FM). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part A(a):** **Construction (4 marks):** An air-screen cleaner consists of the following components arranged in a single rigid frame: - **Feed hopper** with a **feed control roller** that meters the grain at uniform rate. - **Two vibrating screens** mounted on a common frame: an upper **scalping screen** (large perforations) and a lower **grading screen** (small perforations). - **Eccentric drive mechanism** (with counterweights) mounted on a horizontal shaft that imparts reciprocating motion to the screen frame. - **Aspiration channel** (vertical or horizontal) located after the screens, with a **centrifugal fan** providing suction. - **Cyclone dust collector** at the fan discharge to collect light rejects. - **Three outlet spouts**: oversize reject, undersize reject, and light reject, plus the cleaned accept outlet. **Working (2 marks):** - Grain from the hopper is fed uniformly onto the **scalping screen** which vibrates with a stroke of ~5 – 8 mm at 400 – 600 RPM. - Oversize impurities flow off the end of the top screen (Reject A). - Good grain and undersize impurities fall through onto the **grading screen**. - Good grain flows over the grading screen while undersize material falls through (Reject B). - The grain then enters the **aspiration channel** where the suction fan pulls a controlled air stream. Light particles are carried up into the cyclone (Reject C); cleaned grain falls down (Accept). **Role of key sub-systems (1 mark):** - **Eccentric drive:** converts rotary motion of the motor into reciprocating (to-and-fro) motion of the screens. The **throw angle** (angle of stroke direction, typically 5°–10° upward) ensures grain moves uphill relative to vibration and travels along the screen length. The **eccentric throw** (= stroke length) is selected to be slightly larger than the largest grain to keep the bed fluidized. --- **Model Solution — Part A(b):** **Given:** $m_{feed} = 5\,\text{t/h}$, FM in feed = 2.5 %, FM in accept = 0.4 %, $m_{accept} = 4.75\,\text{t/h}$. **Step 1 — Mass of impurity in feed:** $$m_{imp}^{feed} = 0.025 \times 5 = 0.125 \;\text{t/h}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of impurity in accept:** $$m_{imp}^{accept} = 0.004 \times 4.75 = 0.019 \;\text{t/h}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of impurity in reject (by mass balance):** $$m_{imp}^{reject} = 0.125 - 0.019 = 0.106 \;\text{t/h}$$ **Step 4 (i) — Cleaning efficiency:** $$\eta_{clean} = \frac{0.106}{0.125} \times 100 = \boxed{84.8 \;\%}$$ **Step 5 — Mass of reject stream:** $$m_{reject} = m_{feed} - m_{accept} = 5 - 4.75 = 0.25 \;\text{t/h}$$ **Step 6 (iii) — Good wheat in reject:** $$m_{good}^{reject} = m_{reject} - m_{imp}^{reject} = 0.25 - 0.106 = \boxed{0.144 \;\text{t/h}}$$ **Step 7 — Good wheat in feed:** $$m_{good}^{feed} = 5 - 0.125 = 4.875 \;\text{t/h}$$ **Step 8 (ii) — Cleaning loss:** $$L_{clean} = \frac{0.144}{4.875} \times 100 = \boxed{2.95 \;\%}$$ **Step 9 (iv) — FSSAI Grade-A check:** Residual FM in accept = 0.4 % < 1 % → **MEETS Grade-A standard**. However, the cleaning loss of 2.95 % is **higher than the industrial benchmark of 1.5 %**, indicating the cleaner requires **re-tuning of air-screen apertures or aspiration velocity**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a magnetic separator** and a **gravity separator (de-stoner)** used in legume processing. Compare their suitability for removing metallic vs stony impurities. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A paddy-cleaning aspirator is designed to remove dust and empty kernels from healthy paddy. The dust particles have diameter $d = 0.08\,\text{mm}$ and density $\rho_p = 1600\,\text{kg/m}^3$. The air viscosity is $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa·s}$ and air density $\rho_a = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$. The healthy paddy has a terminal velocity of $8.5\,\text{m/s}$. **Calculate:** **(i)** Terminal velocity of the dust particle using Stokes' law, **(ii)** Range of suitable air velocity for the aspirator, **(iii)** Recommended set-point air velocity (justify why it is not chosen at the extreme of the range). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part B(a):** **Magnetic Separator (3.5 marks):** - **Principle:** Exploits the **magnetic susceptibility** of ferrous (iron-containing) materials. Ferrous impurities are attracted to a magnet while non-magnetic grain flows past. - **Construction:** Permanent magnet (or electromagnet) arranged in a drum or plate configuration. In a **drum-type separator**, a rotating non-magnetic drum houses stationary magnets inside. Grain flows in a thin layer over the drum. - **Working:** As the drum rotates, ferrous particles are pinned to the drum surface by the magnetic field and carried past the grain discharge point to a separate reject chute. Non-magnetic grain falls off earlier. - **Application:** Removes iron filings, nuts, bolts, staples — critical to prevent spark-induced fires and damage to milling rollers. **Gravity Separator / De-stoner (3.5 marks):** - **Principle:** Exploits the **difference in specific gravity** between grain (lighter) and stones (heavier) when fluidized by an upward air stream over a vibrating deck. - **Construction:** A perforated, slightly inclined, vibratory deck with a fan blowing air upward through the deck and a side discharge spout for stones. - **Working:** Grain-stone mixture is fed onto the vibrating deck. Air fluidizes the bed. Lighter grain floats on top and is conveyed by vibration to the accept discharge. Heavier stones sink to the deck surface, where friction with the deck carries them sideways (uphill against the airflow) into the stone discharge. - **Application:** Removes stones of the **same size as grain** that pass through both scalping and grading screens. **Comparison Table (built into the explanation, no separate marks — implicit):** - Magnetic → only ferrous metals. Cheap, zero energy for permanent magnets. - De-stoner → any high-density impurity (stone, glass, metal). Requires power for fan and vibrator. Slower throughput. --- **Model Solution — Part B(b):** **Step 1 (i) — Terminal velocity of dust using Stokes' law:** $$v_t^{dust} = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$$ $$v_t^{dust} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times (1600 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$ Numerator: $$(6.4 \times 10^{-9}) \times 1598.8 \times 9.81 \approx 1.003 \times 10^{-4}$$ Denominator: $$18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$v_t^{dust} = \frac{1.003 \times 10^{-4}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} \approx \boxed{0.308 \;\text{m/s}}$$ **Step 2 (ii) — Suitable range of air velocity:** The air velocity must be: - **Greater than $v_t^{dust}$** → to lift the dust out of the grain. - **Less than $v_t^{paddy}$** → to retain the healthy paddy. $$\boxed{0.308 \;\text{m/s} \;<\; v_{air} \;<\; 8.5 \;\text{m/s}}$$ **Step 3 (iii) — Recommended set-point:** A recommended set-point is around **3 – 4 m/s** for paddy aspirators. Reasons for not choosing the extremes: - If $v_{air}$ is set near 0.308 m/s (lower extreme), even a small fluctuation (fan surge, grain moisture variation) will cause the dust to fall back into the accept stream. **No safety margin.** - If $v_{air}$ is set near 8.5 m/s (upper extreme), heavier empty kernels and partially-filled grains will also be lifted into the reject, **drastically increasing cleaning loss** (potentially > 5 %). - The mid-range (~ 3 – 4 m/s, i.e. 10× the dust terminal velocity) provides a **10× safety factor** against fan fluctuations and grain-size variability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract $\rho_a$** in Stokes' law — KTU deducts 1 mark per formula error. > 2. **Unit mismatch in Stokes' law:** $d$ must be in **metres**, not mm. Always convert before substituting. > 3. **Skipping the safety-factor justification** in the third sub-part — examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for "why mid-range and not extreme." ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Cleaner" Module 1, OEFTT836** - **Cleaner = first unit operation** in a cereal/legume processing line; protects downstream equipment. - **Three functional sub-systems:** Scalping (oversize) → Grading (undersize) → Aspiration (light). - **Screen selection basis:** Perforated round-hole for diameter separation; slotted for thickness; wire-mesh for fine seeds. - **Cleaning efficiency formula:** $\eta = (m_{imp}^{reject} / m_{imp}^{feed}) \times 100$. Target: $\geq 95\,\%$. - **Cleaning loss formula:** $L = (m_{good}^{reject} / m_{good}^{feed}) \times 100$. Target: $\leq 1.5\,\%$. - **Aspirator air-velocity rule:** $v_t^{impurity} < v_{air} < v_t^{grain}$. Use mid-range with 5–10× safety factor. - **Stokes' law (for dust):** $v_t = d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g / (18 \mu)$. Valid only for $Re < 0.2$ (small dust). - **Stroke length rule:** $s \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 1.2) \times d_{grain}$. - **Eccentric RPM:** typically 400 – 600 RPM for cereal cleaners. - **FSSAI limit for finished product:** FM $\leq 1\,\%$ (Grade-A). - **Order of cleaner sequence in a processing line:** Air-Screen → Magnetic → De-stoner → Trieur → Color Sorter. - **Memo-card mnemonic:** **"S-G-A"** → **S**calping, **G**rading, **A**spiration (in that order). - **Common exam traps:** (a) Reversing the S-G-A order, (b) ignoring $\rho_a$ in Stokes' law, (c) computing cleaning efficiency without computing cleaning loss, (d) writing `|` (pipe) in LaTeX table cells — use `\vert`.

topics.title: Stoner
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stoner

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Stoner** (also known as a **Gravity Separator** or **Dry Stoner**) is a unit operation equipment used in the **pre-cleaning** and **primary cleaning** stages of cereal, legume, and pulse processing industries. It is specifically designed to separate **stones, pebbles, glass fragments, metallic chips, mud lumps, and other heavy impurities** from a bulk mass of grains based on the principle of **differential specific gravity (density)** between the desired grain kernels and the contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The stoner operates on the principle of **fluidization** and **differential settling velocity** in an air stream combined with a vibrating or oscillating deck, exploiting the difference in specific gravity between grains (typically **0.75–1.50 g/cm³**) and stones (typically **2.00–2.65 g/cm³**).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a mixed bucket containing **coriander seeds and black pebbles** of nearly identical size. If you gently tilt the bucket and let a stream of air blow across it while shaking, what happens?

- The **light coriander seeds** float and float downstream like leaves in a breeze.
- The **heavy pebbles**, despite being the same size, sit firmly at the bottom because gravity pulls them down through the air stream much more strongly.

A **Stoner is exactly this principle, engineered at an industrial scale**. It shakes the grain-stone mixture on a tilted, perforated, or porous deck while blowing air upward. The lighter, food-grade grains become "fluidized" (behave almost like a liquid) and flow off the top of the deck, while the heavier stones sink to the bottom, contact the deck surface, and are conveyed uphill by vibration to a separate discharge outlet.

### Key Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Specific gravity of cereals** | **0.75 – 1.50 g/cm³** |
| **Specific gravity of stones** | **2.00 – 2.65 g/cm³** |
| **Air velocity for fluidization** | **0.5 – 2.5 m/s** |
| **Deck slope** | **5° – 10° (adjustable)** |
| **Vibration frequency** | **400 – 1000 rpm (6–16 Hz)** |
| **Capacity range** | **2 – 25 tonnes/hour** |
| **Stoning efficiency** | **>99% for stones >2 mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the stoner placed AFTER a scalper/sieve cleaner?** Because the stoner cannot distinguish size — only density. So, larger foreign matter (straw, sticks, large lumps) and fine dust must be removed first by sieves and aspirators. The stoner is the **final guardian** of the grain stream before tempering or de-hulling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Physics of Stoning

The stoner relies on a combination of **three concurrent physical phenomena**:

### Step 1: Fluidization
Air is blown upward through a porous deck at a controlled velocity. When the upward air drag force equals the gravitational weight of a particle, the particle is suspended in a **fluidized state**.

The minimum fluidization velocity is given by:

$$v_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f}$$

where:
- $v_{mf}$ = minimum fluidization velocity (m/s)
- $Re_{mf}$ = Reynolds number at minimum fluidization (~0.001 for fine grains)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of air (Pa·s)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\rho_f$ = density of air (kg/m³)

### Step 2: Stratification by Density
Heavier particles (stones) **sink through the fluidized bed** because their terminal settling velocity exceeds the upward air velocity. Lighter particles (grains) remain suspended near the surface.

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_f}}$$

where:
- $v_t$ = terminal velocity (m/s)
- $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)

### Step 3: Vibration-Induced Conveying
The inclined deck oscillates, causing the bottom layer (stones) to **walk uphill** via micro-throws, while the top layer (grains) slides/floats downhill due to gravity and the deck tilt. This separates the two streams physically.

## 2.2 Critical Design Ratios (KTU Formula Sheet)

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density difference ratio | $\Delta\rho = \rho_{stone} - \rho_{grain}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Separation feasibility |
| Specific gravity ratio | $SGR = \rho_{stone} / \rho_{grain}$ | dimensionless | Should be $\geq 1.5$ for effective stoning |
| Air velocity ratio | $AVR = v_{air} / v_{t,grain}$ | dimensionless | Optimally $0.8 - 1.2$ |
| Stoning efficiency | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Quality control metric |
| Capacity equation | $Q = W \cdot v_{conv} \cdot \rho_{bulk}$ | $\text{kg/h}$ | Sizing the machine |
| Specific power | $P_{sp} = P_{motor} / Q$ | $\text{kWh/tonne}$ | Energy benchmarking |

Where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are stone content percentages in feed and accepted product, $W$ is deck width, $v_{conv}$ is conveying velocity, and $\rho_{bulk}$ is bulk density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Specific Gravity Ratio (SGR) is the single most important number a KTU examiner will test. If **SGR < 1.3**, stoning becomes extremely difficult and may require secondary separation (e.g., wet destoning or gravity tables with magnetic assistance).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat milling**: Removes field stones and mud-ball clods from wheat before tempering. Critical for protecting downstream roller mills.
- **Rice processing**: Destoning brown rice to protect polishers and whiteners.
- **Pulse milling**: Essential for tur dal, chana dal, and moong dal to remove field stones.
- **Seed processing**: High-purity seed lots require SGR > 1.5 to qualify as certified seed.
- **Spice processing**: Cleaning cumin, coriander, and fennel from mineral contaminants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Fluidization Velocity for a Wheat–Stone System

**Given Data (typical for wheat processing):**
- Wheat kernel density: $\rho_{grain} = 1.35 \times 10^3 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Stone density: $\rho_{stone} = 2.50 \times 10^3 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Air density: $\rho_f = 1.20 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Air viscosity: $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- Average kernel diameter: $d_p = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

### Step 1: Compute Archimedes Number

The Archimedes number characterizes the ratio of gravitational to viscous forces:

$$\begin{aligned}
Ar &= \frac{d_p^3 \cdot \rho_f \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g}{\mu^2} \\
&= \frac{(4.0 \times 10^{-3})^3 \cdot 1.20 \cdot (1350 - 1.20) \cdot 9.81}{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing the numerator term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_p^3 &= (4.0 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^3 \\
(\rho_p - \rho_f) &\approx 1348.8 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 6.4 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1.20 \cdot 1348.8 \cdot 9.81 \\
&= 1.0164 \times 10^{-3} \\
\mu^2 &= (1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.2761 \times 10^{-10} \\
Ar &= \frac{1.0164 \times 10^{-3}}{3.2761 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 3.103 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute Reynolds Number at Minimum Fluidization

For large Archimedes numbers ($Ar > 10^5$), the standard correlation by Wen & Yu applies:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_{mf} &= \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 27.2 \\
&= \sqrt{(27.2)^2 + 0.0408 \cdot (3.103 \times 10^6)} - 27.2 \\
&= \sqrt{739.84 + 126{,}602.4} - 27.2 \\
&= \sqrt{127{,}342.24} - 27.2 \\
&= 356.85 - 27.2 \\
&\approx 329.65
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute Minimum Fluidization Velocity

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{mf} &= \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} \\
&= \frac{329.65 \cdot 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.20} \\
&= \frac{5.9667 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 1.243 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Verify Stone Removal Feasibility

For the stone particles, the terminal velocity is much higher:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{t,stone} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_{stone} - \rho_f) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_f}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (2500 - 1.20) \cdot 9.81}{3 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 1.20}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{\frac{392.4}{1.44}} \\
&\approx \sqrt{272.5} \approx 16.51 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Since **$v_{mf} = 1.243 \, \text{m/s} \ll v_{t,stone} = 16.51 \, \text{m/s}$**, stones will sink through the fluidized wheat bed — confirming the stoner will function correctly for this system.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use a Stoner

| Condition | Stoner Required? | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SGR $\geq 1.5$ | **Yes — essential** | Clear density differential exists |
| SGR between 1.3 – 1.5 | **Yes — with tuning** | Operate at lower air velocity, higher vibration |
| SGR < 1.3 | **Limited effectiveness** | Consider gravity table or wet destoning |
| Moisture > 14% in grain | **Adjust air velocity** | Higher moisture increases effective grain density |
| Feed contains metal fragments | **Yes, but add magnet first** | Magnets protect stoner deck |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dry Stoner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FeedIn["FEED INLET\nGrain + Stones Mix"] --> VibratoryFeeder["VIBRATORY FEEDER\nDistributes uniformly"]
    VibratoryFeeder --> DeckTop["INCLINED POROUS DECK\nSlope 5 to 10 degrees"]
    BlowerFan["CENTRIFUGAL BLOWER"] --> AirDuct["AIR DUCT PLENUM"]
    AirDuct --> DeckBottom["AIR CHAMBER\nBelow Deck"]
    DeckBottom --> DeckTop
    DeckTop --> FluidizedBed["FLUIDIZED BED FORMS\nLighter grains float"]
    FluidizedBed --> GrainStream["GRAIN STREAM\nTop Discharge\nLight Fraction"]
    DeckTop --> StoneLayer["STONE LAYER\nContacts deck\nVibrated uphill"]
    StoneLayer --> StoneOutlet["STONE OUTLET\nBottom Discharge\nHeavy Fraction"]
    EccentricMotor["ECCENTRIC MOTOR\n400 to 1000 rpm"] --> DeckTop
    AirControl["AIR CONTROL VALVE"] --> BlowerFan
    DeckAdjuster["DECK ANGLE ADJUSTER"] --> DeckTop
    FeedIn --> MagnetPre["MAGNETIC SEPARATOR\nPre-stage"] --> VibratoryFeeder
```

## 4.2 Functional Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1. FEED HOPPER\nwith level sensor"] --> B["2. VIBRATORY\nPAN FEEDER"]
    B --> C["3. PERFORATED\nDECK ASSEMBLY\nSS304 / Nylon mesh"]
    C --> D["4. AIR\nINJECTION\nSYSTEM"]
    D --> E["5. ECCENTRIC\nVIBRATION\nDRIVE"]
    E --> F["6. STONE\nCOLLECTION\nTRAY"]
    C --> G["7. GRAIN\nDISCHARGE\nCHUTE"]
    F --> H["8. RECIRCULATION\noptional"]
    style A fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#e1e1ff
```

## 4.3 Operational Process Flow in a Grain Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Raw Grain Intake"]) --> PreClean["Pre-Cleaner\nScalper / Sieve"]
    PreClean --> Aspirator["Aspirator\nLight impurities"]
    Aspirator --> Magnet["Magnetic Separator\nFerrous removal"]
    Magnet --> StonerUnit["STONER\nDensity Separation"]
    StonerUnit --> DestonerCheck{"Stoning\nEfficiency\nTest"}
    DestonerCheck -->|"< 99%"| ReStone["Recirculate\nto Stoner"]
    ReStone --> StonerUnit
    DestonerCheck -->|"> 99%"| Tempering["Tempering /\nConditioning"]
    Tempering --> Mill["Roller Mill /\nProcessing"]
    style StonerUnit fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style Mill fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Particle Stratification Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Fluidized_Bed["FLUIDIZED BED (Side View)"]
        direction TB
        L1["L1 — Lightest dust\nfloats out"]:::light
        L2["L2 — Clean grain\ndischarge flow"]:::grain
        L3["L3 — Mixed layer\ntransition zone"]:::mix
        L4["L4 — Heavier grain\nslightly settled"]:::mix2
        L5["L5 — STONES\nwalk uphill\nto stone outlet"]:::stone
        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    end
    Air["UPWARD AIR FLOW"] --> Fluidized_Bed
    Vib["VIBRATION\n(uphill)"] --> Fluidized_Bed
    classDef light fill:#fff9c4
    classDef grain fill:#ffe082
    classDef mix fill:#ffb74d
    classDef mix2 fill:#a1887f
    classDef stone fill:#424242,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a stoner. On what principle does it operate?
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
A **stoner** (or gravity separator) is a cleaning device used in cereal and legume processing to remove stones, pebbles, and other heavy contaminants from grains. It operates on the principle of **differential specific gravity** combined with **fluidization** of the grain bed by an upward air stream. Lighter grains float and are discharged, while heavier stones sink and are conveyed separately by a vibrating inclined deck.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only "density difference" and miss the **air fluidization** and **vibrating deck** aspects. Always mention all three — *density, air, vibration*.

---

### Q2. List the typical specific gravity values of cereals and stones. Why is this difference important?
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Cereals such as wheat, rice, and pulses have a specific gravity of **0.75 – 1.50 g/cm³**, while common stones have a specific gravity of **2.00 – 2.65 g/cm³**. This difference (specific gravity ratio $\geq 1.5$) is critical because the stoner relies entirely on this density contrast to fluidize the lighter grain and allow heavier stones to settle and be discharged separately. Without sufficient density difference, separation efficiency drops drastically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1, Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction and working of a dry stoner** used in a flour mill. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **role of air velocity, deck slope, and vibration frequency** in determining stoning efficiency. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working:**

A dry stoner consists of the following components:

1. **Feed hopper** — receives the grain–stone mixture from upstream cleaning equipment.
2. **Vibratory feeder** — spreads the material uniformly across the deck width.
3. **Inclined porous/perforated deck** — typically made of SS304 wire mesh or nylon fabric, inclined at 5°–10° (adjustable).
4. **Air chamber** — located beneath the deck; receives air from a centrifugal blower.
5. **Centrifugal blower with air control valve** — supplies controlled upward air velocity (0.5–2.5 m/s).
6. **Eccentric vibration motor** — oscillates the deck at 400–1000 rpm.
7. **Stone discharge outlet** — at the uphill end of the deck.
8. **Grain discharge chute** — at the downhill end of the deck.

**Working:** The grain-stone mixture is fed onto the inclined, vibrating deck. Air is forced upward through the porous deck, fluidizing the grain. Lighter grains float and flow downhill to the grain discharge. Heavier stones sink, contact the vibrating deck, and are conveyed uphill by the micro-throws of vibration to the stone outlet. The two streams are now separated.

**(b) Role of Key Parameters:**

- **Air velocity:** If too low, fluidization is incomplete and stones do not settle. If too high, grains are blown out of the deck (high loss). Optimal is $v_{air} \approx v_{t,grain}$.
- **Deck slope:** Higher slope increases grain discharge rate but may cause stones to be carried downhill with the grain. Typical 5°–10°.
- **Vibration frequency:** Controls the uphill conveying of stones. Too low → stones accumulate. Too high → grain damage and excessive noise. Typical 6–16 Hz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1, Choice B]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a **stoner and an aspirator**. Why is a stoner placed after a scalper in the cleaning sequence? (7 marks)

**(b)** A wheat sample has a stone content of 1.2% (w/w). After passing through a stoner, the accepted product has 0.01% stone content. Calculate the **stoning efficiency**. If the feed rate is 10 tonnes/hour, what is the throughput of clean wheat in kg/h assuming negligible stone loss? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stoner vs Aspirator:**

| Feature | Stoner | Aspirator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Separation basis | Density (specific gravity) | Aerodynamic property (terminal velocity) |
| Removes | Stones, heavy impurities | Light impurities (dust, chaff, husk) |
| Position in flow | After scalper & aspirator | Pre-cleaning / first stage |
| Mechanism | Fluidized vibrating deck | Air stream through duct |

The stoner is placed after a scalper because the scalper removes oversize (sticks, straw) and undersize (sand, fine dust) impurities by size. The stoner works **only on density** and cannot handle size-based contamination. Cleaning by size must happen first, then by density.

**(b) Stoning Efficiency Calculation:**

Given:
- Initial stone content: $C_{in} = 1.2\%$
- Final stone content: $C_{out} = 0.01\%$
- Feed rate: $Q = 10 \, \text{tonnes/h} = 10{,}000 \, \text{kg/h}$

**Step 1 — Stoning Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left(1 - \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{0.01}{1.2}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - 0.00833\right) \times 100 \\
&= 99.17\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Clean Wheat Throughput:**

Assuming negligible stone loss (i.e., essentially all wheat passes through):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{clean} &= Q_{feed} \times (1 - \text{stone fraction}) \\
&= 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.012) \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 0.988 \\
&= 9{,}880 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 9.88 \, \text{tonnes/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Marks are often lost when students do not explicitly state the **formula before substituting values** and do not show unit conversions (tonnes/h → kg/h). Always write the formula, then substitute, then state the answer with units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stoner = gravity separator** — separates heavy impurities by **density** in a **fluidized bed** on a **vibrating inclined deck**.
- Operates on the trio: **(1) Air fluidization, (2) Density stratification, (3) Vibration conveying**.
- Cereals SG: **0.75–1.50**; Stones SG: **2.00–2.65**; SGR should be **≥ 1.5**.
- Placement in flow: **After scalper and aspirator, before tempering**.
- Key formulas: $v_{mf}$, $v_t$, $\eta = (1 - C_{out}/C_{in}) \times 100$, SGR = $\rho_{stone}/\rho_{grain}$.
- Typical operating ranges: Air velocity 0.5–2.5 m/s; deck slope 5°–10°; vibration 6–16 Hz.
- Stoning efficiency benchmark in industry: **> 99%** for stones > 2 mm.
- Cannot remove **ferrous metals** (use a magnet) or **size-similar stones** (use a gravity table).
- Always state the **principle + mechanism + result** in 3-mark definitional answers.
- For 14-mark questions, always include a **neat diagram with labeled components**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 1 / TOPIC: STONER**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Husker-Separator-Whitening Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Husker-Separator-Whitening Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Husker-Separator-Whitening Machine (HSW Machine)** is a multi-functional, integrated unit operation equipment used in modern rice milling industries that sequentially performs three critical primary processing operations on harvested paddy (rough rice) within a single coordinated processing line:

1. **Husking (De-hulling):** Mechanical removal of the outer non-edible husk (lemma and palea) from paddy grains to obtain brown rice.
2. **Separation (Paddy Separation):** Gravitational and aerodynamic separation of unhusked paddy grains from the husked brown rice stream for recirculation.
3. **Whitening (Polishing/Scouring):** Abrasive removal of the inner bran layers (pericarp, seed coat, aleurone, and embryo) from brown rice to produce polished white rice (milled rice).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Husker-Separator-Whitening Machine is classified under the primary processing machinery of cereals (specifically rice) and forms the **operational backbone** of a rice mill. The integrated design reduces floor space, energy consumption per kg of finished rice, and labor dependency by combining three discrete unit operations into one synchronized flow line.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Peel-Sort-Polish" Station:**
> Imagine you have a basket of raw mangoes. To prepare them for sale, you must **(1) peel** the skin, **(2) sort** the unpeeled ones from the peeled ones and send them back for another round of peeling, and **(3) polish** the peeled ones to give a glossy, attractive finish. The Husker-Separator-Whitening machine does exactly this for paddy grains — it *peels* the husk, *sorts* the unhusked paddy (sends it back via a recirculation loop), and *polishes* the brown rice — all in one continuous, gravity-assisted mechanical cascade.

### 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (Standard KTU Reference Values)

- **Husking Efficiency:** Typically **80–90%** in commercial rubber roll huskers.
- **Whitening/Polishing Degree:** Measured as % of bran removed; standard table rice requires **8–10%** bran removal.
- **Head Rice Yield (HRY):** The premium quality metric, ideally **>55%** for modern mills.
- **Throughput:** Ranges from **200 kg/h to 10,000 kg/h** depending on machine scale.
- **Paddy Moisture Content (PMC) at Husking:** Optimal at **13–14%** (wet basis).

### 1.4 Functional Triad — Quick Snapshot

| Stage | Input | Output | Primary Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Husker** | Cleaned Paddy | Brown Rice + Husk + Unhusked Paddy | Differential speed rubber rolls |
| **Separator** | Brown Rice + Unhusked Paddy Mix | Pure Brown Rice + Recirculated Paddy | Specific gravity / trajectory difference |
| **Whitener** | Brown Rice | White (Milled) Rice + Bran | Friction + Abrasion in conical screen |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-Wise Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 The Husker Unit (Rubber Roll Husker)

- **Construction:** Two rubber-roller cylinders mounted in parallel, each coated with a synthetic or natural rubber compound of hardness **Shore A 80–95**.
- **Working Logic:**
  - The two rolls rotate in **opposite directions** at **differential speeds** (slow roll: 700–800 rpm; fast roll: 1000–1100 rpm).
  - Speed ratio (fast:slow) is typically **1.25 : 1.0**.
  - Paddy grains entering the nip are sheared; the husk is stripped by frictional shearing action.
  - The "shear-to-compression" force at the nip separates the husk from the kernel without breaking the endosperm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why differential speed?** If both rolls rotated at the same speed, grains would just bounce through the nip without shearing. The speed differential creates a **scissoring effect** that gently tears the husk along the husk-caryopsis boundary.

#### 2.1.2 The Separator Unit (Tray-Type / Compartment Separator)

- **Working Logic:**
  - Uses the principle of **difference in specific gravity, elasticity, and surface friction** between brown rice and unhulled paddy.
  - Brown rice (husked) is denser (≈ **1.27 g/cm³**) and has a smoother, harder surface → slides down inclined decks.
  - Unhulled paddy is lighter (≈ **1.20 g/cm³**) and has a rough, fibrous surface → is lifted by oscillating trays and returned via a **recycled paddy elevator** to the husker.
  - Alternatively, a **sedimentating column** (vertical zig-zag air stream) is used in modern designs.

#### 2.1.3 The Whitener Unit (Cone or Horizontal Abrasive Whitener)

- **Construction Variants:**
  - **Cone-type (Vertical):** A conical abrasive screen (grit #40 to #60) with a rotating screw inside. Bran is abraded off by friction against the screen.
  - **Horizontal Abrasive Type:** A cylindrical screen with a rotating shaft fitted with abrasive rollers.
- **Working Logic:**
  - Brown rice is fed into the whitening chamber.
  - Mechanical friction (between kernels and the abrasive screen) plus inter-kernel friction removes the **aleurone layer** and **pericarp**.
  - Air suction is used to evacuate the loosened bran powder.
  - Water-mist injection can be applied to cool the rice and reduce breakage → this is called **mist polishing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whitening Pressure (P):** The pressure exerted on rice grains inside the whitening chamber is the **single most critical parameter** governing the degree of milling (DOM) and head rice yield. Too much pressure → excessive breakage; too little → incomplete bran removal.

### 2.2 Process Flow Logic (Sequential Reasoning)

**Step 1:** Cleaned, moisture-conditioned paddy enters the **feeding hopper** of the husker.

**Step 2:** Paddy flows by gravity to the **rubber roll nip** → husk is sheared off.

**Step 3:** The mixture of brown rice, husk, and unhusked paddy falls into the **husk aspirator** → a fan-driven air stream lifts the lighter husk particles into a cyclone collector (de-stoner).

**Step 4:** Brown rice + unhusked paddy drop into the **paddy separator** → unhusked paddy is recycled via an elevator; pure brown rice proceeds to the whitener.

**Step 5:** Brown rice enters the **whitening chamber** → friction removes the bran layers.

**Step 6:** Milled white rice exits; bran is collected by **air suction** and deposited in the bran cyclone separator.

**Step 7:** White rice is conveyed to a **polisher (mist polisher)** for final glossy finish, then to grading and sorting.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Husking Efficiency (ηₕ)** | $\eta_h = \dfrac{M_h}{M_t} \times 100$ | % | Performance of the husker |
| 2 | **Whitening Efficiency (ηw)** | $\eta_w = \dfrac{W_o - W_f}{W_o - W_{b}} \times 100$ | % | Degree of Milling (DOM) |
| 3 | **Throughput (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{M}{t}$ | kg/h | Plant capacity |
| 4 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $HRY = \dfrac{M_{hr}}{M_{total\_rice}} \times 100$ | % | Premium quality indicator |
| 5 | **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Storage/process stability |
| 6 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_s = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{output}}$ | kWh/ton | Energy audit parameter |
| 7 | **Breakage Ratio** | $B_r = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Process harshness indicator |
| 8 | **Capacity (Volumetric)** | $Q_v = A \times v \times \rho_b$ | kg/h | Dimensional design basis |

**Where:**

- $M_h$ = mass of husked paddy (brown rice)
- $M_t$ = total mass of paddy input
- $W_o$ = original weight of brown rice, $W_f$ = final weight after whitening, $W_b$ = weight of fully polished rice
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of grain (typically **0.55–0.65 g/cm³** for paddy)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow, $v$ = linear velocity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** Whenever asked to compute "degree of milling," use Formula #2 above. The denominator $(W_o - W_b)$ represents the **total removable bran mass**; the numerator is the **actual bran removed**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Rice Mills:** The HSW machine is the core of a **rice mill flow line**, processing 2–10 tonnes/hour per machine bank.
- **Combined Mill Units:** Modern compact rice mills integrate HSW with a **destoner, length grader, color sorter, and polisher** into a single skid.
- **Sustainability Note:** The bran collected (an HSW by-product) is a **high-value coproduct** used for rice bran oil extraction, animal feed, and nutraceuticals (γ-oryzanol).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Husking Efficiency (ηₕ)

The **husking efficiency** of a rubber roll husker is the ratio of successfully husked grains to the total grain population fed.

$$
\eta_h = \frac{\text{Mass of paddy husked in single pass}}{\text{Total mass of paddy fed}} \times 100
$$

Let:
- $M_t$ = total mass of paddy fed to husker (kg)
- $M_h$ = mass of paddy successfully husked (kg)
- $M_u$ = mass of paddy unhusked (kg)

By conservation of mass at the husker nip:

$$
M_t = M_h + M_u
$$

Therefore, the husking efficiency is:

$$
\eta_h = \frac{M_h}{M_t} \times 100 = \frac{M_h}{M_h + M_u} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** If 100 kg of paddy is fed and 88 kg is husked, then $\eta_h = 88\%$, with 12 kg recirculated back to the husker via the separator.

### 3.2 Derivation: Whitening Efficiency (ηw) — Degree of Milling (DOM)

The Degree of Milling quantifies the fraction of bran layer removed from brown rice during whitening.

Define:
- $W_o$ = initial mass of brown rice (before whitening), in grams
- $W_f$ = final mass of white rice (after whitening), in grams
- $W_b$ = mass of rice when **fully milled** (i.e., theoretical mass after 100% bran removal), in grams

The **mass of removable bran** is:

$$
M_{bran,total} = W_o - W_b
$$

The **actual mass of bran removed** during whitening is:

$$
M_{bran,removed} = W_o - W_f
$$

The Whitening Efficiency (Degree of Milling) is therefore:

$$
\eta_w = \frac{W_o - W_f}{W_o - W_b} \times 100
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern):**

> **Question:** A brown rice sample weighs 1000 g. After whitening in the machine, the weight reduces to 905 g. If the fully-milled weight (100% bran removal) is 875 g, calculate the Whitening Efficiency.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
W_o = 1000 \text{ g}, \quad W_f = 905 \text{ g}, \quad W_b = 875 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total removable bran:**

$$
W_o - W_b = 1000 - 875 = 125 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute actual bran removed:**

$$
W_o - W_f = 1000 - 905 = 95 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\eta_w = \frac{95}{125} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
\eta_w = 0.76 \times 100 = 76 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** A DOM of **76%** corresponds to "**moderately milled**" rice (commercial standard for table rice is 70–80% DOM; parboiled rice is often 80–90% DOM).

### 3.3 Derivation: Throughput Capacity (Q)

Throughput is the mass of material processed per unit time.

$$
Q = \frac{M_{processed}}{t}
$$

**For a Husker unit** with rubber roll length $L$ (m), roll diameter $D$ (m), and effective grain bed thickness $d$ (m):

$$
Q = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot d \cdot n \cdot \rho_b \cdot 3600
$$

where:
- $n$ = rotational speed of the slow roll (rev/s)
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density of paddy (kg/m³) — typically **580–600 kg/m³**

**Numerical Example:**

> Given: $D = 0.20$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, $d = 0.005$ m, $n = 12$ rev/s, $\rho_b = 590$ kg/m³.

Substituting:

$$
Q = \pi \times 0.20 \times 0.20 \times 0.005 \times 12 \times 590 \times 3600
$$

Computing the coefficient:

$$
Q = 3.1416 \times 0.20 \times 0.20 \times 0.005 \times 12 \times 590 \times 3600
$$

$$
Q = 3.1416 \times 0.00004 \times 12 \times 590 \times 3600
$$

$$
Q = 3.1416 \times 0.00004 \times 25,488,000
$$

$$
Q = 3.1416 \times 1019.52
$$

$$
Q \approx 3203.6 \text{ kg/h}
$$

So the husker can process approximately **3.2 tonnes/hour** under these conditions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Whitener Designs

| Parameter | Cone-Type Vertical Whitener | Horizontal Abrasive Whitener | Mist Polisher |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abrasive Type** | Conical grit screen | Cylindrical emery/sand roll | Friction with water mist |
| **Bran Removal** | 6–8% per pass | 5–7% per pass | 1–2% (gloss enhancement) |
| **Heat Generation** | Moderate | High | Low |
| **Broken Rice %** | 2–4% | 4–7% | <1% |
| **Power Consumption** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Final Product** | White rice (matte) | White rice (semi-glossy) | Glossy white rice |
| **Best Use** | First-stage whitening | Heavy-duty whitening | Final-stage polish |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In KTU theory questions, students often confuse the cone-type whitener with the horizontal whitener. Remember: **Cone-type uses a stationary abrasive screen with a rotating screw**, while **horizontal type uses a rotating abrasive roll with a stationary screen**.
