- **Phase 3 (Curing):** 80–110 °C, develops color/flavor (Maillard).
- Final malt moisture: **3.0–4.5%**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flour Extraction Rate** | $E = \frac{M_{\text{flour}}}{M_{\text{wheat}}} \times 100$ | % | Mill yield optimization |
| **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $M_{wb} = \frac{W_{\text{water}}}{W_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Process control |
| **Dry Matter** | $DM = 100 - M_{wb}$ | % | Mass balance calculations |
| **Malt Extract (Congress Mash)** | $E_{malt} = \frac{m_{\text{dissolved solids}}}{m_{\text{malt}}} \times 100$ | % (typical 80–82%) | Brewhouse yield |
| **Break Release (BR)** | $BR = \frac{\text{Mass through 20W sieve}}{\text{Total mass fed to break 1}} \times 100$ | % (target 15–25%) | Milling performance KPI |
| **Friability** | $F = \frac{\text{Mass through 1.0 mm screen}}{\text{Total mass}} \times 100$ | % (target >85%) | Malt modification index |
| **Steep Water Uptake (Fick's 1st Law)** | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Steeping design |
| **Drying Rate (constant period)** | $\frac{dX}{dt} = -k(X - X^*)$ | dimensionless | Kiln design |
| **Respiration Loss** | $L_r = \frac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0} \times 100$ | % (5–7%) | Germination control |
| **Ash Content (flour spec)** | $A = \frac{m_{\text{ash}}}{m_{\text{flour, db}}} \times 100$ | % (0.50–0.65%) | Refining efficiency indicator |
| **Protein Content** | $P = \%N \times 5.7$ (wheat factor) | % (8–15%) | End-use suitability |
| **Falling Number (Hagberg)** | $FN = t_{\text{fall}}$ (s) | seconds (250–350) | α-amylase activity / sprout damage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use of these formulas:** In a real commercial mill/brewery, these metrics are tracked in real time on SCADA dashboards. For example, a shift miller might adjust **roll gap** or **feed rate** if **break release** drifts above 25% (over-reduction, bran contamination) or below 15% (under-extraction, economic loss).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat milling** sits at the core of the global baking industry (≈700 million tonnes/year). Process engineers use the formulas above to optimize flour yield without compromising ash/protein specs.
- **Barley malting** is the upstream process of brewing (≈1.85 billion hL beer/year globally). The Friability/Falling Number/Ethanol Extract triad defines malt quality contracts worth billions of dollars.
- Food safety: controlling **Falling Number** avoids sprout-damaged wheat, which produces gummy, sticky doughs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Balance Across the Milling Break System

Consider a wheat mill with input stream $M_{in}$ (cleaned, tempered wheat) and output streams $\{M_{flour}, M_{bran}, M_{germ}, M_{shorts}\}$.

### Step 1: Total Mass Balance (Conservation)

By conservation of mass:

$$M_{in} = M_{flour} + M_{bran} + M_{germ} + M_{shorts} + M_{loss}$$

> **Logic:** Whatever enters the mill must leave as one of the product streams plus inevitable losses (dust, evaporation, respiration — typically 0.5–1.0% in milling).

### Step 2: Dry-Matter Mass Balance

To remove the effect of varying moisture, we convert to dry matter using $DM_i = m_i(1 - M_{wb,i})$:

$$DM_{in} = DM_{flour} + DM_{bran} + DM_{germ} + DM_{shorts} + DM_{loss}$$

### Step 3: Ash Component Balance (Indicator of Refining)

Since ash is concentrated in the bran (≈5–7%) and germ (≈5%) but very low in endosperm (≈0.4%), tracking ash is a powerful proxy for refining:

$$DM_{in} \cdot A_{in} = DM_{flour} \cdot A_{flour} + DM_{bran} \cdot A_{bran} + DM_{germ} \cdot A_{germ}$$

Where $A_i$ is the ash fraction (decimal).

### Step 4: Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a mill processes **1000 kg wheat** at 14% moisture (DM = 860 kg), producing **720 kg flour** at 14% moisture with **0.55% ash** (d.b.). The ash in flour is:

$$DM_{flour} = 720 \times (1 - 0.14) = 619.2 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{ash,flour} = 619.2 \times 0.0055 = 3.4056 \text{ kg}$$

If bran (≈18% of feed, ash ≈6% d.b.) and germ (≈2%, ash ≈5% d.b.) are also tracked:

$$DM_{bran} = 180 \times 0.86 = 154.8 \text{ kg},\ m_{ash,bran} = 154.8 \times 0.06 = 9.288 \text{ kg}$$

$$DM_{germ} = 20 \times 0.86 = 17.2 \text{ kg},\ m_{ash,germ} = 17.2 \times 0.05 = 0.86 \text{ kg}$$

Total ash accounted for: $3.4056 + 9.288 + 0.86 = 13.55$ kg. Remainder (ash in shorts + flour fraction) closes the balance.

### Step 5: Extraction Rate Calculation

$$E = \frac{M_{flour}}{M_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72\%$$

> **Interpretation:** This 72% extraction is a typical "long-patent" flour yield, leaving 28% as by-products (bran, germ, shorts/middlings).

## 3.2 Derivation: Steeping Kinetics (Fickian Diffusion Model)

For a spherical barley kernel of radius $R$, transient moisture diffusion is governed by:

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left( \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial M}{\partial r} \right)$$

With boundary conditions: $M(r,0) = M_0$ (initial ~12%), $M(R,t) = M_s$ (surface saturation).

### Step 1: Series Solution for Sphere (Crank, 1975)

$$\frac{\bar{M}(t) - M_s}{M_0 - M_s} = \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\!\left(-n^2 \pi^2 D_{eff} t / R^2 \right)$$

### Step 2: Short-Time Approximation (Useful for Steeping Design)

For $D_{eff} t / R^2 < 0.05$:

$$\frac{\bar{M}(t) - M_s}{M_0 - M_s} \approx \frac{6}{R} \sqrt{\frac{D_{eff} t}{\pi}}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

For barley with $R = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $D_{eff} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s, and target $\bar{M} = 0.45$ from $M_0 = 0.12$, $M_s \approx 0.50$:

$$\frac{0.45 - 0.50}{0.12 - 0.50} = \frac{0.1316}{}$$

Solving iteratively yields $t \approx 36$–$48$ hours, consistent with industrial steeping practice (typically broken into wet/dry cycles over 36–48 hr).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wheat vs. Barley End-Use

| Property | Hard Wheat (HRW/HRS) | Soft Wheat (SRW/White) | Durum Wheat | Malting Barley |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Protein (%) | 11–15 | 8–11 | 12–15 | 9–12 |
| Gluten quality | Strong, elastic | Weak, extensible | Strong (glutenin) | N/A (washed out in mashing) |
| Starch damage | High | Low | High | Controlled (modification) |
| Primary product | Bread, buns | Cakes, biscuits, crackers | Semolina → pasta | Malt → beer/whisky |
| Key quality test | Farinograph, Alveograph | Cookie spread factor | Gluten wash | Friability, Extract, FN |

> **Key insight:** The *same* plant metabolite (starch + protein) is being fractionated differently based on end-use: wheat milling targets **physical separation** of bran from endosperm, while barley malting targets **enzymatic conversion** of endosperm reserves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wheat Milling Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wheat Intake] --> B[Pre-Cleaning & Magnetic Separation]
    B --> C[Aspiration & Destoning]
    C --> D[Optical Sorter]
    D --> E[Tempering Bins]
    E --> F[First Break Roller Mill]
    F --> G[Plansifter 1]
    G --> G1[Flour Stream]
    G --> G2[Semolina / Middlings]
    G --> G3[Bran Stream]
    G1 --> H[Flour Blending]
    G2 --> I[Purifier]
    I --> J[Reduction Rolls R1 to R10]
    J --> K[Reduction Plansifters]
    K --> K1[Finished Flour]
    K --> K2[Bran Reducers]
    G3 --> L[Germ Cyclones]
    L --> M[Finished Bran]
    L --> N[Finished Germ]
    H --> O[Finished Flour Bin]
    K1 --> O
    O --> P[Bagging / Bulk Loading]
```

## 4.2 Barley Malting Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Barley Reception & Grading] --> B[Cleaning & Sieving]
    B --> C[Steeping Tanks 1 to 3]
    C --> D[Air Rest Cycles]
    D --> E[Germination Vessel - Saladin Box]
    E --> F[Respiration Monitoring CO2]
    F --> G[Kiln Phase 1 Free Drying 50C]
    G --> H[Kiln Phase 2 Bound Drying 65C]
    H --> I[Kiln Phase 3 Curing 80 to 110C]
    I --> J[Deculming / Polishing]
    J --> K[Storage & Dispatch]
    K --> L[Brewery / Distillery]
    L --> M[Mashing]
    M --> N[Fermentation]
    N --> O[Maturation]
    O --> P[Finished Beer / Whisky]
```

## 4.3 Wheat Kernel Anatomy (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheat Kernel Cross Section] --> B[Pericarp Outer Husk]
    A --> C[Testa Pigment Layer]
    A --> D[Aleurone Layer High in Enzymes Minerals]
    A --> E[Starchy Endosperm Bulk of Kernel]
    A --> F[Scutellum Interface]
    A --> G[Embryo Germ]
    A --> H[Brush End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Milling implication:** The aleurone layer is *technically* part of the botanical bran but, unlike the pericarp, it is rich in protein, B-vitamins, and beneficial phenolic compounds. Whole-wheat flour retains the aleurone; refined white flour excludes it. This is why aleurone retention is a hot topic in functional-food milling.

## 4.4 Barley Kernel Structure (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Barley Kernel] --> B[Husk Palea and Lemma]
    A --> C[Pericarp]
    A --> D[Testa]
    A --> E[Aleurone]
    A --> F[Starchy Endosperm]
    A --> G[Embryo]
    A --> H[Acrospire Future Shoot]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why "covered" barley matters:** Unlike wheat, malting barley is typically "covered" (hulled). The husk acts as a natural filter bed during lautering in the brewhouse — a key reason brewers prefer barley malt over wheat or rye malts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEFTT836]**

### Q1. Define **tempering** of wheat. Mention typical moisture targets for hard and soft wheat classes. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Tempering (or conditioning) is the controlled addition of water to cleaned wheat followed by a resting period of 8–24 h, intended to toughen the bran layers and mellow the endosperm for efficient milling. Typical targets:
- **Hard wheat: 15.5–16.5% moisture (w.b.)**
- **Soft wheat: 14.0–14.5% moisture (w.b.)**

Higher moisture in hard wheat is required because the bran is more tightly bound to the endosperm and needs to be loosened.

---

### Q2. List the three stages of the **barley malting** process with their principal objective. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Steeping** – Raise moisture from ~12% to 42–48% to initiate germination metabolism.
2. **Germination** – Hold at 15–20 °C, >95% RH for 4–6 days; enzymes (amylases, proteases) develop and modify the endosperm.
3. **Kilning** – Apply controlled heat ramps to dry the green malt to 3–4% moisture and develop color/flavor compounds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEFTT836]**

---

### Question A: Wheat Processing

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a flow diagram, describe the **roller milling process** of wheat. List the major product streams. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The roller milling process consists of sequential unit operations designed to progressively separate bran, germ, and endosperm:

1. **Pre-cleaning:** Scalper, magnetic separator, aspirator, destoner, and optical sorter remove foreign matter, metal, dust, and broken/discolored kernels.
2. **Tempering (conditioning):** Water added to 15.5–16.5% (hard) or 14.0–14.5% (soft), rested 8–24 h to toughen bran.
3. **Breaking system (B1–B5):** Corrugated roller pairs open the kernel; gap reduces progressively (~0.6 mm at B1 to ~0.10 mm at B5). Break release at B1 is 15–25% through 20W sieve.
4. **Sizing system (S1–S2):** Slightly corrugated rolls fragment endosperm chunks.
5. **Purification:** Combined aspiration + sieving separates endosperm middlings from bran by density.
6. **Reduction system (R1–R10):** Smooth rolls grind purified middlings into flour, with repeated sifting after each stage.
7. **Flour blending and treatment:** Streams blended to spec, optionally enriched (Fe, B-vitamins, folate).

**Product streams (with approximate yields):**
- **Flour (patent + clear): 70–78%**
- **Bran: 14–18%**
- **Germ: 2–3%**
- **Shorts/Middlings: 4–7%**
- **Losses: 0.5–1%**

**Flow diagram (textual representation):**
Wheat → Cleaner → Temper → B1 → Sifter1 → B2/B3/B4/B5 → Purifiers → R1–R10 → Flour → Blending → Pack.

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the **quality parameters** used to characterize wheat flour. Why is **Falling Number** important? `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

Key flour quality parameters:
- **Moisture content (w.b.):** Target 13.5–14.5% for storage stability.
- **Protein content:** Calculated as $\%N \times 5.7$. Determines end-use (bread requires >11%, biscuits 8–9%).
- **Ash content:** Indicator of refinement. Patent flour: 0.50–0.65% (14% m.b.).
- **Falling Number (Hagberg-Perten):** Time (s) for a stirrer to fall through a hot flour-water gel. Standard: **250–350 s**.
- **Gluten quality (wet/dry gluten, gluten index).**
- **Starch damage:** Affects water absorption and gas retention.
- **Farinograph (water absorption, dough development time, stability).**
- **Alveograph (W, P/L ratio).**
- **Amylograph (pasting properties).**

**Importance of Falling Number:**
The Falling Number test measures **α-amylase activity** in the flour. If wheat was sprouted in the field (pre-harvest rain), the kernel's native α-amylase is activated and degrades starch during baking, producing a **sticky, gummy crumb** and reduced loaf volume. A Falling Number below 250 s indicates unacceptable sprouting damage; above 350 s indicates sound wheat. The value directly determines whether a wheat lot is fit for bread, biscuits, or must be downgraded to feed.

---

### Question B: Barley Processing

**Q.B (a)** Describe the **malting process of barley** in detail. Mention the target moisture, temperature, and duration of each stage. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

Malting is a three-stage controlled germination process:

1. **Steeping (36–48 h total, 15–18 °C)**
   - Wet/dry cycles: e.g., 8 h wet → 8 h air rest → 8 h wet → 8 h air rest → final wet-out.
   - Water uptake raises moisture from ~12% to **42–48% (w.b.)**.
   - Air rests prevent anaerobic respiration, supply O₂, and allow CO₂ to dissipate.
   - Steep-out criterion: even, glossy, chit appearance; moisture uniformity ±1%.

2. **Germination (4–6 days, 15–20 °C, >95% RH)**
   - Conducted in **Saladin boxes** (modern) or traditional floors.
   - Continuous slow turning prevents matting of rootlets and provides uniform aeration.
   - **Respiration losses** 5–7% (acceptable).
   - End-point criteria:
     - **Acrospire length:** ¾ to 1× kernel length.
     - **Chit count:** ≥95% visible chit.
     - **Friability (Friabilimeter):** >85% (well-modified).
     - **β-glucan reduction:** <150 mg/L in Congress wort.
   - Key enzymes developed: **α-amylase, β-amylase, limit dextrinase, β-glucanase, endo-protease**.

3. **Kilning (18–30 h total)**
   - **Phase 1 (free drying):** 50–60 °C, 8–12 h; moisture falls to ~25%.
   - **Phase 2 (bound drying):** 60–75 °C, 6–10 h; moisture falls to ~10%.
   - **Phase 3 (curing):** 80–110 °C, 2–4 h; develops Maillard flavor; final moisture 3.0–4.5%.
   - Pilsner malt: cure at 80–85 °C. Crystal malt: 65 °C saccharification step then high-temp cure. Black malt: 200–220 °C (specialty).

**Output:** Crisp, friable, aromatic green malt → deculmed (rootlets removed) → storage.

---

**Q.B (b)** What is **pearling** of barley? Compare **two-row and six-row barley** for malting quality. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Pearling (Pearling of Barley):**
Pearling is an abrasive milling process in which the outer hull and bran layers of barley are progressively removed by friction against carborundum or emery stones inside a rotating pearling machine. The result is **pearl barley** — a polished, rounded grain with ~25–35% of outer layers removed. Applications include:
- Soups, stews, breakfast cereals.
- **Pot or Scotch barley:** Pearled to ~75% extraction.
- Pearl barley is a prebiotic-rich, low-GI food ingredient.
- Continuous pearling in vertical or horizontal abrasives; yields 65–80% polished grain.

**Comparison — Two-row vs. Six-row Barley for Malting:**

| Parameter | Two-row Barley | Six-row Barley |
|---|---|---|
| Kernel uniformity | Plump, large, uniform | Smaller, more variable |
| Extract potential | Higher (~82%) | Slightly lower (~80%) |
| Enzyme activity (α-amylase) | Moderate | High |
| Modification rate | Slower, more even | Faster |
| Brewers' preference | Premium lagers, ales (EU) | Adjunct-heavy beers (US) |
| Husk content | Higher (good for filter bed) | Lower |
| Protein content | Usually 9–11% | Often 11–13% |

**Conclusion:** Two-row barleys are generally preferred for **malting** because their larger, plumper kernels give higher extract yields, more uniform modification, and contribute more husk material for lautering. Six-row barleys, with their higher enzyme activity, are favored in **high-adjunct brewing** where supplemental enzyme action is advantageous (e.g., US light lagers using rice or corn adjuncts).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Wheat & Barley Processing:**
> 1. **Conflating tempering and steeping** — Tempering is wheat-specific (raises to 14–16%); steeping is barley-specific (raises to 42–48%). Examiners *will* deduct if you mix up the moisture targets.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always mention % (w.b.) or % (d.b.) for moisture and ash. Naked numbers lose marks.
> 3. **Skipping enzymes in malting** — Naming just "amylase" is too vague. Mention α-amylase, β-amylase, β-glucanase, and proteases.
> 4. **Confusing break release with extraction rate** — Break release is a *per-stage* KPI (~15–25% through 20W); extraction rate is the *overall* mill yield (72–78%).
> 5. **Forgetting aleurone layer in wheat anatomy** — Examiners specifically test this in Module 2 questions on whole-wheat vs. refined flour.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⭐ KTU Rapid-Revision — Wheat & Barley Processing (Module 2)**

**WHEAT — Quick Facts:**
- [x] Kernel composition: **14% bran, 83% endosperm, 3% germ**.
- [x] Six milling stages: **Clean → Temper → Break → Sift → Purify → Reduce → Blend**.
- [x] **Break rolls = corrugated; Reduction rolls = smooth.**
- [x] Tempering moisture: **15.5–16.5% (hard), 14.0–14.5% (soft)**.
- [x] Break release target: **15–25%** through 20W sieve.
- [x] Flour yield: **72–78%** refined, **100%** whole wheat.
- [x] Protein factor: **N × 5.7** (specific to wheat).
- [x] Falling Number: **250–350 s** is sound; <250 s = sprouted damage.
- [x] Ash content reflects refinement: lower ash = purer endosperm flour.
- [x] **Aleurone layer** is nutritionally rich — retained in whole-wheat, lost in refined.

**BARLEY — Quick Facts:**
- [x] Malting = **Steep + Germinate + Kiln** (the "3-stage" answer examiners want).
- [x] Steep-out moisture: **42–48%**; steep temp: **15–18 °C**.
- [x] Germination: **4–6 days, 15–20 °C, >95% RH**; acrospire ¾ length; friability >85%.
- [x] Key enzymes synthesized: **α- & β-amylase, β-glucanase, proteases**.
- [x] Kilning: 50 °C (free) → 65 °C (bound) → 80–110 °C (cure) → final **3–4% moisture**.
- [x] Respiration loss acceptable: **5–7%**.
- [x] Two-row > six-row for premium malting (plumper, higher extract).
- [x] Barley is **covered** (hulled) — husk aids brewhouse lautering.
- [x] Pearl barley: abrasive removal of 25–35% outer layers.
- [x] Malt extract (Congress mash): **~80–82%** on dry basis.

**Cross-Comparison Memory Hooks:**
- Wheat = *physical fractionation* (mill to separate).
- Barley = *biological transformation* (germinate to enzymatically modify).
- Both start with cleaning, both care about moisture — but to dramatically different end-values.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheat Nutrient Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wheat Nutrient Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheat Nutrient Composition** refers to the comprehensive quantitative and qualitative profile of macronutrients (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and dietary fiber), micronutrients (vitamins and minerals), and bioactive phytochemicals present in the wheat kernel (*Triticum aestivum* L. / *Triticum durum* Desf.). It is a critical determinant of end-use quality, nutritional value, and functional behavior in milling, baking, and pasta-making applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The wheat kernel is a caryopsis consisting of three principal morphological components — the **endosperm (~80–85%)**, the **bran (~13–17%)**, and the **germ (~2–3%)** — each contributing distinct nutrients to the overall composition. The nutrient distribution is **not homogeneous**; the germ is rich in lipids and vitamins, bran in fiber and minerals, while the endosperm is dominated by starch and gluten proteins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a wheat grain as a **three-layer sandwich** sealed inside a hard outer jacket (the pericarp):

- The **innermost filling (endosperm)** is pure white, starchy "fuel" — basically a calorie warehouse made of starch granules glued together by protein (gluten). This is what the bakery industry wants.
- The **middle bread layer (bran)** is the rough, fibrous "fiber jacket" packed with vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants — the part nutritionists praise in whole wheat.
- The **top cap (germ)** is the "nutrient bomb" — small but loaded with healthy oils, vitamin E, and B-vitamins; this is the embryo that would sprout into a new plant.

When we refine wheat into *maida* (refined flour), we **strip off the bran and germ**, leaving only the endosperm. This is why whole wheat flour is nutritionally superior — it contains **all three layers**, while refined flour is essentially a "calorie-only" product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reporting Basis (KTU Expected):** Nutrient values are typically expressed per **100 grams of edible portion** on a fresh-weight basis (FW) or dry-weight basis (DW). Moisture content of wheat is reported as approximately **10–14%** for safe storage.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Energy density of wheat (whole)**: approximately **340 kcal / 100 g**
- **Specific heat of wheat**: ~**1.55 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Bulk density**: ~**780 kg/m³** (varies with moisture)
- **Specific gravity of wheat kernel**: ~**1.3 – 1.4**
- **Standard Kjeldahl conversion factor for wheat protein**: **5.7** (NOT 6.25, which is for meat/dairy)
- **Test weight (hectolitre weight)**: ≥ **78 kg/hL** for sound wheat

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Macronutrient Breakdown — Structured Analysis

### A. Carbohydrates (≈ 60–75% of dry weight)

Carbohydrates dominate the wheat kernel. The reasoning: wheat is a **staple energy crop**, and the endosperm is evolutionarily designed as a starch reservoir to fuel seedling germination.

- **Starch**: **55–65%** of grain weight; composed of two glucose polymers:
  - **Amylose** (20–30%): linear, unbranched α-1,4-linked glucose chains.
  - **Amylopectin** (70–80%): highly branched α-1,4 and α-1,6-linked chains.
- **Non-starch polysaccharides (Dietary Fiber)**: **10–15%** in whole wheat, mainly:
  - **Arabinoxylan (AX)** — major hemicellulose (~5–8%)
  - **β-glucan** (~0.5–1%)
  - **Cellulose** (~2–3%)
  - **Lignin** (minor)
- **Sugars**: trace amounts of sucrose, glucose, fructose, and maltose (~2–3%).

### B. Proteins (≈ 8–15% of dry weight)

Wheat proteins are unique among cereals because they form **gluten** — an elastic, viscoelastic network responsible for the dough's gas-holding capacity.

- **Gluten proteins (storage proteins, 80–85% of total wheat protein)**:
  - **Gliadins** (40–50% of gluten): soluble in 70% ethanol; contribute extensibility and viscosity.
  - **Glutenins** (40–50% of gluten): insoluble; contribute elasticity and strength (via disulfide bonds).
- **Non-gluten proteins (15–20%)**:
  - Albumins (water-soluble, mostly enzymatic/structural)
  - Globulins (salt-soluble)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gluten-forming behaviour is exclusive to wheat** (and closely related rye/barley). This is why wheat is the "king of bread-making cereals" — its protein quality is technologically unmatched.

### C. Lipids (≈ 1.5–2.5% whole wheat; up to 6–11% in germ)

Wheat is a **low-oil cereal** (compared to maize/sorghum which have 4–6%).

- Primarily located in the **germ** and **aleurone layer**.
- Composition: **Linoleic acid (C18:2, ~55–60%)** is dominant, followed by oleic, palmitic, and linolenic acids.
- Contain **tocopherols (Vitamin E)** — natural antioxidants.

### D. Moisture (≈ 10–14%)

Critical for safe storage. Above **14% moisture**, microbial spoilage and insect infestation become probable. Below **10%**, the grain becomes brittle and prone to mechanical damage.

## 2.2 Micronutrient Breakdown

### E. Vitamins
- **B-complex vitamins** (concentrated in bran & germ): Thiamine (B₁), Riboflavin (B₂), Niacin (B₃), Pyridoxine (B₆), Folate.
- **Vitamin E (tocopherols)**: concentrated in the germ (~30 mg/100g in germ oil).
- **Vitamin A precursors**: negligible (wheat has no carotenoids of nutritional significance, except yellow pigments in some durum varieties — lutein).

### F. Minerals (Ash content ≈ 1.5–2.0%)

| Mineral | Approx. Content (mg/100g whole wheat) | Physiological Role |
|---|---|---|
| Phosphorus (P) | 320–360 | Energy metabolism, phytic acid binding |
| Potassium (K) | 350–400 | Electrolyte balance |
| Magnesium (Mg) | 120–140 | Enzyme cofactor |
| Calcium (Ca) | 35–45 | Bone health |
| Iron (Fe) | 3.0–4.5 | Oxygen transport (haemoglobin) |
| Zinc (Zn) | 2.5–3.5 | Immune function, 200+ enzymes |
| Selenium (Se) | 0.005–0.07 | Antioxidant (glutathione peroxidase) |

## 2.3 Bioactive Phytochemicals & Anti-Nutrients

- **Phytic acid (Phytate)**: **0.5–1.5%** — binds minerals (Zn, Fe, Ca), reducing bioavailability (anti-nutritional).
- **Phenolic acids (Ferulic acid)**: 0.5–1.0 mg/g — natural antioxidants.
- **Alkylresorcinols**: 300–700 µg/g — biomarkers of whole-grain intake.
- **Lignans, Flavonoids, Sterols**: minor but bioactive.
- **Trypsin inhibitors, Lectins**: minor anti-nutritional factors, deactivated by heat.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Protein content (from Nitrogen) | $\% \text{ Protein} = \% N \times 5.7$ | Kjeldahl factor for wheat |
| 2 | Moisture content (oven method) | $\% MC = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$ | $W_0$ = dish wt, $W_1$ = wet wt, $W_2$ = dry wt |
| 3 | Total Energy (Atwater factors) | $E = 4P + 9F + 4C$ | kcal per 100 g; P=Protein, F=Fat, C=Carb (g) |
| 4 | Wet Gluten | Hand-washing or Glutomatic | % of flour weight |
| 5 | Dry Gluten | Wet gluten − moisture loss at 105°C | % of flour weight |
| 6 | Gluten Index | $\frac{\text{Gluten retained on sieve}}{\text{Total wet gluten}} \times 100$ | Strength indicator |
| 7 | Falling Number (Hagberg) | Viscosity-based α-amylase activity | Seconds; >300 s = low enzyme |
| 8 | Zeleny Sedimentation | SDS-induced swelling of gluten | mL; high = strong gluten |
| 9 | Test Weight | Mass per hectolitre | kg/hL |
| 10 | Thousand Kernel Weight (TKW) | Mass of 1000 kernels | grams |
| 11 | Hardness Index (SKCS) | Single Kernel Characterization System | PSI units |
| 12 | Amylose content | Iodine binding colorimetry | % of starch |
| 13 | Dietary fiber (TDF) | $\text{TDF} = \text{IDF} + \text{SDF}$ | Insoluble + Soluble DF |
| 14 | Phytic acid | $\text{IP}_6 = \text{Total P} \times \text{stoichiometric factor}$ | mg/g |
| 15 | Dry matter (DM) | $\text{DM} = 100 - \%MC$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline integrity note:** When asked to compute the energy contributed by macronutrients in wheat, always use the **Atwater specific factors** (4, 9, 4) — partial factors (4, 9, 3.75) are sometimes used for high-fiber foods but the standard KTU exam expects 4, 9, 4 unless the problem specifies otherwise.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Milling industry**: Protein content & hardness determine flour type (bread vs. biscuit vs. pasta).
- **Bakery industry**: Gluten quantity/quality controls dough rheology and loaf volume.
- **Nutritional labelling**: Regulatory mandate under FSSAI (India) / FDA / Codex to declare proximate composition.
- **Biofortification programs**: HarvestPlus and ICAR programs target Fe/Zn/Se enrichment in wheat.
- **Animal feed**: Low-grade wheat used as energy feed; nutrient composition determines feed formulation.
- **Functional food design**: Adding bran/germ fractions to boost fiber & micronutrients in bakery products.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Energy Content of Whole Wheat Flour (Standard Problem)

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark variant):** Given the following proximate composition of a whole wheat sample on a fresh-weight basis, calculate the gross energy content in kcal/100 g and in kJ/100 g.

| Component | Value (%) |
|---|---|
| Moisture | 12.0 |
| Crude Protein | 11.5 |
| Crude Fat (Ether extract) | 2.0 |
| Total Carbohydrate (by difference) | 72.0 |
| Ash | 2.5 |

### Step 1: Verify the proximate sum

The proximate composition must sum to 100% (moisture basis). Let us check:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= \%MC + \%P + \%F + \%C + \%Ash \\
&= 12.0 + 11.5 + 2.0 + 72.0 + 2.5 \\
&= 100.0\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since the sum is exactly 100%, the "carbohydrate by difference" value (72.0%) is consistent and need not be re-computed. If it were off, we would have applied:

$$
\%C_{by\,diff} = 100 - (\%MC + \%P + \%F + \%Ash)
$$

### Step 2: Apply Atwater energy factors

The Atwater factors assign mean physiological fuel values:
- Protein = **4 kcal/g**
- Fat = **9 kcal/g**
- Carbohydrate = **4 kcal/g**

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kcal}} &= (4 \times P) + (9 \times F) + (4 \times C) \\
&= (4 \times 11.5) + (9 \times 2.0) + (4 \times 72.0) \\
&= 46.0 + 18.0 + 288.0 \\
&= 352.0 \text{ kcal per 100 g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to kilojoules

Conversion factor: **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kJ}} &= 352.0 \times 4.184 \\
&= 1472.77 \text{ kJ per 100 g} \\
&\approx 1473 \text{ kJ/100 g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Express on dry-weight basis (for scientific comparison)

Dry matter (DM) = 100 − %MC = 100 − 12 = **88%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{DM basis}} &= \frac{352.0}{0.88} = 400.0 \text{ kcal/100 g DM} \\
E_{\text{DM basis (kJ)}} &= \frac{1472.77}{0.88} = 1673.6 \text{ kJ/100 g DM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Many KTU students forget that reporting energy per 100 g **fresh weight** is for labelling, but per 100 g **dry weight** is the scientific comparator (since moisture varies widely between samples). Always mention **both** if the question allows.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Protein Content from Kjeldahl Nitrogen

**Problem:** A Kjeldahl digestion of 1.500 g wheat flour consumed 18.0 mL of 0.1 N HCl for titration (blank = 0.4 mL). Calculate the % crude protein.

### Step 1: Determine the volume of acid actually used by ammonia

$$
V_{\text{acid}} = V_{\text{sample}} - V_{\text{blank}} = 18.0 - 0.4 = 17.6 \text{ mL}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the mass of nitrogen

1 mL of 1 N HCl ≡ 14 mg of N (atomic mass of N = 14 g/mol, and equivalent weight = 14 for NH₃).
So 1 mL of 0.1 N HCl ≡ **1.4 mg N**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of N} &= V_{\text{acid}} \times N_{\text{acid}} \times 14 \\
&= 17.6 \times 0.1 \times 14 \\
&= 24.64 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate % Nitrogen in the sample

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%N &= \frac{\text{Mass of N (mg)}}{\text{Sample weight (mg)}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{24.64}{1500} \times 100 \\
&= 1.643\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to % Crude Protein using wheat-specific factor (5.7)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ Crude Protein} &= \%N \times 5.7 \\
&= 1.643 \times 5.7 \\
&= 9.36\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often use **6.25** (universal factor) for wheat — this gives a falsely high value (10.27%). Wheat proteins are **nitrogen-rich but contain more non-protein nitrogen** in the form of free amino acids, amides, and nucleic acid breakdown products. The empirically determined factor of **5.7** is mandated by ICC (International Association for Cereal Science and Technology) and AOAC for wheat. This single point is **worth 1 mark** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hard vs. Soft Wheat Composition

| Property | Hard Wheat (e.g., Bread wheat) | Soft Wheat (e.g., Biscuit wheat) |
|---|---|---|
| Protein content | 11–15% | 8–11% |
| Endosperm texture | Vitreous, translucent | Mealy, opaque |
| Starch granule integrity | Intact, tightly bound | Partially damaged |
| Gluten quality | Strong, elastic | Weak, extensible |
| Typical products | Bread, chapati, pasta (durum) | Biscuits, cakes, pastries |
| Milling energy | Higher | Lower |
| Starch damage % | Low–moderate | High |
| Water absorption | High | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wheat Nutrient Composition Mapping

Since the wheat kernel's physical cross-section is best illustrated as a botanical drawing, the following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of nutrient distribution** across the three morphological zones and the analytical workflow used to quantify them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHEAT KERNEL CARYOPSIS] --> B[PERICARP and TESTA Outer Protective Layers]
    A --> C[ENDOSPERM 80 to 85 percent of kernel]
    A --> D[GERM EMBRYO 2 to 3 percent]

    B --> B1[Cellulose and Hemicellulose]
    B --> B2[Phenolic Acids]
    B --> B3[Mineral Ash Silica]

    C --> C1[Starch Granules 55 to 65 percent]
    C --> C2[Gluten Proteins 8 to 14 percent]
    C --> C3[Sugars Sucrose Glucose]

    C1 --> C1A[Amylose 20 to 30 percent]
    C1 --> C1B[Amylopectin 70 to 80 percent]

    C2 --> C2A[Gliadins Extensibility]
    C2 --> C2B[Glutenins Elasticity]
    C2 --> C2C[Albumins and Globulins]

    D --> D1[Lipids 6 to 11 percent of germ]
    D --> D2[Vitamin E Tocopherols]
    D --> D3[B Complex Vitamins Thiamine Niacin]
    D --> D4[Phytic Acid]

    A --> E[ALEURONE LAYER]
    E --> E1[Minerals P K Mg Zn Fe]
    E --> E2[Phytic Acid]
    E --> E3[Vitamin B Complex]
    E --> E4[Enzymes Phytase Lipase]

    A --> F[QUANTITATIVE ANALYSIS]
    F --> F1[Proximate Composition AOAC]
    F --> F2[Minerals ICP AAS]
    F --> F3[Vitamins HPLC]
    F --> F4[Gluten Quality Glutomatic]
    F --> F5[Starch Pasting RVA]

    F1 --> F11[Moisture Oven 105C]
    F1 --> F12[Protein Kjeldahl N x 5.7]
    F1 --> F13[Fat Soxhlet Ether Extract]
    F1 --> F14[Ash Muffle 550C]
    F1 --> F15[Fiber Crude or Total Dietary]
```

## 4.2 Functional Interpretation of the Diagram

- **Top tier (A)**: The unified wheat kernel as the parent node.
- **Second tier (B, C, D, E)**: The four anatomical zones — pericarp/testa, endosperm, germ, and aleurone (technically part of the endosperm but functionally distinct in nutritional analysis).
- **Third tier**: Specific nutrient families housed in each zone.
- **Bottom tier (F)**: The analytical methodology chain that **transforms a physical kernel into a numerical nutrient profile** — this is the core of food chemistry and is the focus of most KTU practical/analytical questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Where is Vitamin E located in wheat?", do NOT just say "in the germ" — the **aleurone layer** is the second-richest source, especially in bran-enriched whole wheat flours. Cross-questions on compartmentalization of micronutrients are common and worth 2–3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "proximate composition" of wheat. List the six standard parameters determined under proximate analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Proximate composition refers to the **systematic quantification of the major nutrient groups** in a food material, providing a rapid nutritional fingerprint.

The **six standard parameters** (Weende analysis, AOAC method) are:

1. **Moisture** — determined by oven drying at 105 °C to constant weight.
2. **Crude Protein** — determined by Kjeldahl method (%N × 5.7 for wheat).
3. **Crude Fat (Ether Extract)** — determined by Soxhlet extraction using petroleum ether.
4. **Total Ash (Minerals)** — determined by incineration in a muffle furnace at 550 °C.
5. **Crude Fiber** — determined by acid–alkali digestion (Weende method).
6. **Carbohydrates (Nitrogen-free extract, NFE)** — calculated **by difference**:

$$
\%NFE = 100 - (\%MC + \%P + \%F + \%Ash + \%CF)
$$

(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for parameters + 0.5 mark for the by-difference concept)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between "hard wheat" and "soft wheat" with respect to protein content, endosperm texture, and end-use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Hard Wheat | Soft Wheat |
|---|---|---|
| Protein content | High (11–15%) | Low (8–11%) |
| Endosperm texture | Vitreous, translucent, tightly packed starch granules | Mealy, opaque, loosely bound starch |
| End-use | Bread, chapati, pasta (durum), buns | Biscuits, cakes, cookies, pastries |

(1 mark per row — 3 marks total)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Enumerate the **major macro- and micronutrients** present in the wheat kernel. Explain the distribution of these nutrients across the three morphological components (bran, endosperm, germ). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are **gluten proteins**? Differentiate between **gliadins and glutenins** with respect to their solubility, molecular structure, and rheological contribution to dough. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Macronutrients & Micronutrients with Distribution (7 marks):**

**Macronutrients:**

1. **Carbohydrates (60–75%)** — Dominated by **starch** in the **endosperm** (amylose 20–30% + amylopectin 70–80%) and **dietary fiber** (arabinoxylan, cellulose, β-glucan) concentrated in the **bran** and **aleurone** layer.
2. **Proteins (8–15%)** — Classified as:
   - **Storage (gluten) proteins** — predominantly in the **endosperm** (gliadins + glutenins).
   - **Cytoplasmic proteins** — albumins & globulins, more concentrated in the **germ and aleurone**.
3. **Lipids (1.5–2.5% in whole grain; 6–11% in germ)** — Concentrated in the **germ** and **aleurone layer**; rich in unsaturated fatty acids (linoleic acid dominant).
4. **Moisture (10–14%)** — Distributed throughout but with the **germ** having the highest moisture (often >14%).

**Micronutrients:**

5. **Vitamins** — **B-complex** (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B₆, folate) in **bran and aleurone**; **Vitamin E (tocopherols)** in the **germ**.
6. **Minerals (1.5–2.0% ash)** — Phosphorus, potassium, magnesium, iron, zinc, selenium, concentrated in the **aleurone layer** and **bran**.

**Distribution summary (2 marks):**
- **Endosperm**: starch, gluten proteins, sugars.
- **Bran/Aleurone**: dietary fiber, B-vitamins, minerals, phytic acid, phenolic acids.
- **Germ**: lipids, vitamin E, B-vitamins, enzymes (phytase, lipase).

**(b) Gluten Proteins — Gliadins vs. Glutenins (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Gluten is a **viscoelastic protein complex** formed when wheat flour is hydrated and kneaded. It is responsible for the unique gas-holding capacity of wheat dough. Gluten comprises ~80–85% of total wheat protein.

| Property | Gliadins | Glutenins |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility | Soluble in **70% aqueous ethanol** | Insoluble in ethanol; soluble/dispersible only in dilute acid/alkali |
| Molecular structure | **Monomeric** (single polypeptide chains), spherical, MW ~28–55 kDa | **Polymeric** (disulfide-bonded aggregates), elongated, MW up to several million Da |
| Disulfide bonds | Mostly intramolecular | Both inter- and intramolecular (extensive cross-linking) |
| Rheological role | Contribute **extensibility and viscosity** to dough | Contribute **elasticity and strength** to dough |
| Functional analogue | Acts as a "plasticizer" | Acts as a "structural backbone" |
| Classification | α/β, γ, ω-gliadins | HMW-GS (High Molecular Weight Glutenin Subunits) + LMW-GS (Low Molecular Weight) |
| Effect on bread | Loaf volume ↑ but crumb structure weak if only gliadin | Dough stability ↑; crumb structure strong & chewy |

(6 marks for table-style differentiation + 1 mark for gluten definition and technological importance)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative]`

**(a)** Discuss the **vitamin and mineral profile of wheat**, highlighting the difference in micronutrient retention between **whole wheat flour** and **refined wheat flour (maida)**. Explain the significance of the **aleurone layer** in micronutrient density. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheat sample yields the following Kjeldahl data: Sample weight = 1.250 g; Titre volume of 0.1 N HCl = 15.6 mL; Blank = 0.2 mL. Calculate the **% crude protein** using the wheat-specific factor. State two reasons why wheat uses a factor of **5.7** instead of the generic **6.25**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Vitamin, Mineral Profile and Whole vs. Refined Wheat (7 marks):**

**Vitamin profile of whole wheat (3 marks):**

| Vitamin | Content (mg/100g whole wheat) | Primary Location |
|---|---|---|
| Thiamine (B₁) | 0.30–0.45 | Bran & aleurone |
| Riboflavin (B₂) | 0.10–0.15 | Germ & aleurone |
| Niacin (B₃) | 4.0–5.5 | Bran |
| Pyridoxine (B₆) | 0.30–0.50 | Germ |
| Folate (B₉) | 0.04–0.08 | Aleurone |
| Vitamin E (tocopherols) | 1.0–1.5 (whole) / 25–35 in germ | Germ |

**Mineral profile of whole wheat (2 marks):**
Phosphorus (320–360 mg), Potassium (350–400 mg), Magnesium (120–140 mg), Calcium (35–45 mg), Iron (3.0–4.5 mg), Zinc (2.5–3.5 mg), Selenium (trace). Most are concentrated in the **aleurone layer**, which is removed during milling into maida.

**Whole vs. Refined wheat (2 marks):**
- **Whole wheat flour** retains bran, aleurone, and germ → ~**80–100%** of vitamins, minerals, and fiber are preserved.
- **Refined flour (maida)** retains only the endosperm → loses **70–90% of B-vitamins**, **60–80% of minerals**, **~100% of dietary fiber** and almost all vitamin E.
- This is why many countries (including India) mandate **fortification of refined flour** with iron, folic acid, and vitamin B₁₂.

**Significance of the aleurone layer (½ mark):** The aleurone is a **single-cell layer** enveloping the starchy endosperm; it is the **densest source of minerals, B-vitamins, and phytic acid**. Although anatomically part of the bran, it is nutritionally the most valuable fraction.

**(b) Protein Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Corrected titre volume**

$$
V = 15.6 - 0.2 = 15.4 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of nitrogen**

$$
m_N = V \times N \times 14 = 15.4 \times 0.1 \times 14 = 21.56 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 3: % Nitrogen**

$$
\%N = \frac{21.56}{1250} \times 100 = 1.725\%
$$

**Step 4: % Crude Protein (factor = 5.7)**

$$
\%\text{Protein} = 1.725 \times 5.7 = 9.83\%
$$

**Step 5: Why 5.7 and not 6.25 (2 marks):**

1. **Non-protein nitrogen (NPN) fraction:** Wheat contains a higher proportion of NPN (free amino acids, amides like glutamine and asparagine, nucleic acid bases, and small peptides) compared to animal proteins. Using 6.25 would **overestimate** the true protein content by ~10%.
2. **Empirical determination by Jones (1931):** The factor 5.7 was experimentally derived by isolating pure wheat gluten protein and directly measuring its nitrogen content. The actual N content of wheat protein (~17.5%) yields a factor of 100/17.5 ≈ **5.71**, rounded to **5.7**.
3. *Bonus (any 2 of the above are sufficient).*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using factor 6.25 instead of 5.7 for wheat** → **−1 mark deducted** silently in many papers.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the blank titre** in Kjeldahl problems → entire calculation becomes wrong → **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 3. **Reporting moisture content as %DW instead of %FW** (or vice versa) without specifying basis → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Stating "carbohydrates = 100 − protein"** and ignoring moisture, fat, ash, fiber → **conceptual error → −2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing phytic acid (anti-nutrient) with phosphorus (nutrient)** — phytic acid is the *storage form* of P, not the bioavailable form. Examiners **specifically** test this distinction.
> 6. **Writing "fiber" generically** — always specify *crude fiber* (Weende) vs. *total dietary fiber (TDF)* vs. *insoluble dietary fiber (IDF) vs. soluble dietary fiber (SDF)*. KTU expects the **AOAC enzymatic-gravimetric method** for TDF (Prosky method).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Wheat is a **caryopsis** with three zones: bran (13–17%), endosperm (80–85%), germ (2–3%).
- ✅ Proximate composition follows the Weende scheme: **Moisture, Protein, Fat, Ash, Crude Fiber, NFE** (by difference).
- ✅ **Protein factor for wheat = 5.7** (NOT 6.25). Use this in Kjeldahl calculations.
- ✅ **Atwater energy formula**: $E_{kcal/100g} = 4P + 9F + 4C$; convert to kJ by **× 4.184**.
- ✅ Wheat is a **low-oil cereal** (1.5–2.5%); germ is the **lipid-rich fraction** (6–11%).
- ✅ **Starch is ~70% amylopectin + ~25% amylose**; waxy wheat has ~100% amylopectin.
- ✅ **Gluten = gliadins (extensibility) + glutenins (elasticity)**; gliadins are monomeric, glutenins are polymeric.
- ✅ **Aleurone layer** = densest source of minerals, B-vitamins, and phytic acid.
- ✅ **Phytic acid** binds Fe/Zn/Ca → reduces bioavailability; mitigated by phytase, soaking, fermentation, or germination.
- ✅ **Refined flour loses ~70–90% of vitamins and 60–80% of minerals** because bran and germ are removed.
- ✅ **Hard wheat = high protein (bread)**; **soft wheat = low protein (biscuits/cakes)**.
- ✅ **Linoleic acid (C18:2)** is the dominant fatty acid in wheat lipids (~55–60%).
- ✅ **Vitamin E (tocopherols)** is concentrated in germ oil (~30 mg/100g).
- ✅ **Biofortification** programs (e.g., HarvestPlus) target Fe (~77 ppm), Zn (~33 ppm) in wheat.
- ✅ **Falling Number (Hagberg-Perten)** measures α-amylase activity; **>300 s** indicates low enzymatic activity (sound wheat).
- ✅ **Test weight (kg/hL)** ≥ 78 indicates sound wheat; lower values indicate shriveled/damaged grain.
- ✅ **Zeleny sedimentation & Gluten Index** are key indicators of gluten strength and bread-making quality.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Wheat Nutrient Composition Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — OEFTT836 Cereals and Legume Technology*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Classification of Wheat ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > *Wheat (genus **Triticum**) is a self-pollinated, hexaploid (most commonly) annual grass belonging to the family **Poaceae** (Gramineae). Its **botanical classification** is determined by **ploidy level, chromosome number, glume tenacity, and grain texture**, while its **commercial classification** is determined by **grain hardness, protein content, gluten quality, endosperm texture, and growing season**.* Wheat is unique among cereals because it is the only major cereal whose classification is governed by **both botanical taxonomy AND end-use quality parameters** (a dual-track classification system adopted by USDA, ICAR, FSSAI, and Codex Alimentarius). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Wheat Classification like a Student ID Card:** > Just as a student is identified by (a) their **genus/surname** (botanical/species) AND (b) their **class, batch, and stream** (commercial category), wheat too is identified by its **species (Triticum aestivum, T. durum, etc.)** AND its **end-use class (Hard Red, Soft White, Durum, etc.)**. Two wheats can be of the *same species* but belong to *different commercial classes*, OR they can be of *different species* but used for *similar end-uses*. For example: - *Triticum aestivum* can be a **Hard Red Winter** wheat (used for bread) OR a **Soft White** wheat (used for cakes). - *Triticum durum* is **always** used for pasta and semolina — it cannot be used for cake-making regardless of how it is grown. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Classification | Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Hectoliter Weight (Test Weight)** | **76–82 kg/hL** | Indicates soundness & milling yield | | **Thousand Kernel Weight (TKW)** | **30–60 g** | Indicates kernel plumpness | | **Moisture Content (Storage Safe)** | **≤ 12–14%** | Storage safety limit | | **Protein Content (Bread Wheat)** | **11–14%** | Baking quality indicator | | **Protein Content (Durum Wheat)** | **12–15%** | Pasta quality indicator | | **Gluten Content (Wet)** | **25–35%** (strong) | Dough elasticity | | **Starch (Amylose : Amylopectin)** | **~25:75** | Granule property | | **Falling Number (Hagberg)** | **> 250 sec** | Sprout damage check | > [!NOTE] > **Key Classification Authorities:** USDA (United States Dept. of Agriculture), ICAR-Indian Institute of Wheat & Barley Research (IIWBR), Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO), and FSSAI (India). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two-Tier Classification Philosophy Wheat classification operates on **two parallel hierarchical systems** running simultaneously: **Tier-1: Botanical/Taxonomic Classification** → based on *what the plant genetically is* - Chromosome number - Ploidy level - Glume tenacity (free vs. tough) - Spike morphology **Tier-2: Commercial/End-Use Classification** → based on *what the grain is used for* - Grain hardness (HR/Soft) - Color (Red/White) - Protein content - Growing season (Winter/Spring) - Gluten strength ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Botanical Classification > **Step 1 — Identify Ploidy Level:** > Wheat species naturally exist in three ploidy levels because of evolutionary hybridization in the Fertile Crescent (~10,000 years ago). $$\text{Ploidy Level} = \frac{2n}{x}$$ Where $n$ = somatic chromosome number, $x$ = base number (= 7 in Triticum). > **Step 2 — Match to Genome Formula:** > Each wheat species is described by its **genome formula** (A, B, or D genome): | Species | Common Name | Ploidy | Genome | 2n | |---|---|---|---|---| | *T. monococcum* | Einkorn | Diploid | AA | 14 | | *T. dicoccum* | Emmer | Tetraploid | AABB | 28 | | *T. durum* | Durum / Macaroni | Tetraploid | AABB | 28 | | *T. compactum* | Club wheat | Hexaploid | AABBDD | 42 | | *T. aestivum* | Bread wheat | Hexaploid | AABBDD | 42 | | *T. spelta* | Spelt / Dinkel | Hexaploid | AABBDD | 42 | > **Step 3 — Evaluate Glume Tenacity (Free-hulling vs. Hulled):** > - **Free-threshing (naked)**: Glumes separate easily during threshing → bread & durum wheats. > - **Hulled (speltoid)**: Glumes remain attached to grain → emmer, einkorn, spelt (require dehulling). > **Step 4 — Classify Spike Morphology:** > - *T. compactum* = short, compact spike > - *T. aestivum* = long, lax spike > - *T. spelta* = long spike with brittle rachis ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Commercial Classification > **Step 1 — Kernel Texture Test (Sk hardness index):** > Grain hardness is determined by the **Puroindoline genes (Pina-D1, Pinb-D1)** located on chromosome **5D** of bread wheat. > **Step 2 — Vitreousness Evaluation:** > The endosperm is examined via **farinator / cut-test**: > - **Vitreous (translucent, glassy, horny)** = high protein, hard wheat > - **Mealy (opaque, starchy, chalky)** = low protein, soft wheat > **Step 3 — Color Pigmentation Assessment:** > - **Red wheat**: dominant *R* gene (seed coat phenolic pigment) > - **White wheat**: recessive *r* allele (no pigmentation) > - **Amber / Yellow-amber**: durum wheats (lutein pigment in endosperm) > **Step 4 — Protein & Gluten Quantification:** > - **Strong wheat**: Protein > 13%, Wet Gluten > 30%, Gluten Index > 70 > - **Medium wheat**: Protein 10–13%, Wet Gluten 25–30% > - **Weak wheat**: Protein < 10%, Wet Gluten < 25% ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Ploidy determination | $2n = x \cdot \text{Ploidy Level}$ | dimensionless | $x = 7$ for Triticum | | Hectoliter (Test) Weight | $W_{HL} = \frac{\text{Weight of grain (kg)}}{\text{Volume (hL)}}$ | kg/hL | Indicates plumpness | | Thousand Kernel Weight | $TKW = \frac{\text{Weight of 1000 kernels (g)}}$ | g | Plumpness indicator | | Hardness Index (SKCS) | $H = 0$ to $100$ (scale) | index | <33 soft, 33–60 medium, >60 hard | | Protein content | $P\% = N\% \times 5.7$ | % | Wheat-specific factor (5.7) | | Wet Gluten Yield | $WG\% = \frac{W_{gluten} - W_{flour}}{W_{flour}} \times 100$ | % | AACCI 38-12 method | | Gluten Index | $GI = \frac{W_{gluten\ retained\ on\ sieve}}{W_{total\ gluten}} \times 100$ | % | Strength measure | | Sedimentation Value (Zeleny) | $SV \geq 30$ mL (bread) | mL | Gluten quality proxy | | Falling Number | $FN > 250$ s acceptable | seconds | Sprout damage | | Starch Damage (SD) | Hard wheat: 7–10%; Soft wheat: 3–5% | % of starch | Milling fingerprint | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Protein-to-Gluten Conversion Note:** For wheat, the nitrogen-to-protein factor is **5.7** (not 6.25 used for general foods). This is because wheat proteins are richer in non-protein nitrogenous compounds. ### 2.5 Engineering / Industry Utility The dual classification is not academic — it directly governs: - **Milling industry**: determines break release, siftability, and flour yield - **Baking industry**: dictates water absorption, dough development time, loaf volume - **Pasta industry**: requires durum semolina with >11% protein and >70% vitreousness - **International trade**: USDA/ICAR grading certificates are mandatory for export - **Breeding programs**: cultivar development targets specific class targets (e.g., HRW with 12% protein) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Ploidy Number from Chromosome Count > **Problem:** A wheat species shows 14 chromosomes in its gametophyte. Determine its somatic chromosome number and ploidy level. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Given: } n_{\text{gamete}} &= 14 \\ \text{Somatic (2n)} &= 2 \times n_{\text{gamete}} \\ &= 2 \times 14 \\ &= 28 \text{ chromosomes} \\ \text{Base number (x)} &= 7 \ (\text{universal for Triticum}) \\ \text{Ploidy Level} &= \frac{2n}{x} \\ &= \frac{28}{7} \\ &= 4 \\ \therefore \text{Species is TETRAPLOID} &\rightarrow \text{likely } T.\ durum \text{ or } T.\ dicoccum \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Triticum has 7 as its **monoploid base**. Any somatic count divisible by 7 yields an integer ploidy. We check: $42/7 = 6$ (hexaploid, *T. aestivum*), $28/7 = 4$ (tetraploid, *T. durum*), $14/7 = 2$ (diploid, *T. monococcum*). ### 3.2 Derivation: Identifying Commercial Class from Given Parameters > **Problem:** A wheat lot submitted to a quality lab shows: Protein = 14.2%, Vitreousness = 90%, Endosperm = hard & amber-colored, growing season = spring. Identify its commercial class. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Check Ploidy assumption (not given, assume bread wheat)} &\rightarrow T.\ aestivum \\ \text{Step 2: Check Hardness: Hard endosperm} &\rightarrow \text{Hard Red or Hard White} \\ \text{Step 3: Check Color: Amber + red pigment} &\rightarrow \text{Red Series (not white)} \\ \text{Step 4: Check Protein: } 14.2\% &> 13\% \rightarrow \text{STRONG gluten class} \\ \text{Step 5: Check Season: Spring-sown} &\rightarrow \text{Spring Wheat} \\ \therefore \text{Class} &= \textbf{Hard Red Spring (HRS)} \\ &\text{(Used for premium pan breads, bagels, hard rolls)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Correct Class | Property Observation | Likely Class | Justification | |---|---|---| | Kernel vitreous > 75%, amber, used for semolina | **Durum** | Tetraploid, AABB genome | | Kernel soft, white, low protein (~8%), grown in winter | **Soft White Winter (SWW)** | Cake/cookie flour | | Kernel hard, red, protein ~12%, gluten strong, winter | **Hard Red Winter (HRW)** | General purpose bread | | Kernel hard, red, protein > 13%, gluten very strong, spring | **Hard Red Spring (HRS)** | Premium pan bread | | Kernel hard, white (cream), protein ~12%, spring | **Hard White (HW)** | Asian noodles, partial-bake breads | | Hulled, brittle rachis, used as whole grain | **Spelt / Emmer** | Specialty health foods | | Diploid, hulled, 1 grain per spikelet | **Einkorn** | Ancient grain, lower gluten | ### 3.4 Detailed Analytical Comparison: Bread Wheat vs. Durum Wheat $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Parameter} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \textbf{Bread Wheat } (T.\ aestivum) \quad \text{vs.} \quad \textbf{Durum Wheat } (T.\ durum) \\ \text{Chromosome } (2n) \quad &\rightarrow \quad 42 \ \text{vs.} \ 28 \\ \text{Genome} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{AABBDD} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{AABB} \\ \text{Gluten Type} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Elastic, extensible (glutenin + gliadin)} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{Less elastic, high tenacity} \\ \text{Color} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Red / White} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{Amber / Yellow} \\ \text{End Use} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Bread, biscuits, noodles, cakes} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{Pasta, semolina, couscous} \\ \text{Milling Fraction} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Flour (~72%)} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{Semolina (~65–70%)} \\ \text{Puroindoline Genes} \quad &\rightarrow \quad \text{Present (soft → hard)} \ \text{vs.} \ \text{Absent (always hard)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Durum is "Naturally Hard":** The **D-genome** of bread wheat carries the *Puroindoline* (Pin) genes that regulate grain hardness. Durum lacks the D-genome, so it is **constitutively hard** — it cannot be made soft by any breeding or environmental manipulation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Botanical + Commercial) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Wheat Grain Sample] --> B{Determine Species
via Ploidy and Genome} B -->|2n=14, AA| C[T. monococcum
Einkorn - Diploid] B -->|2n=28, AABB| D{Glume Tenacity?} B -->|2n=42, AABBDD| E{Glume Tenacity?} D -->|Hulled| F[T. dicoccum
Emmer] D -->|Free-threshing| G[T. durum
Durum - Tetraploid] E -->|Hulled| H[T. spelta
Spelt - Hulled Hexaploid] E -->|Free-threshing| I{Spike Morphology} I -->|Short Compact| J[T. compactum
Club Wheat] I -->|Long Lax| K[T. aestivum
Bread Wheat] K --> L{End-Use
Classification Begins} L --> M{Color of Bran?} M -->|Red| N[RED SERIES] M -->|White| O[WHITE SERIES] M -->|Amber| P[DURUM - Already Decided] N --> Q{Hardness?} O --> Q Q -->|Hard - Puroindoline absent or mutant| R{Hard Sub-class} Q -->|Soft - Puroindoline wild type| S{Soft Sub-class} R --> T{HRD: Hard Red} R --> U{HRW: Hard Red Winter} R --> V{HRS: Hard Red Spring} R --> W{HW: Hard White} S --> X{SRW: Soft Red Winter} S --> Y{SWW: Soft White Winter} S --> Z{SWS: Soft White Spring} style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style P fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style T fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style U fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style V fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style W fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666 style X fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style Y fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666 style Z fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666 ``` ### 4.2 Indian Commercial Class Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Indian Wheat
Marketed Lots] --> B[SHARBATI Class
Amber, Semi-Hard] A --> C[LOK-1 Class
Hard, Off-white] A --> D[HD-2967 / DURUM
Amber, Hard, Vitreous] A --> E[SONALIKA / LERMA
Soft White] B --> B1[Used for: Premium Atta,
Maida, Suji] C --> C1[Used for: Chapati Atta,
Medium Biscuit] D --> D1[Used for: Semolina,
Pasta, Rawa] E --> E1[Used for: Cake, Pastry,
Biscuit Flour] style A fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b6914,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#faf0e6,stroke:#996633 style D fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b style E fill:#f5f5dc,stroke:#808000 ``` ### 4.3 End-Use Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Need-based Selection]) --> Need1[Want LEAVENED BREAD?] Start --> Need2[Want PASTA / NOODLES?] Start --> Need3[Want CAKES / BISCUITS?] Start --> Need4[Want CHAPATI / ROTI?] Need1 -->|Yes| HRS1[Select HRS or HRW
Protein 12-14%] Need2 -->|Yes| DUR1[Select DURUM
Protein 12-15% Vitreous 80%] Need3 -->|Yes| SW1[Select Soft White or Soft Red
Protein 8-10%] Need4 -->|Yes| MED1[Select Medium Hard / Sharbati
Protein 10-12%] HRS1 --> Outcome1[Strong Gluten Network
CO2 Retention] DUR1 --> Outcome2[Tenacious Gluten
Cooked Firmness] SW1 --> Outcome3[Tender Crumb
Low Water Absorption] MED1 --> Outcome4[Moderate Sheeting
Pliable Dough] style Start fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:3px style HRS1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#990000 style DUR1 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600 style SW1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666 style MED1 fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b6914 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > **"List any three botanical species of wheat with their chromosome number and indicate which is the most commercially important."** **Model Answer:** 1. ***Triticum aestivum*** — 2n = **42** (hexaploid, AABBDD) — most important; occupies >90% of world wheat cultivation; used for bread, biscuits, noodles, cakes. 2. ***Triticum durum*** — 2n = **28** (tetraploid, AABB) — second most important; used exclusively for pasta, semolina, couscous. 3. ***Triticum dicoccum*** (Emmer) — 2n = **28** (tetraploid, AABB, hulled) — niche ancient grain, used in traditional Indian foods (e.g., khichdi, fermented porridges). *(3 marks: 1 mark each species with chromosome number, 0 marks if chromosome omitted)* > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** > **"Differentiate between hard and soft wheat in terms of (i) protein content, (ii) endosperm texture, and (iii) primary end-use."** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Hard Wheat | Soft Wheat | |---|---|---| | (i) Protein content | **11–15%** (high) | **8–11%** (low) | | (ii) Endosperm texture | **Vitreous, horny, translucent** (denser starch-protein matrix) | **Mealy, starchy, opaque** (loose starch granules) | | (iii) Primary end-use | **Bread, hard rolls, bagels** (requires strong gluten) | **Cakes, cookies, pastries, biscuits** (requires tender, low-water dough) | *(3 marks: 1 mark per row)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Explain the **botanical classification of wheat** based on ploidy level and genome. List at least **four species** with their scientific names, ploidy, genome formula, and 2n chromosome number. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Discuss the **commercial classification of wheat** with reference to grain hardness, color, protein content, and growing season. Provide **specific end-uses** for at least four commercial classes. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for (a): > Wheat is classified botanically into species primarily on the basis of **ploidy (chromosome number)**, **genome composition**, and **glume/spike morphology**. The base chromosome number of Triticum is **x = 7**, giving rise to three ploidy groups: **(1) Diploid species (2n = 14, Genome = AA):** - *Triticum monococcum* (Einkorn) — one of the earliest cultivated wheats; remains a niche ancient grain today. Hulled, with brittle rachis. **(2) Tetraploid species (2n = 28, Genome = AABB):** - *Triticum durum* — the most important tetraploid; **free-threshing**, amber-colored, vitreous endosperm. Used for semolina, pasta. - *Triticum dicoccum* (Emmer) — **hulled** tetraploid; cultivated in parts of India and Ethiopia for traditional foods. **(3) Hexaploid species (2n = 42, Genome = AABBDD):** - *Triticum aestivum* — the most cultivated wheat worldwide (~95% of total); free-threshing; gives bread, biscuits, cakes, noodles. - *Triticum compactum* (Club wheat) — short, compact spike; used in crackers and specialty pastries. - *Triticum spelta* (Spelt) — hulled hexaploid; popular in health-food markets. > The **AABBDD hexaploid genome** arose through natural hybridization of *T. dicoccoides* (AABB) and *Aegilops tauschii* (DD) ~10,000 years ago. *(7 marks: 1 mark per species (4 species × 1) + 1 mark ploidy explanation + 1 mark genome + 1 mark evolutionary note)* #### ✅ Model Solution for (b): > Commercial classification is **end-use driven** and based on four criteria: **(1) Grain Hardness (Puroindoline-controlled):** - **Hard wheats** — high protein, vitreous kernels, strong gluten. → for bread. - **Soft wheats** — low protein, mealy kernels, weak gluten. → for cakes. **(2) Bran Color:** - **Red wheats** (dominant *R* gene) — phenolic pigment in seed coat; longer dormancy. - **White wheats** (recessive *r*) — no pigment; milder flavor, used in noodles. **(3) Protein Content:** - **>13%** = Strong wheat; **10–13%** = Medium; **<10%** = Weak wheat. **(4) Growing Season:** - **Winter wheats** — sown in autumn, require vernalization. - **Spring wheats** — sown in spring, no vernalization needed. **Specific end-uses of four major classes:** | Class | Acronym | End-Use | |---|---|---| | Hard Red Winter | **HRW** | Pan bread, hamburger buns, general-purpose flour | | Hard Red Spring | **HRS** | Premium hearth breads, bagels, croissants (highest protein) | | Soft Red Winter | **SRW** | Cookies, crackers, biscuits, pretzels | | Soft White Winter | **SWW** | Cakes, pastries, Asian noodles, steamed buns | | Durum | — | Semolina, pasta, couscous, bulgur (no class prefix) | *(7 marks: 2 marks hardness + 1 mark color + 1 mark protein + 1 mark season + 2 marks end-uses)* --- > **QUESTION B — Alternative Choice [KTU Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Describe the **Indian system of wheat classification** as per ICAR / FSSAI standards. Compare **Sharbati, Lok-1, HD-2967, and Sonalika** wheats. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Explain in detail the **chemical composition of wheat kernel** (bran, endosperm, germ fractions) and show how composition influences **commercial class assignment**. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for (a): > ICAR (through IIWBR, Karnal) classifies Indian wheat into the following commercial groups based on **grain color, hardness, and end-use**: **(1) Sharbati** — amber/hard, semi-hard endosperm, grown mainly in MP. Used for premium atta, suji, rawa. Protein ~10–12%. **(2) Lok-1** (also HD-2189 cross-derived) — hard, off-white, medium-strength. Widely used for chapati atta. Protein ~10–11%. **(3) HD-2967 / Durum varieties (PDW-233, WHD-943)** — amber, fully hard, vitreous, high protein (~12–14%). Used for semolina and pasta. Cultivated in Punjab, MP, Karnataka. **(4) Sonalika / Lerma Rojo** — soft white, low protein (~8–10%). Used for biscuits, cookies, weaning foods. Grown in eastern and peninsular India. > The **FSSAI grading** also requires that wheat for human consumption must have: > - Moisture < 14% > - Foreign matter < 1% > - Damaged grains < 2% > - Weevilled grains < 1% *(7 marks: 4 marks for 4 varieties × 1 + 1 mark protein table + 2 marks FSSAI limits)* #### ✅ Model Solution for (b): > The wheat kernel is a **caryopsis** (single-seeded fruit) comprising three distinct anatomical fractions: **(1) Bran (~14–16% of kernel weight):** - Outer pericarp, testa, aleurone. - **Composition**: cellulose, hemicellulose, B-vitamins (thiamine, niacin), minerals (Fe, Zn, Se), phenolic acids. - High fiber → *whole wheat flour* has more dietary fiber than refined flour. **(2) Endosperm (~80–85%):** - Starch granules (70–75% of endosperm), proteins (10–14%, mainly gluten). - **Sub-fraction**: aleurone layer (outermost, protein-rich) vs. starchy endosperm. - **Hard wheats** = vitreous endosperm (protein-starch matrix is continuous). - **Soft wheats** = mealy endosperm (loose starch granules, air pockets). - This is the principal **milling fraction** → 72% flour yield. **(3) Germ (~2–3%):** - Embryo; rich in lipids (8–10% of germ), tocopherols (Vit. E), B-vitamins, enzymes (lipase, protease). - Removed during milling → extends shelf life of refined flour. > **Influence on Commercial Class:** > - High protein + vitreous endosperm → Hard class (HRW/HRS) → suitable for **bread**. > - Low protein + mealy endosperm → Soft class (SRW/SWW) → suitable for **cakes**. > - Very high protein + amber + vitreous → Durum class → suitable for **pasta**. *(7 marks: 2 marks bran + 2 marks endosperm + 1 mark germ + 2 marks class linkage)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. **Writing "T. aestivum" without chromosome number (2n = 42)** → lose 1 mark even if rest is correct. > 2. **Confusing "hexaploid" with "polyploid" in general** → be specific: hexaploid = 6x; tetraploid = 4x. > 3. **Writing "HRS = 42 chromosomes, Durum = 28" but forgetting genome formulas** → examiners expect AABBDD / AABB explicitly. > 4. **Mixing up "vitreous" (protein-rich, hard) and "mealy" (starch-rich, soft)** — a classic 1-mark error. > 5. **Failing to state the *Puroindoline gene* connection to grain hardness** → important 1-mark "reason why" in 2024 scheme questions. > 6. **Confusing wheat protein factor (5.7) with general food factor (6.25)** → costs 0.5–1 mark in numerical problems. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 📋 **WHEAT CLASSIFICATION — Quick-Fire Revision** - ⭐ **Botanical classification is genetic (ploidy/genome); commercial classification is functional (end-use).** - ⭐ **Three ploidy levels exist in Triticum: 2x, 4x, 6x (base x = 7).** - ⭐ **T. aestivum (2n=42, AABBDD)** = bread wheat → ~95% of world production. - ⭐ **T. durum (2n=28, AABB)** = pasta wheat → ~5% of world production, constitutively hard. - ⭐ **D-genome carries Puroindoline genes** (absent in durum → always hard). - ⭐ **Hard wheat = vitreous + high protein + strong gluten → bread.** - ⭐ **Soft wheat = mealy + low protein + weak gluten → cakes, cookies.** - ⭐ **Color split: Red (phenolic pigment, dominant R) vs. White (recessive r).** - ⭐ **Season split: Winter (vernalization needed) vs. Spring (no vernalization).** - ⭐ **Five major commercial classes in USA: HRW, HRS, SRW, HRS, Durum, White classes.** - ⭐ **Indian classes: Sharbati (amber hard), Lok-1 (medium), HD-2967 (durum), Sonalika (soft white).** - ⭐ **Protein factor for wheat = 5.7 (not 6.25).** - ⭐ **Strong gluten: > 30% wet gluten, Gluten Index > 70, Zeleny SV > 30 mL.** - ⭐ **Gluten = gliadin (extensibility) + glutenin (elasticity).** - ⭐ **Hulled wheats (spelt, emmer, einkorn) require dehulling; bread & durum are free-threshing.** - ⭐ **FSSAI limits: Moisture < 14%, Foreign matter < 1%, Damaged grains < 2%.** - ⭐ **Cluster of 14 chromosome count → tetraploid; 42 → hexaploid (always check: count ÷ 7 = ploidy).** - ⭐ **Test weight (hectoliter weight) > 76 kg/hL is the standard soundness marker for Grade-1 wheat.**

topics.title: Milling of Wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Milling of Wheat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wheat Milling** is the industrial process of transforming cleaned, conditioned wheat kernels (*Triticum aestivum* — common bread wheat, or *Triticum durum* — macaroni wheat) into refined endosperm flour by sequential mechanical operations — primarily **cleaning**, **conditioning (tempering)**, **breaking**, **scratching**, **sizing**, and **reduction** — using corrugated and smooth roller mills, sifters, and purifiers, while separating the **bran** (pericarp + testa + aleurone), **germ** (embryo), and **endosperm** (starchy protein matrix) into defined mill streams of graded purity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the unit operation of wheat milling is classified under **Cereals Processing** and is benchmarked by the **Codex Alimentarius** flour grade standards, the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011 (FSSAI)**, and the **Prevention of Food Adulteration (PFA) Act** definitions for *atta*, *maida*, *semolina*, and *whole wheat flour*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Onion Peeling" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Peeling an Onion in Layers**
> Imagine a wheat kernel as a tightly wrapped onion where each skin layer is a different botanical tissue. The miller's job is to:
> 1. **Wash and soften** the outer layers (cleaning & conditioning).
> 2. **Crack open** the kernel in stages (breaking rolls).
> 3. **Gently scrape** the soft white interior off the skins (scratch rolls).
> 4. **Sieve** each flake by size (plansifters) and **blow away** the light chaff (purifiers).
> 5. Finally, **grind** the purified white flakes into fine flour (reduction rolls).
>
> The miller is essentially a "surgical onion-peeler" who wants every layer separated cleanly without contaminating the pure white heart with brown skin dust.

## 1.3 The Wheat Kernel — Anatomical Blueprint

The wheat kernel (caryopsis) has three principal anatomical fractions, each with distinct chemical and mechanical properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Fractions of the Wheat Kernel (by mass):**
> - **Endosperm (~80–83 %)** — starchy, protein-rich, white, the desired flour component.
> - **Bran (~14–16 %)** — multi-layered pericarp, testa, and aleurone, high in fiber, B-vitamins, and minerals.
> - **Germ (~2–3 %)** — embryo, lipid-rich (~10 % fat), enzyme-active, removed to extend flour shelf-life.

**Standard Reference Constants (bolded for exam recall):**

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (harvested) | **10–14 %** | Determines tempering water addition |
| Hard vitreous kernel count | **> 75 %** = Hard wheat | High protein (>11 %) |
| Test weight | **> 76 kg/hL** | Grade 1 wheat under FSSAI |
| Thousand-kernel weight (TKW) | **30–45 g** | Indicator of milling yield |

## 1.4 Why Milling is Performed — Engineering Objectives

- **Separation efficiency:** Maximize endosperm recovery while minimizing bran/germ contamination.
- **Particle size control:** Achieve flour granulation in the **75–150 µm** range.
- **Starch damage management:** Target **Damaged Starch = 7–9 %** (Farinograph-optimal for bread).
- **Flour color (L\* value):** Target **L\* > 90** on Hunter colorimeter for refined flour.
- **Microbial reduction:** Milling lowers surface microbial load by 1–2 log cycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Milling Flow

Wheat milling is executed as a strictly ordered, counter-current flow consisting of **six operational stages**. The 2024 Scheme NEP module credits weightage to each, with highest marks (≈40 %) on **Breaking** and **Reduction** systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Sequence:**
> 1. **Intake & Pre-cleaning** (sieving, magnetic separation, destoning).
> 2. **Conditioning / Tempering** (water addition + rest period).
> 3. **First Break (B1) → Sixth Break (B6)** with intermediate sifters.
> 4. **Scratch System** (S1, S2) — light scraping of bran.
> 5. **Purification** (aspiration + sieve stratification).
> 6. **Reduction System (C1 → C12)** + **Grader / Finisher**.

## 2.2 Stage 1 — Pre-Cleaning and Wheat Grading

- **Magnetic separator** removes ferrous fragments.
- **Drum separator / disc separator** length-grades wheat.
- **Gravity table (destoner)** separates stones by **specific gravity (ρ_wheat ≈ 1.35 g/cm³ vs ρ_stone ≈ 2.60 g/cm³)**.
- **Aspirator** removes light impurities (dust, chaff, broken kernels).
- **Scourer / Entoleter** abrades surface microbes and insect eggs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleaning efficiency is benchmarked by:**
> - **SPC (Seconds per Centimetre)** for sieving intensity.
> - **Aspiration ratio (light fraction removed / total feed) = 0.5–1.5 %**.

## 2.3 Stage 2 — Conditioning (Tempering)

Tempering raises wheat moisture to a target level so the **bran becomes pliable** and the **endosperm becomes brittle**, allowing clean separation.

| Wheat Type | Target Moisture | Rest Time | Rest Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard wheat | **16.0–17.0 %** | 18–24 h | 20–25 °C |
| Soft wheat | **14.5–15.5 %** | 12–18 h | 20–25 °C |
| Durum wheat | **16.5–17.5 %** | 24–36 h | 25–30 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-stage tempering** (warm + cold) is preferred: first warm tempering at 35–40 °C for 1–2 h to drive water into the bran, then cold tempering at ambient for 16–24 h to equilibrate moisture to the endosperm.

## 2.4 Stage 3 — The Break System (B1 → B6)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Break rolls are fluted (corrugated) rolls** that crack open the kernel and scrape endosperm off the bran. The corrugation count, spiral (lead), differential, and roll gap are progressively varied.

| Break | Roll Gap (mm) | Differential (Fast:Slow) | Spiral (mm/m) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B1** | 0.7–0.9 | 2.5 : 1 | 8–10 | Coarse cracking, max release |
| **B2** | 0.4–0.5 | 2.0 : 1 | 6–8 | Further scraping |
| **B3** | 0.2–0.3 | 1.75 : 1 | 5–7 | Bran starts forming |
| **B4–B6** | < 0.15 | 1.5 : 1 | 4–5 | Gentle brushing of bran |

- **Differential** = speed ratio of fast roll to slow roll. Higher differential = more scraping action.
- **Spiral (lead)** of corrugation determines the angle of cut; lower spiral at later breaks (less aggressive).
- **Sharp-to-sharp** (S-S) for hard wheat; **Dull-to-dull (D-D)** for soft wheat.

## 2.5 Stage 4 — The Sifter and Purifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Sifters (Plansifters)** are gyratory sieving machines with stacked silk/nylon or metal wire meshes. Each mesh has a precise **aperture size in microns** corresponding to a flour grade.

- **Sieve aperture rule:** 1,400 µm (scalping) → 1,000 µm → 700 µm → 500 µm → 250 µm → 150 µm.
- **Purifier** combines **sieving + air aspiration** to remove fine bran powder (called *bran powder* or *bee-hive* material) from the semolina.

## 2.6 Stage 5 — Reduction System (C1 → C12)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reduction rolls are smooth (non-fluted) rolls** that progressively grind purified endosperm chunks into flour with **minimal starch damage** and **minimal bran powder contamination**.

- Roll speeds are **near-equal (differential 1.0–1.25 : 1)** — the action is compression, not scraping.
- Each reduction step is followed by a sifter to separate the resulting flour from the residual stock.
- **C1 flour** is the highest-grade, highest-purity stream.
- **C9–C12 flours** contain more bran contamination and are diverted to *low-grade flour* (used for biscuits, feed).
