## 2.7 Stage 6 — Grading, Blending and Bagging

- Flour streams are pneumatically conveyed to a **blending bin** where proportional streams yield a final product meeting the **ash specification**.
- The blended flour passes through a **finisher** (impact mill) for final granulation control.
- A **magnet** and **detector** perform final metal/Foreign Body (FB) removal.
- Flour is **bulk-loaded** or **bagged (50 kg / 25 kg)** and may be **fortified** with Fe, Folic acid, Vitamin B12 (per FSSAI 2018 fortification mandate).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations. They appear directly in KTU Part B numericals.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{add} = W \times \dfrac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t}$ | Water addition for tempering | kg water / kg wheat |
| 2 | $E_R = \dfrac{M_{flour}}{M_{wheat\_cleaned}} \times 100$ | Extraction rate | % |
| 3 | $A_F = A_B \times E_R \times 10^{-2}$ | Ash balance (Keller's equation) | % db |
| 4 | $L^* = 116 \left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{1/3} - 16$ | CIE Lab L\* (whiteness) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $DS = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1}\right)$ | Damaged starch (AACC 76-31) | % |
| 6 | $Y_{endosperm} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{A_F - A_{endo}}{A_{bran} - A_F}}$ | Endosperm yield from ash data | % |
| 7 | $N_{teeth} = \dfrac{60 \times v_f}{\pi \times D \times \tan\beta}$ | Flute count (break roll) | flutes |
| 8 | $P_{roll} = \mu \times F_N \times v_{rel}$ | Power consumed by roll pair | W |
| 9 | $TKW = \dfrac{1000 \times M_{1000}}{N_{1000}}$ | Thousand-kernel weight | g |
| 10 | $PE = \dfrac{M_{endosperm\_recovered}}{M_{endosperm\_in\_wheat}} \times 100$ | Process efficiency | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables key:**
> - $M_t$ = target moisture, $M_i$ = initial moisture, $W$ = wheat mass.
> - $A_F, A_B, A_{endo}$ = ash of flour, bran, pure endosperm (% db).
> - $v_f$ = feed velocity, $D$ = roll diameter, $\beta$ = spiral angle.
> - $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $F_N$ = normal force, $v_{rel}$ = relative roll velocity.
> - $C_1, C_2$ = current in amperes (Farrand method for damaged starch).

## 2.9 Why This Knowledge Matters in Industry

- **Millers' extraction rate** drives profit: an extra 1 % extraction ≈ ₹15–25 per quintal of revenue.
- **Ash control** defines flour grade: < 0.55 % for *maida* (FSSAI), 0.7–1.0 % for *atta*.
- **Starch damage** affects baking: low damage → weak dough; high damage → dark crumb.
- **Stock flow balancing** (B, S, C, D, M streams) is the miller's daily optimization problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tempering Water Addition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the formula for the mass of water to be added to a known wheat mass to raise its moisture content to a target level.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- $W$ = mass of wheat to be tempered (kg).
- $M_i$ = initial moisture of wheat (% w.b.).
- $M_t$ = target moisture of wheat (% w.b.).
- $W_{add}$ = mass of water to be added (kg).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define moisture content (wet basis).**

$$M = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{water} + m_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Apply the conservation of dry matter.**

The dry mass of wheat does not change during tempering; only water is added.

$$m_{dry} = W \times \dfrac{100 - M_i}{100}$$

**Step 3 — Express the total mass after tempering.**

After adding $W_{add}$ kg of water, the total mass becomes $W + W_{add}$, and the moisture is $M_t$.

$$M_t = \dfrac{W_{add} + W \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{100}}{W + W_{add}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic rearrangement.**

Multiply both sides by $(W + W_{add}) / 100$:

$$W_{add} + W \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{100} = \dfrac{M_t (W + W_{add})}{100}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $W_{add}$.**

Multiply through by 100:

$$100 W_{add} + W M_i = M_t W + M_t W_{add}$$

Collect $W_{add}$ terms:

$$100 W_{add} - M_t W_{add} = M_t W - W M_i$$

$$W_{add}(100 - M_t) = W (M_t - M_i)$$

**Step 6 — Final formula.**

$$\boxed{W_{add} = W \times \dfrac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t}}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $W = 1000 \text{ kg}, M_i = 11.0\,\%, M_t = 16.5\,\%$.
> **Substitute:**
>
> $$W_{add} = 1000 \times \dfrac{16.5 - 11.0}{100 - 16.5} = 1000 \times \dfrac{5.5}{83.5} = 65.87 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** About **65.9 kg of water** must be added per tonne of wheat. This is ≈ **6.6 %** addition — a typical plant value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Keller's Ash Balance Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Show how ash content of flour can be back-calculated from a known extraction rate and the ash content of the parent wheat — a direct KTU 2024 exam favourite.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let:
- $A_B$ = ash of clean wheat (branny wheat) on dry basis, %.
- $A_{endo}$ = ash of pure endosperm, %.
- $A_{bran}$ = ash of pure bran fraction, %.
- $A_F$ = ash of resulting flour, %.
- $E_R$ = extraction rate (fraction, 0–1).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass balance of ash.**

Total ash in wheat = ash in flour + ash in offals (bran + shorts).

$$W_{wheat} \cdot A_B = W_{flour} \cdot A_F + W_{offal} \cdot A_{offal}$$

**Step 2 — Express offal in terms of flour and extraction.**

By definition, $E_R = W_{flour} / W_{wheat}$, so $W_{offal} = W_{wheat}(1 - E_R)$.

$$W_{wheat} A_B = W_{wheat} E_R A_F + W_{wheat}(1 - E_R) A_{offal}$$

**Step 3 — Use linear blend assumption for offal ash.**

The offal's ash lies between $A_{endo}$ and $A_{bran}$ as a weighted function of contamination. Keller simplified by assuming $A_{offal}$ can be expressed as:

$$A_{offal} = \dfrac{A_B - E_R A_F}{1 - E_R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange for $A_F$.**

After algebraic simplification (full expansion shown):

$$A_F = \dfrac{A_B - (1 - E_R) A_{offal}}{E_R}$$

**Step 5 — Practical inverse form used by millers.**

For routine calculations, a useful corollary is:

$$\boxed{E_R = \dfrac{A_B - A_{offal}}{A_F - A_{offal}}}$$

> This is the form examiners love. If $A_B$ and $A_{offal}$ are known, $E_R$ can be computed from a single $A_F$ measurement.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Verification

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** $A_B = 1.65\,\%$, $A_{offal} = 4.20\,\%$, $A_F = 0.55\,\%$ (target *maida* ash).
> **Compute:**
>
> $$E_R = \dfrac{1.65 - 4.20}{0.55 - 4.20} = \dfrac{-2.55}{-3.65} = 0.6986 = \mathbf{69.9\,\%}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** To produce a *maida* of 0.55 % ash, the mill must operate at an extraction of ≈ **70 %**, leaving ≈ 30 % as offals. This is consistent with FSSAI *maida* specifications.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Damaged Starch (Farrand Equation)

**Step 1 — Empirical basis (AACC 76-31).**

Starch damage increases the susceptibility of granules to fungal alpha-amylase hydrolysis, releasing reducing sugars that reduce Fe³⁺ (ferricyanide) to Fe²⁺.

**Step 2 — Farrand equation.**

The percentage damaged starch is empirically fitted to a current reading in the Farrand amperometric apparatus:

$$DS = k_1 - k_2 \cdot C_2$$

where $k_1, k_2$ are constants from a calibration table (e.g., $k_1 = 5.4, k_2 = 0.02$ for standard Farrand). For KTU exam, the simpler fractional form suffices:

$$\boxed{DS = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}\right)}$$

where $C_1$ = blank current (no damage), $C_2$ = sample current.

### 3.3.1 Numerical Check

> [!NOTE]
> $C_1 = 9.8 \text{ mA}, C_2 = 9.0 \text{ mA}$.
>
> $$DS = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{9.0}{9.8}\right) = 100 \times 0.0816 = \mathbf{8.16\,\%}$$
>
> This is within the **7–9 % target** for bread flour — perfect for Hearth/pan bread.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Wheat Stream Composition

| Stream | Approx. % of Wheat | Ash (%) | Protein (%) | Granulation (µm) | Destination |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B1 Flour** | 4–6 | 0.40 | 9.5 | 100–150 | Premium break flour |
| **B2 Flour** | 4–7 | 0.45 | 10.0 | 100–150 | Patent flour |
| **B3 Flour** | 3–5 | 0.55 | 11.0 | 150–200 | Clear flour |
| **C1 Flour** | 8–12 | 0.42 | 9.0 | < 100 | Patent flour (creamy) |
| **C2–C4** | 15–22 | 0.50 | 10.5 | 80–120 | Straight-run flour |
| **C5–C8** | 8–10 | 0.70 | 12.0 | 80–120 | First clear flour |
| **C9–C12** | 5–8 | 1.40 | 14.0 | 80–120 | Low-grade (biscuit) |
| **Bran** | 14–16 | 5.5 | 14.5 | flakes | Animal feed / bran oil |
| **Shorts** | 4–6 | 3.5 | 14.0 | granular | Feed / pellet |
| **Germ** | 2–3 | 4.0 | 25.0 | flaky | Germ oil extraction |
| **Wheatgerm meal** | 1–2 | 4.5 | 28.0 | powder | Health food |

> [!WARNING]
> The numbers above are **plant-average industrial values** and may shift ±0.3 % for ash, ±1 % for protein, depending on wheat variety and roll gap.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
> *Define the term "extraction rate" in wheat milling. What is the standard extraction rate for "maida" and "whole wheat atta" as per FSSAI regulations?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Extraction rate** is the percentage of flour obtained from cleaned wheat by weight, expressed as
>
> $$E_R = \dfrac{M_{flour}}{M_{wheat\_cleaned}} \times 100$$
>
> **FSSAI specifications (FSSAI, 2011):**
> - **Maida (refined wheat flour):** 70–72 % extraction with ash content < 0.55 % db.
> - **Whole wheat atta:** ≈ 95–98 % extraction, ash 1.5–1.8 % db.
> - **Semolina (suji/rava):** 5–10 % of cleaned wheat.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> *Differentiate between break rolls and reduction rolls in a roller flour mill. Mention any four points of difference.*

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Parameter | Break Rolls (B) | Reduction Rolls (C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface | Corrugated (fluted) | Smooth (polished) |
| 2 | Differential | High (2.5 : 1) | Low (1.0–1.25 : 1) |
| 3 | Action | Crushing + scraping | Compression + grinding |
| 4 | Position | First stage | Last stage |
| 5 | Stock handled | Whole kernels → chunks | Purified semolina → flour |
| 6 | Number in plant | 4–6 | 8–12 |
| 7 | Spiral/lead | High → Low across B1–B6 | N/A (smooth) |
| 8 | Objective | Release endosperm from bran | Reduce endosperm to flour |

## 5.2 Part B — Essay-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
> *(a) Describe the wheat conditioning (tempering) process with the objectives and methods. Derive the formula for water addition and compute the water required for tempering 10 tonnes of wheat from 11 % to 16.5 % moisture (wet basis). (7 marks)*
> *(b) Draw a labeled flow diagram of a modern roller flour mill and explain the function of each break pass (B1–B6). (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objectives of tempering:**
> 1. To **soften the bran** so it does not shatter into fine particles that contaminate flour.
> 2. To **harden the endosperm** (especially in soft wheats) so it fractures cleanly into large semolina.
> 3. To **equalize moisture** within and between kernels.
> 4. To **loosen the germ** from the endosperm for easier separation.
> 5. To **reduce power draw** at the first break (less binding).

**Methods:**
- **Cold tempering:** Add water, hold 12–24 h at 20–25 °C. Most common.
- **Warm tempering:** 35–40 °C for 1–2 h, then cold tempering. Faster moisture penetration.
- **Hot tempering:** 46–52 °C for short periods; reduces microbial load.
- **Two-stage tempering:** First to 14 %, rest 6 h; then to 16.5 %, rest 18 h. Best practice.

**Derivation of water addition formula:**

> Following the derivation in Section 3.1.2:
>
> $$W_{add} = W \times \dfrac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t}$$

**Numerical computation:**

> $W = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}, M_i = 11.0\,\%, M_t = 16.5\,\%$
>
> $$W_{add} = 10{,}000 \times \dfrac{16.5 - 11.0}{100 - 16.5} = 10{,}000 \times \dfrac{5.5}{83.5}$$
>
> $$W_{add} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0659 = \mathbf{658.7 \text{ kg}}$$

> **Answer:** ≈ **659 kg of water** must be added to 10 tonnes of wheat to raise moisture to 16.5 %.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Flow diagram:** Refer to the Block Diagram in **Section 4.1** above for the complete architecture.

**Functions of break passes:**

| Break | Function |
|---|---|
| **B1** | Coarse opening; releases 15–20 % of endosperm as large semolina; coarset flutes (10 flutes/inch). |
| **B2** | Scrapes more endosperm; releases another 25–30 %; branny particles begin to flatten. |
| **B3** | Reduces semolina to medium; branny pieces flatten into flakes. |
| **B4** | Gentle scraping; minimal endosperm left; bran flakes form clearly. |
| **B5** | Final scraping of bran; very small gap, slow spiral. |
| **B6** | Polishing of bran to maximize recovery; sent to feed/shorts. |

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
> *(a) Explain the concept of extraction rate, ash content, and damaged starch in wheat flour. How are they inter-related? (7 marks)*
> *(b) A mill produces 700 kg of flour from 1000 kg of cleaned wheat. If the ash content of the flour is 0.55 % (db) and the ash content of the offals is 4.20 % (db), determine the ash content of the parent wheat using Keller's equation. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition triplet:**
>
> - **Extraction Rate (E_R):** Percentage of cleaned wheat that emerges as flour. Commercial ranges:
>   - *Patent flour:* 65–70 %
>   - *Straight-run flour:* 72–78 %
>   - *Whole wheat atta:* 95–98 %
>
> - **Ash Content (A_F):** Mineral residue (% db) left after incineration at 550 °C for 6 h. Endosperm has 0.30 % ash; bran has 5.5–6.0 % ash. Therefore, ash is a **proxy for bran contamination** of flour.
>
> - **Damaged Starch (DS):** Percentage of starch granules physically ruptured during milling. Measured by the Farrand amperometric method (AACC 76-31) or the Chopin SDmatic. Target for bread: 7–9 %; for biscuits: 4–6 %.

**Inter-relationship:**

$$\underbrace{A_F \uparrow}_{\text{ash rises}} \iff \underbrace{E_R \uparrow}_{\text{extraction rises}} \iff \underbrace{DS \uparrow}_{\text{damage rises}}$$

Higher extraction means more bran and germ in the flour, raising ash and possibly increasing damaged starch due to more aggressive later breaks. The miller balances these by:

1. **Stream selection** (excluding C9–C12 from the blend).
2. **Tightening roll gap** (reduces damage but reduces capacity).
3. **Purity control at purifiers** (lower ash, lower DS).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $W_{wheat} = 1000 \text{ kg}$
- $W_{flour} = 700 \text{ kg}$
- $E_R = 0.70$
- $A_F = 0.55\,\% \text{ db}$
- $A_{offal} = 4.20\,\% \text{ db}$

**Keller's equation (rearranged for $A_B$):**

$$E_R = \dfrac{A_B - A_{offal}}{A_F - A_{offal}}$$

**Solve:**

$$0.70 = \dfrac{A_B - 4.20}{0.55 - 4.20}$$

$$0.70 \times (-3.65) = A_B - 4.20$$

$$-2.555 = A_B - 4.20$$

$$A_B = 4.20 - 2.555 = \mathbf{1.645\,\% \text{ db}}$$

> **Answer:** The ash content of the parent cleaned wheat is **≈ 1.65 % (db)**, which is typical of a hard Indian wheat variety (e.g., Lokwan, Sharbati).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently sign-mishandle the denominator $(A_F - A_{offal})$ which is **negative** because $A_F < A_{offal}$. Forgetting the negative sign leads to a negative $A_B$, which is unphysical. Always re-check: ash of clean wheat should lie between 1.4 % and 1.9 % db.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Examiner's Eye-View):**
> 1. **Confusing dry basis (db) and wet basis (wb)** ash values — the formula is sensitive to this. Always convert if necessary: $A_{db} = A_{wb} / (1 - M)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 100-multiplier** in the extraction rate formula — many students write $E_R = W_f / W_w$ without the ×100, getting 0.70 instead of 70 %.
> 3. **Calling break rolls "smooth"** — they are fluted; this is a recurring, easily avoidable 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing up *maida* and *atta* specifications** — *maida* = < 0.55 % ash, 70 % extraction; *atta* = 1.5–1.8 % ash, 95–98 % extraction.
> 5. **Ignoring units in numerical problems** — write kg, %, % db explicitly.
> 6. **Diagrams without arrows** — flow direction must be shown, otherwise 0.5 mark is clipped per the KTU 2024 valuation key.
> 7. **In tempering formula, dividing by 100 − M_t** — students often mistakenly divide by 100 − M_i. This is a recurring 2-mark deduction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - Wheat kernel = **83 % endosperm, 14 % bran, 3 % germ** (mass basis).
> - Milling = **cleaning → conditioning → breaking → sifting → purifying → reducing → blending**.
> - **Tempering water formula:** $W_{add} = W(M_t - M_i)/(100 - M_t)$.
> - Hard wheat: temper to **16.5 %** for 18–24 h; soft wheat: 14.5–15.5 % for 12–18 h.
> - **Break rolls** are fluted; **reduction rolls** are smooth; **scratch rolls** (S1, S2) are fine-fluted for soft branny stocks.
> - **Differential** of break rolls: **2.5 : 1 (B1) → 1.5 : 1 (B6)**; reduction rolls: **1.0–1.25 : 1**.
> - **Spiral (lead)** of corrugation decreases from 8 mm/m (B1) to 4 mm/m (B6).
> - **Keller's equation:** $E_R = (A_B - A_{offal})/(A_F - A_{offal})$.
> - **Maida:** < 0.55 % ash, 70 % extraction. **Atta:** 1.5–1.8 % ash, 95–98 % extraction.
> - **Damaged starch target:** 7–9 % for bread; 4–6 % for biscuits.
> - **Sieve apertures** in plansifter: 1400, 1000, 700, 500, 250, 150 µm.
> - **B1, C1 flour** = highest-grade *patent* flour. **C9–C12** = *low-grade* / biscuit flour.
> - **Bran** is sold as feed or for bran oil extraction (rich in tocopherol).
> - **Germ** is rich in lipids (10 % fat) and is removed to extend flour shelf-life.
> - **Two-stage tempering** (warm + cold) is industry best practice.
> - **Damaged starch** measured by AACC 76-31 (Farrand method) or Chopin SDmatic.
> - **Power** consumed by a roll pair: $P = \mu F_N v_{rel}$.
> - **Test weight** > 76 kg/hL indicates Grade 1 wheat under FSSAI.
> - **TKW** of 30–45 g is typical for healthy Indian bread wheats.
> - **FSSAI fortification (2018):** Fe (28–42.5 mg/kg), Folic acid (1.0 mg/kg), Vit B12 (0.75 µg/kg) for *maida* and *atta*.
> - **Extraction rate** drives profit: each 1 % extraction = ₹15–25/quintal revenue uplift.
> - **Millers' ash balance** uses Keller's equation for daily quality monitoring.
> - **Stream blending** is the modern way to guarantee ash specifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipments Used in Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipments Used in Milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Milling Equipments

**Milling Equipments** refer to the comprehensive set of mechanical, pneumatic, and electrical machines used in the food processing industry to transform raw cereal grains (wheat, rice, maize, oats, barley) and legume seeds (lentils, chickpeas, beans, peas) into finished or semi-finished products such as flour, semolina, grits, dhal, splits, and dehusked whole grains.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 OEFTT836 syllabus**, milling equipment is broadly classified into **five functional categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (Module 2):**
> "Milling equipments are specialized machinery and unit operations employed in the dry/wet processing of cereals and legumes, encompassing cleaning, conditioning, dehulling, size reduction, separation, and grading, designed to achieve desired particle size distribution, purity, and end-product quality."

| **S.No** | **Functional Category** | **Primary Purpose** |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Cleaning & Grading Equipments | Removal of foreign matter, dust, stones, and immature grains |
| 2 | Conditioning Equipments | Moisture addition and tempering to toughen bran |
| 3 | Dehulling / Hulling Equipments | Removal of outer husk/hull from grains |
| 4 | Size Reduction Equipments | Particle size reduction (crushing, grinding, pulverizing) |
| 5 | Separation & Sifting Equipments | Classification by particle size, shape, and density |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Kitchen to Factory Transformation**
>
> Imagine you have a sack of raw wheat brought directly from a farm. It contains sand, tiny stones, straw, dust, broken grains, insects, and the grain kernels — all mixed together. If you tried to bake a cake with this raw material, the bread would be gritty, contaminated, and unfit to eat.
>
> **Milling equipment is essentially a "factory-scale cleaning and transformation kitchen":**
> - **Magnetic Separators & Destoners** → act like a metal detector and a vibrating filter tray, removing metal shavings and stones.
> - **Dampeners & Tempering Bins** → similar to soaking rice overnight to soften the husk.
> - **Hullers & Polishers** → analogous to rubbing lentils between your palms to peel the skin off.
> - **Roller Mills & Hammer Mills** → function like a heavy rolling pin that progressively crushes grain into fine flour.
> - **Plansifters & Purifiers** → work like nested kitchen sieves that separate flour from bran.

Each piece of equipment has a **dedicated role**, and the entire milling process is a **sequential flow** where the output of one machine becomes the input of the next.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics Used in Milling Equipments:**
> - **Standard Feed Rate (Wheat):** 0.5 to 12 tonnes/hour per roller mill (industrial scale).
> - **Particle Size:** Finished wheat flour — less than **180 µm**; semolina — between 200–500 µm.
> - **Moisture Content (Tempered Wheat):** 14.5 to 16.5 % (w.b.) for soft wheat; up to 17 % for hard wheat.
> - **Aspiration Velocity:** 4 to 25 m/s (depends on grain density).
> - **Roll Surface Speed (Roller Mill):** Differential ratio of **1.25 : 1 to 2.5 : 1** (Fast roll : Slow roll).
> - **Rotation Speed (Hammer Mill):** 1500 to 4000 rpm.
> - **Ambient Air Relative Humidity for Tempering:** 60 to 75 %.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Classification of Milling Equipments

#### **Category 1: Cleaning Equipments**

Cleaning is the **first unit operation** in any milling process. The objective is to remove impurities categorized as:
- **Foreign seeds** (weed seeds, other grain species)
- **Inorganic matter** (stones, sand, metal pieces)
- **Organic contaminants** (straw, husks, dust)
- **Damaged/immature grains**

**Key Equipments:**

**(a) Magnetic Separator**
- Uses **permanent magnets** or **electromagnets** with field strength of **50–400 mT (milliTesla)**.
- Traps ferrous metal contaminants.
- Installed at the receiving conveyor.

**(b) Destoner (Specific Gravity Separator)**
- Works on the principle of **density difference**.
- Uses a **vibrating inclined deck** with air flowing upward through a wire-mesh screen.
- Stones settle at the bottom; grains float to the top and are discharged forward.
- Air velocity: **0.5 to 5 m/s**.

**(c) Disk Separator (Indent Cylinder / Trieur)**
- A **rotating horizontal cylinder** with internal indented pockets.
- Indents lift **short grains** out of the long-grain stream.
- Pocket sizes: **1.6 to 25 mm**.

**(d) Aspirator (Air Classifier)**
- Removes **light impurities** (dust, chaff, empty grains) using upward air stream.
- **Terminal velocity** of grain is the design parameter.

**(e) Gravity Separator (Gravity Table)**
- Vibrating inclined table with **perforated deck**; air blown upward.
- Separates by **specific gravity** differences.

---

#### **Category 2: Conditioning Equipments**

**(a) Dampener (Water Addition Unit)**
- Adds controlled quantity of water (typically **0.5 to 3 %** by weight) to the grain.
- Uses **rotary water jets** or **atomizing spray nozzles**.

**(b) Tempering Bin (Resting Bin)**
- Cylindrical or rectangular bin where dampened grain rests for **8 to 24 hours**.
- Allows **moisture migration** to grain interior.

**Conditioning Formula:**

$$M_f = \frac{(100 - M_i)}{(100 - M_t)} \times 100$$

Where:
- $M_f$ = Final mass of grain after water addition (kg)
- $M_i$ = Initial mass of dry grain (kg)
- $M_t$ = Target moisture content (% w.b.)

---

#### **Category 3: Dehulling / Hulling Equipments**

**Hulling** is the removal of the **outer husk** (lemma + palea) from grains like rice, oats, and barley. **Dehulling** in legumes refers to removal of the **seed coat**.

**(a) Rubber Roll Huller (Rice)**
- Two rubber rolls rotating in opposite directions.
- Differential speed: **2:1 to 3:1**; roll gap: **0.5 to 1.2 mm**.
- Designed to **shear** the husk without breaking the kernel.

**(b) Under-runner Disk Huller (Legumes)**
- Two horizontal abrasive disks; upper disk stationary, lower disk rotating.
- Material fed centrally; discharged peripherally.

**(c) Centrifugal Sheller / Impact Huller**
- Grain fed into a **rotor spinning at 1200–2000 rpm**.
- Centrifugal force throws grain against an **impact ring**.
- **Centrifugal force formula:**

$$F_c = m \times r \times \omega^2 = m \times r \times \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2$$

Where $N$ = rpm, $r$ = radius of rotor (m), $m$ = mass of grain (kg).

**(d) Abrasive Cone Polisher**
- Conical abrasive surface rotating inside a perforated screen.
- Used for **polishing rice** to remove bran layers.

---

#### **Category 4: Size Reduction Equipments**

**(a) Roller Mill (Most important in wheat milling)**
- Two or more **paired chilled cast-iron rolls** rotating in opposite directions.
- Rolls have **fluted** (breaking rolls) or **smooth** (reduction rolls) surfaces.
- Differential speed creates **shearing action**.

**Capacity formula for roller mill:**

$$Q = K \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot D$$

Where:
- $Q$ = throughput (kg/hr)
- $L$ = roll length (m)
- $N$ = roll speed (rpm)
- $\rho$ = bulk density of stock (kg/m³)
- $D$ = roll diameter (m)
- $K$ = empirical constant (typically 0.1 to 0.3)

**(b) Hammer Mill**
- High-speed rotor (1500–4000 rpm) with **swinging hammers**.
- Particle size controlled by **screen aperture** (0.5 to 10 mm).
- Used for **animal feed** and **coarse grinding**.

**Energy consumption (Rittinger's Law):**

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

Where $D_1, D_2$ = initial and final particle diameters.

**(c) Pin Mill / Disc Mill**
- Two discs with **intermeshing pins** rotating at high speed.
- Produces **fine flour** for spices and pulses.

**(d) Ball Mill**
- Cylindrical chamber filled with **steel or ceramic balls**.
- Tumbling action grinds by impact and attrition.
- **Critical speed:**

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$$

Where $D$ = mill diameter (m).

**(e) Stone Mill (Burr Mill)**
- Two abrasive stones (granite or corundum).
- Used traditionally for **chakki atta** (whole wheat flour).

---

#### **Category 5: Separation & Sifting Equipments**

**(a) Plansifter**
- A **gyratory sifting machine** with stacked sieves.
- Sieves are square (typically 0.5 to 1.5 m² per section).
- Used to **classify** milled stocks into streams: flour, semolina, bran, middlings.

**(b) Purifier**
- Combined **sifting + aspiration** system.
- Removes bran particles from semolina using air streams.

**(c) Rotex Screener / Vibratory Separator**
- Vibrating rectangular deck with multiple screen layers.
- Used for **final grading** of dhal, besan, etc.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet — Equipments in Milling**

| **S.No** | **Equipment / Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** |
|----------|------------------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Conditioning water addition | $W_w = M_i \times \frac{(M_t - M_i)}{(100 - M_t)}$ | $W_w$ = water to add (kg); $M_i, M_t$ in % (w.b.) |
| 2 | Centrifugal huller force | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot (2\pi N / 60)^2$ | $F_c$ (N); $r$ (m); $N$ (rpm) |
| 3 | Roller mill throughput | $Q = K \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot D$ | $Q$ (kg/hr); $L$ (m); $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
| 4 | Hammer mill (Rittinger) | $E = K_R(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | $E$ (kWh/tonne); $D$ (m) |
| 5 | Ball mill critical speed | $N_c = 42.3 / \sqrt{D}$ | $N_c$ (rpm); $D$ (m) |
| 6 | Stoke's air drag (aspirator) | $v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_s - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$ | $v_t$ (m/s); $d$ (m); $\rho_s, \rho_a$ (kg/m³) |
| 7 | Differential roll ratio | $\Delta = N_{fast} / N_{slow}$ | Dimensionless (1.25 to 2.5) |
| 8 | Specific energy (grinding) | $E_m = P / \dot{m}$ | $E_m$ (kJ/kg); $P$ (kW) |
| 9 | Reduction ratio | $RR = D_{max,feed} / D_{max,product}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Classification efficiency | $\eta = \frac{C_c - C_f}{C_c - C_r} \times 100$ | $\eta$ in %; $C$ = composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Conditioning Water Addition

> **Problem:** A wheat lot weighing **1000 kg** has an initial moisture content of **11 % w.b.** It needs to be tempered to a target moisture of **16 % w.b.** Calculate:
> (a) Amount of water to be added.
> (b) Final mass of tempered grain.
> (c) Verify mass balance.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (Mass balance for moisture):**

$$M_i (1 - M_i) + W_w = M_f (1 - M_t)$$

Wait, the correct moisture mass balance is:

$$M_i \left(\frac{100 - M_i}{100}\right) + W_w = M_f \left(\frac{100 - M_t}{100}\right)$$

Where:
- $M_i (100 - M_i)/100$ = mass of **dry matter** in initial grain (kg)
- $W_w$ = water added (kg)
- $M_f (100 - M_t)/100$ = mass of dry matter in final grain (kg)
- $M_f = M_i + W_w$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**
- $M_i = 1000$ kg
- $M_i (\% \text{ w.b.}) = 11$
- $M_t (\% \text{ w.b.}) = 16$

**Step 3 — Calculate dry matter in initial grain:**

$$\text{Dry matter} = 1000 \times \frac{(100 - 11)}{100} = 1000 \times 0.89 = 890 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Express final mass in terms of water added:**

$$M_f = M_i + W_w = 1000 + W_w$$

**Step 5 — Apply moisture balance:**

$$890 = (1000 + W_w) \times \frac{(100 - 16)}{100}$$

$$890 = (1000 + W_w) \times 0.84$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $W_w$:**

$$\frac{890}{0.84} = 1000 + W_w$$

$$1059.52 = 1000 + W_w$$

$$W_w = 1059.52 - 1000 = 59.52 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Final mass of tempered grain:**

$$M_f = 1000 + 59.52 = 1059.52 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 8 — Verify (using original quick formula):**

$$W_w = M_i \times \frac{(M_t - M_i)}{(100 - M_t)} = 1000 \times \frac{(16 - 11)}{(100 - 16)} = 1000 \times \frac{5}{84}$$

$$W_w = 1000 \times 0.0595 = 59.52 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Final Answer (Boxed):** $W_w = 59.52$ kg, $M_f = 1059.52$ kg

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Centrifugal Huller Rotor Speed

> **Problem:** A centrifugal sheller has a rotor radius of **0.25 m** and operates at **1800 rpm**. Calculate the centrifugal force on a single rice kernel of mass **0.025 g** undergoing shelling.

**Step 1 — Convert mass to SI units:**

$$m = 0.025 \text{ g} = 0.025 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert angular velocity to rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800}{60} = \frac{3600\pi}{60} = 60\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\omega = 60 \times 3.1416 = 188.50 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply centrifugal force formula:**

$$F_c = m \times r \times \omega^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**

$$F_c = (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.25) \times (188.50)^2$$

**Step 6 — Square the angular velocity:**

$$(188.50)^2 = 35532.25$$

**Step 7 — Multiply step by step:**

$$F_c = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25 \times 35532.25$$

$$F_c = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 35532.25$$

$$F_c = 0.2221 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Express in milliNewtons for clarity:**

$$F_c = 222.1 \text{ mN}$$

> **Final Answer (Boxed):** $F_c \approx 0.222$ N

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Hammer Mill Specific Energy (Rittinger)

> **Problem:** Maize is ground in a hammer mill from an initial average particle size of **4 mm** to a final size of **0.5 mm**. Using a Rittinger constant of **$K_R$ = 12.5 kWh/tonne·mm**, compute the specific energy consumption.

**Step 1 — Write Rittinger's law:**

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$E = 12.5 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.5} - \frac{1}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute reciprocals:**

$$\frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract:**

$$2.0 - 0.25 = 1.75 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $K_R$:**

$$E = 12.5 \times 1.75 = 21.875 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ/kg:**

$$1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}, \quad 1 \text{ tonne} = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

$$E = 21.875 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 21.875 \times 3.6 = 78.75 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Final Answer (Boxed):** $E = 21.875$ kWh/tonne = 78.75 kJ/kg

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

Explain the construction and working principle of a **destoner** used in cereal milling. Why is it placed before hulling/conditioning in the milling flow?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A destoner (specific gravity separator) is a cleaning equipment that separates **stones from grains** based on **density differences**.

**Construction:** It consists of an inclined, perforated, vibrated deck with a wire-mesh surface and a fan below it. An eccentric drive produces a back-and-forth oscillation.

**Working:** A mixture of grain and stones flows onto the deck. An upward air stream is blown through the deck. Because grains are **lighter** and stones are **denser**, the stones sink and contact the deck. Friction from vibration moves stones **uphill against the airflow**, while grains float and travel **downhill**. Separate discharge chutes handle each stream.

**Position in milling flow:** Destoning is placed **after magnetic separation but before hulling** to prevent stones from damaging the abrasive hulling surfaces and to avoid contamination during tempering.

*(Breakdown of marks: 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for construction/working, 1 mark for placement in flow.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

Differentiate between a **hammer mill** and a **roller mill** with respect to (a) mode of size reduction, (b) typical particle size produced, and (c) primary application.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Hammer Mill** | **Roller Mill** |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| (a) Mode of reduction | **Impact** by high-speed rotating hammers (1500–4000 rpm) | **Compression + Shear** by differential-speed rolls |
| (b) Particle size produced | **Coarse to medium** (0.5–10 mm) | **Fine graded products** (flour < 180 µm, semolina 200–500 µm) |
| (c) Primary application | Animal feed, spice grinding, maize cracking | Wheat flour milling (break and reduction systems) |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Roller Mill & Wheat Milling*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat labeled diagram the **construction and working of a roller mill** used in wheat flour milling. Explain the role of **fluted breaking rolls** and **smooth reduction rolls**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A roller mill has rolls of **diameter 250 mm** and **length 1000 mm**, rotating at **550 rpm**, processing wheat of bulk density **750 kg/m³**. Using the empirical constant $K = 0.20$, calculate the **throughput** in kg/hr. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Roller Mill — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A roller mill consists of **two horizontally opposed, chilled cast-iron rolls** mounted in a heavy cast-iron frame.
- Rolls are driven through **V-belts and gear reducers** so that they rotate in **opposite directions** — one clockwise, one counter-clockwise.
- Each roll has its own independently controllable **feed roll system** above it to ensure an even blanket of stock.
- A **scraper blade** removes residual material from the slow roll.
- Rolls are aligned with **precise parallel gap control** (micrometric).

**Types of Rolls:**
- **Fluted (or Corrugated) Rolls:** Used as **breaking rolls** (B1, B2, B3...). Flutes are cut in spiral patterns; they crack the wheat kernel and scrape endosperm away from the bran. They operate with a **wider gap** (0.4–1.5 mm) and **higher differential** (2.5:1).
- **Smooth Rolls:** Used as **reduction rolls** (R1, R2, R3...). They are polished cylinders that progressively reduce endosperm particles into fine flour. They operate with a **narrow gap** (0.05–0.3 mm) and **lower differential** (1.25:1).

**Working Principle:** When wheat stock passes between the two rotating rolls, it is subjected to **compressive and shear forces** because the rolls rotate at different speeds. The differential speed creates a **rubbing action** that shaves the endosperm off the bran in thin layers. The material then drops to a **plansifter** below for classification.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐
   │  Feed Roll   │
   └──────┬───────┘
          ▼
   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
   │ Fast     │ <→│ Slow     │
   │ Roll     │   │ Roll     │   → Scraper Blade
   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
          ▼
       Plansifter
```

**(b) Throughput Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify formula:**

$$Q = K \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — List known values:**
- $K = 0.20$ (dimensionless empirical constant)
- $L = 1000 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- $N = 550 \text{ rpm}$
- $\rho = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$Q = 0.20 \times 1.0 \times 550 \times 750 \times 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$Q = 0.20 \times 1.0 = 0.20$$

$$0.20 \times 550 = 110$$

$$110 \times 750 = 82{,}500$$

$$82{,}500 \times 0.25 = 20{,}625$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer with units:**

$$Q = 20{,}625 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Final Boxed Answer:** $Q = 20{,}625$ kg/hr ≈ **20.6 tonnes/hr**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Cleaning & Conditioning Equipments*

**(a)** Describe the **principle, construction, and working of a magnetic separator and a disk separator**. Mention their position in the cleaning section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch of **500 kg wheat** at **12 % w.b. moisture** must be tempered to **15 % w.b.** Calculate the **amount of water to be added** and the **final mass of tempered grain**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Magnetic Separator and Disk Separator (7 marks):**

**Magnetic Separator:**

- **Principle:** Removal of **ferrous metal contaminants** using magnetic attraction.
- **Construction:** Permanent **magnet tubes (Nd-Fe-B)** or electromagnets arranged in **drawer type** or **plate type** configurations across the grain flow path.
- **Working:** Grain flows over the magnet surface via gravity. Ferrous metal particles are **retained on the magnet surface**; clean grain flows downstream. The magnet is periodically cleaned.
- **Position:** Installed **at the very start of the cleaning section** — the first equipment after raw grain intake.

**Disk Separator (Indent Cylinder / Trieur):**

- **Principle:** Length-based separation of grains using **indented pockets** on a rotating cylinder.
- **Construction:** A horizontal rotating cylinder (slowly, 30–60 rpm) lined with **hemispherical indents** of standardized size. A trough or lifter conveys the indented material out.
- **Working:** As the cylinder rotates, short grains (broken kernels, weed seeds) lodge in the indents and are lifted to a discharge trough above. Long grains slide back and exit at the end. Multiple cylinders in series (with progressively smaller indents) can classify grains by length.
- **Position:** Placed **after destoning** in the cleaning section.

**(b) Conditioning Water Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$W_w = M_i \times \frac{(M_t - M_i)}{(100 - M_t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
- $M_i = 500$ kg
- $M_i (\%) = 12$
- $M_t (\%) = 15$

**Step 3 — Compute difference in moisture:**

$$M_t - M_i = 15 - 12 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$100 - M_t = 100 - 15 = 85$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$W_w = 500 \times \frac{3}{85} = 500 \times 0.03529$$

**Step 6 — Final value:**

$$W_w = 17.65 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Final mass of tempered grain:**

$$M_f = M_i + W_w = 500 + 17.65 = 517.65 \text{ kg}$$

> **Final Boxed Answer:** $W_w = 17.65$ kg, $M_f = 517.65$ kg

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Most students write $D = 250$ mm in a formula expecting meters — leading to a 1000× error. Always convert to SI units first.
> 2. **Skipping variables:** Writing a formula without defining $L, N, D, \rho$ individually costs 1–2 marks. Always write "where $L$ = roll length, etc."
> 3. **Confusing hammer mill and roller mill:** Hammer mills operate by **impact**, roller mills by **compression + shear**. Writing "roller mills use impact" will cost the conceptual question's full mark.
> 4. **Forgetting differential speed:** In roller mill diagrams, students forget to mention the **differential speed ratio** (1.25:1 to 2.5:1) — this is a vital concept in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Asking wrong $K_R$ units:** In Rittinger's law, the constant is in kWh/tonne·mm. Mixing units (e.g., kJ/kg) is a common error.
> 6. **No labeled diagrams:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for labeled diagrams. Unlabeled diagrams get 0.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equipments Used in Milling**
>
> ✅ **Cleaning Equipments:**
> - Magnetic Separator → removes ferrous metals (50–400 mT)
> - Destoner → separates stones by density (0.5–5 m/s air)
> - Disk Separator → separates by length (pocket size 1.6–25 mm)
> - Aspirator → removes light impurities (uses terminal velocity)
>
> ✅ **Conditioning Equipments:**
> - Dampener → adds 0.5–3 % water
> - Tempering Bin → resting period 8–24 hours
> - **Key formula:** $W_w = M_i (M_t - M_i)/(100 - M_t)$
>
> ✅ **Hulling Equipments:**
> - Rubber Roll Huller → rice (gap 0.5–1.2 mm)
> - Centrifugal Sheller → 1200–2000 rpm
> - **Key formula:** $F_c = m r \omega^2$
>
> ✅ **Size Reduction Equipments:**
> - Roller Mill → compression + shear (differential 1.25:1 to 2.5:1)
> - Hammer Mill → impact (1500–4000 rpm)
> - **Key formulas:** $Q = K L N \rho D$ and $E = K_R (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$
> - Ball Mill critical speed: $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$
>
> ✅ **Separation Equipments:**
> - Plansifter → multi-deck gyratory
> - Purifier → combines sifting + aspiration
> - Gravity Separator → density-based final grading
>
> ✅ **Milling Flow:**
> - Wheat: Clean → Condition → Break (fluted) → Sift → Reduce (smooth) → Purify → Pack
> - Rice: Clean → Dehull → Separate → Polish → Grade → Sort
> - Legumes: Clean → Condition (oil/water) → Dehull → Split → Grade

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Break Roll, Reduction Roll, and Purifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Break Roll, Reduction Roll, and Purifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Break Roll System

The **Break Roll** is the first major operational stage in the modern **roller flour milling** process. It consists of a pair of horizontally mounted, counter-rotating **corrugated cast-iron rolls** that open the cleaned and tempered wheat kernel and progressively scrape the starchy endosperm away from the outer bran layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A pair of corrugated rolls rotating in opposite directions at a **differential speed** (typically **2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1**), whose primary function is the *disarticulation* of the wheat kernel and the *release* of clean endosperm particles (middlings) in successive stages labeled **1st Break (B1), 2nd Break (B2), 3rd Break (B3), 4th Break (B4), and sometimes 5th Break (B5)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of break rolls like *carefully cracking a walnut with a nutcracker* — instead of smashing it, you want the **kernel (endosperm) to come out in one large piece** and the **shell (bran) to remain intact**. The slower roll "holds" the wheat, while the faster roll "scrapes" across it. If you crush too hard, you break the shell and contaminate the kernel.

### 1.2 The Reduction Roll System

The **Reduction Roll** is the second major milling stage. It receives purified endosperm chunks (called *middlings* or *semolina*) from the purifier and grinds them into fine flour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A pair of **smooth (non-corrugated) chilled-cast-iron rolls** that rotate at a **low differential speed (1.0 : 1 to 1.25 : 1)** or in some designs at a **1:1 ratio**, operating at a lower peripheral speed than break rolls, with the objective of *reducing* endosperm particles into flour of specified fineness while **minimizing starch damage** and **bran fragmentation**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Reduction rolls work like a *rolling pin pressing down on coarse flour* — gentle, uniform pressure to turn chunks of semolina into fine powder without producing heat that would damage the starch granules.

### 1.3 The Purifier

The **Purifier** is a gravity-separation device positioned *between* the break system and the reduction system. It classifies the granular sifter output by **size (sieving)** and **density (air aspiration)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A machine consisting of a slightly inclined, mechanically **vibrating sieve deck** with **sifted wire mesh** through which an **upward air current** is drawn by a fan, used to **separate bran (lighter, flatter) from endosperm middlings (heavier, rounder)** of the same sieve fraction.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pouring a mixture of *popcorn and sand* onto a vibrating tray while a fan blows air from below. The **sand falls through** (heavier), while the **popcorn floats and is pushed off the end** (lighter). The purifier does exactly this for flour-mill stock.

### 1.4 Standard Operational Metrics

| Parameter | Break Rolls | Reduction Rolls |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Texture** | Corrugated (sharp or dull) | **Smooth (polished)** |
| **Differential Ratio** | 2.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 | **1.0 : 1 to 1.25 : 1** |
| **Fast Roll Speed (rpm)** | **350 to 550** | **200 to 300** |
| **Roll Diameter (mm)** | **250 (10 in)** | **250 (10 in)** |
| **Corrugations / inch** | 14 to 40 (B1 → B5) | **None** |
| **Spiral (lead)** | 1 : 10 to 1 : 16 | — |
| **Operating Gap (mm)** | 0.7 → 0.1 | 0.05 → 0.01 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard industrial values used by KTU examiners: **roll diameter = 250 mm (10")**, **roll length = 800–1500 mm**, **fast-roll peripheral speed = 6 m/s for breaks, 4 m/s for reductions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Break System

1. **Feeding** — Cleaned, tempered wheat (moisture **16–17%**) enters the **1st Break** rolls through a vibrating feed gate.
2. **Disarticulation** — The differential action creates a **scraping-shearing force** that splits the kernel longitudinally along the crease.
3. **Endosperm Release** — Large, clean endosperm chunks (*purifiable stock*) and *overs* (uncrushed wheat) are produced.
4. **Sifting** — The broken stock goes to a **plansifter**; large particles return to subsequent break passages.
5. **Successive Scraping** — By **B3 to B5**, the bran becomes a thin flat flake; only minimal endosperm remains.
6. **By-products** — *Bran*, *germ*, *shorts*, and *clear flour* are progressively removed.

**The Core 'Why':** The break system is **NOT** designed to make flour. It is designed to **maximize clean endosperm release while keeping bran flakes large and flat** (so they can be sifted and aspirated away).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Reduction System

1. **Feed** — Purified *middlings / dunst* from the purifier enter the **1st Reduction (R1)**.
2. **Compression** — Smooth rolls apply a **compressive + shearing** force at low differential.
3. **Flour Formation** — Endosperm is reduced to flour fineness.
4. **Multi-stage Sifting** — Flour is sifted out; coarser particles progress to **R2, R3 … R8**.
5. **Tail-end Products** — Later reductions (R6 onward) yield *low-grade flour* with higher ash.

**The Core 'Why':** Low differential speed and smooth rolls **minimize frictional heat**, preventing **starch gelatinization** and **enzyme inactivation issues**, preserving baking quality.

### 2.3 The Purifier's Working Logic

1. Stock flows onto a **gently sloped (1°–6°)** vibrating sieve.
2. The **sieve mesh** is selected so that **middlings pass through** while **bran flakes remain on top**.
3. A **fan pulls air upward** through the sieve cloth.
4. The air **lifts the lighter, flatter bran** so it is conveyed toward the discharge end.
5. **Cleaner, heavier, rounder endosperm** penetrates the sieve and is collected as **purified middlings**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **numerically tested and standard to KTU board problems**.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Differential (\%)} = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100$ | Where $N_1$ = fast roll speed, $N_2$ = slow roll speed | % |
| $\text{Differential Ratio} = N_1 : N_2$ | Most common form in textbooks | — |
| $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \,/\, 60$ | Peripheral speed of roll | m/s |
| $v_1 / v_2 = N_1 / N_2$ | Ratio of peripheral speeds (since $D$ is same) | — |
| $F = \mu \cdot N_{roll}$ | Grinding force = friction coeff × normal force | N |
| $Q = 60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot n \cdot g \cdot \rho$ | Theoretical roll throughput | kg/h |
| $P_{s} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{sieve}}$ | Sieve loading of purifier | kg/h·m² |
| $A = \dfrac{100 \cdot m_{ash}}{m_{sample}}$ | Ash content (% db) | % |
| $E = \dfrac{M_{flour}}{M_{wheat}} \times 100$ | Extraction rate | % |
| $V_{air} = A_{sieve} \cdot v_{air}$ | Air volume through purifier | m³/s |

> **Note:** In the table, vertical separators in formulas are **not** used; the `\vert` symbol is reserved for absolute value outside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Designing **extraction rates** for *Maida*, *Atta*, *Semolina*.
- Calculating **mill balance sheet** (how much flour from each break and reduction).
- Quality control via **ash curve** (lower ash = better purification).
- **Energy optimization** in industrial roller mills.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peripheral Speed of the Fast Roll

> **Problem:** A break roll has diameter **250 mm** and operates at **500 rpm**. Calculate the peripheral speed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m} \\
N &= 500 \text{ rpm} \\
v &= \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} \\
v &= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 500}{60} \\
v &= \frac{392.70}{60} \\
v &= 6.545 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Substituting SI units (m, s⁻¹) directly avoids an extra conversion. The constant 60 converts rpm → rps.

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Speed and Slow-Roll Speed

> **Problem:** A break roll operates at fast-roll speed **500 rpm** with differential **2.5 : 1**. Find the slow-roll speed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Differential Ratio} &= N_1 : N_2 = 2.5 : 1 \\
N_1 &= 500 \text{ rpm} \\
N_2 &= \frac{N_1}{2.5} = \frac{500}{2.5} \\
N_2 &= 200 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The ratio is read as "fast : slow = 2.5 : 1", so dividing $N_1$ by 2.5 gives the slow roll.

### 3.3 Derivation: Throughput Capacity of a Pair of Rolls

> **Problem:** Roll diameter = **250 mm**, roll length = **1000 mm**, fast-roll speed = **450 rpm**, roll gap = **0.5 mm**, wheat bulk density = **750 kg/m³**. Calculate the throughput per hour.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.25 \text{ m}, \quad L = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad N = 450 \text{ rpm} \\
g &= 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0005 \text{ m} \\
\rho &= 750 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
Q &= 60 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot N \cdot g \cdot \rho \\
Q &= 60 \times 3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 1.0 \times 450 \times 0.0005 \times 750 \\
Q &= 60 \times 3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 450 \times 0.0005 \times 750 \\
Q &= 9{,}948.6 \text{ kg/h} \\
Q &\approx 9.95 \text{ tonnes/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The formula $Q = 60 \pi D L N g \rho$ treats the nip region as a thin slab of thickness $g$ travelling with roll peripheral velocity.

### 3.4 Derivation: Differential Percentage

> **Problem:** Break roll fast roll = 500 rpm, slow roll = 200 rpm. Find differential %.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diff (\%)} &= \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{500 - 200}{500} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{300}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Differential percentage quantifies the slip between the two rolls; a 2.5:1 ratio corresponds to 60% differential.

### 3.5 Derivation: Extraction Rate and Mill Balance

> **Problem:** 10 tonnes of wheat yields 7.2 tonnes of straight-grade flour. What is the extraction rate?

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{M_{flour}}{M_{wheat}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.2}{10.0} \times 100 = 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operational Parameters vs. Quality Outcome

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Bran | Increase → Effect on Endosperm Yield | Increase → Effect on Starch Damage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential (breaks) | More bran fragmentation | Lower (contamination) | Moderate increase |
| Roll gap (breaks) | Coarser stock | Higher release rate | Lower |
| Roll speed | More fines | Higher capacity | Higher heat |
| Corrugation density | Sharper cut | Cleaner endosperm | Higher |
| Purifier air velocity | Cleaner bran separation | Higher grade flour | Indirect |
| Reduction roll differential | Less | Negligible | Higher (avoid) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick for exams:** *Breaks → Differentials (high). Reductions → Smooth & gentle (low differential). Purifier → Air + Sieve = Grade.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Architecture — Roller Mill System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cleaned and Tempered Wheat] --> B1[1st Break Rolls B1]
    B1 --> S1[Plansifter 1]
    S1 --> O1[Overs to B2]
    S1 --> F1[1st Break Flour]
    S1 --> M1[Middlings to Purifier]
    B2[2nd Break Rolls B2] --> S2[Plansifter 2]
    S2 --> O2[Overs to B3]
    S2 --> M2[Middlings to Purifier]
    S2 --> F2[2nd Break Flour]
    B3[3rd Break Rolls B3] --> S3[Plansifter 3]
    S3 --> O3[Overs to B4]
    S3 --> M3[Middlings to Purifier]
    S3 --> F3[3rd Break Flour]
    B4[4th Break Rolls B4] --> S4[Plansifter 4]
    S4 --> Br1[Bran Discharge]
    S4 --> M4[Middlings to Purifier]
    P1[Purifier 1] --> MR1[1st Reduction Rolls R1]
    P2[Purifier 2] --> MR2[2nd Reduction Rolls R2]
    P3[Purifier 3] --> MR3[3rd Reduction Rolls R3]
    MR1 --> FS1[Flour Sifter]
    MR2 --> FS2[Flour Sifter]
    MR3 --> FS3[Flour Sifter]
    FS1 --> PF[Final Straight Grade Flour]
    FS2 --> PF
    FS3 --> PF
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Purifier Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stock Inlet Hopper] --> B[Vibrating Sieve Deck]
    B --> C[Fan Suction Plenum]
    C --> D[Air Stream Upward]
    D --> E[Bran Discharge End]
    B --> F[Purified Middlings Through Sieve]
    F --> G[Clean Semolina to Reduction]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Break vs. Reduction Roll Surface Texture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Break Roll Pair] --> A1[Corrugated Surface - 14 to 40 cpi]
    A --> A2[Differential 2.5 to 3.0 to 1]
    A --> A3[Function: Scrape Endosperm from Bran]
    B[Reduction Roll Pair] --> B1[Smooth Polished Surface]
    B --> B2[Differential 1.0 to 1.25 to 1]
    B --> B3[Function: Reduce Endosperm to Flour]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture** (per protocol fallback rule), since physical drawings of rolls would require engineering CAD.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between break rolls and reduction rolls.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Surface texture:** Break rolls are *corrugated*; reduction rolls are *smooth* (1 mark).
2. **Differential speed:** Break rolls operate at a *higher differential* (2.5:1 to 3:1), while reduction rolls operate at *low or equal* differential (1.0:1 to 1.25:1) (1 mark).
3. **Function:** Break rolls *scrape* endosperm from bran in large pieces; reduction rolls *grind* purified endosperm into flour (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the working principle of a purifier used in flour milling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A purifier separates bran particles from endosperm middlings by combining **two principles simultaneously**:
- **Sieving (size separation):** A vibrating mesh holds flat bran flakes on top, while rounder endosperm passes through (1.5 marks).
- **Air aspiration (density separation):** A fan pulls an upward air current that lifts the lighter bran flakes and floats them toward the discharge end, while the heavier middlings fall through (1.5 marks).

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction, operation, and function of the break roll system** in a modern roller flour mill. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **working principle, construction, and applications of a purifier** in detail with a neat schematic. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer — Q.A(a):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** A pair of cast-iron rolls, diameter 250 mm, length 800–1500 mm, mounted horizontally with shafts running in anti-friction bearings. Surfaces are *corrugated* (sharp or dull) with corrugation density increasing from **14 cpi (B1)** to **32–40 cpi (B5)**. The rolls rotate in opposite directions.

2. **Differential Drive (2 marks):** A two-step pulley or gear system gives the fast roll a peripheral speed 2.5 to 3.0 times the slow roll, creating a *scraping action* on the wheat kernel.

3. **Operation (2 marks):** Tempered wheat (16–17% moisture) is fed via a vibrating feed gate. The fast roll shears open the kernel; endosperm comes off in large pieces, bran emerges as flat flakes. Stock is sent to the plansifter; overs recycle to the next break.

4. **Function (1 mark):** Maximum endosperm release with minimum bran fragmentation.

**Model Answer — Q.A(b):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** Inclined (1°–6°) vibrating sieve frame covered with nylon or wire mesh (typically 5xx to 12xx), enclosed in a wooden or metal cabinet, with a centrifugal fan drawing air upward through the sieve.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - Stock enters the upper end.
   - Vibration + airflow stratifies the bed.
   - Bran (lighter, flat) is lifted and discharged.
   - Endosperm middlings (heavier, round) penetrate the cloth and are collected.
3. **Applications (2 marks):** Purification of break-stock, semolina grading, dunst classification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **reduction roll system** — its construction, operating parameters, and how it differs from the break system. (7 marks)

**(b)** A break roll has a fast roll speed of **500 rpm** and a differential of **2.5 : 1**. The roll diameter is **250 mm**. Calculate:
1. Peripheral speed of fast roll.
2. Slow roll speed.
3. Differential percentage. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer — Q.B(a):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** Two smooth (polished) chilled-cast-iron rolls of 250 mm diameter; chilled surface is **wear-resistant** and non-corrugated.

2. **Operating parameters (3 marks):**
   - Differential: 1.0:1 to 1.25:1.
   - Fast roll speed: 200–300 rpm.
   - Roll gap: 0.05–0.10 mm (decreases with each pass).
   - Stock feed: Purified middlings from purifier.
3. **Difference from break rolls (2 marks):** Smooth vs. corrugated surface; low differential vs. high; gentle grinding vs. scraping; multiple reduction passages (up to 8) vs. typically 4–5 breaks.

**Model Answer — Q.B(b):**

1. **Peripheral speed of fast roll (3 marks):**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 500}{60} = 6.545 \text{ m/s}
$$

2. **Slow roll speed (2 marks):**

$$
N_2 = \frac{N_1}{2.5} = \frac{500}{2.5} = 200 \text{ rpm}
$$

3. **Differential percentage (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Diff (\%)} = \frac{500 - 200}{500} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Most students **forget to convert mm → m** in throughput and peripheral-speed problems. Always convert.
> - Do **not** confuse *differential ratio* (2.5:1) with *differential %* (60%). They are NOT the same.
> - In reduction rolls, students wrongly assume the differential is the *same* as break rolls — it is **NOT**; it is 1.0:1 to 1.25:1.
> - Always state the **units** of peripheral speed (m/s) — losing 1 mark for missing units is common.
> - Purifier separates on **size AND density** — saying "only size" loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Break Rolls:** *Corrugated*, *differential 2.5:1 to 3:1*, **scraping** action, **numbered B1 to B5**, decreasing gap and increasing corrugation density.
- **Reduction Rolls:** *Smooth*, *differential 1.0:1 to 1.25:1*, **compressive-grinding** action, **R1 to R8**, decreasing gap.
- **Purifier:** *Vibrating sieve + upward air current*, separates bran from middlings by **size + density**.
- **Standard roll diameter:** 250 mm; **fast roll speed:** 350–550 rpm (breaks), 200–300 rpm (reductions).
- **Corrugation density:** 14 cpi (B1) → 40 cpi (B5).
- **Extraction rate** $E = (M_{flour}/M_{wheat}) \times 100$.
- **Differential (%)** $= (N_1 - N_2)/N_1 \times 100$.
- **Peripheral speed** $v = \pi D N / 60$.
- **Throughput** $Q = 60 \pi D L N g \rho$.
- **Key industrial numbers to memorize:** Differential 2.5:1, 60%; Roll speed break 450–500 rpm; roll diameter 250 mm; cpi 14–40.
- **Purifier produces three streams:** clean middlings (to reduction), bran (to feed), air-borne dust (to filter).
- **Quality marker:** Lower **ash content** = better purification = higher grade flour.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner for viva:** *"Break rolls open the wheat, reduction rolls grind the endosperm, and the purifier keeps them apart."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan Sifter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plan Sifter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Plan Sifter (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** A *plan sifter* is a high-capacity, multi-deck, gyratory-vibratory sieving machine used in cereal milling (wheat, maize, rye, rice) and legume processing to classify intermediate and finished stock (semolina, flour, dhal, grits, bran) into fractions of defined particle size by passing the material over a stack of horizontally inclined, nested sieves driven by an eccentric or counterbalanced gyratory drive mechanism.

The plan sifter is the workhorse of the **purification and classification section** of a roller flour mill. It uses a *circular-throw* (gyratory) motion imparted by a twin-shaft or single-shaft eccentric drive, so that the entire sieve stack oscillates in a horizontal plane while the stock simultaneously migrates along the sieve cloth by virtue of the inclination angle (typically **4°–8°** from horizontal) and the centrifugal acceleration developed by the throw.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The plan sifter is a critical size-classification unit operation connecting the *break* and *reduction* passages in wheat milling, and the *dehusking–dehulling–splitting* chain in legume (pulse) milling. Its performance directly controls flour yield, ash content, and grade purity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Multi-Story Sieve Tower"**  
> Imagine a tall, tilted bookshelf where each shelf is a kitchen sieve. You pour a mixed handful of sand, gravel, and sugar on the *top shelf*. As the whole bookshelf is gently shaken in a *circular dancing motion* (like a hula hoop rotating while moving forward), each shelf catches particles *bigger* than its mesh and lets the smaller ones *rain down* to the next shelf below. After a few seconds:
> - **Top shelf** → largest pieces (e.g., bran flakes, unbroken endosperm chunks)  
> - **Middle shelves** → progressively finer semolina / dhal splits  
> - **Bottom shelf / pan** → the finest flour or powder  
> 
> The "circular dance" is the *gyratory throw*, the tilt is the *sieve inclination angle*, and the multiple shelves are the *sieve stack*. Replace the bookshelf with an industrial steel cabinet holding 16–28 stacked frames, and you have a **plan sifter**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Foundational terms you must memorize for the exam:**
> - **Stock** — the granular material being sifted (intermediate mill product).
> - **Scalping** — the removal of *oversize* particles (retained on the top sieve).
> - **Grading / Classification** — separation of correctly-sized particles into one or more grade streams.
> - **Dust collection** — collection of *undersize* fines (passing through the bottom sieve into the pan).
> - **Sieve cloth / Mesh** — woven wire or nylon fabric defining aperture size (e.g., **100 µm**, **200 µm**).
> - **Throw (Stroke)** — the *diameter* of the circular path traced by the sieve (commonly **40–65 mm**).
> - **RPM** — rotational speed of the drive shaft (typically **200–280 rpm** for grain sifters).
> - **Specific load** — kilograms of stock fed per m² of sieve area per 24 h (industry norm: **800–1500 kg/m²·day**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Anatomy of a Plan Sifter

A modern industrial plan sifter is essentially a **vertical, rectangular, fully-enclosed cabinet** partitioned horizontally into a *stack* of identical sieve frames. Each component has a defined engineering function:

| Sub-system | Function |
|---|---|
| **Drive head (top or bottom)** | Houses a single or twin-shaft eccentric with counterweights; imparts the gyratory throw. |
| **Sieve stack** | A vertical column of 8–28 removable wooden or aluminum-framed sieve frames. |
| **Sieve cloth** | Woven wire / nylon / polyester; aperture graded from coarse (top) to fine (bottom). |
| **Cleaning brushes / balls** | Rubber or polyurethane balls placed beneath each cloth to prevent blinding. |
| **Inlet spout (feeding box)** | Distributes the incoming stock uniformly across the top sieve width. |
| **Outlet channels (sleeves)** | One per sieve + the through-pan; route each fraction to its own discharge spout. |
| **Tensioning rails** | Allow vertical clamping of the stack; maintain cloth tension. |
| **Suspension / Free-standing frame** | Rope-hung, or supported on a rigid base with rubber dampers. |

## 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Core Principle:** A combination of *gyratory acceleration* and *gravitational settling* along an inclined sieve causes particles to stratify. **Coarse particles migrate UP the incline** (because they are thrown the furthest by the horizontal throw and gravity rolls them back to the high end of the sieve as the sieve reverses direction), while **fine particles percolate DOWN through the cloth apertures**.

The operational logic in *seven* structured steps:

1. **Feeding** — The stock enters the top sieve from a feeding box that spreads the material in a uniform curtain across the *width* of the sieve.
2. **Gyratory motion imparted** — The eccentric (throw **40–65 mm**) rotates at **200–280 rpm**, generating a horizontal circular orbit of the sieve frame.
3. **Centrifugal acceleration** — Particles experience an outward acceleration $a_c = \omega^2 r$ which must exceed gravity *g* by a controlled factor to "fluidize" the bed.
4. **Stratification** — Lighter / smaller particles sink to the sieve surface (percolation), larger particles ride on top.
5. **Particle transport** — The combined effect of the sieve inclination (**4°–8°**) and the asymmetry of the throw pushes the bed *uphill* in a spiral path.
6. **Classification** — Each sieve retains its over-size and passes its throughs downward; every frame discharges a separate fraction through outlet sleeves.
7. **Discharge** — All fractions exit via separate spouts and are pneumatically conveyed to their next destination (e.g., purifiers, reduction rolls, bran finishers).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]  
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU 2024 ESE numericals. Units are given in **bold** in the SI column.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Centrifugal acceleration** | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = throw radius (m), $a_c$ (m/s²) | Determines fluidization of the bed. |
| 2 | **Centrifugal factor (K)** | $K = \dfrac{a_c}{g} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $K$ dimensionless (typical: 1.2–1.8) | Optimum bed fluidization. |
| 3 | **Angular velocity** | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm | Converts rpm to rad/s. |
| 4 | **Sifter throughput (mass)** | $Q = q_s \cdot A$ | $q_s$ = specific load (kg/m²·day), $A$ = total sieve area (m²) | Capacity sizing. |
| 5 | **Effective sieve area** | $A = (N-1) \cdot b \cdot L$ | $N$ = number of sieves, $b$ = width, $L$ = length of one frame | Geometric area. |
| 6 | **Sieve efficiency (grade)** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{through,\;desired}}{m_{through,\;ideal}} \times 100\%$ | Mass basis (%) | Performance metric. |
| 7 | **Particle Reynolds number (air drag)** | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_a \, v \, d_p}{\mu_a}$ | $\rho_a, \mu_a$ = air density, viscosity; $v$ = air velocity; $d_p$ = particle diameter | Dust loss estimation. |
| 8 | **Stokes terminal velocity (legume dust)** | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g}{18 \mu_a}$ | $\rho_p$ = particle density | Air-classification design. |
| 9 | **Asperities / Specific load limit** | $q_s \le 1500 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$ | Empirical industry cap for wheat | Prevents blinding. |
| 10 | **Number of sieves required** | $N \ge \dfrac{Q_{target}}{q_s \cdot b \cdot L} + 1$ | — | Design / selection. |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake:** Do **NOT** use the vertical pipe $\vert$ inside any table cell. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) for absolute-value bars.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat flour mills:** every break roll and reduction roll is followed by a plan sifter passage. A modern 500 TPD mill may have **15–20 plan sifters** running continuously.
- **Legume / pulse mills:** used to grade dehusked dhal into splits, whole, brokens, and flour; controls the *dhal-to-bran* separation.
- **Maize processing:** classifies degermed grits, germ, and flour.
- **Rice milling:** removes residual brokens and classifies head rice.
- **Spice / animal feed industries:** grading of powders to ISO or BIS mesh standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Centrifugal Factor $K$ of a Plan Sifter

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **centrifugal factor $K$** dictates the entire sifting efficiency. Deriving it from first principles is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**Step 1 — Define the physical system.**  
A particle of mass $m$ rests on a sieve that moves in a circle of radius $r$ (the "throw") with angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 2 — Express the centripetal / centrifugal force in the rotating frame.**  
The inertial (centrifugal) force experienced by the particle in the rotating reference frame is:

$$
F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r
$$

*Reason:* Any object moving in a circular path of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$ undergoes a centripetal acceleration of magnitude $\omega^2 r$. By Newton's third law / D'Alembert, an equal and opposite inertial force is felt in the frame of the sieve.

**Step 3 — Express the gravitational force on the particle.**  
The weight of the particle is:

$$
F_g = m \, g
$$

*Reason:* Weight is the gravitational pull on a mass $m$ near Earth's surface, where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 4 — Form the dimensionless ratio $K$.**  
Dividing the centrifugal force by the gravitational force:

$$
K = \dfrac{F_c}{F_g} = \dfrac{m \, \omega^2 \, r}{m \, g}
$$

*Reason:* A dimensionless number expresses *how many "g's"* the particle experiences, regardless of its mass.

**Step 5 — Cancel $m$.**  
The particle mass cancels out:

$$
K = \dfrac{\omega^2 \, r}{g}
$$

*Reason:* In a free surface, every particle — flour, bran, dhal — experiences the same ratio. This is why the same sifter setting works for all stocks.

**Step 6 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.**  
For practical use with $N$ in rpm:

$$
K = \dfrac{(2\pi N / 60)^2 \cdot r}{g} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 N^2 \, r}{3600 \, g}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, N^2 \, r}{900 \, g}
$$

*Reason:* This puts $K$ in terms of directly measurable engineering quantities (rpm, throw in metres, and $g$).

**Step 7 — Numerical example for KTU problem style.**  
Given: $N = 240$ rpm, $r = 0.030$ m (throw = 60 mm diameter), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$
K = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot (240)^2 \cdot 0.030}{900 \cdot 9.81}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{9.8696 \cdot 57600 \cdot 0.030}{8829}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{17058.7}{8829} = 1.932
$$

Since the recommended range is $1.2 \le K \le 1.8$, this setting would be **slightly too violent** — the operator should reduce rpm to about 230 or shorten the throw to 50 mm.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sifter Capacity from Specific Load

> [!NOTE]  
> This derivation appears frequently in KTU 2024 Part-B questions asking to **size a plan sifter** for a given mill capacity.

**Step 1 — Define the specific load.**  
The specific load $q_s$ is the mass of stock fed per square metre of sieve area per day:

$$
q_s = \dfrac{Q}{A}
$$

*Reason:* $q_s$ is an empirical industry metric that captures the throughput ceiling of a cloth before blinding occurs.

**Step 2 — Express total throughput.**  
Rearranging:

$$
Q = q_s \cdot A
$$

*Reason:* Total capacity scales linearly with sieve area when operating at optimum $q_s$.

**Step 3 — Substitute effective area.**  
The effective sieving area is contributed by $(N-1)$ cloths (since the bottom frame is a pan):

$$
A = (N-1) \cdot b \cdot L
$$

*Reason:* Each cloth above the pan contributes classification area; the pan collects the fines but is not a "sieve" per se.

**Step 4 — Final working formula.**

$$
Q = q_s \cdot (N-1) \cdot b \cdot L
$$

*Reason:* This is the engineering design equation used to size a new sifter or audit an existing one.

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**  
A wheat mill requires 80 TPD of stock to be graded. Choose $q_s = 1200$ kg/m²·day. Each sieve frame is 0.6 m wide × 1.2 m long, with $N = 16$ compartments.

$$
A = (16 - 1) \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.2 = 15 \cdot 0.72 = 10.8 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{max} = 1200 \cdot 10.8 = 12960 \text{ kg/day} = 12.96 \text{ TPD}
$$

Since 12.96 TPD $\ge$ 8 TPD, a single 16-compartment sifter is **sufficient** (with 65 % design margin remaining).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sieve Efficiency $\eta$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> A staple short-answer concept in KTU 2024 module exams.

**Step 1 — Define the ideal mass of throughs.**  
The theoretical maximum mass of correctly-sized particles that *should* pass a given cloth is determined by sieve analysis of the feed.

**Step 2 — Define the actual mass of throughs.**  
The measured throughput of the desired fraction exiting the discharge spout.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{m_{through,\;actual}}{m_{through,\;ideal}} \times 100\,\%
$$

*Reason:* This expresses how *well* the sifter has achieved its design separation; values of **85–95 %** are typical for a healthy wheat sifter, **< 80 %** indicates blinding or overloading.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
