# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Hard Wheat Flour and Soft Wheat Flour in terms of protein content, gluten strength, and typical end-use applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hard Wheat Flour:**
- Protein content: **12 – 14%**
- Gluten strength: Strong, elastic, tenacious
- End-uses: Pan bread, pizza base, hard rolls, croissants

**Soft Wheat Flour:**
- Protein content: **7 – 10%**
- Gluten strength: Weak, extensible but non-elastic
- End-uses: Cakes, biscuits, pastries, crackers

> **Valuation Note (1 extra mark):** Mention that hard wheat corresponds to *Triticum aestivum* sub-classes (HRW, HRS) while soft wheat is typically Soft Red Winter (SRW) or Soft White (SW).

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is Atta? How does it differ chemically from refined Maida (3 key parameters)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Atta** is whole wheat flour produced by grinding cleaned wheat kernels (often *Triticum aestivum* — common wheat or a *dicoccum* blend) without separating bran, germ, and endosperm.

| Parameter | Atta | Maida |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Ash content | 1.5 – 2.0% d.b. | ≤ 0.62% d.b. |
| Crude fiber | ≥ 2.0% | < 0.5% |
| Color | Light brown | Bright white |

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and provide **internal choice** within the same module. Both options below are *of equal complexity and mark distribution*, calibrated to **Dec 2023 / May 2024 KTU pattern**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 2 Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Classify wheat flours based on (i) extraction rate, (ii) protein content, and (iii) end-use application. Tabulate the classification with at least one commercial example for each category.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Extraction Rate:**

| Extraction Rate | Example | Notes |
|-----------------|---------|-------|
| 100% | Whole wheat (Atta) | No by-product removal |
| 72 – 75% | Straight-run refined | Industry-standard roller mill yield |
| 55 – 65% | Patent flour | Brightest, purest endosperm |
| 5 – 8% | Clear flour | By-product of patent production |

**(ii) Classification by Protein Content:**

| Protein Range | Commercial Name | Application |
|---------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 7 – 9% | Cake / Pastry flour | Sponge cakes, biscuits |
| 9 – 11% | All-Purpose / Maida | Chapati, biscuits, naan |
| 12 – 14% | Bread / High-gluten flour | Pan bread, pizza |
| 14%+ | High-gluten (vital wheat gluten enriched) | Bagels, hard rolls |

**(iii) Classification by End-Use Application:**

| End-Use | Preferred Flour | Reason |
|---------|-----------------|--------|
| Bread | Bread flour (high protein) | Strong gluten network traps CO₂ |
| Cake | Cake flour (low protein) | Tender, fine crumb |
| Pasta | Durum semolina | Yellow pigment + dense gluten |
| Chapati | Atta | Bran-rich, fibre-balanced |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A roller mill produces 740 kg of refined flour from 1000 kg of cleaned wheat. The ash content of the flour is 0.62% (d.b.) and protein content is 11.0% (d.b.). Determine (1) extraction rate, (2) protein yield per 1000 kg wheat input, and (3) state whether this qualifies as patent, straight-run, or clear flour under Codex norms. Justify with ash logic.**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Extraction Rate:**

$$ER = \frac{740}{1000} \times 100\% = 74\%$$

**(2) Protein Yield per 1000 kg Wheat:**

Protein in flour = 11.0% of 740 kg

$$W_{protein} = 0.11 \times 740 = 81.4 \, \text{kg}$$

**Protein yield per 1000 kg wheat input:**

$$\text{Protein Yield} = \frac{81.4}{1000} \times 100\% = 8.14\%$$

**(3) Classification by Codex Ash Norms:**

According to **Codex Standard 152-1985**:

| Flour Type | Ash (% d.b.) |
|------------|--------------|
| Patent flour | ≤ 0.50 |
| Straight-run flour | 0.50 – 0.80 |
| Clear flour | > 0.80 |

The computed ash of **0.62% d.b.** lies in the **0.50 – 0.80** band.

**Conclusion:** This flour qualifies as a **Straight-Run Flour** under Codex norms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing wet vs. dry basis:** Always declare the basis explicitly.
> - **Forgetting to multiply % by mass:** Many students write "11% protein" without computing the absolute mass.
> - **Wrong Codex thresholds:** Some textbooks cite older FAO limits; the **Codex 152-1985** thresholds (≤0.50 / 0.50-0.80 / >0.80) are the official reference.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 2 Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the manufacturing process of Semolina (Sooji) from durum wheat. Highlight the critical quality parameters that distinguish a good semolina from a substandard one.**

**Model Solution:**

**Manufacturing Process of Semolina (Sooji):**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Selection:** Premium *Triticum durum* kernels are selected. Visual grading checks for **vitreousness > 80%**, **amber color**, and **absence of black-tip fungus**.

**Step 2 — Cleaning:** Magnetic separators, destoners, and scourers remove foreign matter, stones, and surface microbes.

**Step 3 — Tempering:** Water is added to raise moisture to **16 – 17%** for 12 – 24 hours. This toughens bran and softens the endosperm, allowing clean separation of the starchy particles.

**Step 4 — Break Rolls:** A series of 4 – 5 corrugated break rolls progressively scrape endosperm particles from the bran. The endosperm emerges as **coarse, granular semolina** (250 – 500 µm).

**Step 5 — Purification (Purfing):** Semolina passes through aspirators and gravity tables to remove residual bran specks. This is the **most critical step** — incomplete purification yields off-color semolina.

**Step 6 — Sizing & Grading:** Semolina is graded by particle size:
- Coarse semolina → pasta manufacturing
- Medium semolina (Rawa) → halwa, upma
- Fine semolina → baby cereals, instant mixes

**Critical Quality Parameters:**

| Parameter | Standard | Substandard If |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| Granulation | 250 – 500 µm | < 200 µm (too fine) or > 600 µm |
| Ash content | ≤ 0.85% d.b. | > 1.2% d.b. |
| Color (Minolta L*) | > 70 | < 60 (bran contamination) |
| Speck count | < 5 per 50 g | > 15 per 50 g |
| Protein | 10 – 12% | < 9% (weak gluten) |
| Moisture | ≤ 14% | > 14.5% (spoilage risk) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A bakery receives 500 kg of a wheat flour sample. Lab analysis gives: Protein = 12.5%, Wet Gluten = 32%, Ash = 0.58% (d.b.), Moisture = 13.2%. Recommend the appropriate end-use (Bread, Biscuit, or Cake). Justify with three quantitative arguments.**

**Model Solution:**

**Recommended End-Use: PAN BREAD**

**Quantitative Argument 1 — Protein Content:**
- Sample protein = **12.5%** falls in the **Bread Flour** band (12 – 14%).
- Biscuit flour requires **8 – 9%** protein → **rejected**.
- Cake flour requires **7 – 8%** protein → **rejected**.

**Quantitative Argument 2 — Wet Gluten:**
- Sample wet gluten = **32%** is **> 30%**, characteristic of strong, elastic gluten.
- Such gluten can retain CO₂ during long, high-temperature pan bread fermentation.
- For biscuits and cakes, this high gluten would cause excessive chewiness and shrinkage.

**Quantitative Argument 3 — Ash and Moisture:**
- Ash = **0.58% d.b.** falls in the **straight-run flour band** (0.50 – 0.80%), consistent with refined bread flour.
- Moisture = **13.2%** is **below 14%** → safe for long shelf-life storage of packaged bread.

**Conclusion:** This flour is **best suited for pan bread and bun manufacture**, not for biscuits or cakes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Single-argument answers** lose 3–4 marks. Always provide **three quantitative justifications**.
> - **Generic recommendations** ("good for baking") without numbers fetch zero credit.
> - **Ignoring ash logic** — Ash is the *single most reliable* indicator of refinement; neglecting it loses the full 2-mark ash justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision flashcard — read this twice before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Wheat kernel anatomy:** Bran (14%) + Endosperm (82.5%) + Germ (2.5%). Maida = only endosperm.
- [ ] **Extraction Rate** $ER = W_{flour}/W_{wheat} \times 100\%$ — must specify whether straight-run or patent.
- [ ] **Protein factor for wheat = 5.7** (not 6.25!).
- [ ] **FSSAI moisture limit:** ≤ 14.0%.
- [ ] **Codex ash bands:** Patent ≤ 0.50, Straight-run 0.50 – 0.80, Clear > 0.80.
- [ ] **Atta** = whole wheat, **100% extraction**, brownish, bran-rich.
- [ ] **Maida** = refined endosperm flour, **72–75% extraction**, bright white.
- [ ] **Semolina (Sooji)** = durum wheat, **250 – 500 µm**, used for pasta/halwa/upma.
- [ ] **Bread flour** has the **highest protein** (12 – 14%); **cake flour** has the **lowest** (7 – 9%).
- [ ] **Wet gluten > 30%** = strong flour for bread; **wet gluten < 22%** = weak flour for cakes.
- [ ] **Falling Number** indicates α-amylase activity; **> 250 s** = sound wheat.
- [ ] **Durum wheat (*T. durum*)** is exclusively used for **pasta and Sooji** because of its vitreous, carotenoid-rich endosperm.
- [ ] **Self-rising flour** = Maida + salt + baking powder; **enriched flour** = Maida + iron/folic acid (mandatory in India under FSSAI).
- [ ] **Stone-ground flour** retains more germ and produces shorter shelf-life due to oil oxidation.
- [ ] **Always quote results on a stated basis** (wet vs. dry) to avoid KTU mark deductions.
- [ ] **Process flow:** Cleaning → Tempering → Break rolls → Sifting → Reduction rolls → Purification → Blending → Storage → Packaging.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barley
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Barley

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Barley (*Hordeum vulgare* L.)** is a monocotyledonous annual cereal grain belonging to the family **Poaceae (Gramineae)** and the tribe **Triticeae**. It is one of the oldest cultivated cereal crops, ranking **fourth** in global cereal production after maize, rice, and wheat. Botanically, barley is classified as a **spike** (or *ear*), where each rachis node bears **three single-flowered spikelets**, making it uniquely distinguishable from wheat (which bears spikelets singly on alternate sides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Under the Cereal Technology framework of OEFTT836, Barley is studied extensively for its unique **husked-grain morphology**, **malting quality**, **β-glucan content**, and its dual usage as a **food and feed** cereal. It is the principal grain used globally for the production of **malt**, the key raw material in brewing and distilling industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of barley as **"the industrial workhorse of the brewing world and the nutritional powerhouse of the cereal family."**

Imagine a wheat grain as a polished, well-dressed professional — it sheds its outer coat (husk) naturally during harvesting. Barley, on the other hand, is like a grain still wearing its protective jacket (the **husk** or **lemma/palea**) that is tightly fused to the kernel. This single morphological difference dictates the entire downstream processing logic:

- Because the **husk remains attached**, barley grains are perfectly designed to act as natural **filters** during the brewing process (mashing), where water must pass through a bed of grains to extract sugars.
- The tightly bound husk also traps moisture and nutrients inside the kernel, making barley exceptionally good for **malting** (controlled germination).

Just as a coconut retains its fibrous husk to protect the inner kernel, barley retains its hull to protect the starchy endosperm.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Botanical Name | *Hordeum vulgare* L. |
| Family | Poaceae (Gramineae) |
| Ploidy | Diploid (2n = 14) |
| Global Rank in Cereals | 4th |
| Test Weight | **62 – 70 kg/hL** (good malting barley) |
| Protein Content | **8 – 13 %** (malting grade: 9.5 – 12 %) |
| Moisture Content (safe storage) | **≤ 13 %** |
| Thousand Kernel Weight (TKW) | **35 – 50 g** |
| Kernel Diameter | **2.5 – 3.5 mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is barley 4th in production?** Although it is the 4th most produced cereal worldwide (approx. **150 million tonnes/year**), barley is the **1st** cereal in terms of tonnage used for **malting** purposes. The brewing industry alone consumes over **30 %** of the global barley output.

## 1.4 Historical & Economic Context

Barley is one of the **Founder Crops of the Neolithic Revolution**, domesticated around **10,000 BC** in the **Fertile Crescent** (modern-day Israel, Jordan, Syria, Iraq). The wild progenitor is *Hordeum spontaneum*. Its **short growing season** (90 – 110 days) and **tolerance to drought, salinity, and cold** make it a crop of choice in semi-arid regions like **Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, and the northern plains of India**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India Specific:** India produces approximately **1.7 – 1.9 million tonnes** of barley annually on **0.6 – 0.7 million hectares**. Rajasthan alone accounts for **~ 50 %** of India's barley acreage. The crop is predominantly used as **animal feed (~ 60 %)** and for **malting (~ 25 %)** in the Indian brewing industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Botanical & Morphological Classification

Barley kernels are structurally divided into three principal parts, each with distinct roles in processing:

### 2.1.1 The Three Principal Parts of a Barley Kernel

1. **Husk (Lemma + Palea)**
   - The outer glumes that remain attached to the kernel after threshing.
   - Composed primarily of **cellulose (35 %)**, **hemicellulose (28 %)**, **lignin (18 %)**, and **silica (5 %)**.
   - **Function:** Acts as a natural filter bed during mashing; provides fiber.

2. **Pericarp + Testa (Outer Layers)**
   - Pericarp is the maternal tissue; testa is the seed coat.
   - Testa contains **polyphenols** (responsible for color).

3. **Endosperm (Starchy Reserve)**
   - **Starch granules:** Large (Type A: 10 – 20 μm) and small (Type B: 1 – 5 μm).
   - **Protein matrix:** Contains **hordeins** (prolamins), **glutelins**, **albumins**, and **globulins**.
   - **β-glucans:** A soluble fiber unique to barley (**3 – 11 %**), important for both nutrition and brewing (high β-glucan = lautering problems).

4. **Embryo (Germ)**
   - Rich in lipids, vitamins (especially **Vitamin E**), and enzymes.
   - Activated during **malting**.

5. **Aleurone Layer**
   - A single layer of living cells just beneath the testa.
   - During malting, it synthesizes and secretes **α-amylase**, **β-amylase**, and **proteolytic enzymes** under the influence of **Gibberellic Acid (GA₃)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hulled vs. Hull-less Distinction:** In **hulled barley**, the husk adheres tightly to the caryopsis at maturity. In **naked barley (hull-less)**, the husk separates easily during threshing (similar to wheat). Naked barley is preferred for direct food use (like *chalona* in Indian cuisine); hulled barley is preferred for malting.

## 2.2 Spike Morphology — The Row Types

Barley is uniquely classified by the arrangement of its spikelets on the rachis:

| Row Type | Description | Kernel Symmetry | End Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2-Row Barley** | Only central floret at each rachis node is fertile; laterals are sterile. | Plump, symmetrical, large kernels. | **Malting/brewing** (preferred globally) |
| **6-Row Barley** | All three florets at each rachis node are fertile. | Mixed kernel size (central kernels are symmetrical, laterals are twisted/asymmetrical). | **Animal feed**, some malting (in USA) |
| **Irregular / 4-Row (Abyssinian)** | Intermediate; rare; grown in Ethiopia. | Variable. | Local food use. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Two-row barleys (e.g., *Bere*, *Golden Promise*, *Bowman*) are **universally preferred for malting** because of their **uniform kernel size, lower protein content, and higher starch extract yield**. India traditionally grows 6-row barleys, but the trend is shifting toward 2-row varieties for malting.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates all standard formulas, indices, and conversion parameters essential for solving problems on Barley technology.

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Weight (TW)** | $\text{TW} = \dfrac{W}{V} \times 100$ | $\text{kg/hL}$ | Quality grading; malting potential |
| **Thousand Kernel Weight (TKW)** | $\text{TKW} = \dfrac{W \times 1000}{N}$ | grams | Plumpness indicator |
| **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $\text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Drying / storage calculations |
| **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying engineering |
| **Hectolitre Mass** | $\text{HM} = \dfrac{M}{V} \times 100$ | $\text{kg/hL}$ | Maltster specifications |
| **Hot Water Extract (HWE)** | $\text{HWE} = \dfrac{\text{Soluble solids} \times \text{V}}{\text{Grist mass}}$ | $\% \text{ (as-is)}$ or $\text{L°/kg}$ | Mashing efficiency |
| **Extract Yield (db)** | $E_{db} = \dfrac{E_{as-is}}{1 - \text{MC}_{db}}$ | $\text{L°/kg}$ | Standardized malt extract |
| **Diastatic Power (DP)** | $\text{DP} = \dfrac{\text{mg maltose}}{100 \text{ g malt} \cdot \text{hr}}$ | $\text{Windisch-Kolbach units}$ | Enzyme activity of malt |
| **Kolbach Index** | $\text{KI} = \dfrac{\text{Soluble protein}}{\text{Total protein}} \times 100$ | % | Malt modification indicator |
| **β-Glucan Content** | $\text{Mixed-linkage } \beta\text{-glucan}$ | $\% \text{ db}$ | Nutritional fiber; wort viscosity |
| **Protein Conversion** | $\text{Protein} = \text{N} \times 5.7 \text{ (or } 6.25\text{)}$ | factor | Kjeldahl analysis |
| **Starch Damage** | Measured by **Farrand / Megazyme** | $\% \text{ starch damaged}$ | Milling quality |
| **Water Sensitivity** | $\text{WS} = \dfrac{\text{GE}_{4 \text{mL}} - \text{GE}_{8 \text{mL}}}{\text{GE}_{8 \text{mL}}} \times 100$ | % | Germination vigour |
| **Germinative Energy (GE)** | Standard lab method (4 mL vs. 8 mL H₂O) | % | Malting grade assessment |
| **Storage Moisture (Safe Limit)** | $M_s \le 13 \text{ \% (wb)}$ | % | Mycotoxin prevention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these specific formulas matter in KTU exams:** A typical 14-mark question in Module 2 may ask you to **calculate test weight, TKW, extract yield, or the Kolbach Index** for a given batch of malt. The **conversion between wet-basis and dry-basis moisture** is a recurring favorite of KTU examiners.

## 2.4 The Malting Process — Operational Logic

Malting is the **controlled, partial germination** of barley grains to mobilize the starchy endosperm into a sugar-rich substrate. It consists of three sequential operations:

1. **Steeping (36 – 48 hours):** The dry barley is immersed in water at **12 – 18 °C** with periodic air-rest cycles. Moisture is raised from **~ 12 %** to **~ 43 – 46 %**. This activates the embryo and triggers **gibberellin** synthesis.

2. **Germination (4 – 5 days):** The steeped grain is spread on a malting floor or in a **Saladin box / drum malting** unit. Temperature held at **15 – 18 °C**; humidity at **> 95 % RH**. The aleurone layer secretes hydrolytic enzymes that degrade the cell walls and protein matrix surrounding starch granules.

3. **Kilning (Drying + Curing):** Germination is arrested by heating. The green malt is dried:
   - **Free drying stage:** 50 – 60 °C, moisture reduced from ~ 45 % to ~ 12 %.
   - **Curing stage:** 80 – 110 °C for 2 – 4 hours. This develops **color and flavor** (Maillard reaction) and stabilizes enzymes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Steeping–Germination–Kilning (SGK)** sequence is the heart of brewing. A KTU examiner may ask you to draw the **flow chart of malting**, or describe the **biochemical changes** during germination. The dominant biochemical changes are:
> 1. **Starch → Maltose + Maltotriose + Dextrins** (by α-amylase, β-amylase, limit-dextrinase)
> 2. **Protein → Amino acids + Peptides** (by endo- and exo-proteases)
> 3. **β-Glucans → Glucose** (by β-glucanase)
> 4. **Lipids → Free Fatty Acids** (by lipase)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Barley Quality Parameters

This section provides the **complete algebraic transition** for the most commonly tested KTU calculations involving barley and malt quality.

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Wet-Basis to Dry-Basis Moisture Conversion

In barley drying, kilning, and storage engineering, you must convert between **wet-basis moisture content (wb)** and **dry-basis moisture content (db)**.

**Step 1: Define the basic variables.**
Let $W_w$ = weight of wet barley sample (g) and $W_d$ = weight of completely dry barley (g).

**Step 2: Express weight of moisture.**
The weight of water present in the sample is the difference between wet and dry weights:

$$W_{H_2O} = W_w - W_d$$

**Step 3: Write the wet-basis formula.**
By definition, the wet-basis moisture content expresses the water weight as a percentage of the wet (original) sample weight:

$$\text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Write the dry-basis formula.**
By definition, the dry-basis moisture content expresses the water weight as a percentage of the dry matter:

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Step 5: Derive the conversion relationship.**
Let $m_w = \text{MC}_{wb} / 100$ (decimal form) and $m_d = \text{MC}_{db} / 100$. From Step 3:

$$m_w = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} = 1 - \dfrac{W_d}{W_w}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{W_d}{W_w} = 1 - m_w$$

From Step 4:

$$m_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} - 1$$

Substitute the reciprocal of $\dfrac{W_d}{W_w}$ from above:

$$m_d = \dfrac{1}{1 - m_w} - 1$$

**Step 6: Simplify to the final compact form.**

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{wb}}{1 - \text{MC}_{wb}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{db}}{1 + \text{MC}_{db}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The dry-basis value is always **larger** than the wet-basis value. For safe storage, the safe limit is **MC ≤ 13 % wb** (≈ 14.9 % db). This conversion is essential when using psychrometric charts for grain drying calculations.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Extract Yield Calculation in Malting

The **Hot Water Extract (HWE)** measures the amount of soluble material obtained when ground malt (grist) is mashed with water under standard conditions. It is reported in **Liters ° Plato per kg of malt** (L°/kg) — a unit representing the volume of wort at standard strength produced per kg of grist.

**Step 1: Standard mashing conditions (EBC Congress mashing).**
A mass $M_g$ of grist (typically 50 g) is mashed with 400 mL of water at 45 °C, then subjected to programmed temperature rises (45 → 70 °C at 1 °C/min) to enzymatic conversion.

**Step 2: Definition of Plato (°P).**
The Plato scale measures the percentage of dissolved solids (mainly sugars) in wort by mass. A wort of 1 °P contains 1 g of extract per 100 g of wort (≈ 10 g/L of wort, since wort density ≈ 1.000 kg/L at low strength).

**Step 3: Mathematical formulation.**
If $V$ is the volume of wort collected (in liters) and $S$ is the extract content (in % Plato), then the **total mass of soluble extract** obtained is:

$$M_{extract} = V \times S \times 10 \quad \text{(g of extract)}$$

The divisor of 10 arises because 1 °P ≈ 10 g of sugar per liter of wort at low concentrations.

**Step 4: Calculate extract per kg of grist (as-is basis).**
The extract per kg of grist (as-is) is:

$$E_{as\text{-}is} = \dfrac{V \times S \times 10}{M_g} \quad \text{(L°/kg)}$$

**Step 5: Correct to dry basis.**
Since the grist has moisture $M_{db}$ (decimal form), the dry mass of grist is $M_g \times (1 - M_{db})$. The dry-basis extract is therefore:

$$E_{db} = \dfrac{V \times S \times 10}{M_g \times (1 - M_{db})}$$

**Step 6: Express in final compact form.**

$$E_{db} = \dfrac{E_{as\text{-}is}}{1 - M_{db}} \quad \text{in L°/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A premium 2-row malting barley yields **~ 81 – 83 % extract (db)**. Feeds yield 75 – 78 %; 6-row malts yield 78 – 80 %. A standard mashing problem may provide $V$, $S$, and $M_{db}$ and ask you to compute $E_{db}$.

---

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix: Barley vs. Wheat vs. Maize (Composition)

The following analytical matrix enables you to compare barley with other major cereals — a frequent Part A question topic in KTU.

| Constituent | Barley (Husked) | Wheat | Maize |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Starch | **55 – 65 %** | 60 – 72 % | **70 – 78 %** |
| Protein | 8 – 13 % | 10 – 15 % | 8 – 11 % |
| β-Glucan | **3 – 11 %** | < 0.5 % | trace |
| Fiber (TDF) | **15 – 25 %** | 11 – 14 % | 8 – 12 % |
| Lipid | 2 – 3 % | 2 – 2.5 % | **4 – 5 %** |
| Ash | 2 – 3 % | 1.5 – 2 % | 1.2 – 1.8 % |
| Lysine (essential AAs) | **High** | Low | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The exceptionally high **β-glucan** content in barley is both a **nutritional asset** (cholesterol-lowering, prebiotic, FDA-approved heart-health claim) and a **brewing liability** (high wort viscosity, lautering problems). Modern maltsters use **Cytolytic (CY) modification** indices to measure β-glucanase activity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Pattern)

**Problem:** A barley sample weighing 100 g (wet) is oven-dried to a constant weight of 86 g. Calculate:
(a) Moisture content on wet basis.
(b) Moisture content on dry basis.
(c) The safe storage status (safe limit: 13 % wb).

**Solution:**

**(a) Wet-basis moisture content:**

$$\text{MC}_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \dfrac{100 - 86}{100} \times 100 = 14 \text{ \% (wb)}$$

**(b) Dry-basis moisture content:**

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \dfrac{100 - 86}{86} \times 100 = 16.28 \text{ \% (db)}$$

**Cross-verification using the derived formula:**

$$\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{0.14}{1 - 0.14} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.86} = 0.1628 \text{ (decimal)} = 16.28 \text{ \% (db)}$$

**(c) Safe storage status:**
Since $14 \text{ \% wb} > 13 \text{ \% wb}$, the sample is **NOT safe** for long-term storage. It must be dried further (e.g., to 12 % wb) to prevent fungal growth and mycotoxin (aflatoxin, ochratoxin) contamination.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hot Water Extract (HWE) of malt. State its typical range for premium malting barley.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Hot Water Extract (HWE) is the percentage of soluble solids (sugars, proteins, dextrins) obtained when ground malt (grist) is mashed with water under standardized EBC Congress mashing conditions. It is measured in **L°/kg** (Liters ° Plato per kg of malt). For premium 2-row malting barley, the HWE is typically **81 – 83 % on dry basis (db)**, while 6-row malts yield 78 – 80 % db. Higher HWE indicates better starch conversion and is a key quality parameter for brewers.

### Question 2: Differentiate between 2-row and 6-row barley. State one industrial use of each.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 2-Row Barley | 6-Row Barley |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fertility at rachis node | Only the central floret is fertile; two laterals are sterile | All three florets are fertile |
| Kernel size | Plump, large, uniform | Mixed (central kernels are symmetrical, lateral kernels are twisted/asymmetrical) |
| Starch content | Higher | Lower |
| Protein content | Lower (9.5 – 11.5 %) | Higher (11 – 14 %) |
| Industrial use | **Malting / brewing** (preferred) | **Animal feed** (and US-style adjunct brewing) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Describe the **botanical classification and morphology of barley**. With a neat labeled diagram, explain the structure of a barley kernel and state the biochemical composition of its endosperm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete malting process** of barley, including steeping, germination, and kilning. State the biochemical changes occurring at each stage and the role of gibberellic acid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Botanical classification and morphology (7 marks):**

- **Classification:**
  - Kingdom: Plantae; Family: **Poaceae (Gramineae)**; Genus: *Hordeum*; Species: *Hordeum vulgare* L.
  - Ploidy: Diploid (2n = 14 chromosomes).
- **Spike structure:** The barley inflorescence is a **spike** (ear) consisting of a central **rachis** bearing spikelets in groups of **three** at each node.
- **Row types:** Based on fertility, barley is classified into **2-row** (central floret fertile, laterals sterile) and **6-row** (all three florets fertile) varieties.
- **Kernel structure** (refer to the Section 4 mermaid diagram):
  1. **Husk** (lemma + palea) – fibrous outer covering; 6 – 13 % of kernel weight.
  2. **Pericarp + Testa** – protective seed coats.
  3. **Endosperm** (~ 75 – 80 % of kernel) – starchy reserve containing:
     - **Starch granules:** Large (Type A: 10 – 20 μm) and small (Type B: 1 – 5 μm).
     - **Proteins:** Hordeins (prolamins), glutelins, albumins, globulins.
     - **β-glucans:** Mixed-linkage (1→3, 1→4)-β-D-glucan at 3 – 11 % db.
  4. **Aleurone layer** – enzyme-secreting living cells.
  5. **Embryo (germ)** – rich in lipids, vitamins, and GA₃ precursors.

**(b) Malting process (7 marks):**

Malting is the **controlled partial germination** of barley, consisting of three stages:

1. **Steeping (36 – 48 hours):** Dry barley (12 % moisture) is immersed in water at 12 – 18 °C in cycles of wet and air-rest. Moisture is raised to **43 – 46 %**. The embryo becomes metabolically active, and **gibberellic acid (GA₃)** is synthesized in the scutellum and transported to the aleurone.

2. **Germination (4 – 5 days):** Steeped grain is held at 15 – 18 °C, > 95 % RH in a Saladin box or drum malting unit. GA₃ stimulates the aleurone to secrete:
   - **α-amylase** and **β-amylase** (starch → maltose + dextrins)
   - **Endopeptidases** (proteins → amino acids + peptides)
   - **β-glucanases** (β-glucans → glucose)
   - **Lipases** (lipids → free fatty acids)
   These enzymes **modify** (break down) the endosperm cell walls and protein matrix, releasing starch granules for subsequent mashing.

3. **Kilning:** Green malt is dried to arrest germination and develop flavor.
   - **Free drying:** 50 – 60 °C, moisture reduced from ~ 45 % to ~ 12 %.
   - **Curing:** 80 – 110 °C for 2 – 4 hours, developing **Maillard reaction** products (color, aroma).

**Role of Gibberellic Acid (GA₃):** Acts as a hormonal signal from the embryo to the aleurone, triggering the synthesis and secretion of hydrolytic enzymes that mobilize the starchy endosperm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **chemical composition of barley** in detail. Compare the composition of barley with wheat and maize. Highlight the unique role of **β-glucans** in barley. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **test weight, thousand kernel weight (TKW), germinative energy, and Kolbach index**. State their standard values for good malting barley. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Chemical composition of barley (7 marks):**

The chemical composition of barley (on a dry basis) is summarized below:

- **Starch:** 55 – 65 % (main carbohydrate reserve; amylose:amylopectin ratio ≈ 25:75).
- **Protein:** 8 – 13 % (with high **lysine** content — better than wheat).
- **β-Glucans (Mixed-linkage):** **3 – 11 %** — the highest among cereals.
- **Dietary Fiber (TDF):** 15 – 25 % (highest among major cereals).
- **Lipids:** 2 – 3 % (mostly in the germ).
- **Minerals (ash):** 2 – 3 % (rich in **selenium**, **phosphorus**).
- **Vitamins:** B-complex, Vitamin E (tocopherols + tocotrienols).
- **Polyphenols:** In the testa (especially in blue/black barley varieties).

**Comparison table (refer to Section 3.1.3):** Barley has **higher β-glucan, higher fiber, and higher lysine** than wheat and maize. Maize has the highest starch content.

**Unique role of β-glucans:**
- **Nutritional (health) role:** FDA-approved health claim for **cholesterol reduction** and **reduced risk of coronary heart disease** (≥ 3 g/day). Prebiotic; helps glycemic control.
- **Brewing role:** High β-glucan in malt leads to **high wort viscosity**, **slow lautering**, and **haze formation** in finished beer. Maltsters use the **β-glucanase enzyme** (developed during germination) to break down β-glucans.

**(b) Definition of quality parameters (7 marks):**

1. **Test Weight (TW):** Mass of barley per unit volume, expressed in **kg/hL**. For premium malting barley, TW should be ≥ **65 kg/hL**. Low TW indicates shriveled kernels (poor starch content).

2. **Thousand Kernel Weight (TKW):** Weight of 1000 kernels in grams. Premium malting barley has TKW = **35 – 50 g**. High TKW indicates plump, well-filled kernels with high extract potential.

3. **Germinative Energy (GE):** Percentage of kernels capable of germinating under standard conditions (within a defined time, typically 72 hours, with 4 mL or 8 mL of water). For good malting barley, **GE ≥ 95 %**. A difference between GE (4 mL) and GE (8 mL) is the **water sensitivity** indicator.

4. **Kolbach Index (KI):** Ratio of soluble protein to total protein in malt, expressed as a percentage:
$$\text{KI} = \dfrac{\text{Soluble protein}}{\text{Total protein}} \times 100$$
For well-modified malt, KI = **40 – 45 %**. Higher KI = greater proteolysis = better modification; lower KI = under-modified malt.

**Summary table of standard malting barley specifications:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Test Weight | ≥ 65 kg/hL |
| TKW | 35 – 50 g |
| Protein | 9.5 – 11.5 % |
| Germinative Energy (72 hr) | ≥ 95 % |
| Moisture (safe storage) | ≤ 13 % wb |
| Kolbach Index (malt) | 40 – 45 % |
| Hot Water Extract | 81 – 83 % db |
| β-Glucan (malt) | < 200 mg/L (in wort) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture:** Students often forget to convert. The safe storage limit is **13 % wb**, not 13 % db.
> 2. **Mixing up test weight and TKW:** Test weight is **volume-based** (kg/hL); TKW is **kernel-count based** (g per 1000 kernels).
> 3. **Forgetting the husk role:** Always mention that the **husk adherence** is the unique morphological feature of barley that makes it superior to wheat for **mashing/brewing** applications.
> 4. **Misnaming enzymes:** In malting, it is the **aleurone layer** that secretes enzymes, *not* the starchy endosperm. The embryo provides the **gibberellic acid** signal.
> 5. **Skipping the role of β-glucans:** This is a high-yield KTU topic — always mention **both** the nutritional benefit and the brewing liability.
> 6. **Row-type confusion:** In 6-row barley, the **lateral kernels are twisted/asymmetrical**, not the central kernels. The central kernels of 6-row are symmetrical and plump.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### A. Quick-Recall Bullets

- **Botanical name:** *Hordeum vulgare* L. **Family:** Poaceae. **Ploidy:** Diploid (2n = 14).
- **Global rank:** 4th in cereal production; **1st** in malting cereal.
- **Spike type:** Three spikelets per rachis node (unique among cereals).
- **Row types:** **2-row** (preferred for malting) and **6-row** (preferred for feed).
- **Key morphological feature:** **Husk adherence** (lemma + palea remain attached).
- **Hulled vs. Hull-less:** Hulled for malting; hull-less (*naked*) for direct food use.
- **Moisture limit for safe storage:** **≤ 13 % wb** (≈ 14.9 % db).
- **Test weight:** ≥ **65 kg/hL** (premium malting barley).
- **TKW:** **35 – 50 g** (plumpness indicator).
- **β-glucan content:** **3 – 11 % db** (highest among cereals; nutritional asset + brewing liability).
- **Aleurone layer:** Secretes α-amylase, β-amylase, proteases, and β-glucanases under GA₃ signal.
- **Malting stages:** **Steeping → Germination → Kilning** (SGK).
- **Steeping moisture target:** 43 – 46 %.
- **Kilning temperatures:** 50 – 60 °C (free drying) → 80 – 110 °C (curing).
- **Hot Water Extract (HWE):** **81 – 83 % db** (premium 2-row).
- **Kolbach Index:** **40 – 45 %** for well-modified malt.
- **Germinative Energy:** **≥ 95 %** for good malting barley.
- **Formula (wet to dry basis):** $\text{MC}_{db} = \dfrac{\text{MC}_{wb}}{1 - \text{MC}_{wb}}$.
- **Formula (extract dry basis):** $E_{db} = \dfrac{E_{as\text{-}is}}{1 - M_{db}}$.
- **Formula (Kolbach Index):** $\text{KI} = \dfrac{\text{Soluble N}}{\text{Total N}} \times 100$.
- **End products:** Beer, whisky, malt syrup, pearl barley, animal feed, bioethanol.
- **India-specific:** Rajasthan contributes **~ 50 %** of Indian barley production; primarily used as **feed (~ 60 %)** and for **malting (~ 25 %)**.

### B. Memory Hooks for KTU Exam Hall

1. **"HUSK = HELPFUL"** for brewing.
2. **"2-row = Plump = Malting"** vs. **"6-row = Mixed = Feed"**.
3. **"SGK"** = Steeping, Germination, Kilning — the maltster's mantra.
4. **"GA₃ = Green Light for Enzymes"** in the aleurone.
5. **"β-Glucan = Boon + Bane"** — nutritional hero, brewing villain.
6. **"65 kg/hL"** — the magic number for malting-grade test weight.
7. **"13 % wb = Safe Storage"** — never forget this number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutrient Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nutrient Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nutrient Composition** refers to the quantitative and qualitative profile of chemical constituents — both macro and micro — present in food grains (cereals and legumes). It encompasses the measurement and expression of **moisture, protein, carbohydrate (starch, dietary fibre, sugars), lipid (fat), ash (minerals), vitamins, antinutritional factors, and the calorific energy** derived from these constituents, expressed conventionally per **100 g** of edible portion on a fresh-weight or dry-weight basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Anchor):**
> Nutrient composition is the systematic analysis of **proximate principles** (moisture, ash, fat, protein, carbohydrate) and **micronutrient density** (vitamins, minerals, phytochemicals) in cereals and legumes, serving as the foundation for nutritional labelling, dietary recommendation, breeding selection, and product formulation in Food Technology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick-and-Mortar Building" Model

Imagine the human body as a **building under construction**:

- **Carbohydrates** = The *electricity* that powers every light, fan, and machine on site (immediate + reserve energy).
- **Proteins** = The *bricks, steel rods, and cement* — they form the actual walls, beams, and structural framework (muscle, enzyme, hormone synthesis).
- **Lipids (Fats)** = The *insulation and fuel reserve tank* — stored for emergencies, also serve as thermal insulation around organs.
- **Minerals** = The *nails, screws, and electrical wiring* — required in tiny amounts but indispensable for structural integrity.
- **Vitamins** = The *site engineers and supervisors* — they do not become part of the building but *catalyse* every reaction that uses the bricks.
- **Water (Moisture)** = The *transport medium* — moves materials, dissolves reagents, maintains workability.

**Cereals** are predominantly **carbohydrate-rich** (the "electricity supply") — they are the world's primary energy currency. **Legumes**, in contrast, are **protein-dense** (the "structural brick supplier") and complement cereals by supplying what cereals lack.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Tiers of Nutrients:**

| Tier | Class | Typical Daily Need | Energy/Structural Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macronutrients** | Carbohydrates | 45–65 % of total energy | Primary fuel |
| | Proteins | 10–35 % of total energy | Tissue building |
| | Lipids (Fats) | 20–35 % of total energy | Reserve fuel, insulation |
| | Water | 2–3 L | Solvent, transport |
| **Micronutrients** | Vitamins (A, B-complex, C, D, E, K) | mg–µg levels | Metabolic regulators |
| | Minerals (Ca, Fe, Zn, P, K, Mg) | mg levels | Skeletal, enzymatic |

## 1.4 Standard Reporting Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Proximate composition** is reported in **g per 100 g** edible portion.
- **Energy** is reported in **kcal** or **kJ** (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ).
- **Vitamin content** is reported as **mg or µg per 100 g**.
- **Mineral content** is reported as **mg per 100 g**.
- **Amino acid score** is dimensionless (no unit).
- **Dry-weight basis (DWB)** vs **fresh-weight basis (FWB)** conversions are standard KTU-level numerical exercises.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Proximate Principles — Logic Steps

The classical "**Weende Analysis**" (Henneberg & Stohmann, 1860) reduces any food to six measurable fractions. Modern cereal-legume science retains this framework:

### Step 1 — Moisture Determination
- **Method:** Hot-air oven drying at **130 °C for 1 h** (cereals) or **105 °C to constant weight** (general).
- **Why:** Moisture is the single largest variable; all other nutrients are reported on dry-weight basis for accurate comparison.

### Step 2 — Ash (Total Mineral Content)
- **Method:** Muffle furnace incineration at **550 ± 10 °C** until grey-white ash obtained.
- **Why:** Ash represents the inorganic residue — proxy for total mineral load.

### Step 3 — Crude Fat (Lipid)
- **Method:** **Soxhlet extraction** with petroleum ether (boiling range 40–60 °C) for 6–8 h.
- **Why:** Non-polar solvent extracts only the neutral lipids (triglycerides, sterols); phospholipids and glycolipids remain unextracted.

### Step 4 — Crude Protein
- **Method:** **Kjeldahl** or **Dumas** method; nitrogen is converted to ammonia, distilled, titrated.
- **Conversion factor:**
$$\% \text{ Crude Protein} = \% \text{ Nitrogen} \times F$$
- **F = 6.25** for general foods, **5.70** for wheat, **5.95** for rice, **6.25** for most legumes, **5.36** for nuts.

### Step 5 — Crude Fibre
- **Method:** Sequential acid (1.25 % H₂SO₄) and alkali (1.25 % NaOH) digestion.
- **Why:** Residue represents cellulose + lignin — *indigestible* carbohydrate.

### Step 6 — Nitrogen-Free Extract (NFE) = Carbohydrate by Difference
$$\text{NFE (\%)} = 100 - (\text{Moisture} + \text{Ash} + \text{Fat} + \text{CP} + \text{CF})$$

## 2.2 Composition of Major Cereals (per 100 g, edible portion)

| Cereal | Moisture (g) | Protein (g) | Fat (g) | CHO (g) | Fibre (g) | Ash (g) | Energy (kcal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rice (brown, milled) | 12–14 | 7.5 | 2.7 | 76 | 1.0 | 1.2 | 362 |
| Wheat (whole) | 12 | 12.1 | 1.7 | 71 | 2.0 | 1.5 | 340 |
| Maize (yellow) | 14 | 9.0 | 4.5 | 73 | 2.7 | 1.2 | 365 |
| Sorghum | 12 | 10.4 | 3.1 | 70 | 1.6 | 1.6 | 349 |
| Pearl Millet (Bajra) | 12 | 11.6 | 5.0 | 67 | 1.2 | 2.3 | 361 |
| Finger Millet (Ragi) | 13 | 7.3 | 1.3 | 72 | 3.6 | 2.7 | 328 |
| Oats | 8 | 13.0 | 7.0 | 67 | 10.6 | 2.0 | 389 |

## 2.3 Composition of Major Legumes (per 100 g, raw)

| Legume | Moisture (g) | Protein (g) | Fat (g) | CHO (g) | Fibre (g) | Ash (g) | Energy (kcal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chickpea (Bengal gram) | 11 | 20.0 | 5.6 | 60 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 360 |
| Pigeon pea (Tur) | 11 | 22.0 | 1.7 | 63 | 1.0 | 3.5 | 335 |
| Lentil (Masoor) | 12 | 24.0 | 1.0 | 60 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 317 |
| Green gram (Moong) | 10 | 25.0 | 1.2 | 56 | 1.0 | 3.5 | 334 |
| Black gram (Urad) | 11 | 24.0 | 1.4 | 59 | 1.0 | 3.2 | 340 |
| Soybean | 8 | 38.0 | 19.0 | 30 | 4.5 | 4.5 | 446 |
| Cowpea | 11 | 24.0 | 1.0 | 60 | 1.0 | 3.0 | 323 |
| Field bean (Rajma) | 12 | 22.0 | 1.6 | 60 | 4.0 | 3.0 | 333 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam favourites** for OEFTT836 Module 2.

### (A) Energy Calculation — Atwater Factors

$$E_{\text{total}} \; (\text{kcal}) = 4 \cdot P + 4 \cdot C + 9 \cdot F$$

where $P$, $C$, $F$ are masses of protein, carbohydrate, fat in grams.

### (B) Dry-Weight Basis Conversion

$$X_{\text{DWB}} = \frac{X_{\text{FWB}} \times 100}{(100 - M)}$$

where $M$ is moisture percentage and $X$ is the nutrient concentration.

### (C) Protein Quality — Chemical Score

$$\text{CS} = \frac{\text{mg of EAA per g test protein}}{\text{mg of same EAA per g reference (FAO) protein}} \times 100$$

The limiting amino acid gives the score. **Lysine** limits cereals; **Methionine** limits legumes.

### (D) Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)

$$\text{PER} = \frac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein consumed (g)}}$$

### (E) Biological Value (BV)

$$\text{BV (\%)} = \frac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N absorbed}} \times 100$$

### (F) Net Protein Utilization (NPU)

$$\text{NPU} = \text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}$$

### (G) Essential Amino Acid Index (EAAI)

$$\text{EAAI} = \sqrt[n]{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{a_i}{b_i}} \times 100$$

where $a_i$ = % of EAA in test protein, $b_i$ = % of same EAA in reference protein, $n$ = number of EAAs considered.

### (H) Nitrogen-to-Protein Factor

$$\% \text{Protein} = \% N \times F_{N}$$

with $F_{N}$ values: wheat = **5.70**, rice = **5.95**, maize = **6.25**, soybean = **5.71**, milk = **6.38**.

## 2.5 Mineral & Vitamin Highlights — Comparative Snapshot

| Nutrient | Cereals (rich source) | Legumes (rich source) |
|---|---|---|
| Iron | Sorghum, bajra (~8 mg) | Soybean, lentil (~7–10 mg) |
| Zinc | Wheat bran (~15 mg) | Chickpea, soybean (~3–4 mg) |
| Calcium | Ragi (~350 mg) | Rajma (~150 mg) |
| B-Vitamins | Whole wheat, brown rice | All legumes (esp. thiamine) |
| Vitamin A precursor | Yellow maize | — |
| Vitamin C | — | Germinating pulses (sprouts) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nutritional labelling** under FSSAI (India) / FDA mandates exact declaration of proximate values.
- **Food product formulation** (e.g., "maida-fortified besan biscuits") requires precise composition balance.
- **Breeding programmes** (ICAR-IIMR, ICRISAT) screen germplasm using chemical scores.
- **Complementary food design** (poshak, bal-aahar) uses cereal-legume blending (typically **70:30** cereal:legume) to overcome the *lysine–methionine* gap.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sample Numerical 1 — Energy Computation from Atwater Factors

**Problem (KTU-style):** A food product contains **12 g protein, 8 g fat, 70 g carbohydrate, and 5 g moisture** per 100 g. Compute the total metabolisable energy in **kcal** and convert to **kJ**.

### Step 1 — Identify the three energy-yielding macronutrients

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 12 \text{ g (protein)} \\
F &= 8 \text{ g (fat)} \\
C &= 70 \text{ g (carbohydrate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Moisture does not contribute energy.

### Step 2 — Apply the Atwater coefficient formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kcal}} &= 4 \cdot P + 9 \cdot F + 4 \cdot C \\
&= 4 \cdot (12) + 9 \cdot (8) + 4 \cdot (70)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute each term

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 \cdot P &= 48 \text{ kcal} \\
9 \cdot F &= 72 \text{ kcal} \\
4 \cdot C &= 280 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kcal}} &= 48 + 72 + 280 \\
&= 400 \text{ kcal per 100 g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to kilojoules

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kJ}} &= E_{\text{kcal}} \times 4.184 \\
&= 400 \times 4.184 \\
&= 1673.6 \text{ kJ per 100 g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Energy = **400 kcal (1673.6 kJ) per 100 g**

---

## 3.2 Sample Numerical 2 — Dry-Weight Basis (DWB) Conversion

**Problem:** Wheat contains **12.1 % protein on a fresh-weight basis (FWB)** and has **12 % moisture**. Express protein on a **dry-weight basis (DWB)**.

### Step 1 — Write the DWB formula

$$
X_{\text{DWB}} = \frac{X_{\text{FWB}} \times 100}{(100 - M)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
X_{\text{DWB}} = \frac{12.1 \times 100}{(100 - 12)}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify denominator

$$
X_{\text{DWB}} = \frac{1210}{88}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the quotient

$$
X_{\text{DWB}} = 13.75 \text{ \% protein (DWB)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by logic:** Since we removed the moisture "dilution", the DWB concentration must be **higher** than FWB. Indeed $13.75 > 12.1$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical 3 — Chemical Score Determination (Limiting Amino Acid)

**Problem:** A cereal protein sample contains **lysine at 25 mg/g protein**. The FAO reference pattern requires **lysine at 58 mg/g protein** for the limiting case. Compute the **Chemical Score (CS)** and comment on whether lysine is limiting.

### Step 1 — Recall CS formula

$$
\text{CS} = \frac{\text{EAA in test protein}}{\text{EAA in reference}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
\text{CS} = \frac{25}{58} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Compute

$$
\text{CS} = 43.10 \text{ \%}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

A score **< 100 %** indicates lysine is the limiting amino acid. Cereals typically score **35–55 %** in lysine — the very reason they must be complemented with legumes (which are rich in lysine).

---

## 3.4 Sample Numerical 4 — EAAI Computation

**Problem:** A legume protein contains the following EAAs as a fraction of the reference:

- Lysine: 1.20
- Methionine: 0.55
- Threonine: 0.85
- Tryptophan: 1.00

Compute the **Essential Amino Acid Index (EAAI)** for $n = 4$.

### Step 1 — Apply EAAI formula

$$
\text{EAAI} = \sqrt[4]{\prod_{i=1}^{4} \frac{a_i}{b_i}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the product

$$
\begin{aligned}
\prod \frac{a_i}{b_i} &= 1.20 \times 0.55 \times 0.85 \times 1.00 \\
&= 1.20 \times 0.55 = 0.66 \\
&= 0.66 \times 0.85 = 0.561 \\
&= 0.561 \times 1.00 = 0.561
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Take the 4th root

$$
\text{EAAI} = (0.561)^{1/4} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{EAAI} = 0.8660 \times 100 = 86.60 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An EAAI > 80 % indicates a **good-quality protein**. The legume is slightly limited by **methionine** (the lowest ratio, 0.55).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| If the question asks for… | Apply formula… | Section reference |
|---|---|---|
| Energy in kcal from macros | Atwater 4-4-9 | §2.4(A) |
| Convert FWB → DWB | $\frac{X_{FWB} \times 100}{100 - M}$ | §2.4(B) |
| Identify limiting amino acid | Chemical Score | §2.4(C) |
| Overall protein quality | EAAI or NPU | §2.4(G)/(F) |
| Protein from N value | $N \times F_N$ | §2.4(H) |
| Carbohydrate by difference | 100 − Σ others | §2.1 Step 6 |

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Summary — Cereal vs Legume

| Parameter | Cereals | Legumes |
|---|---|---|
| Primary nutrient | Carbohydrate (60–80 %) | Protein (20–38 %) |
| Limiting EAA | Lysine | Methionine (+ cysteine) |
| Fat content | Low (1–7 %), except oats, maize | Variable; soybean very high (19 %) |
| Fibre | Whole grain rich | Variable; soybean, rajma high |
| Energy density | Moderate (330–390 kcal/100 g) | Moderate-high (315–445 kcal/100 g) |
| Glycemic index | High (rice, wheat) to low (oats, barley) | Low to moderate |
| Antinutrients | Phytic acid (bran) | Lectins, tannins, trypsin inhibitors |
| Complementary synergy | Supplies methionine | Supplies lysine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synergy Rule (KTU Favourite):** Mix cereals and legumes in a ratio of **70:30 (cereal:legume)** — the resulting mixture's Chemical Score for both lysine AND methionine exceeds **85 %**, making it equivalent to animal protein of moderate quality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Nutrients in Cereals & Legumes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Grain Nutrient Pool"]:::root --> B["Proximate Principles"]:::branch
    A --> C["Micronutrients"]:::branch
    A --> D["Phytochemicals and Antinutrients"]:::branch
    A --> E["Energy"]:::branch

    B --> B1["Moisture 5 to 15 percent"]
    B --> B2["Ash 1 to 3 percent"]
    B --> B3["Crude Protein 7 to 40 percent"]
    B --> B4["Crude Fat 1 to 19 percent"]
    B --> B5["Crude Fibre 1 to 10 percent"]
    B --> B6["NFE Carbohydrate 30 to 80 percent"]

    C --> C1["Vitamins B complex and A precursor"]
    C --> C2["Minerals Iron Zinc Calcium Phosphorus"]

    D --> D1["Phytic Acid in bran"]
    D --> D2["Trypsin Inhibitors in legumes"]
    D --> D3["Lectins and Tannins"]
    D --> D4["Saponins and Oxalates"]

    E --> E1["From Carbohydrate 4 kcal per g"]
    E --> E2["From Protein 4 kcal per g"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
