
## 4.2 Proximate Analysis Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Sample 100 g ground grain"]:::stage --> S2["Moisture by Oven 105 degree C"]
    S2 --> S3["Defatted residue"]
    S3 --> S4["Soxhlet Fat extraction 6 to 8 h"]
    S4 --> S5["Crude Protein by Kjeldahl N x F"]
    S4 --> S6["Crude Fibre by Acid Alkali digestion"]
    S3 --> S7["Ash by Muffle furnace 550 degree C"]
    S7 --> S8["NFE by Difference 100 minus sum"]
    S8 --> S9["Energy Atwater 4 4 9"]

    classDef stage fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Cereal–Legume Complementary Synergy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CE["CEREAL Pool"]:::cereal --> CE1["Rich in Methionine 2.5 percent"]
    CE --> CE2["Deficient in Lysine 35 to 55 percent CS"]
    CE --> CE3["High Carbohydrate 65 to 80 percent"]

    LG["LEGUME Pool"]:::legume --> LG1["Rich in Lysine 6.5 to 7.0 percent"]
    LG --> LG2["Deficient in Methionine 0.8 to 1.2 percent CS"]
    LG --> LG3["High Protein 20 to 40 percent"]

    CE1 --> MIX["Blend 70 to 30 cereal legume"]:::blend
    LG1 --> MIX
    CE2 --> MIX
    LG2 --> MIX
    MIX --> OUT["Balanced Protein Chemical Score greater than 85 percent"]
    MIX --> OUT2["Adequate Energy 350 to 400 kcal per 100 g"]

    classDef cereal fill:#fde2c5,stroke:#b35900,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef legume fill:#d8efd0,stroke:#2d6a2d,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef blend fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Protein Quality Evaluation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Protein Quality Assessment"]:::top --> Q1["Chemical Score CS single EAA"]
    Q --> Q2["EAAI All EAA combined"]
    Q --> Q3["Biological Value BV in vivo rat"]
    Q --> Q4["NPU Net Protein Utilization"]
    Q --> Q5["PER Protein Efficiency Ratio"]

    Q1 --> OUT["Rapid screening of limiting EAA"]:::out
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
    Q4 --> OUT
    Q5 --> OUT

    classDef top fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a08000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "proximate composition" of a food grain. List the six fractions determined in Weende's proximate analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Proximate composition is the quantitative determination of the major nutrient classes in a food grain, expressed as percentages on a 100 g basis. The six Weende fractions are:
1. Moisture (hot-air oven drying)
2. Ash (muffle furnace incineration)
3. Crude fat (Soxhlet extraction)
4. Crude protein (Kjeldahl N × factor)
5. Crude fibre (acid–alkali digestion)
6. Nitrogen-Free Extract / NFE (carbohydrate by difference).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is "methionine" the limiting amino acid in legumes, while "lysine" is limiting in cereals? How does blending solve this?**

**Model Answer:**
Legumes have low sulfur-containing amino acids (Met + Cys ≈ 1 %) but high lysine (~6.5 %). Cereals have adequate methionine but only 35–55 % of the FAO lysine requirement. When blended in a 70:30 cereal:legume ratio, the lysine-rich legume compensates for the cereal deficit, and the methionine-rich cereal compensates for the legume deficit. The combined Chemical Score rises above 85 %, achieving nutritional equivalence with moderate-quality animal protein.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Always write the **units explicitly** (g/100 g, mg, kcal). Marks are *routinely* lost when units are omitted.
> 2. When using the **N × F** conversion, students commonly use F = 6.25 for **all** foods — but for wheat it is **5.70** and rice is **5.95**. Wrong factor = full mark cut.
> 3. In "chemical score" problems, state **which amino acid is the limiting one** and **why** — don't just compute the percentage.
> 4. Numerical answers must show **all substitutions**, not just the final answer.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the proximate composition of **cereals and legumes** with reference to the data given in the following table. Explain why they are considered nutritionally complementary.

**Tabulated Data (per 100 g):**

| Parameter | Rice (milled) | Wheat (whole) | Chickpea | Soybean |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Protein (g) | 7.5 | 12.1 | 20.0 | 38.0 |
| Fat (g) | 2.7 | 1.7 | 5.6 | 19.0 |
| Carbohydrate (g) | 76.0 | 71.0 | 60.0 | 30.0 |
| Iron (mg) | 0.8 | 4.0 | 6.0 | 8.0 |

#### Model Solution (a):

**Observation 1 — Protein asymmetry:**
Cereals (rice 7.5 %, wheat 12.1 %) supply only **half** the protein of legumes (chickpea 20 %, soybean 38 %). Soybean is the densest plant protein source known.

**Observation 2 — Carbohydrate asymmetry:**
Cereals are **carbohydrate-dominant** (71–76 %), whereas legumes contain 30–60 %. Soybean is the lowest, making it suitable for diabetic-friendly formulations.

**Observation 3 — Fat asymmetry:**
Legumes generally have higher fat (chickpea 5.6 %, soybean 19 %) than cereals. Soybean's lipid fraction contains health-promoting **linoleic and linolenic acids**.

**Observation 4 — Mineral synergy:**
Iron content of legumes (chickpea 6, soybean 8 mg) is roughly **2–10 × higher** than rice (0.8 mg) and comparable to whole wheat (4 mg). This compensates for the phytic acid loss during milling.

**Nutritional Complementarity Conclusion:**
Cereals provide **cheap energy + methionine**; legumes provide **protein + lysine + iron**. A 70:30 blend delivers an essential amino acid profile equivalent to animal protein at a fraction of the cost — the foundation of India's Mid-Day Meal Scheme and ICDS supplementary nutrition.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A weaning food formulation contains **rice flour (60 g)**, **soybean flour (25 g)**, and **sugar (15 g)** per 100 g. Compute its **crude protein content** given that rice has **7.5 %** and soybean **38 %** protein (sugar = 0 %).

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Protein contributed by rice flour:**
$$
P_{\text{rice}} = 60 \times \frac{7.5}{100} = 4.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Protein contributed by soybean flour:**
$$
P_{\text{soy}} = 25 \times \frac{38}{100} = 9.5 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Protein contributed by sugar:**
$$
P_{\text{sugar}} = 15 \times \frac{0}{100} = 0 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Total crude protein in 100 g mixture:**
$$
P_{\text{total}} = 4.5 + 9.5 + 0 = 14.0 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Express per 100 g (already per 100 g):**
$$
\boxed{\text{CP} = 14.0 \text{ g per 100 g weaning mix}}
$$

**Verification:** This exceeds the 12–13 g/100 g threshold recommended by WHO/UNICEF for complementary foods — the formulation is nutritionally adequate in protein density.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Atwater energy factors (4-4-9)** with reference to the heat of combustion and digestibility of macronutrients. Compute the metabolisable energy of **100 g of oats** containing **13 g protein, 7 g fat, and 67 g carbohydrate**.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Conceptual Explanation:**
The Atwater factors account for the *gross energy of combustion* (Bomb calorimeter values) minus the *energy lost in faeces, urine, and as methane*. The corrected, physiologically available values are:

- **4 kcal/g for carbohydrate** — Gross energy 4.15 kcal/g minus ~4 % faecal loss.
- **4 kcal/g for protein** — Gross energy 5.65 kcal/g minus faecal + urinary nitrogenous losses (urea, creatinine).
- **9 kcal/g for fat** — Gross energy 9.40 kcal/g minus minimal faecal loss (high digestibility).

**Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Identify inputs:**
$$
P = 13 \text{ g}, \quad F = 7 \text{ g}, \quad C = 67 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Atwater equation:**
$$
E = 4P + 9F + 4C
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$
E = 4 \cdot (13) + 9 \cdot (7) + 4 \cdot (67)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
4P &= 52 \text{ kcal} \\
9F &= 63 \text{ kcal} \\
4C &= 268 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum:**
$$
E = 52 + 63 + 268 = 383 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ:**
$$
E = 383 \times 4.184 = 1602.5 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{oats}} = 383 \text{ kcal} \approx 1603 \text{ kJ per 100 g}}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Biological Value (BV)** and **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)**. A protein has **BV = 70 %** and **digestibility = 85 %**. Compute its NPU and comment on its quality classification.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Definitions:**
- **Biological Value (BV):** The proportion of absorbed nitrogen that is retained by the body for growth and maintenance, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{BV (\%)} = \frac{\text{N retained}}{\text{N absorbed}} \times 100
$$

- **Net Protein Utilization (NPU):** The proportion of ingested nitrogen that is retained — combines digestibility with BV.

$$
\text{NPU} = \text{BV} \times \text{Digestibility}
$$

**Computation:**

**Step 1 — Convert BV to decimal:**
$$
\text{BV} = 70 / 100 = 0.70
$$

**Step 2 — Convert digestibility to decimal:**
$$
D = 85 / 100 = 0.85
$$

**Step 3 — Apply NPU formula:**
$$
\text{NPU} = 0.70 \times 0.85
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**
$$
\text{NPU} = 0.595 = 59.5 \text{ \%}
$$

**Quality Classification (FAO Reference):**

| NPU Range | Quality Class |
|---|---|
| > 70 % | Excellent (egg, milk, soy isolate) |
| 50–70 % | Good (cereal-legume blends) |
| 40–50 % | Fair (single cereals) |
| < 40 % | Poor (gelatin) |

**Comment:** The computed NPU of **59.5 %** places this protein in the **"Good"** category — comparable to well-formulated cereal-legume blends. It is acceptable for adult maintenance but suboptimal for rapidly growing children, who require NPU > 70 %.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Before-Exam One-Page Summary**

- **Proximate composition** = Moisture + Ash + Fat + Protein + Fibre + NFE.
- **Carbohydrate by difference** (NFE) is the largest fraction in cereals; **protein** is the largest fraction in legumes.
- **Atwater 4-4-9 rule:** $E = 4P + 4C + 9F$ kcal; multiply by **4.184** for kJ.
- **N × F protein factors:** wheat = 5.70, rice = 5.95, milk = 6.38, soybean = 5.71, default = 6.25.
- **DWB conversion:** $X_{\text{DWB}} = \frac{X_{\text{FWB}} \times 100}{100 - M}$.
- **Limiting EAA:** Lysine in cereals, methionine in legumes.
- **70:30 cereal:legume blend** → Chemical Score > 85 %, equivalent to moderate animal protein.
- **Protein quality tests:** Chemical Score (in vitro), PER, BV, NPU, EAAI (in vivo rat assays).
- **Ragi** is the richest **calcium** source among cereals (~350 mg/100 g); **soybean** is the richest plant **protein** (~38 %); **yellow maize** is the only cereal with significant **β-carotene** (provitamin A).
- **Antinutrients:** phytic acid (cereal bran), trypsin inhibitors + lectins (raw legumes — destroyed by cooking/autoclaving).
- **Energy density rule:** Fat content × 9 dominates; soybean (19 % fat) = highest energy among legumes.
- Always show **units**; always state the **limiting amino acid** explicitly; always verify **DWB > FWB** in numericals.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Atwater 4-4-9:** *"**Fats are Fat Cats** — they give **nine** times more energy than protein or carbohydrate, which both give **four**."*

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — NUTRIENT COMPOSITION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Production of Malt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Commercial Production of Malt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Malt** is a value-added, enzymatically active food ingredient obtained by the controlled, partial **germination (modification)** of a cleaned cereal grain (most commonly **barley (*Hordeum vulgare* L.)**, but also wheat, sorghum, rye, oats, and millets), followed by **kilning (drying)** to arrest the germinative metabolism, stabilize the developed enzyme system, and develop characteristic colour, aroma, and flavour. The *Codex Alimentarius* and *Brewing Science* literature define malt as "the product obtained from barley or other cereals that has undergone a process of steeping, germination, and drying under controlled conditions, such that the endosperm modification yields a balanced profile of amylolytic, proteolytic, and cytolytic enzymes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Malt is **NOT** a simple sprouted grain. It is a *biotechnologically controlled intermediate* where germination is **deliberately stopped** at a point of maximum enzymatic activity and minimum metabolic reserve loss. The key word is **"controlled modification"**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a cereal grain (especially barley) as a **sealed time-capsule** filled with starch (the food reserve for the future plant) and a sleeping enzyme factory. When a farmer plants barley in the field, nature activates this factory — the enzymes wake up, break down the starch into sugar to feed the growing seedling, and the rootlets/sprout emerge. The farmer then harvests the *green malt* before it is ruined.

**Commercial malting is essentially "industrial babysitting of this natural process."** We mimic spring conditions in giant climate-controlled boxes, allow the enzymes to build up, then quickly dry the grain in a kiln (essentially a giant oven) to **pause** nature in its sweetest, most enzymatically-active moment.

| Stage | Natural Process (Field) | Commercial Process (Malthouse) |
|---|---|---|
| Water supply | Rain | **Steeping tanks** (controlled) |
| Temperature | Ambient | Thermostatted germination floors |
| Time | Days to weeks | **4 – 6 days** |
| Endpoint | Plant matures | **Kilned** (dried) at 50 – 85 °C |
| Product | Barley plant | **Malt** (preserved enzyme kit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Malted Milk" connection:** Just as the food industry uses malt in **malted milk powder, Horlicks, Bournvita, breakfast cereals, malt vinegar, malted bread (e.g., Brioche) and maltodextrins**, the brewing industry uses it as the *enzymatic heart* of beer. Both are essentially using the *same* concentrated enzyme cocktail that nature designed for seed germination.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU Board Favourites)

- **Steeping Moisture Target:** 42 – 46 % (w/w) for barley (critical window; below 38 % = incomplete modification; above 48 % = water-sensitive, mould risk).
- **Germination Temperature:** 15 – 20 °C (optimum ≈ 18 °C).
- **Germination Relative Humidity:** > 95 %.
- **Germination Duration:** 4 – 7 days (4–5 days for pale malt, 5–7 days for highly modified malt).
- **Kilning — Free Drying Stage:** 50 – 65 °C for 12–18 h.
- **Kilning — Curing Stage:** 75 – 85 °C (pale malt) up to 220 °C (roasted malt).
- **Final Malt Moisture:** 3 – 5 % (water activity *a*w ≈ 0.20 – 0.35, microbially stable).
- **Malt Yield:** typically 78 – 82 % (kg malt / kg raw barley).
- **Standard Reference Unit of Diastatic Power:** 1 °Lintner = the amylase activity that produces 1 g of maltose from 100 g of malt under standard conditions (in 1 h at 20 °C) = approximately **0.42 °WK** (Windisch–Kolbach).
- **Standard Extract for Pale Barley Malt:** 79 – 82 % (Fine Grind, as-is basis).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** KTU examiners love the 80 % rule — pale barley malt typically yields ~80 % extract, has ~80 % malt yield from raw grain, and the diastatic power of a good lager malt is roughly ~80 °Lintner. Memorize this triad.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Macro-Stages of Commercial Malting

The commercial malting process is universally described in **three sequential macro-stages**, each governed by distinct physical and biochemical targets. We deconstruct each below.

#### **Stage A — Steeping (Hydration Phase)**

Steeping is the **controlled immersion and aeration** of cleaned, graded barley in water (often with intermittent "air rests") to raise the grain moisture content from the as-harvested level (~12 %) to the target **42 – 46 %** required for metabolic activation.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Cleaning & Grading** — barley is passed through magnetic separators, de-stoners, and indented cylinders to remove foreign matter, broken kernels, and *half-corns* (the latter are *dead* and will not germinate).
2. **Wet Steep (submerged)** — grain is immersed in 12 – 18 °C water for 8 – 12 h. The embryo imbibes water; gibberellic acid (GA) biosynthesis begins in the scutellum.
3. **Air Rest** — water is drained; grain is exposed to humidified air for 6 – 12 h. This critical step supplies **O₂** (essential because submerged embryos ferment anaerobically, producing ethanol and becoming dormant again) and allows CO₂ to dissipate.
4. **Alternate Wet/Air Cycles** — 2 – 3 wet cycles are typically used. Total steeping time = **36 – 48 h**.
5. **Final Moisture Check** — moisture is measured by conductivity or NIR; chitted (radicle-emerged) grains should be ~ 75 – 85 % of the lot by the end of steeping.
6. **Gibberellic Acid Boost (optional)** — in modern malthouses, exogenous **GA₃ (0.1 – 0.5 ppm)** is added to the final steep water to accelerate enzyme synthesis and reduce germination time (the *Gibberellin–Barley Endosperm Protocol*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Air Rest"?** Without O₂, the embryo switches to anaerobic respiration, producing ethanol, slowing GA migration to the aleurone layer, and producing *steep-out* off-flavours. This is a common KTU 2-mark question.

#### **Stage B — Germination (Modification Phase)**

This is the **biochemical heart of malting**. The wetted grain is held in a controlled environment (germination floor, Saladin box, drum malter, or pneumatic box) for 4 – 7 days.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Spreading / Loading** — chitted grain is loaded at a depth of 0.6 – 1.5 m onto a perforated floor or into a rotating drum.
2. **Temperature & Humidity Control** — air at 15 – 20 °C and > 95 % RH is continuously blown through the bed; the grain temperature is held within ±0.5 °C of setpoint.
3. **Turning** — the bed is turned every 8 – 12 h (in floor systems) or continuously (in drum/pneumatic systems) to break up CO₂ pockets, prevent matting of rootlets, and ensure uniform modification.
4. **Modification** — GA₃ diffuses from scutellum → aleurone layer → triggers synthesis of:
   - **α-Amylase** (EC 3.2.1.1) — cleaves α-1,4 glycosidic bonds in starch.
   - **β-Amylase** (EC 3.2.1.2) — cleaves maltose units from non-reducing ends.
   - **Limit Dextrinase** (EC 3.2.1.41) — attacks α-1,6 branch points.
   - **Endo- and Exo-Proteinases** — degrade hordein storage proteins.
   - **β-Glucanases & Pentosanases (Cytolytic enzymes)** — degrade cell walls.
5. **Acrospire Growth** — the acrospire (the embryonic shoot) grows under the husk; in well-modified malt it should be ¾ to full length of the kernel.
6. **Green Malt** — the final germinated product is called *green malt*; moisture is ~ 40 – 45 %; rich in enzymes but microbially unstable — must be kilned within hours.

#### **Stage C — Kilning (Drying & Flavour Development)**

Kilning serves **three coupled objectives**: (i) arrest germination by removing water, (ii) preserve enzymes (most amylases survive up to 80 °C), and (iii) develop **colour, aroma, and flavour** through Maillard reactions and caramelization at higher curing temperatures.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Free Drying Phase** — 12 – 18 h at 50 – 65 °C; air-on temperature ~ 65 °C, air-off ~ 25 °C. Moisture drops from 45 % → ~ 20 %.
2. **Intermediate Phase** — 60 – 75 °C for 4 – 6 h; moisture drops to ~ 10 %.
3. **Curing Phase** — temperature raised to 75 – 85 °C (pale/Pilsner) or 100 – 220 °C (Munich, Vienna, Caramel, Roasted). Maillard + pyrolysis reactions generate melanoidins and aroma.
4. **Final Moisture** — brought to **3 – 5 %** (4.0 – 4.5 % is industrial standard for shelf-stability).
5. **Deculming (Rootlet Removal)** — rootlets (culms) are brittle after drying; they are broken off by friction in a *deculex* or *polishing drum*. They are sold as a high-protein animal-feed by-product.
6. **Storage & Maturation** — malt is held in silos for 2 – 6 weeks (a *rest period*) to equilibrate moisture and allow slow flavour maturation. It is then screened, polished, and bagged or bulk-loaded.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Trade-off in Kilning:** Higher curing temperature = more flavour/colour BUT lower enzyme survival. Pilsner malt is cured at 80 °C to preserve enzymes; Chocolate malt is roasted at 220 °C and is essentially *enzyme-dead* (a flavouring specialty malt only).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (No Pipe Symbols)

| Formula / Parameter | Equation (LaTeX-safe) | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Malt Yield** (mass balance) | $\text{Yield \%} = \dfrac{m_{\text{malt, dry}}}{m_{\text{barley, dry}}} \times 100$ | 78 – 82 % | Plant economics, raw material procurement |
| **Moisture Gain during Steeping** | $\Delta M_{\text{steep}} = M_{\text{final}} - M_{\text{initial}}$ | ~ 30 percentage points | Process control in steep tanks |
| **Moisture Loss during Kilning** | $\Delta M_{\text{kiln}} = M_{\text{green malt}} - M_{\text{cured malt}}$ | ~ 40 percentage points | Energy requirement, dryer sizing |
| **Fine-Coarse Extract Difference (F-C)** | $\Delta E = E_{\text{fine grind}} - E_{\text{coarse grind}}$ | 1 – 2 % (well-modified) | *Most sensitive* index of modification |
| **Kolbach Index (KI)** | $\text{KI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{soluble}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | 35 – 45 % (pale malt) | Proteolytic modification indicator |
| **Diastatic Power conversion** | $^\circ\text{Lintner} \approx 2.3 \times ^\circ\text{WK} - 16$ | Lintner ≈ 2.3 × WK – 16 | International label reconciliation |
| **Friability** | $\text{Friability \%} = \dfrac{\text{mass of soft, modified grains}}{\text{total mass}} \times 100$ | > 80 % (well-modified) | Mechanical hardness of malt |
| **Malt Hydration Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{steep}} = \dfrac{\text{water absorbed}}{\text{steep time} \times \text{capacity}}$ | Plant-specific | Malthouse throughput |
| **Strecker Degradation Index (Maillard)** | $\sum_{i} c_{\text{aldehydes}}$ (qualitative) | Increases with T, t | Flavour chemistry proxy |
| **Final Aw of cured malt** | $a_w = f(M_{\text{final}}, T)$ | 0.20 – 0.35 | Microbial shelf-stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note on Pipe Replacement:** All mathematical absolute-value bars in table cells (e.g., for $\vert x \vert$) have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table-parser breakage.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The commercial malting process is the **archetypal industrial biotechnology platform** — predating even the word "biotechnology" itself. Its engineering significance spans:

- **Brewing industry** — provides fermentable wort from malt (without malt, there is no beer; without diastatic power, there is no starch-to-sugar conversion in the *mash tun*).
- **Distilling industry** — high-DP malt provides amylases that convert adjunct starches (maize, rice) into fermentable sugars (e.g., Scotch whisky, Irish whiskey, bourbon).
- **Breakfast cereal & confectionery** — malt extract (the water-soluble fraction of malt) is used in Horlicks, Milo, Ovaltine, and malted milk balls; the natural sugars and amino acids provide both nutrition and the characteristic brown colour.
- **Bakery industry** — malt flour (enzymatically active) is added to bread doughs to *correct* weak flour by supplementing α-amylase activity, improving crumb colour and crust browning.
- **Vinegar production** — malt vinegar is the oxidation product of malt wort fermented first by yeast, then by *Acetobacter aceti*.
- **Diabetic & low-glycemic foods** — partially modified (low-DP) malt is used as a slow-digestion starch source.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Balance for Commercial Malt Yield

**Problem Statement:** A malthouse receives **10,000 kg of clean barley** at a moisture content of **12.0 %**. The final cured malt has a moisture of **4.0 %** and weighs **8,250 kg**. Compute the (a) malt yield on an as-is basis, (b) malt yield on a dry basis, and (c) the water lost during the process.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

Let $m_{b}$ = mass of barley (as-is) = 10,000 kg, $M_{b}$ = barley moisture = 0.120, $m_{m}$ = mass of malt (as-is) = 8,250 kg, $M_{m}$ = malt moisture = 0.040.

**(a) As-is malt yield:**

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{as-is}} = \dfrac{m_{m,\text{as-is}}}{m_{b,\text{as-is}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{as-is}} = \dfrac{8{,}250}{10{,}000} \times 100 = 82.5\ \text{\%}$$

**(b) Dry-basis malt yield:**

First compute dry masses:

$$m_{b,\text{dry}} = m_{b,\text{as-is}} \times (1 - M_b) = 10{,}000 \times 0.880 = 8{,}800\ \text{kg}$$

$$m_{m,\text{dry}} = m_{m,\text{as-is}} \times (1 - M_m) = 8{,}250 \times 0.960 = 7{,}920\ \text{kg}$$

Then:

$$\text{Yield}_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{7{,}920}{8{,}800} \times 100 = 90.0\ \text{\%}$$

**Interpretation (the 'Why' and 'How'):** The 90 % dry-basis yield tells the engineer that 10 % of the *dry matter* is lost — this is the sum of (i) rootlet mass removed during deculming (~ 4 %), (ii) respiratory loss of the embryo as CO₂ during germination (~ 4 %), and (iii) leaching losses into steep water (~ 1 – 2 %). KTU expects this qualitative decomposition.

**(c) Total water lost during processing:**

Water in barley (initial) = $m_{b} \times M_b = 10{,}000 \times 0.120 = 1{,}200\ \text{kg}$.

Water in malt (final) = $m_{m} \times M_m = 8{,}250 \times 0.040 = 330\ \text{kg}$.

Water balance on process:

$$W_{\text{lost}} = W_{\text{initial}} + W_{\text{steep water absorbed}} - W_{\text{final}} - W_{\text{in rootlets}}$$

Simplified net water lost (assuming steep water is in excess, and that rootlets are not measured as malt):

$$W_{\text{net lost to kiln}} \approx 1{,}200 - 330 = 870\ \text{kg}$$

This is the **minimum water** the kiln fans must remove; real kilns must also remove the steep water (typically 1,000 – 1,500 kg per batch above) — a critical *energy audit* number.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kolbach Index (KI) and Proteolytic Modification

**Problem Statement:** A pale barley malt sample has **Total Nitrogen (TN) = 1.55 % (d.b.)** and **Soluble Nitrogen (SN) = 0.620 % (d.b.)**. A well-modified Pilsner malt must have KI = 38 – 42 %. Assess the modification.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$\text{KI} = \dfrac{\text{SN}}{\text{TN}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.620}{1.55} \times 100$$

$$\text{KI} = 40.0\ \text{\%}$$

**Interpretation (the 'Why' and 'How'):** This malt lies at the **lower-middle of the Pilsner specification band**, indicating moderate-to-good proteolytic modification. A KI < 35 % suggests under-modification (insufficient FAN for yeast nutrition → stuck fermentations), while a KI > 45 % suggests over-modification (excess FAN → haze instability and excessive yeast growth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1 % rise in KI corresponds approximately to a 4 – 5 mg/L rise in Free Amino Nitrogen (FAN) in the wort. KTU examiners love this linkage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fine-Coarse Extract Difference (Modification Index)

**Problem Statement:** A laboratory mashes a malt under two grind conditions. The fine-grind extract is **82.0 %** and the coarse-grind extract is **78.4 %**. Compute the F-C difference and assess the modification.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{fine}} - E_{\text{coarse}} = 82.0 - 78.4 = 3.6\ \text{\%}$$

**Interpretation (the 'Why' and 'How'):** A well-modified malt should have $\Delta E \leq 1.5$ % — meaning that even with a coarse grind, the enzymes can access and solubilize nearly all the starch. A $\Delta E$ of 3.6 % indicates that under-modification, the cell walls of the endosperm are not fully degraded, so the starch granules remain physically trapped behind a $\beta$-glucan barrier. Coarse-grind mashing cannot break this barrier; fine-grind does mechanically, hence the wide gap.

**Practical Engineering Action:** Extend the germination time by 24 – 48 h, OR add 0.25 ppm exogenous GA₃, OR raise the germination temperature by 1 °C.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Diastatic Power (Lintner ↔ Windisch-Kolbach)

**Problem Statement:** A malt analyst reports a Diastatic Power of **210 °WK**. Convert this to °Lintner for an international buyer.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

Using the standard empirical equation:

$$^\circ\text{Lintner} \approx 2.3 \times ^\circ\text{WK} - 16$$

$$^\circ\text{Lintner} \approx 2.3 \times 210 - 16 = 483 - 16 = 467\ ^\circ\text{Lintner}$$

**Interpretation (the 'Why' and 'How'):** The Lintner scale (used in USA, UK) and the Windisch-Kolbach scale (used in Germany, Europe) differ because of the reference temperature and the choice of substrate concentration in the original assay. The 2.3 factor is empirically derived; the -16 is the *zero-shift* (a 16 °Lintner malt reads 0 °WK — they don't both start at zero). KTU expects the equation, not just a numerical answer.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Steeping Water Uptake Kinetics

**Problem Statement:** A batch of 8,000 kg barley is steeped. After the first wet cycle (12 h), the grain weighs 11,200 kg. After the second wet cycle (12 h more), it weighs 13,200 kg. After the air rest (8 h), it weighs 12,400 kg. Compute (a) the moisture content after each phase, and (b) the *net water uptake rate* during the second wet cycle.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

Initial dry matter: $m_{d} = 8{,}000 \times 0.88 = 7{,}040\ \text{kg}$.

(a) **Moisture after each phase:**

$$\text{After 1st wet:}\ M_1 = \dfrac{11{,}200 - 7{,}040}{11{,}200} = \dfrac{4{,}160}{11{,}200} = 37.1\ \text{\%}$$

$$\text{After 2nd wet:}\ M_2 = \dfrac{13{,}200 - 7{,}040}{13{,}200} = \dfrac{6{,}160}{13{,}200} = 46.7\ \text{\%}$$

$$\text{After air rest:}\ M_3 = \dfrac{12{,}400 - 7{,}040}{12{,}400} = \dfrac{5{,}360}{12{,}400} = 43.2\ \text{\%}$$

**Interpretation (the 'Why' and 'How'):** The moisture *overshoots* during the wet phase (because of surface water) and *equilibrates* (decreases slightly) during the air rest, which is exactly the desired pattern. The target is ~ 43 – 46 %.

(b) **Net water uptake rate during 2nd wet cycle:**

$$\dot{W} = \dfrac{(13{,}200 - 11{,}200)\ \text{kg}}{12\ \text{h}} = \dfrac{2{,}000\ \text{kg}}{12\ \text{h}} \approx 166.7\ \text{kg/h}$$

This is the design basis for steep-tank pump capacity.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Parameter | Pale Malt (Pilsner) | Munich Malt | Caramel Malt | Roasted Malt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Curing temperature (°C) | 80 – 85 | 100 – 110 | 65 → 150 (in vessel) | 200 – 220 |
| Final moisture (%) | 3.5 – 4.5 | 3.0 – 4.0 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| Diastatic power (°Lintner) | > 80 (high) | 30 – 50 (medium) | < 5 (low) | 0 (none) |
| Colour (°EBC) | 3 – 4 | 15 – 25 | 60 – 120 | 900 – 1400 |
| Kolbach Index (%) | 38 – 42 | 30 – 38 | n/a | n/a |
| Extract (% d.b.) | 80 – 82 | 78 – 80 | 72 – 76 | 65 – 70 |
| End-use | Lager beer | Bock, Märzen | Ales, colour/flavour | Stout, porter |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Commercial Malting Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Barley Intake] --> A2[Cleaning and Grading]
    A2 --> A3[Storage Silos]
    A3 --> A4[Steeping Tank 1 - Wet]
    A4 --> A5[Air Rest Cycle]
    A5 --> A6[Steeping Tank 2 - Wet]
    A6 --> A7[Chit Inspection 75 to 85 percent]
    A7 --> B1[Germination Vessel - Saladin or Drum]
    B1 --> B2[Day 1 to 2 Acrospire Emerges]
    B2 --> B3[Day 3 to 4 Enzyme Synthesis Peaks]
    B3 --> B4[Day 5 to 6 Modification Complete]
    B4 --> B5[Green Malt Transfer to Kiln]
    B5 --> C1[Kiln Free Drying 50 to 65 C]
    C1 --> C2[Kiln Intermediate 65 to 75 C]
    C2 --> C3[Kiln Curing 75 to 85 C]
    C3 --> C4[Kiln Curing Specialty up to 220 C]
    C4 --> D1[Deculming and Polishing]
    D1 --> D2[Rootlet Byproduct to Feed Mill]
    D2 --> D3[Storage Silos Rest Period 2 to 6 Weeks]
    D3 --> D4[Final Screening and Bagging]
    D4 --> E1[Pale Malt]
    D4 --> E2[Munich Malt]
    D4 --> E3[Caramel and Crystal Malt]
    D4 --> E4[Roasted and Chocolate Malt]
    E1 --> F1[Brewing Industry]
    E1 --> F2[Distilling Industry]
    E1 --> F3[Bakery and Confectionery]
    E1 --> F4[Malt Extract and Breakfast Foods]
```

### 4.2 Enzyme & Modification Network (KTU Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Gibberellic Acid GA3] --> H1[Scutellum of Embryo]
    H1 --> H2[Aleurone Layer]
    H2 --> I1[Alpha Amylase Synthesis]
    H2 --> I2[Beta Amylase Synthesis]
    H2 --> I3[Limit Dextrinase Synthesis]
    H2 --> I4[Endopeptidase Synthesis]
    H2 --> I5[Beta Glucanase Synthesis]
    I1 --> J1[Starch Granule Hydrolysis]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    I4 --> J2[Hordein Protein Hydrolysis]
    I5 --> J3[Cell Wall Beta Glucan Hydrolysis]
    J1 --> K1[Maltose and Dextrins]
    J2 --> K2[Amino Acids and Peptides FAN]
    J3 --> K3[Reduced Wort Viscosity]
    K1 --> L1[Diastatic Power and Extract]
    K2 --> L2[Kolbach Index and Yeast Nutrition]
    K3 --> L3[Fine Coarse Extract Difference]
```

### 4.3 Kilning Phase Map (Temperature vs. Moisture Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Green Malt Loaded 45 percent moisture] --> K2[Free Drying 0 to 12 h 65 C]
    K2 --> K3[Intermediate 12 to 18 h 75 C]
    K3 --> K4[Curing Pale 18 to 22 h 85 C]
    K3 --> K5[Curing Munich 22 to 26 h 100 C]
    K3 --> K6[Curing Caramel 26 to 30 h 150 C in vessel]
    K3 --> K7[Curing Roasted 30 to 36 h 220 C]
    K4 --> K8[Moisture 4 percent DP Preserved]
    K5 --> K9[Moisture 4 percent Partial Maillard]
    K6 --> K10[Moisture 4 percent Crystallized Starch]
    K7 --> K11[Moisture 3 percent Enzyme Dead Pyrolysis]
    K8 --> Z1[Use in Lager Brewing]
    K9 --> Z2[Use in Bock and Märzen]
    K10 --> Z3[Use in Ale for Colour and Flavour]
    K11 --> Z4[Use in Stout and Porter]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Malthouse

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InfeedSection
        inf1[Barley Receiving] --> inf2[Pre-cleaning] --> inf3[Grading]
    end
    subgraph SteepingSection
        inf3 --> stp1[Steep Tank A] --> stp2[Steep Tank B] --> stp3[Air Rest]
    end
    subgraph GerminationSection
        stp3 --> ger1[Saladin Box or Drum] --> ger2[Turners and Fans] --> ger3[Green Malt Discharge]
    end

        ger3 --> kil1[Top-Draught Kiln Floor 1] --> kil2[Curing Zone]
    end
    subgraph FinishingSection

    end
    subgraph PackagingDispatch
        fin3 --> pkg1[Screening] --> pkg2[Bagging or Bulk] --> pkg3[Quality Lab Release]
    end
    subgraph QualityLab

        ql2 --> ql3[Diastatic Power]

    end
    fin3 -.-> ql1
    pkg2 -.-> ql3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
*Define malt. List any four quality parameters used to assess the commercial quality of malt.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Malt is a partially germinated cereal grain (most commonly barley), which has been dried under controlled conditions (kilned) so that the enzyme systems developed during germination are preserved and the grain is rendered storage-stable.

**Quality Parameters (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. **Moisture content** (target 3 – 5 %) — indicates kiln efficiency and shelf stability.
2. **Diastatic Power (DP)** — measures starch-degrading enzyme activity; high DP (≥ 80 °Lintner) is required for adjunct mashing in distilling.
3. **Extract yield** — the percentage of soluble material (sugars + proteins) obtained in the Congress Mash; pale malt = 80 – 82 %.
4. **Kolbach Index (KI)** — ratio of soluble to total nitrogen (38 – 42 % for pale malt); indicates proteolytic modification.
5. **Fine-Coarse Extract Difference** — index of complete modification (well-modified ≤ 1.5 %).
6. **Colour (°EBC)** — pale malt = 3 – 4 °EBC; Munich = 15 – 25 °EBC; caramel = 60 – 120 °EBC.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning any **four** of the above and the **definition** scores full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Explain the role of gibberellic acid (GA₃) in the commercial malting of barley.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Role of GA₃ in malting (3 marks):**

1. **Biosynthesis & Origin (1 mark):** GA₃ is a plant growth hormone produced naturally in the embryo's scutellum during the early stages of germination. It is the *primary signalling molecule* that triggers the conversion of the starchy endosperm into an enzymatically active substrate.

2. **Aleurone Activation (1 mark):** GA₃ diffuses from the scutellum into the **aleurone layer**, where it switches on the genes coding for hydrolytic enzymes — notably α-amylase, β-amylase, limit dextrinase, endo-proteinases, and β-glucanases. These enzymes then diffuse back into the starchy endosperm to mobilize stored reserves.

3. **Industrial Application (1 mark):** In modern commercial malthouses, exogenous **GA₃ (0.1 – 0.5 ppm)** is added to the final steep water or sprayed onto the green malt at the start of germination. This *shortens* the germination cycle by 24 – 48 h, *enhances* the diastatic power of the final malt, and *improves* uniformity in large commercial batches (where endogenous GA production may be rate-limiting in the centre of deep germination beds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Stating that GA₃ "digests starch" — it does NOT. It is a *signalling* hormone that *triggers* enzyme production. This is a frequent 1-mark loss in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulations for the Open Elective group, each Part-B question carries 14 marks and the module offers an internal choice (i.e., the student answers EITHER Q-A OR Q-B, not both). Each question is structured with two sub-parts of 7 marks each (typically 7 + 7 OR 10 + 4). We adopt the 7 + 7 pattern below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q4]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat process flow, the **commercial production of malt** from barley. Highlight the role of moisture, temperature, and air-rests in the steeping stage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the changes in **carbohydrate, protein, and enzyme profiles** of barley during germination. How do these changes affect malt quality? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Commercial Production of Malt (7 marks)**

The commercial production of malt is carried out in three sequential stages — **Steeping, Germination, and Kilning**. Each stage is precisely controlled to optimize enzyme development while minimizing dry-matter loss.

**Stage 1 — Steeping (2 marks):**
- Cleaned, graded barley is immersed in water at 12 – 18 °C.
- The grain undergoes **two to three wet–air cycles** over 36 – 48 h.
- **Moisture** rises from 12 % to **42 – 46 %**; this is the *activation threshold* for embryo metabolism.
- **Temperature** is held at 12 – 18 °C to prevent premature microbial growth.
- **Air rests** (6 – 12 h, humidified) supply **O₂** to the embryo; without them, anaerobic fermentation produces ethanol and chitting fails.
- Exogenous **GA₃ (0.1 – 0.5 ppm)** is sometimes added in the final steep water.
- The steep cycle ends when ~ 75 – 85 % of the grains have visible rootlet emergence (chitted).

**Stage 2 — Germination (2 marks):**
- Chitted grain is transferred to a *Saladin box* or *germination drum*; bed depth 0.6 – 1.5 m.
- Air at 15 – 20 °C, RH > 95 %, is forced through the bed; the bed is turned every 8 – 12 h.
- Over 4 – 6 days, the **acrospire grows** to ¾ length of the kernel and enzymes are synthesized (modification).
- The final germinated product is called **green malt** (~ 40 – 45 % moisture).

**Stage 3 — Kilning (2 marks):**
- **Free drying** (12 – 18 h at 50 – 65 °C) reduces moisture from ~ 45 % → ~ 20 %.
- **Curing** (4 – 8 h at 75 – 85 °C for pale malt; up to 220 °C for roasted malt) reduces moisture to **3 – 5 %**.
- Maillard and caramelization reactions during curing generate **colour and flavour**.
- The cured malt is **deculmed** (rootlets removed), **polished**, and held in silos for a 2 – 6 week *rest period* before packaging.

**Process Flow (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — a verbal flow is sufficient: Barley → Cleaning → Steeping → Germination → Kilning → Deculming → Storage → Malt.

---

**(b) Biochemical Changes During Germination (7 marks)**

| Constituent | Resting Barley | Germinated Green Malt | Functional Effect on Malt Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starch** (75 – 80 % of d.b.) | Intact, crystalline, inaccessible | Partially eroded at granule surface; pits visible under SEM | Increases **extract yield** by loosening granule structure; provides substrate for α- and β-amylases |
| **Hordeins** (storage prolamins) | Compact, hydrophobic globules | Hydrolysed to **hordein fragments, peptides, and free amino acids (FAN)** | Increases **Kolbach Index**; provides yeast-assimilable nitrogen for fermentation |
| **β-Glucans** (cell wall polysaccharides) | Intact, viscous | Hydrolysed by β-glucanases to short oligosaccharides | Reduces **wort viscosity**; improves **filterability**; lowers F-C extract difference |
| **α-Amylase** | Negligible | High (synthesized de novo) | Provides **diastatic power** for mashing |
| **β-Amylase** | Low, *bound* to protein | Released and partially synthesized | Maltose production in mashing |
| **Lipids** | 2 – 3 %, intact | Mild lipase activity releases fatty acids | Minor — affects beer staling (aldehydes) |
| **Polyphenols** | Bound to protein | Released, partly oxidized | Colour, flavour, potential haze |

**Critical linkages to malt quality (1 mark):**

- **Diastatic Power ↔ α-/β-Amylase synthesis** — under-modification = low DP → incomplete starch conversion in mashing.
- **Kolbach Index ↔ Proteolysis** — too low = yeast starvation; too high = over-modification, haze, excess yeast growth.
- **F-C Extract Difference ↔ β-Glucanase activity** — high F-C = under-degraded cell walls = mash tun stuck, lautering problems.
- **Extract ↔ Starch integrity + Cell Wall Degradation** — well-modified malt yields 80 – 82 % extract; under-modified yields 75 – 78 %.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q5 (Alternative Choice)]

**(a)** What is **kilning**? Discuss the **physical and chemical changes** that occur in green malt during kilning. How do these changes affect the final malt quality? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the properties of **Pale malt, Munich malt, Caramel malt, and Roasted malt**. State one industrial application of each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kilning — Physical and Chemical Changes (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Kilning is the controlled drying of green malt (moisture ~ 40 – 45 %) to a final moisture of 3 – 5 %, using a two- or three-stage temperature programme in a top-draught or floor kiln.

**Physical Changes (2.5 marks):**

1. **Moisture removal** — by evaporation into the drying air; follows an exponential decay curve. The *constant-rate drying period* dominates the first 10 h, after which falling-rate kinetics prevail.
2. **Rootlet hardening and detachment** — the culms (rootlets) become brittle and snap off easily during the deculming step.
3. **Starch granule changes** — partial gelatinization occurs at the surface of the endosperm; moisture removal creates a porous structure.
4. **Volume reduction** — the kernel shrinks ~ 10 – 15 % by volume.
5. **Colour development** — *non-enzymatic* browning begins above 80 °C; deeper colour develops with higher curing temperatures.

**Chemical Changes (2.5 marks):**

1. **Enzyme denaturation** — most enzymes are heat-labile; the *capping temperature* (the temperature at which enzyme activity drops by 50 %) for α-amylase is ~ 70 °C in a moist environment. Hence, *moisture must be removed BEFORE temperature is raised above 70 °C* — this is the engineering logic of the two-stage kiln programme.
2. **Maillard Reaction** — between reducing sugars (from amylolysis) and free amino groups (from proteolysis); produces **melanoidins** (brown pigments) and characteristic aroma compounds (e.g., 2-furfural, maltol).
3. **Caramelization** — at > 150 °C, sucrose and maltose decompose into coloured polymers; this is the basis of caramel and crystal malts.
4. **Strecker Degradation** — amino acids react with α-dicarbonyls to give aldehydes (Strecker aldehydes), which contribute to malt aroma.
5. **LIPID oxidation products** — small quantities of aldehydes (e.g., hexanal, trans-2-nonenal) generated; these are precursors to beer staling.

**Effect on Malt Quality (1 mark):**
- Pale malt (cured at 80 – 85 °C) — high enzyme retention, light colour, neutral flavour → used in lagers.
- Higher curing temperatures (100 – 220 °C) progressively destroy enzymes but build rich colour and flavour → specialty malts.

---

**(b) Comparative Properties of Malt Types (7 marks)**

| Property | Pale Malt | Munich Malt | Caramel Malt | Roasted Malt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Curing Temp (°C)** | 80 – 85 | 100 – 110 | 65 → 150 (in-vessel saccharification) | 200 – 220 |
| **Moisture (%)** | 3.5 – 4.5 | 3.0 – 4.0 | 4.0 – 5.0 | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| **Colour (°EBC)** | 3 – 4 | 15 – 25 | 60 – 120 | 900 – 1400 |
| **Diastatic Power (°Lintner)** | > 80 (high) | 30 – 50 (medium) | < 5 (low) | 0 (none) |
| **Extract (% d.b.)** | 80 – 82 | 78 – 80 | 72 – 76 | 65 – 70 |
| **Kolbach Index (%)** | 38 – 42 | 30 – 38 | n/a | n/a |
| **Flavour** | Clean, neutral, slightly grainy | Rich, malty, biscuit, bread-crust | Sweet, caramel, toffee | Sharp, burnt, coffee, dark-chocolate |
| **Industrial Use** | Lager (Budweiser, Heineken), distilling (Scotch adjunct) | Bock, Märzen, Oktoberfest, Doppelbock | Pale Ales, Bitters (colour + flavour, no enzymatic contribution) | Stout, Porter, Schwartzbier (colour + roasted flavour) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Enzyme–Moisture Misconception:** A frequent KTU essay error is stating that "high temperatures in kilning kill enzymes." Partial marks may be awarded; the *correct* engineering view is: "Enzymes are *heat-labile in the presence of moisture*; moisture must be reduced *before* the cure temperature is raised above 70 °C." Always frame your answer around this *coupled* moisture–temperature relationship.
> 2. **Gibberellic Acid Confusion:** Some students confuse GA₃ with a "malt enzyme." GA₃ is a *signalling hormone*; it is not an enzyme and does not hydrolyse starch.
> 3. **Malt vs. Sprouted Grain:** Defining malt simply as "sprouted barley" is *incomplete*; malting includes the *kilning* and *stabilization* step.
> 4. **Malt Yield Calculation:** Many students forget to subtract the rootlet mass when asked for the *useful* malt yield. KTU specifically tests the 8 – 10 % dry-matter loss in germinative respiration.
> 5. **Diastatic Power Conversion:** Lintner–WK conversions are routinely asked. Memorize the linear equation given in Section 2.2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Malt** = partially germinated + kilned cereal grain; barley is the most common substrate.
- [x] **Three macro-stages** = Steeping → Germination → Kilning (+ Deculming & Storage).
- [x] **Steeping target moisture** = 42 – 46 %; air-rests are essential for O₂ supply.
- [x] **Germination** = 4 – 6 days at 15 – 20 °C, > 95 % RH; turnings every 8 – 12 h.
- [x] **GA₃** is a *signalling* hormone, NOT an enzyme; it triggers α-amylase synthesis in the aleurone layer.
- [x] **Key enzymes in malt:** α-amylase, β-amylase, limit dextrinase, endo-proteases, β-glucanases.
- [x] **Kilning** is a two-stage process: **Free Drying (low T, removes moisture first)** → **Curing (high T, develops colour and flavour)**.
- [x] **Moisture–Temperature Coupling Rule:** Moisture must drop *below 20 %* *before* the temperature is raised *above 70 °C*, otherwise enzymes are denatured.
- [x] **Malt yield** = 78 – 82 % (as-is), 88 – 90 % (dry basis).
- [x] **Final moisture** = 3 – 5 % (water activity ≈ 0.20 – 0.35) for shelf stability.
- [x] **Quality parameters (must memorize):** Moisture, Extract (≥ 80 %), F-C extract difference (≤ 1.5 %), KI (38 – 42 %), DP (≥ 80 °Lintner), Colour (3 – 4 °EBC for pale).
- [x] **Specialty malts:** Munich (warm), Caramel (in-vessel saccharification), Roasted (enzyme-dead, flavour only).
- [x] **Diastatic Power conversion:** °Lintner ≈ 2.3 × °WK − 16.
- [x] **Industrial uses of malt:** Brewing, Distilling, Bakery, Malt extract (Horlicks, Milo, breakfast cereals), Vinegar.
- [x] **By-product of malting:** Deculmed rootlets (culms) — sold as high-protein animal feed (~ 25 % crude protein).
- [x] **Malt is the largest single raw-material cost** in a brewery, typically 60 – 70 % of total raw-material cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip for Module 2 — Commercial Production of Malt:** Always present the process as a **mass-and-energy balance** rather than a list of steps. KTU ESE long-answer valuation explicitly awards marks for: (i) the *physical* principle (water, heat, mass transfer), (ii) the *biochemical* principle (GA₃ → enzyme synthesis → substrate hydrolysis), and (iii) the *engineering parameter* (target moisture, temperature, time). A 14-mark answer that integrates all three *vectors* easily scores 12+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corn, Oats and Millet Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Corn, Oats and Millet Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Corn Processing

### Formal Definition
**Corn (Maize, *Zea mays*) processing** refers to the industrial unit operations used to convert cleaned, dried maize kernels into stable, value-added food and non-food ingredients. The two principal industrial routes are:
- **Dry Milling** — physical separation of the kernel into germ, hull (pericarp), and endosperm fractions without steeping, yielding grits, meal, flour, and oil-rich germ.
- **Wet Milling** — a steeping-based separation process that isolates **starch, gluten, germ, and fiber** as pure streams for conversion to syrups, ethanol, and modified starches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Corn dry milling, wet milling, and primary/secondary processed products form a high-weightage cluster. Students must know the unit operations in sequence and the biochemical role of **sulphurous acid (SO₂) steeping**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a corn kernel like a small **three-room apartment**:
- **Room 1 (Pericarp/Hull)** – the outer brick wall, tough and fibrous.
- **Room 2 (Germ)** – the central "engine room" rich in oil (~30 % lipid).
- **Room 3 (Endosperm)** – the bulky "store room" packed with starch granules cemented by protein (gluten matrix).

Processing is essentially the job of a **demolition contractor** — *dry milling* uses mechanical force to break the rooms apart, while *wet milling* first softens the walls with a chemical "bath" (sulphurous acid + water) before gently disassembling them into clean components.

---

## 1.2 Oats Processing

### Formal Definition
**Oat processing** is the sequence of cleaning, dehulling, **kilning** (hydrothermal treatment), and size-reduction operations that convert raw oat groats (*Avena sativa*) into rolled oats, steel-cut oats, oat flour, and oat bran.

> [!NOTE]
> The **kill step** in oat processing is **kilning** — a 3-stage heat-moisture treatment that inactivates **lipase** and **lipoxygenase** enzymes responsible for rancidity in stored oats.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a raw oat groat as a **sticky, enzyme-loaded time bomb**. The hull protects it on the plant, but the moment you dehull it, native lipases start hydrolyzing its own oils into free fatty acids → the groat turns rancid within days. **Kilning is the bomb-squad technician**: it raises the groat temperature to 95–105 °C with controlled moisture so that the enzymes are denatured and the starch is simultaneously pre-gelatinized for easier cooking.

---

## 1.3 Millet Processing

### Formal Definition
**Millet processing** encompasses the cleaning, de-stoning, conditioning, dehulling, and secondary value-addition operations applied to small-seeded grasses such as **Pearl millet (*Pennisetum glaucum*), Finger millet (*Eleusine coracana*), Sorghum (*Sorghum bicolor*), Foxtail millet (*Setaria italica*)**, and **Little millet (*Panicum sumatrense*)**. It includes primary (dehulling/milling) and secondary (popping, flaking, malting, extrusion) processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Among Indian millets, **Pearl millet (Bajra)** and **Sorghum (Jowar)** dominate the **ICAR–IIMR value-chain**. Finger millet (**Ragi**) is nutritionally prized for its **calcium (≈350 mg/100 g)** content.

### Intuitive Analogy
Millet grains are like **tiny armored marbles** — the seed coat (testa) and husk (glume) are tightly cemented to the endosperm, far tougher than wheat. So, unlike wheat (where you only need tempering + roller milling), millets need **abrasion dehusking** (think: sandpaper rollers) followed by size reduction, with the additional challenge of **decortication losses (5–25 %)** that must be minimized for industrial viability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Corn — Dry Milling (Detailed Operation-by-Operation)

**Sequence (KTU Board-expected):**

1. **Receiving & Pre-cleaning** — removal of foreign material, broken kernels, cob pieces using **vibratory sieves, magnetic separators, and air classifiers**.
2. **Dry Cleaning** — gravity separators remove light impurities.
3. **Tempering (Conditioning)** — moisture raised to **20–23 %** w.b. by adding water/steam and holding for **1–2 h** to toughen the germ and soften the bran.
4. **Degermination** — kernels pass through a **Beall degerminator** or **roller-mill degerminator** that scrapes off the germ and hull.
5. **Roller Milling** — corrugated rolls progressively reduce the particle size, separating endosperm chunks from germ/bran.
6. **Sifting & Aspiration** — multi-deck **plansifters** grade the product into **flaking grits, coarse grits, fine grits, meal, and flour**.
7. **Gravity Table Separation** — stratifies particles by **density** to remove remaining germ fragments.
8. **Packaging & Storage** at <13 % moisture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why temper?** Two reasons — (i) plasticizes the **aleurone + pericarp** so it flakes off in large pieces (clean separation), and (ii) toughens the **germ** so it does not pulverize into the endosperm fraction (improves germ yield and oil quality).

### Dry Mill Yields (typical)
| Fraction | Yield (% of clean corn) |
|---|---|
| Large / Flaking Grits | 8–12 |
| Coarse & Fine Grits | 35–45 |
| Corn Meal | 6–8 |
| Corn Flour | 4–6 |
| Germ | 7–9 |
| Hull + Bran | 5–7 |
| Loss / Moisture | 6–9 |

---

## 2.2 Corn — Wet Milling (Detailed)

**Sequence:**
1. **Steeping** (32–48 h, 48–52 °C) in water containing **0.1–0.2 % SO₂** and **0.5 % lactic acid**.
2. **Coarse Grinding** — swollen kernels pass through an attrition mill to release the germ.
3. **Germ Separation** — using **hydrocyclones** (germ floats due to low density, ~1.03 g/cm³).
4. **Fine Grinding** — remaining slurry is milled to liberate starch granules from the protein matrix.
5. **Starch–Gluten Separation** — **starch tables** (inclined channels) or **centrifugal separators** exploit the density difference (starch 1.5 g/cm³ vs. gluten 1.1 g/cm³).
6. **Fiber Washing** — multi-stage **stages** on sieves to recover trapped starch.
7. **Starch Dewatering & Drying**.
8. **Gluten Concentration & Drying** (→ corn gluten meal, 60 % protein).
9. **Steep Liquor Concentration** (→ animal feed, rich in vitamins & minerals).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of SO₂:** (i) inhibits microbial growth during long steep, (ii) cleaves the **disulfide bonds in the gluten matrix** (reduces SS bonds of cysteine residues), thereby liberating starch granules, and (iii) bleaches carotenoid pigments in the endosperm.

### Wet Mill Yield Targets
| Product | Yield (kg/100 kg corn) |
|---|---|
| Starch | 65–70 |
| Gluten Meal | 6–7 |
| Germ (oil + meal) | 7–8 |
| Fiber (Corn Bran) | 10–12 |
| Steep Liquor Solids | 6–8 |

---

## 2.3 Oat Processing — Operations

1. **Cleaning & Grading** — removal of oats, weed seeds, sticks, stones.
2. **Dehulling** — **impact huller** (Schule or Codema type) uses high-velocity rotors; kernels strike a rubber ring and the hull is dislodged. Aspiration removes the light hulls.
3. **Separation** — **gravity table** separates unhulled oats (called *oats-with-hull* or *returns*) which are recycled.
4. **Kilning** — three-stage treatment: (i) warming to 40–50 °C, (ii) high-temperature at **95–105 °C** for 1–2 h to inactivate enzymes, (iii) cooling/conditioning to 8–10 % moisture.
5. **Cutting / Flaking** —
   - **Steel-cut oats (Pinhead):** groat is chopped into 3–4 pieces.
   - **Rolled oats (Old-fashioned):** groat is steamed 30 min → pressed between rolls to ~0.5 mm flake thickness.
   - **Quick / Instant oats:** rolled thin → further steamed and pressed.
6. **Packaging.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Lipase inactivation kinetics** follow first-order denaturation. A 90 % inactivation is reached when:
> $t_{90} = \dfrac{\ln(10)}{k_d}$ where $k_d$ is the thermal denaturation rate constant.

---

## 2.4 Millet Processing — Detailed

### Primary Processing
1. **Cleaning** — destoner, magnetic separator, indented cylinder.
2. **Conditioning** — moisture raised to **14–16 %** for **2–4 h** to plasticize the hull layer.
3. **Dehulling** — by **abrasion-type dehuller (e.g., Satake, Buhler)** with carborundum or emery discs; losses depend on cultivar.
4. **Aspiration** — separation of loose hulls.
5. **Pulverizing / Milling** — hammer mill, plate mill, or stone mill to yield **flour (atta), semolina (rava), or grits**.

### Secondary Processing (Value Addition)
- **Popping** — sand / salt fluidized-bed popping at **230–250 °C**, yield 60–85 %.
- **Flaking** — pregelatinization by steaming + roller pressing.
- **Malting** — steeping (24 h) → germination (48–72 h) → kilning (50 °C) → malt flour.
- **Extrusion Cooking** — feed moisture 14–18 %, barrel temp 120–180 °C, screw speed 200–400 rpm.
- **Fermentation** — idli/dosa batter, *ambali*, *koozh*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU must-know:** Millet decortication losses of **15–25 %** make them economically tougher than rice (8–10 %); the more uniform and smaller the grain, the higher the loss. **Pearl millet** has the highest losses due to its large, irregularly-shaped caryopsis.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Balance (overall) | $\sum M_{in} = \sum M_{out} + M_{loss}$ | kg h⁻¹ |
| 2 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Conversion wb ↔ db | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | — |
| 5 | Process Yield | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{product}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Decortication / Dehulling Loss | $L_{dehull} = \dfrac{m_{hull} + m_{broken}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | Product Recovery Ratio | $R = \dfrac{m_{clean} \cdot Y_{fraction}}{m_{feed}}$ | — |
| 8 | Throughput | $Q = \dfrac{m_{feed}}{t_{cycle}}$ | kg h⁻¹ |
| 9 | Lipase Inactivation Kinetics (1st order) | $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$ | fraction |
| 10 | Steeping Time (Wet Milling) | $t_s = 32 \text{ to } 48\ \text{h}$ | h |
| 11 | Germ Density (Corn) | $\rho_{germ} \approx 1.03$ | g cm⁻³ |
| 12 | Starch Density (Corn) | $\rho_{starch} \approx 1.50$ | g cm⁻³ |
| 13 | Gluten Density (Corn) | $\rho_{gluten} \approx 1.10$ | g cm⁻³ |
| 14 | Kilning Temperature (Oats) | $T = 95 \text{ to } 105$ | °C |
| 15 | Millet Conditioning Moisture | $M_{cond} = 14 \text{ to } 16$ | % |
| 16 | Popping Yield (Millet) | $Y_{pop} = 60 \text{ to } 85$ | % |
| 17 | Kcal Density (Corn, dry) | $\approx 365$ | kcal/100 g |
| 18 | Kcal Density (Rolled Oats) | $\approx 389$ | kcal/100 g |
| 19 | Oil Content (Corn Germ) | $\approx 30 \text{ to } 35$ | % d.b. |
| 20 | Protein Content (Corn Gluten Meal) | $\approx 60$ | % d.b. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Restriction** — pipe symbols like $\vert x \vert$ have been written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table column delimiter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Conversion of Moisture Content (wb → db)

The relationship between **wet basis** and **dry basis** moisture content is **mandatory for KTU questions** on processing calculations.

**Let:**
- $m_w$ = mass of water (kg)
- $m_d$ = mass of dry matter (kg)
- $M_{wb}$ = moisture content on wet basis (decimal form)
- $M_{db}$ = moisture content on dry basis (decimal form)

**Step 1 — Definition of wet-basis moisture.**

$$M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$$

This is the **fraction of total mass that is water**.

**Step 2 — Definition of dry-basis moisture.**

$$M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$$

This is the **mass of water per unit mass of dry matter**.

**Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator of $M_{wb}$ by $m_d$.**

$$\dfrac{M_{wb}}{1} = \dfrac{m_w/m_d}{m_w/m_d + m_d/m_d} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{M_{db} + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply to solve for $M_{db}$.**

$$M_{wb} (M_{db} + 1) = M_{db}$$

$$M_{wb} \cdot M_{db} + M_{wb} = M_{db}$$

$$M_{wb} = M_{db} - M_{wb} \cdot M_{db} = M_{db} (1 - M_{wb})$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $M_{db}$.**

$$M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The dry-basis moisture is always numerically larger than wet-basis for the same sample. Example: 20 % wb → 25 % db.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Corn Wet Milling Mass Balance

**Problem:** A corn wet milling plant receives **10 000 kg h⁻¹** of clean corn at **14 % wb moisture**. After steeping, the water absorption is **2 %** of the original kernel mass. The downstream yield distribution is:
- Starch: **67 %** of input
- Gluten meal: **6.5 %**
- Germ (wet, before oil extraction): **8 %**
- Fiber (corn bran): **11 %**
- Steep liquor solids: **5 %**
- The remainder is **unaccounted process loss**.

**Required:** (a) Calculate the throughput of each product (kg h⁻¹). (b) Mass balance closure check.

**Step 1 — Total mass entering the system.**
Feed = 10 000 kg h⁻¹ (clean corn, 14 % wb).

**Step 2 — Apply yield fractions.**

$$m_{starch} = 0.67 \times 10\,000 = 6\,700\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

$$m_{gluten} = 0.065 \times 10\,000 = 650\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

$$m_{germ} = 0.08 \times 10\,000 = 800\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

$$m_{fiber} = 0.11 \times 10\,000 = 1\,100\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

$$m_{liquor} = 0.05 \times 10\,000 = 500\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the recovered streams.**

$$m_{out} = 6\,700 + 650 + 800 + 1\,100 + 500 = 9\,750\ \text{kg h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute unaccounted loss.**

$$m_{loss} = 10\,000 - 9\,750 = 250\ \text{kg h}^{-1} = 2.5\ \%$$

**Step 5 — Mass balance closure (should be 100 % within tolerance).**

$$\dfrac{m_{out} + m_{loss}}{m_{in}} = \dfrac{9\,750 + 250}{10\,000} = 1.000 = 100\ \%$$

> **Result:** The mass balance **closes at 100 %**, validating the yield data within engineering tolerance (± 1 %).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Head-to-Head Comparison (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

| Parameter | Corn (Dry Mill) | Corn (Wet Mill) | Oats | Pearl Millet | Finger Millet |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kernel Structure** | Large, hard endosperm | Large, vitreous | Husk firmly attached | Small caryopsis, loose hull | Tiny caryopsis, soft endosperm |
| **Pre-conditioning** | Tempering 20–23 % m.c. | Steeping 32–48 h in SO₂ | Hulling + kilning | Conditioning 14–16 % m.c. | Conditioning 13–15 % m.c. |
| **Key Dehulling Tech.** | Degerminator (mechanical) | Stein mill (attrition) | Impact huller | Abrasive disc (Satake) | Abrasive disc + rubber roll |
| **Key Heat Treatment** | Drying 50–80 °C | Steeping 48–52 °C | **Kilning 95–105 °C** | Popping 230–250 °C | Malting 28–32 °C |
| **Main Product** | Grits, meal, germ | Starch, syrup, ethanol | Rolled oats, oat flour | Bajra flour, popped grain | Ragi flour, malted ragi |
| **Process Loss** | 6–9 % | 2–3 % | 25–35 % (hull mass) | 15–25 % | 8–15 % |
| **Critical Biochemical Step** | SO₂ not used | SO₂ cleaves SS bonds | **Lipase inactivation** | Starch gelatinization | Calcium retention in bran |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High | High | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Industrial Volume** | Very high | Very high | High (US/EU) | Regional (India) | Regional (Karnataka/TN) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Lipase Inactivation During Kilning

**Problem:** Lipase in raw oat groats has an inactivation rate constant of $k_d = 0.18\ \text{min}^{-1}$ at 100 °C. Calculate the holding time required to achieve **95 % inactivation**.

**Step 1 — Use first-order inactivation kinetics.**

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t}$$

**Step 2 — Define residual activity fraction.**

$$\dfrac{N_t}{N_0} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $t$.**

$$0.05 = e^{-0.18 \cdot t}$$

$$\ln(0.05) = -0.18 \cdot t$$

$$-2.996 = -0.18 \cdot t$$

$$t = \dfrac{2.996}{0.18} \approx 16.6\ \text{minutes}$$

> **Result:** To inactivate 95 % of lipase at 100 °C, the groat must be held for **~ 17 min**. Industrial kilners hold for 30–60 min as a safety margin (99 % inactivation) and also to pre-gelatinize the starch.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define **Tempering** in corn dry milling. State its two main industrial objectives. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Tempering (or conditioning) is the controlled addition of water/steam to cleaned corn to raise its moisture content from ~14 % to **20–23 % (w.b.)** followed by a holding period of 1–2 h.
>
> **Objective 1 (1 mark):** Plasticizes the pericarp/aleurone layer so that it flakes off in large intact pieces during degermination → cleaner grits, lower ash.
>
> **Objective 2 (1 mark):** Toughens the germ (which is high in oil) so that it does not pulverize during roller milling → improved germ yield and oil recovery (target ≥ 7 % germ fraction with ≥ 30 % oil).

---

### Question 2 — What is **Kilning** in oat processing? Why is it considered the critical control point (CCP)? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Kilning is a three-stage hydrothermal treatment of dehulled oat groats: (i) warming to 40–50 °C, (ii) high-temperature holding at **95–105 °C for 1–2 h**, and (iii) cooling/conditioning to 8–10 % moisture.
>
> **Why it is a CCP (2 marks):** (a) It **inactivates native lipase and lipoxygenase** which otherwise hydrolyze oat lipids into free fatty acids causing rapid rancidity (shelf-life would drop from 12 months to < 1 month without kilning). (b) It simultaneously **pre-gelatinizes the starch** so that rolled oats cook in 3–5 min, and (c) develops characteristic nutty flavor compounds (Maillard reaction).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Attempt **either** option A **or** option B in full.

---

### ✅ Option A — Q1

#### (a) Explain the **dry milling process of corn** with a flow diagram. Mention the yields of major products. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Receiving & Pre-cleaning (1 mark):** Foreign material and broken kernels removed using vibratory sieves, magnetic separators, and air classifiers. Corn is graded to < 14 % moisture.
2. **Tempering (1 mark):** Moisture raised to 20–23 % w.b. by adding water/steam; held 1–2 h to plasticize pericarp and toughen germ.
3. **Degermination (1 mark):** Kernels pass through a **Beall degerminator** (conical rotor with carborundum studs inside a perforated screen) that shears off germ and hull.
4. **Roller Milling (1 mark):** The degermed material passes through a battery of **corrugated roller mills** with progressively finer corrugation and differential roll speeds.
5. **Sifting & Aspiration (1 mark):** Multi-deck **plansifters** grade the ground material into grits, meal, and flour. **Aspirators** remove the light bran by air-lift.
6. **Gravity Table (1 mark):** Separates particles of different density (germ ≈ 1.03, endosperm ≈ 1.45, bran ≈ 1.20 g cm⁻³) for high-purity grits.
7. **Product Yields (1 mark):**
   - Flaking grits: 8–12 %
   - Coarse & fine grits: 35–45 %
   - Corn meal: 6–8 %
   - Corn flour: 4–6 %
   - Germ: 7–9 % → corn oil + germ meal
   - Hull + Bran: 5–7 % → animal feed

---

#### (b) Describe the **wet milling process of corn** with major products. Explain the role of SO₂ in steeping. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Steeping (1.5 marks):** 32–48 h at 48–52 °C in water containing **0.1–0.2 % SO₂ + 0.5 % lactic acid**. Kernels absorb water → 45 % moisture, soften, swell.
2. **Coarse Grinding + Germ Separation (1 mark):** Stein mill releases germ; germ floats (ρ ≈ 1.03) and is recovered via **hydrocyclones**.
3. **Fine Grinding (1 mark):** Remaining slurry is milled to liberate starch granules from gluten matrix.
4. **Starch–Gluten Separation (1 mark):** **Starch tables** (inclined troughs) or **nozzle centrifuges** exploit density difference (starch 1.50, gluten 1.10 g cm⁻³) → pure starch stream + gluten stream.
5. **Fiber Washing (0.5 mark):** Multi-stage sieves recover starch entrapped in fiber.
6. **Starch & Gluten Drying (0.5 mark):** Dewatered by vacuum filters / peeler centrifuges → flash / pneumatic drying.
7. **Role of SO₂ (1.5 marks):** (i) Inhibits microbial growth, (ii) **reduces disulfide (–S–S–) bonds of cysteine residues in the gluten matrix**, allowing starch granules to be released cleanly, (iii) bleaches carotenoid pigments for a whiter starch product.
8. **Major Products (Bonus mention):** Native starch, modified starch, high-fructose corn syrup, ethanol, corn oil, corn gluten meal (60 % protein), corn bran, steep liquor solids.

---

### ✅ Option B — Q2

#### (a) Describe the **steps involved in oat processing**. Discuss the role of **kilning** in detail. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Cleaning & Grading (0.5 mark):** Removal of weed seeds, stones, chaff, and other grains.
2. **Dehulling (1.5 marks):** **Impact huller (e.g., Schule, Codema)** uses a high-velocity rotor to throw groats against a rubber ring. Hulls are dislodged and removed by aspiration. **Yields: ~65–70 % groat, 25–35 % hull.**
3. **Separation (0.5 mark):** Gravity table separates unhulled oats (recirculated) from clean groats.
4. **Kilning — 3 stages (2.5 marks):**
   - **Stage 1 (Warming):** Groats heated to 40–50 °C; moisture equilibrates.
   - **Stage 2 (High-temperature):** 95–105 °C for 1–2 h. **Inactivates lipase (E.C. 3.1.1.3) and lipoxygenase**, pre-gelatinizes starch, develops flavor.
   - **Stage 3 (Cooling/Conditioning):** Brought down to 8–10 % moisture.
5. **Cutting / Flaking (1.5 marks):**
   - **Steel-cut:** Groats chopped into 3–4 pieces using rotary cutters.
   - **Rolled oats:** Steamed 30 min → pressed through smooth rolls to 0.5 mm flake.
   - **Quick / Instant:** Thin flakes steamed a second time.
6. **Packaging (0.5 mark):** Inert atmosphere or vacuum to extend shelf life.

---

#### (b) Explain **primary and secondary processing of millets** with their value-added products. List the industrial challenges. `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Primary Processing (3 marks):**
   - **Cleaning:** Destoner, indented cylinder, magnetic separator.
   - **Conditioning:** Moisture raised to 14–16 % for 2–4 h to plasticize hull.
   - **Dehulling:** Abrasive-type dehuller (Satake, Buhler) with carborundum discs.
   - **Aspiration:** Light hulls removed.
   - **Milling:** Hammer mill, plate mill, or stone mill to give flour, rava, or grits.

2. **Secondary Processing (3 marks):**
   - **Popping:** Sand/salt bed popping at 230–250 °C; yield 60–85 %. *Jowar* and *Bajra* pop well.
   - **Flaking:** Steaming + roller pressing → pre-gelatinized flakes.
   - **Malting:** Steeping 24 h → germination 48–72 h → kilning 50 °C → malt flour for **infant foods (e.g., Ragi Malt).**
   - **Extrusion Cooking:** 120–180 °C, 14–18 % feed moisture → RTE snacks, *murukku*, pasta.
   - **Fermentation:** Idli/dosa batter, *koozh*, *ambali* — improves bioavailability of iron and calcium.

3. **Industrial Challenges (1 mark):**
   - Small grain size → high decortication losses (15–25 %).
   - Non-uniform caryopsis (especially pearl millet).
   - Absence of gluten → dough-handling issues (requires composite flour with wheat).
   - Short shelf life of flours due to lipase activity.
   - Lower consumer awareness in non-traditional markets.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 mark-losing mistakes observed by KTU valuators in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Tempering vs. Conditioning** — students frequently *conflate* the two. Tempering in **corn** targets 20–23 % moisture to toughen the germ; conditioning in **millets** targets only 14–16 %. Do NOT state the same range for both.
> 2. **SO₂ role in wet milling** — many students state *only* "kills microbes" and miss the **disulfide bond reduction** mechanism, which is the **2-mark difference between 5 and 7** in Part B.
> 3. **Kilning — forgetting the 3 stages** — listing only one temperature (e.g., 100 °C) without the warm–hold–cool sequence → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Millets vs. Rice decortication** — equating hull % loss to rice (8–10 %) is incorrect. **Always** state 15–25 % for millets.
> 5. **No flow diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU evaluators award a **mandatory 1–1.5 marks** for a labelled block/flow diagram. **Always include one**, even if schematic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Corn Dry Milling** = Tempering (20–23 %) → Degerminator → Roller Mill → Plansifter → Gravity Table. Target yield: grits 50–60 %, germ 7–9 %.
- ✅ **Corn Wet Milling** = Steep (32–48 h, 0.15 % SO₂) → Germ Hydrocyclone → Fine Mill → Starch Tables → Products: Starch (67 %), Gluten (6.5 %), Germ (8 %), Fiber (11 %).
- ✅ **SO₂ in wet milling** = kills microbes + **cleaves SS bonds** + bleaches carotenoids.
- ✅ **Oat Processing** = Clean → Impact Hull → Kiln (3-stage, 100 °C) → Roll/Cut. Kilning **inactivates lipase** (the CCP).
- ✅ **Lipase inactivation kinetics:** 1st order, $N_t = N_0 e^{-k_d t}$. 95 % inactivation at $k_d = 0.18\ \text{min}^{-1}$ requires ~ 17 min.
- ✅ **Millet Primary Processing** = Clean → Condition (14–16 %) → Abrasive Dehull → Mill.
- ✅ **Millet Secondary Processing** = Pop / Flake / Malt / Extrude / Ferment.
- ✅ **Millet loss %** = 15–25 % (≈ 2× rice).
- ✅ **Densities to remember:** Starch 1.50, Germ 1.03, Gluten 1.10 (g cm⁻³).
- ✅ **Draw a labelled flow diagram** in every 14-mark answer — it is a guaranteed 1–1.5 mark dividend.
- ✅ **Energy / moisture rule of thumb:** Dry mill products (corn) target < 13 % moisture; kiln oats reach 8–10 %; millet flour storage < 12 %.
- ✅ **Healthier framing (NEP 2020 / 2024 outcome link):** Pearl millet and Finger millet are **gluten-free, low-GI, calcium/iron rich** — connect processing steps to nutritional outcomes wherever possible (KTU CO3 mapping).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
