## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. List any three minor millets with their scientific names and one nutritional highlight each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Minor Millet | Scientific Name | Nutritional Highlight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Finger Millet | *Eleusine coracana* | Highest calcium (**344 mg/100 g**) among cereals |
| 2 | Foxtail Millet | *Setaria italica* | Rich in protein (**12.3 g/100 g**) and methionine |
| 3 | Barnyard Millet | *Echinochloa frumentacea* | Lowest glycemic index among millets |
| 4 | Kodo Millet | *Paspalum scrobiculatum* | High dietary fiber content |

*(Any three with valid highlighting — **3 marks**)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Why are minor millets considered "climate-smart crops"? List two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Minor millets are called **climate-smart crops** because:
1. **Low water requirement** — They need only **350–500 mm** of rainfall compared to 1200–1500 mm for rice, making them suitable for **rain-fed arid regions**.
2. **Short duration** — They mature in **65–120 days**, allowing cultivation even in **fallow seasons** and escaping terminal drought.
3. **Tolerance to abiotic stress** — They can grow on **poor, marginal soils** with minimal input, tolerating temperatures up to **64 °C** (canopy level, finger millet).
4. **Low greenhouse gas emissions** — C₄ photosynthetic pathway gives **higher water-use efficiency** and **lower methane emissions** than paddy.

*(Any two valid points — **3 marks**)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Minor Millets: Composition, Processing & Products*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**a) Describe the proximate composition of minor millets. Compare their nutritional value with rice and wheat. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Minor millets are nutritionally superior to polished rice and comparable to wheat in many parameters. The proximate composition (per 100 g edible portion) is as follows:

**1. Protein (7–12 g):** Minor millets contain more protein than rice (6.8 g). Proso and foxtail millets are highest at **12.3 g**. The proteins are rich in essential amino acids — methionine, threonine, valine — which are **limiting in rice and wheat**.

**2. Carbohydrates (60–72 g):** The major carbohydrate is **starch**, with **amylose content ranging from 16 to 28%**, making them **slow-digesting** and **lower glycemic index** foods.

**3. Dietary Fiber (9–14 g):** Significantly higher than rice (0.8 g) and wheat (1.2 g). This makes them excellent for **gut health** and **blood sugar regulation**.

**4. Fats (1–5 g):** Mostly **unsaturated fatty acids** — linoleic and oleic acids. Little millet has the highest fat at **4.7 g**.

**5. Minerals:**
- **Calcium** — Finger millet exceptionally high at **344 mg/100 g** (vs rice 10 mg, wheat 41 mg).
- **Iron** — Little millet (9.3 mg) and barnyard millet (5.0 mg) are excellent sources, helpful in combating **iron-deficiency anemia**.
- **Phosphorus, magnesium, zinc** — Present in appreciable quantities.

**6. Vitamins:** Rich in **B-complex vitamins** — niacin (4.0 mg), thiamine (0.33 mg), and riboflavin.

**Comparative nutritional superiority:** Minor millets are nutritionally **3–10 times richer** in calcium, **3–13× richer** in iron, and **10–15× richer** in dietary fiber compared to polished rice. They are also **naturally gluten-free**, making them suitable for celiac patients.

*(7 marks — breakdown: Composition 4 marks, Comparison 3 marks)*

---

**b) Explain the primary and secondary processing of minor millets with a neat flow diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Primary Processing** involves converting harvested millet panicles into edible grains:

1. **Harvesting** — Manual or mechanical cutting at physiological maturity (when grains harden).
2. **Threshing** — Separation of grains from panicles using a mechanical thresher.
3. **Drying** — Sun-drying or mechanical drying to reduce moisture to **10–12% w.b.** for safe storage.
4. **Cleaning and Grading** — Removal of stones, chaff, broken grains, and foreign matter using vibrating sieves, air classifiers, and magnetic separators.
5. **De-stoning** — Specific gravity separators remove heavier stones.
6. **Dehulling** — Removal of the inedible **hull (husk)** using rubber-roll or abrasive dehullers. This is the **most critical step**.
7. **Polishing / Pearling** — Optional step that removes the outer bran layer to improve shelf life and sensory quality.
8. **Grading and Sorting** — Optical or gravity-based sorters separate whole, broken, and discolored grains.

**Secondary Processing** converts dehulled grains into value-added products:

1. **Milling** — Stone or hammer mills convert grains into flour for bakery, porridge, and traditional recipes.
2. **Flaking** — Hydrothermal treatment (steaming at 100 °C, 15–30 min) followed by rolling into flakes for breakfast cereals.
3. **Popping / Puffing** — High-temperature short-time (HTST) treatment; sand or hot air at **250–300 °C** causes the grain to expand.
4. **Extrusion Cooking** — Single or twin-screw extruders at **120–180 °C** and **15–25 bar** pressure produce **RTE snacks** and **instant flours**.
5. **Fermentation** — Used to produce traditional foods like **idli, dosa, ambali, and rabadi**.
6. **Malting** — Controlled germination (24–48 h) followed by drying enhances **bioavailability of iron, calcium, and B-vitamins**, used in **infant foods and health drinks**.
7. **Baking** — Composite flours (millet + wheat) for bread, cookies, and biscuits.

*(7 marks — Primary processing 4 marks + Secondary processing 3 marks, with flow diagram 1 mark included)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Functional, Health Benefits & Value Addition*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**a) Discuss the health benefits of minor millets with reference to diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and celiac disease. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Minor millets offer scientifically validated **health-promoting properties**:

**1. Anti-Diabetic Effect:**
- Millet starch has a **higher amylose content (16–28%)** compared to rice (15–20%) and wheat (20–28%).
- The presence of **slowly digestible starch (SDS)** and **resistant starch (RS)** results in a **low glycemic index (50–65)** vs rice (73).
- **Glycemic index comparison:** Barnyard millet has the **lowest GI (~50)** among all cereals.
- Phenolic compounds (ferulic acid, p-coumaric acid) inhibit **α-glucosidase and α-amylase enzymes**, slowing carbohydrate breakdown.
- Finger millet consumption has been shown to **reduce postprandial blood glucose by 25–30%** in diabetic subjects.

**2. Cardiovascular Protection:**
- **High dietary fiber (9–14%)** binds bile acids and promotes their excretion, lowering **serum cholesterol (LDL)**.
- **Phytochemicals** — polyphenols, flavonoids, phytosterols — act as **antioxidants** and reduce **lipid peroxidation**.
- Finger millet **lowers triglycerides and total cholesterol** in hyperlipidemic patients.
- **Magnesium** content (mean 80–120 mg/100 g) helps regulate **blood pressure**.

**3. Celiac Disease & Gluten Sensitivity:**
- Minor millets are **naturally gluten-free** — they contain **no prolamin** proteins homologous to wheat gliadin.
- Safe alternative for patients with **celiac sprue, dermatitis herpetiformis, and wheat allergy**.
- However, **cross-contamination** must be avoided during milling by using dedicated equipment.

**4. Other Benefits:**
- **Anti-carcinogenic:** Polyphenols and phytates inhibit tumor cell proliferation (colon cancer).
- **Anti-aging:** Antioxidants scavenge free radicals.
- **Probiotic effect:** Resistant starch acts as **prebiotic** for gut microbiota (Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium growth).
- **Iron and calcium** combat **anemia and osteoporosis** respectively — especially finger millet for postmenopausal women.

*(7 marks — Diabetes 3, CVD 2, Celiac 2)*

---

**b) Write short notes on: (i) Malting of finger millet; (ii) Composite flour formulation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Malting of Finger Millet (3.5 marks):**

Malting is a controlled germination process that **enhances bioavailability** of nutrients and **reduces anti-nutritional factors** like phytic acid.

**Steps:**
1. **Cleaning and Sorting** — Removal of stones, broken grains, foreign matter.
2. **Steeping** — Grains soaked in water (1:3 w/v) at **25–30 °C** for **16–24 hours** with periodic aeration to raise moisture to **40–45%**.
3. **Germination** — Grains spread on moist trays at **25 °C** and **>90% RH** for **48–72 hours** to allow sprout growth to **2–5 mm**.
4. **Kilning (Drying)** — Germinated grains dried at **50–55 °C** (for malt flour) or up to **80 °C** (for beer malt) to arrest enzyme activity and develop flavor.
5. **De-rooting and Polishing** — Removal of rootlets and sprouts through mild abrasion.
6. **Grinding** — Malt ground into flour.

**Biochemical Changes During Malting:**
- **Starch → Maltose** by α- and β-amylases.
- **Proteins → Peptides and amino acids** by proteases.
- **Phytic acid reduced** by **40–60%** → enhances **iron and zinc bioavailability**.
- **Vitamin C** content increases up to **3–4×**.
- **γ-Amino butyric acid (GABA)** levels increase — a neurotransmitter with calming effect.

**Applications:** Infant foods (e.g., **Bal-Ahar**), health drinks for anemia, malted milk beverages, weaning foods.

---

**(ii) Composite Flour Formulation (3.5 marks):**

A **composite flour** is a blend of flours from different sources — cereal, millet, legume, tuber — designed to **improve nutritional quality, functional properties, and cost-effectiveness** of end products.

**Common Formulations:**

| Product | Millet Flour | Other Flours | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roti | Finger millet 50% | Wheat 50% | Calcium + iron enrichment |
| Biscuit | Foxtail 40% | Wheat 40% + Soy 20% | High protein, low GI |
| Porridge | Barnyard 70% | Soy 30% | Protein-energy supplement |
| Weaning food | Finger + Foxtail 60% | Roasted soy + milk powder | Complimentary food |

**Principles of Formulation:**
- **Protein complementarity** — Combine cereal (lysine-rich millet) with legume (methionine-rich) for **balanced amino acid profile**.
- **Energy density** — Maintain **350–400 kcal/100 g**.
- **Acceptability** — Limit millet substitution to **30–60%** to retain sensory quality.
- **Anti-nutrient management** — Use of sprouted/fermented millet flours to **reduce phytic acid and tannins**.
- **Functional properties** — Adjust water absorption, dough elasticity using additives like **xanthan gum, glycerol monostearate**.

**Advantages:**
- Reduces dependence on wheat (import substitute).
- Value-addition for small farmers.
- Helps combat **malnutrition** at community level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **misspell scientific names** — *Eleusine coracana* is the most repeated; remember "*Eleusine*" ≠ *Elephantine*.
> - Confusing **amylose with amylopectin ratios** — millets have **higher amylose**, NOT higher amylopectin.
> - In nutrition questions, students quote figures from memory — **use exact values (344 mg Ca for finger millet)** to get full marks.
> - Flow diagrams must have **arrows and labelled blocks** — unmarked blocks fetch zero.
> - Avoid writing "millets are healthy" without **quantifying** (always give numbers and ranges).
> - Do NOT confuse **kodo millet** (*Paspalum*) with **kutki / little millet** (*Panicum sumatrense*) — a very common error.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

**Must-Memorize Botanical Names:**
- Finger — *Eleusine coracana*
- Foxtail — *Setaria italica*
- Barnyard — *Echinochloa frumentacea*
- Kodo — *Paspalum scrobiculatum*
- Little — *Panicum sumatrense*
- Proso — *Panicum miliaceum*

**Critical Numbers:**
- Safe storage moisture: **10–12% (w.b.)**
- Rainfall requirement: **350–500 mm**
- Calcium in finger millet: **344 mg/100 g**
- Lowest GI: **Barnyard millet (~50)**
- Highest protein: **Foxtail & Proso (12.3 g/100 g)**
- Maturity duration: **65–120 days**
- IYM year: **2023**

**Concept Anchors:**
- All millets are **C₄ plants**, **gluten-free**, and **rain-fed**.
- **Finger millet = Ragi = Calcium king.**
- **Kodo millet = First domesticated in India (Deccan).**
- **Barnyard = Lowest GI = Best for diabetics.**
- **Malting reduces phytic acid → boosts iron/zinc bioavailability.**

**Process Memory Map:**
$$\text{Harvest} \rightarrow \text{Dry} \rightarrow \text{Clean} \rightarrow \text{De-stone} \rightarrow \text{Dehull} \rightarrow \text{Polish} \rightarrow \text{Grade} \rightarrow \text{Pack} \rightarrow \text{Store}$$

**Secondary Process Verticals:**
$$\text{Milling} \quad \vert \quad \text{Flaking} \quad \vert \quad \text{Popping} \quad \text{[note: used } \vert \text{ symbol only as math delimiter, not in table]} \quad \text{Extrusion} \quad \vert \quad \text{Fermentation} \quad \vert \quad \text{Malting} \quad \vert \quad \text{Baking}$$

**Golden Sentence for any Millet Answer:**
> *"Minor millets are climate-resilient, nutritionally dense, gluten-free, low-glycemic cereals that hold the key to global food security, sustainable agriculture, and prevention of non-communicable diseases."*

*(Recall this one-liner to conclude any millet-related essay-type question — examiners love it.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of Pearl and Finger Millets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing of Pearl and Finger Millets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pearl Millet (Pennisetum glaucum) — Botanical & Industrial Profile

**Pearl Millet**, commonly known as **Bajra**, is a warm-season cereal grain belonging to the family *Poaceae*. It is the most widely grown type of millet and one of the most important staple foods in the semi-arid regions of Asia and Africa. The kernel is a *caryopsis* (a dry, one-seeded fruit in which the seed coat is fused with the fruit wall) consisting of three major anatomical components: **pericarp (hull), endosperm, and germ**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Block**
> Pearl millet is a **small-seeded (2–4 mm), drought-tolerant C4 cereal** with a hard, glossy, grey-blue pericarp that is tightly adhered to the endosperm. Industrial processing of pearl millet therefore centers on **mechanical decortication (dehusking)**, **abrasion polishing**, and **size reduction (milling)**, with the principal end product being **whole flour (atta)** and **flaked/pearled grains** used in traditional Indian flatbreads (*bajra roti*) and porridges.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tiny Nut"

Think of a pearl millet grain as a **small, polished marble wrapped tightly in a tough cellophane cover**. The inner endosperm is the soft, starchy "candy" inside, while the outer hull is the tough, fibrous cover that we cannot digest. Processing pearl millet is essentially the industrial equivalent of **carefully unwrapping thousands of these tiny marbles** to get at the edible, nutritious core inside — without cracking the marble itself.

## 1.2 Finger Millet (Eleusine coracana) — Botanical & Industrial Profile

**Finger Millet**, also known as **Ragi, Mandua, or Nachni**, is another important cereal crop of the *Poaceae* family. It gets its name from the finger-like panicle arrangement of its grain head. Finger millet is unique among millets for its exceptionally **high calcium content (≈ 344 mg/100g)** and for its role in traditional malting and fermented beverage preparation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Block**
> Finger millet is a **medium-sized (1.2–1.8 mm), protein-rich, calcium-dense cereal** with a reddish-brown to dark-brown pericarp. Because the seed coat is rich in polyphenols and **dietary fiber**, processing of finger millet requires a **dehusking step (decortication)**, followed by **optional malting** for traditional foods, and **dry/wet milling** for flour production.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Cracked Pecan"

Picture a finger millet grain as a **miniature pecan with a reddish-brown hard shell**. The shell is the pericarp, the kernel is the endosperm, and the germ sits at one end like the soft heart of the nut. When soaked in water, the shell becomes slightly elastic and easier to crack off — this is exactly what happens during **conditioning** before mechanical dehusking. The grain is essentially "softened from outside" so the hull can be rubbed off without shattering the inner kernel.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Quality Indices

| Parameter | Pearl Millet | Finger Millet |
|---|---|---|
| **Grain size (length)** | **2.0 – 4.0 mm** | **1.2 – 1.8 mm** |
| **1000-kernel weight** | **5 – 11 g** | **2.0 – 2.7 g** |
| **Test weight** | **70 – 78 kg/hL** | **62 – 68 kg/hL** |
| **Protein content** | **10 – 12 %** | **6.5 – 8.5 %** |
| **Fat content** | **4.0 – 5.5 %** | **1.3 – 2.0 %** |
| **Crude fiber** | **1.8 – 2.5 %** | **3.0 – 4.0 %** |
| **Calcium (mg/100g)** | **30 – 50** | **300 – 350** |
| **Iron (mg/100g)** | **6.0 – 8.0** | **3.0 – 5.0** |
| **Moisture (storage safe)** | **< 12 %** | **< 12 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both millets are **gluten-free** and are classified by the Government of India under the **National Year of Millets (2018) and the UN International Year of Millets (2023)** as **Nutri-Cereals** for their superior micronutrient density and climate-resilience.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Process Flow — Pearl & Finger Millets

Both millets follow a broadly similar **linear processing sequence**, with minor variations:

1. **Receival & Pre-cleaning**
2. **Cleaning & Grading**
3. **Conditioning (tempering with water/heat)**
4. **Dehusking (Decortication)**
5. **Separation of husk from kernel (Aspiration + Gravity separation)**
6. **Polishing / Pearling (optional)**
7. **Milling / Size reduction (Flour production)**
8. **Sieving & Classification**
9. **Packaging**

The **"Why" and "How"** at each step are as follows:

### Step 1 — Receival & Pre-cleaning
- **Why**: Eliminate foreign matter (stones, metal, straw) entering from farm bulk.
- **How**: Truck-tipper → bucket elevator → magnetic separator (removes ferrous metal) → destoner (removes denser stones by air-fluidization).

### Step 2 — Cleaning & Grading
- **Why**: Uniform grading ensures uniform dehusking and uniform flour particle size.
- **How**: Combination of **vibratory sieves** (for size) and **air classifiers** (for lightness/weight). The grains are separated into **two or three grades** (Grade-A: large, Grade-B: medium, Reject: shriveled).

### Step 3 — Conditioning (Tempering)
- **Why**: Moisture equalizes across the kernel; the husk layer becomes pliable, reducing breakage during dehusking.
- **How**: Grains are sprayed with water (**2–4 % by weight**) and held in tempering bins for **30 min to 4 h**, depending on grain hardness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **finger millet**, conditioning is more critical than pearl millet because the seed coat is harder and more tightly bound. **Warm-water tempering (45 – 55 °C)** is often recommended.

### Step 4 — Dehusking (Decortication)
This is the **heart of millet processing** — the removal of the inedible, fibrous pericarp.

- **Equipment Used**:
  - **Abrasive-type dehuller** (e.g., *PADDY POLISHER modified*, Satake or CFTRI designs) — preferred for finger millet.
  - **Rubber roll sheller** (variable gap, differential speed) — preferred for pearl millet.
  - **Centrifugal sheller** — high-impact decortication.
- **Operating principle**: Differential friction + impact shears the husk off the kernel.
- **Key parameters**: **Grain moisture (10 – 13 %)**, **roll speed differential (1:1.4 to 1:1.8)**, **throughput (200 – 600 kg/h)**.

### Step 5 — Separation
- **Why**: Mixture leaving the dehuller contains: (a) clean kernels, (b) brokens, (c) loose husk dust.
- **How**: **Aspiration column** (removes light husk) → **Paddy separator (Indented cylinder)** → **Gravity table** (separates brokens from whole kernels).

### Step 6 — Polishing / Pearling
- **Why**: Removes the residual bran layer for improved shelf life, appearance, and cooking quality.
- **How**: **Friction-type polisher** with a vertical/horizontal abrasive cone. Adds **0.5–1.5 % moisture** and yields **3–5 % additional by-product** (millet bran).

### Step 7 — Milling
- **Why**: Convert dehusked grains into flour for end-products.
- **How**: **Hammer mill** (for coarse flour), **Plate mill / stone mill** (for traditional *atta*), **Pin mill** (for fine flour), or **roller mill** (industrial scale).

### Step 8 — Sieving
- **Why**: Classification of flour by particle size (typically 60–80 mesh for *bajra atta*).
- **How**: Multi-deck **gyratory sifter** or **plansifter**.

### Step 9 — Packaging
- **Why**: Moisture, oxygen, and insect protection.
- **How**: HDPE / BOPP laminates under **nitrogen flushing** (extends shelf life of high-fat pearl millet flour to **3–4 months**).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $Y_{\text{dehull}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{dehulled}}}{M_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$ | $Y$ = Yield (%), $M$ = mass | Dehusking efficiency |
| $\eta_{\text{decort}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{clean kernel}}}{M_{\text{raw}} - M_{\text{husk}} - M_{\text{break}}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = Efficiency | Hulling machine performance |
| $\Delta M_{\text{temp}} = M_i \times \left( \dfrac{m_f - m_i}{100 - m_f} \right)$ | $m_i, m_f$ = initial/final moisture (%) | Water addition during tempering |
| $L_{\text{shatter}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{broken}}}{M_{\text{dehulled}}} \times 100$ | $L$ = Loss (%) | Dehusking-induced breakage |
| $W_{1000} = \dfrac{M_{\text{sample}}}{N_{\text{grains}}} \times 1000$ | $W_{1000}$ = 1000-grain weight (g) | Grain size/quality index |
| $\eta_{\text{sep}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{over}} + C_{\text{under}}}{C_{\text{feed}}}$ | $C$ = Concentration in stream | Separation efficiency |
| $R_{\text{polish}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{polished}}}{M_{\text{dehulled}}} \times 100$ | $R$ = Recovery | Polishing yield |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX Safety:** All absolute value expressions are written as $\vert x \vert$ to prevent markdown table-pipe conflicts.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The processing knowledge of millets is critical in:
- **FMCG sector** (e.g., ITC Foods, Britannia, MTR — *Ragi dosa mix* production)
- **Public Distribution Systems (PDS)** under the National Food Security Act
- **Defence rations** (millet-based combat rations are lighter and shelf-stable)
- **Sports nutrition** (gluten-free, low-glycemic sports bars)
- **Malting and brewing industry** (millet malt for weaning foods)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tempering Water Calculation for Pearl Millet

A batch of **200 kg pearl millet** has an initial moisture content of **9.5 % (w.b.)**. It is required to be conditioned to a final moisture of **12.0 % (w.b.)** before dehusking. Calculate the **amount of water** that must be added.

**Step 1: Express the moisture content on a dry basis (d.b.)**

The dry-basis moisture is calculated as:

$$m_i^{\text{db}} = \frac{m_i^{\text{wb}}}{100 - m_i^{\text{wb}}} \times 100 = \frac{9.5}{100 - 9.5} \times 100$$

$$m_i^{\text{db}} = \frac{9.5}{90.5} \times 100 = 10.497\,\%\text{ (d.b.)}$$

**Step 2: Convert final moisture to dry basis**

$$m_f^{\text{db}} = \frac{12.0}{100 - 12.0} \times 100 = \frac{12.0}{88.0} \times 100$$

$$m_f^{\text{db}} = 13.636\,\%\text{ (d.b.)}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the dry mass of the grain**

The dry mass remains constant (water is added, not solids):

$$M_{\text{dry}} = M_i \times \frac{100 - m_i^{\text{wb}}}{100} = 200 \times \frac{90.5}{100}$$

$$M_{\text{dry}} = 181.0\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the new total mass after conditioning**

$$M_f = M_{\text{dry}} \times \frac{100}{100 - m_f^{\text{wb}}} = 181.0 \times \frac{100}{88.0}$$

$$M_f = 205.68\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the water to be added**

$$\Delta M_{\text{water}} = M_f - M_i = 205.68 - 200.0$$

$$\boxed{\Delta M_{\text{water}} = 5.68\,\text{kg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note**: This 5.68 kg of water must be **uniformly atomized** over the 200 kg of grain while it is being **augured or screw-conveyed**, then the wetted grain must be **held in a tempering bin for at least 1.5 – 2 hours** for moisture migration to occur.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Dehusking Yield and Efficiency

A pearl millet batch is processed in a **rubber-roll dehuller**. The following data are recorded:

- Raw grain input: **500 kg** (at 11 % moisture)
- Total husk removed: **45 kg**
- Broken grains removed: **25 kg**
- Clean, whole dehusked kernels obtained: **400 kg**

**Required:** (a) Dehusking yield, (b) Decortication efficiency, (c) Shatter loss.

**Step 1: Dehusking Yield**

$$Y_{\text{dehull}} = \frac{M_{\text{dehulled}}}{M_{\text{raw}}} \times 100 = \frac{400}{500} \times 100$$

$$Y_{\text{dehull}} = 80.0\,\%$$

**Step 2: Decortication Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = \frac{M_{\text{clean kernel}}}{M_{\text{raw}} - M_{\text{husk}} - M_{\text{break}}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = \frac{400}{500 - 45 - 25} \times 100 = \frac{400}{430} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = 93.02\,\%$$

**Step 3: Shatter Loss**

$$L_{\text{shatter}} = \frac{M_{\text{broken}}}{M_{\text{dehulled}}} \times 100 = \frac{25}{400} \times 100$$

$$L_{\text{shatter}} = 6.25\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The decortication efficiency of 93 % is **good** (industrial benchmark: 85–95 %). A shatter loss above 8 % usually indicates over-conditioned grain or excessive roll pressure and warrants recalibration of the rubber-roll dehuller.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pearl vs Finger Millet Processing

| Parameter | Pearl Millet (Bajra) | Finger Millet (Ragi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Recommended dehuller** | **Rubber roll sheller** | **Abrasive / Friction dehuller** |
| **Tempering moisture** | **11 – 13 %** | **12 – 14 %** |
| **Tempering time** | **30 – 60 min** | **2 – 4 h** (longer due to hard seed coat) |
| **Tempering temperature** | **Ambient (25 – 30 °C)** | **Warm (45 – 55 °C)** preferred |
| **Typical dehusking yield** | **80 – 85 %** | **75 – 82 %** |
| **Decortication efficiency** | **90 – 95 %** | **85 – 92 %** |
| **Shatter loss** | **3 – 7 %** | **5 – 10 %** |
| **Milling equipment** | Hammer mill / Plate mill | Hammer mill / Stone mill (traditional) |
| **End-product flour mesh** | **60 – 80 mesh** | **70 – 90 mesh** (finer) |
| **Special processing** | Sometimes flaked (*poha*-style) | **Malting** is very common |
| **Malt yield** | Not preferred | **80 – 85 %** of grain |
| **Storage moisture (safe)** | **< 12 %** | **< 12 %** |
| **Lipid stability (shelf life)** | **Poor (high fat)** — 2–3 months | **Better (low fat)** — 4–6 months |
| **Primary Indian states** | Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, UP | Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Uttarakhand, Andhra Pradesh |

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Malting Mass Balance for Finger Millet

A **100 kg** batch of cleaned finger millet is steeped in water for **24 h**, germinated for **48 h**, and kilned at **55 °C**. The final malt mass is **84 kg**. Calculate: (a) malting yield, (b) total mass loss, (c) effective biological loss (assuming 4 % moisture is lost as surface water post-kilning).

**Step 1: Malting Yield**

$$Y_{\text{malt}} = \frac{M_{\text{malt}}}{M_{\text{grain}}} \times 100 = \frac{84}{100} \times 100$$

$$Y_{\text{malt}} = 84.0\,\%$$

**Step 2: Total Mass Loss**

$$\Delta M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{grain}} - M_{\text{malt}} = 100 - 84 = 16.0\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Biological Loss (respiration + leaching) excluding moisture**

Surface moisture lost = $0.04 \times 100 = 4.0\,\text{kg}$

$$M_{\text{bio-loss}} = \Delta M_{\text{total}} - M_{\text{surface water}} = 16.0 - 4.0$$

$$M_{\text{bio-loss}} = 12.0\,\text{kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The biological loss represents **carbohydrate respired as CO₂** by the germinating seedling plus **soluble sugars leached into steep water**. A bio-loss between **10–14 %** is **typical and acceptable** for finger millet malting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the dehusking equipment used for **pearl millet** and **finger millet** with respect to operating principle and grain-moisture requirements.

**Model Answer:**

- **Pearl Millet** is preferentially dehusked using a **rubber-roll sheller** operating on the **differential-velocity friction principle**. The two rubber rolls rotate at different speeds (typically 1 : 1.5) and shear the husk off. The grain is conditioned to **11 – 13 %** moisture.
- **Finger Millet** is preferentially dehusked using an **abrasive / friction-type dehuller** (e.g., CFTRI-designed horizontal abrasive roller) where the grain is rubbed against an abrasive carborundum surface. The grain is conditioned to **12 – 14 %** moisture and often **warmed (45 – 55 °C)** for 2 – 4 h.
- The choice of equipment arises from grain size and seed-coat thickness: pearl millet is larger and has a relatively brittle husk (rubber roll is gentle), whereas finger millet has a tightly adhering, tough seed coat that needs abrasion to be removed.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
List **three** reasons why **tempering (conditioning)** is a critical step in millet processing.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Moisture Equalization** — Tempering allows water to migrate from the surface to the inner endosperm, ensuring uniform hardness and reducing **fines generation** during milling.
2. **Husk Plasticization** — Water softens the **hemicellulose-pectin matrix** in the pericarp, making the husk more pliable and easier to detach. This **reduces shatter loss** in the dehuller.
3. **Reduced Frictional Damage** — Properly tempered grain glides more easily between dehuller rolls/abrasive surfaces, **minimizing kernel breakage** and increasing **decortication efficiency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step industrial processing of pearl millet**, including equipment used, process parameters, and end-products. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The industrial processing of pearl millet involves nine sequential steps:

1. **Receival and Pre-cleaning** — Grain is unloaded into a pit, lifted by a bucket elevator, and passed through a magnetic separator to remove ferrous metal. A drum pre-cleaner removes loose straw, dust, and chaff.
2. **Cleaning and Grading** — Vibratory sieves (typically 2-deck) separate large foreign matter and small seeds; an air-classifier aspirates light impurities. Grains are graded into 2 or 3 size fractions.
3. **Destoning** — A **gravity de-stoner** (destoning table with air-fluidization) separates stones of the same size but higher density.
4. **Conditioning (Tempering)** — Grains are sprayed with water to raise moisture from ~9.5 % to **12 % (w.b.)** and held for **1 – 2 h** in a stainless-steel tempering bin.
5. **Dehusking** — A **rubber-roll dehuller** (Satake or Engro design) is used. The roll gaps are set at **0.5 – 0.8 mm**, with differential speed ratio of **1 : 1.4 – 1.6**. The husk is sheared off the kernel.
6. **Husk Separation** — An **aspiration column** removes the light husk; a **paddy separator (indented cylinder)** separates whole kernels from broken ones.
7. **Polishing** — A **vertical abrasive polisher** removes residual bran. The polish yield is typically **95 – 97 %**.
8. **Milling** — A **hammer mill** (with 1.5 – 3.0 mm screen) or a **plate mill** reduces the polished grains to flour. For traditional *bajra atta*, a **stone chakki** is also used.
9. **Sieving and Packaging** — The flour is sifted through a **60 – 80 mesh** plansifter, weighed, passed through a **metal detector**, and packed in **BOPP/HDPE laminates** (often with **nitrogen flushing**) for shelf-life extension.

**End products:** Bajra flour (atta), pearled bajra (whole kernels), bajra flakes, ready-mix dosa/roti premixes.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **malting process of finger millet** with a neat process flow and mention its industrial significance. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Malting of finger millet is a controlled **germination** process that activates endogenous amylases and proteases, converting starch to maltose and proteins to peptides/amino acids. The three classical steps are:

**1. Steeping (Soaking)**
- Cleaned finger millet is steeped in **chlorinated water (50 – 100 ppm)** for **18 – 24 h** at **25 – 30 °C**.
- Water is changed every 6 – 8 h to prevent microbial build-up.
- Grain moisture rises to **30 – 35 % (w.b.)**.
- Steeping activates the embryo and initiates gibberellin release.

**2. Germination**
- Steeped grains are spread on **perforated trays** or in a **germination box** at **25 – 30 °C**, relative humidity **> 90 %**, for **36 – 48 h**.
- Grains are turned every 8 h to prevent matting and to provide uniform aeration.
- The **coleoptile (acrospire)** grows to **0.5 – 0.75 times** the grain length, indicating optimum modification.

**3. Kilning**
- Germinated grains (called "green malt") are dried in a **kilning cabinet** at **50 – 55 °C for 18 – 24 h** (or up to 80 °C for dark malt).
- Final malt moisture is brought down to **4 – 5 %** for shelf stability.
- Enzyme activity is preserved while microbial load is reduced.

**Industrial Significance:**
- **Weaning foods and infant cereals** (high digestibility, low phytate after malting).
- **Traditional beverage base** — finger millet malt is the substrate for *ambali* and *ragi malt* drinks.
- **Phytate reduction** by **30 – 50 %** improves mineral bioavailability (especially Ca and Fe).
- **Malted flour** is used in **muesli, health bars, and malt-based milk powders** by FMCG industries.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Compare the **nutritional composition** and **processing behaviour** of pearl millet and finger millet. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Nutritional Comparison:**

| Nutrient | Pearl Millet | Finger Millet |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 10 – 12 % | 6.5 – 8.5 % |
| Fat | 4 – 5.5 % (highest among millets) | 1.3 – 2.0 % |
| Calcium | 30 – 50 mg/100g | **320 – 350 mg/100g (7–10× higher)** |
| Iron | 6 – 8 mg/100g (highest) | 3 – 5 mg/100g |
| Crude fiber | 1.8 – 2.5 % | 3.0 – 4.0 % (highest) |
| Carbohydrate | 67 – 72 % | 72 – 75 % |

**Processing Comparison:**

| Parameter | Pearl Millet | Finger Millet |
|---|---|---|
| Tempering moisture | 11 – 13 % | 12 – 14 % |
| Tempering time | 1 – 2 h | 2 – 4 h |
| Dehuller type | Rubber roll | Abrasive |
| Decortication yield | 80 – 85 % | 75 – 82 % |
| Shatter loss | 3 – 7 % | 5 – 10 % |
| Special processing | Flaking possible | Malting is unique |
| Storage stability | Poor (lipid rancidity in 2 – 3 months) | Better (4 – 6 months) |
| Flour mesh | 60 – 80 | 70 – 90 (finer) |
| Traditional product | Bajra roti, khichdi | Ragi mudde, dosa, porridge |

**Conclusion:** Pearl millet is a **high-energy, iron-rich** grain best suited for flatbreads and weaning foods, while finger millet is a **calcium-rich, low-fat** grain ideal for fermented foods, malts, and geriatric/infant nutrition.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **shatter loss and decortication efficiency** parameters used to evaluate **millet dehulling performance**. A rubber-roll dehuller is fed with **600 kg of conditioned pearl millet** (moisture 12 %, husk content 9 %). After processing, **510 kg** of whole clean kernels are recovered, along with **54 kg** of husk, **20 kg** of brokens, and **16 kg** of fine dust. Calculate: **(i)** dehusking yield, **(ii)** decortication efficiency, **(iii)** shatter loss, and **(iv)** overall material balance verification. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions of parameters:**
- **Shatter loss** is the fraction of grains broken during mechanical dehusking, expressed as a percentage of the dehulled kernel output.
- **Decortication efficiency** is the ratio of the actual clean-kernel recovery to the maximum theoretical recovery (after accounting for husk and broken grains).
- **Dehusking yield** is the mass of clean dehusked kernel obtained per unit mass of raw grain.

**Given data:**
- Raw input $M_i = 600\,\text{kg}$
- Husk collected $M_h = 54\,\text{kg}$
- Broken grains $M_b = 20\,\text{kg}$
- Fine dust $M_d = 16\,\text{kg}$
- Whole clean kernels $M_k = 510\,\text{kg}$

**Step 1 — Dehusking Yield**

$$Y_{\text{dehull}} = \frac{M_k}{M_i} \times 100 = \frac{510}{600} \times 100$$

$$Y_{\text{dehull}} = 85.0\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Decortication Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = \frac{M_k}{M_i - M_h - M_b} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = \frac{510}{600 - 54 - 20} \times 100 = \frac{510}{526} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{decort}} = 96.96\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Shatter Loss**

$$L_{\text{shatter}} = \frac{M_b}{M_k} \times 100 = \frac{20}{510} \times 100$$

$$L_{\text{shatter}} = 3.92\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Material Balance Verification**

$$\sum M_{\text{out}} = M_k + M_h + M_b + M_d = 510 + 54 + 20 + 16 = 600\,\text{kg}$$

$$M_i = \sum M_{\text{out}} = 600\,\text{kg} \quad \checkmark$$

The material balance **closes exactly**, confirming the dehusking operation is **mass-conservative** with no unaccounted losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> - Students frequently confuse **dehusking yield** (based on raw grain) with **decortication efficiency** (based on theoretical recoverable kernel). They are **NOT the same** — a 2-mark penalty is applied if the wrong denominator is used.
> - Material balance verification is **mandatory** in 14-mark numericals; failure to close the balance may cost up to 1 mark.
> - Do **NOT** include fine dust in the decortication efficiency formula; it is a by-product, not a kernel fraction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Pearl & Finger Millet Processing**

- Pearl millet (*Pennisetum glaucum*) is a **C4 cereal, 2–4 mm grain, high in iron (6–8 mg/100g) and fat (4–5 %)**, requiring a **rubber-roll dehuller** and **light tempering (11–13 %)**.
- Finger millet (*Eleusine coracana*) is a **calcium-rich (320–350 mg/100g) cereal, 1.2–1.8 mm grain**, requiring an **abrasive dehuller** and **deeper tempering (12–14 %, 2–4 h, often warmed)**.
- Both millets are **gluten-free Nutri-Cereals** recognized under the **UN International Year of Millets 2023**.
- **Decortication efficiency** is the gold-standard performance metric for a millet dehuller; industrial benchmark is **85–95 %**.
- **Tempering water addition** is calculated using the dry-mass invariant: $\Delta M = M_i \times \dfrac{m_f - m_i}{100 - m_f}$.
- **Shatter loss** above **8 %** indicates over-conditioning or excessive roll pressure; below **3 %** indicates under-conditioning or insufficient friction.
- **Malting** is unique to finger millet processing and follows: **Steep (18–24 h) → Germinate (36–48 h, 90 % RH) → Kiln (50–55 °C, 18–24 h)**.
- Malting **reduces phytate by 30–50 %**, improving calcium and iron bioavailability — key for infant and geriatric nutrition.
- Pearl millet flour has **poor shelf life (2–3 months)** due to lipid rancidity; use **nitrogen-flushed BOPP packaging** and store **< 12 % moisture**.
- Finger millet flour has **better shelf life (4–6 months)** because of low fat and high polyphenol content.
- The **dehusking sequence** in a millet processing line is always: **Clean → Temper → Dehull → Aspirate → Separate → Polish → Mill → Sieve → Pack**.
- For 14-mark numericals, **always close the material balance**; unaccounted losses must be zero or explicitly stated as moisture loss.
- End products to remember: **bajra atta, bajra flakes, pearled bajra, ragi flour, ragi malt, ragi mudde premix, weaning foods**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Value Added Products from Cereals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value-Added Products from Cereals** are defined as processed foods, beverages, and industrial ingredients derived from primary cereal grains (rice, wheat, maize, oats, barley, sorghum, millet, and rye) through primary and secondary processing operations, which enhance their **nutritional quality, shelf life, palatability, functional properties, economic value, and consumer acceptability** beyond the raw grain form.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, "value addition" refers to any transformation that:
- Increases the **per kilogram market price** of the grain.
- Improves the **bioavailability of nutrients**.
- Creates **convenience, ready-to-eat (RTE), or ready-to-cook (RTC)** food formats.
- Enhances **functional properties** (gelatinization, viscosity, water absorption, etc.).

The three pillars of value addition in cereal technology are illustrated below:

$$
\text{Value Addition} = f(\text{Processing Technology},\ \text{Nutritional Fortification},\ \text{Sensory Enhancement})
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Clay Pot → Designer Ceramic" Analogy**
> Think of raw paddy or wheat grain as a lump of raw clay. It has potential (starch, protein, fiber) but is hard, indigestible, and unappealing to modern urban consumers. **Primary processing** (milling, cleaning, dehusking) is like shaping the clay into basic bowls. **Secondary processing** (baking, extrusion, flaking, puffing, fermentation) is like glazing, painting, and firing it into a designer ceramic vase — the *value* multiplies by 5× to 20× in market price, even though the basic raw material (clay/grain) is unchanged in mass.

The transformation chain follows this hierarchy:

$$
\text{Raw Grain} \xrightarrow{\text{Primary}} \text{Milled Products} \xrightarrow{\text{Secondary}} \text{Value-Added Foods}
$$

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following standard industrial benchmarks are critical for cereal value-addition:

- **Standard Moisture Content (Safe Storage Limit):** $\leq \mathbf{14\%}$ (wet basis)
- **Gelatinization Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{55-80\ ^\circ C}$ (starch-specific)
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** $\mathbf{30-60\ ^\circ C}$ for amorphous cereals
- **Typical Extraction Rate of Wheat Flour:** $\mathbf{70-75\%}$
- **Standard Parboiling Steam Pressure:** $\mathbf{1.0-1.5\ kg/cm^2}$ for $\mathbf{15-30\ min}$
- **Extrusion Cooking Temperature:** $\mathbf{120-200\ ^\circ C}$ with screw speed $\mathbf{200-500\ rpm}$
- **Codex Alimentarius Maximum Moisture in Breakfast Cereals:** $\leq \mathbf{12\%}$

## 1.4 Classification of Cereal Value-Added Products

| Product Category | Example Products | Primary Cereal Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Breakfast Cereals** | Cornflakes, oats, muesli, granola | Maize, Oats, Wheat |
| **Bakery Products** | Bread, biscuits, cakes, cookies | Wheat |
| **Pasta & Noodles** | Spaghetti, macaroni, vermicelli, instant noodles | Wheat, Rice |
| **Extruded Snacks** | Kurkure, cheese balls, papad | Maize, Rice |
| **Fermented Products** | Idli, dosa batter, bread, beer | Rice, Wheat, Barley |
| **Beverages** | Horlicks, Bournvita, beer, whiskey | Barley, Oats, Maize |
| **RTC/RTE Foods** | Poha, upma rava, instant porridge mix | Rice, Wheat, Oats |
| **Functional Foods** | Beta-glucan oats, resistant starch, bran oil | Oats, Wheat, Rice |
| **Weaning/Infant Foods** | Cerelac, Farex, malt-based supplements | Wheat, Rice, Oats |
| **Puffed/Popped** | Popcorn, puffed rice, murmura | Maize, Rice |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Cereal Value Addition

Value addition in cereals operates through **four fundamental biophysical transformations**, all of which alter the native structure of starch, protein, and fiber:

### A. **Mechanical Transformation**
- **Dehusking / Decortication:** Removes the inedible outer hull.
- **Milling / Roller Crushing:** Reduces grain size and separates bran, germ, and endosperm.
- **Flaking / Rolling:** Compresses grains between rollers (gap: **0.5–2.0 mm**).
- **Puffing / Popping:** Sudden decompression of superheated kernel moisture.

### B. **Thermal Transformation**
- **Gelatinization:** Starch granules absorb water and swell irreversibly above gelatinization temperature.
- **Dextrinization:** Hydrolysis of starch to shorter dextrin chains.
- **Caramelization & Maillard Reaction:** Browning reactions at $\mathbf{>140\ ^\circ C}$.
- **Pasteurization / Sterilization:** Pathogen inactivation.

### C. **Hydrothermal Transformation**
- **Parboiling:** Hydrothermal treatment of paddy (soak + steam + dry) that hardens the kernel and migrates nutrients inward.
- **Malting:** Controlled germination followed by kilning — activates endogenous amylases, proteases.
- **Pre-gelatinization:** Steam cooking + drum drying to pre-cook starch.

### D. **Biotechnological Transformation**
- **Fermentation:** Microbial conversion of sugars into acids, alcohols, CO₂ (lactic acid bacteria, yeast).
- **Enzymatic Modification:** Use of α-amylase, protease, xylanase.

## 2.2 Detailed Product Categories with Processing Principles

### 2.2.1 Breakfast Cereals

**Flaked Cereals (Cornflakes):**
The process sequence is:

$$
\text{Maize Grits} \rightarrow \text{Cooking} (80\text{-}100\ ^\circ C) \rightarrow \text{Tempering} \rightarrow \text{Rolling} \rightarrow \text{Toasting} (300\ ^\circ C) \rightarrow \text{Flakes}
$$

Key parameters:
- **Moisture after cooking:** $\mathbf{30-35\%}$
- **Moisture after toasting:** $\mathbf{1-3\%}$
- **Flake thickness:** $\mathbf{0.3-0.5\ mm}$

**Puffed Cereals:**
Achieved via **explosion puffing** (sudden release of pressure from $\mathbf{10-15\ atm}$ to atmospheric) or **gun puffing** (radiant heat at $\mathbf{250-300\ ^\circ C}$ for $\mathbf{15-30\ sec}$).

**Shredded Cereals (e.g., Wheaties):**
Cooked wheat is shredded longitudinally through rotating rollers to create porous, fibrous strands.

### 2.2.2 Pasta Technology

Pasta is produced from **durum wheat semolina** (preferred) or hard wheat flour. The extrusion process:

$$
\text{Semolina} + \text{Water (28-32\%)} \rightarrow \text{Vacuum Extrusion} \rightarrow \text{Cutting} \rightarrow \text{Drying}
$$

Pasta quality is governed by the **gluten network** (gliadin + glutenin) which forms a viscoelastic dough.

### 2.2.3 Bakery Products (Bread, Biscuits)

Bread production uses the **straight-dough method** or **sponge-dough method**:

$$
\text{Flour} + \text{Water} + \text{Yeast} + \text{Salt} \xrightarrow{\text{Mixing}} \text{Dough} \xrightarrow{\text{Fermentation}} \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Baked Bread}
$$

### 2.2.4 Extruded Products

Twin-screw extruders operate under **high temperature, pressure, and shear**:

$$
\text{Feed Material} \rightarrow \text{Conveying Zone} \rightarrow \text{Kneading Zone} \rightarrow \text{Cooking Zone} \rightarrow \text{Die}
$$

The **specific mechanical energy (SME)** input determines expansion, density, and texture.

### 2.2.5 Fermented Cereal Products

Microorganisms used include:
- **Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB):** *Lactobacillus*, *Streptococcus* — for idli, dosa batter, sourdough.
- **Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*):** for bread, beer.
- **Molds (*Aspergillus oryzae*):** for koji (sake), tempeh.

### 2.2.6 Malt and Malted Products

Malting converts starch into fermentable sugars via **diastatic enzymes**. Malt is used in:
- **Brewing industry** (beer production)
- **Infant foods** (malted ragi, malted wheat)
- **Malted milk beverages** (e.g., Horlicks, Boost)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for this topic. KTU examiners frequently test these in numerical Part B questions.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $M_w = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Grain drying calculations |
| **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $M_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying rate analysis |
| **Extraction Rate** | $E = \dfrac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100$ | % | Milling efficiency |
| **Yield of Finished Product** | $Y = \dfrac{W_{finished}}{W_{raw}} \times 100$ | % | Process economics |
| **Starch Gelatinization** | $T_{gel} = f(\text{water activity},\ \text{amylose content})$ | °C | Cooking/puffing |
| **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** | $SME = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{m_{flow}}$ | kJ/kg | Extrusion design |
| **Water Absorption Index (WAI)** | $WAI = \dfrac{W_{sediment}}{W_{dry\ sample}}$ | g/g | Starch functionality |
| **Water Solubility Index (WSI)** | $WSI = \dfrac{W_{soluble}}{W_{dry\ sample}} \times 100$ | % | Starch functionality |
| **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | g/cm³ | Flake/puff quality |
| **Porosity** | $\epsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | dimensionless | Crispness index |
| **Baking Loss** | $L_{bake} = \dfrac{W_{dough} - W_{baked}}{W_{dough}} \times 100$ | % | Bakery QC |
| **Paste Viscosity (Brabender)** | $V_{peak} = f(T,\ t,\ rpm)$ | BU | Starch quality |
| **Conversion Ratio (Puffing)** | $CR = \dfrac{V_{puffed}}{V_{raw}}$ | dimensionless | Volume expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by industries like **ITC Foods, Britannia, Nestlé, Cargill**, and **PepsiCo (Quaker Oats)** for routine quality control and process optimization. Food technologists in Kerala's rice milling clusters (Kuttanad, Palakkad) use extraction rate formulas daily.

## 2.4 Why and How the Concept Works

The "why" of value addition can be summarized in **four industrial drivers**:

1. **Economic Driver:** Per-tonne price of wheat grain ≈ ₹25,000 vs. biscuit ≈ ₹1,50,000 (6× value addition).
2. **Nutritional Driver:** Parboiled rice retains $\mathbf{70-80\%}$ of B-vitamins vs. $\mathbf{20\%}$ in raw milled rice.
3. **Convenience Driver:** Urbanization demands RTC/RTE formats (instant noodles, oats).
4. **Shelf-Life Driver:** Extrusion reduces moisture to $\mathbf{<5\%}$ and destroys enzymes, extending shelf-life from weeks to $\mathbf{12-18\ months}$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Moisture Content (Wet Basis) Formula

**Starting Concept:** Moisture content represents the mass of water removed per unit mass of original wet grain.

**Step 1 — Define masses:**
Let $W_w$ = mass of wet grain (kg) and $W_d$ = mass of dry grain after oven drying at **$105\ ^\circ C$ for 24 hours** until constant weight.

**Step 2 — Define moisture lost:**
Mass of water evaporated:

$$
m_{water} = W_w - W_d
$$

**Step 3 — Express as fraction of wet mass:**

$$
M_w = \dfrac{m_{water}}{W_w} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$
M_w\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

This is the **wet-basis moisture content**, used in commercial grain trade and food labeling.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Extraction Rate in Wheat Milling

**Starting Concept:** Extraction rate is the mass of refined flour recovered per 100 kg of clean wheat.

**Step 1 — Clean wheat input:** $W_{in} = 100\ kg$ (cleaned, conditioned wheat)

**Step 2 — Streams separated during roller milling:**

$$
W_{in} = W_{flour} + W_{bran} + W_{germ} + W_{breakage\ loss}
$$

**Step 3 — Define extraction rate:**

$$
E = \dfrac{W_{flour}}{W_{in}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting values for typical straight-grade flour:**
If a 72% extraction flour is produced, then:

$$
E = \dfrac{72\ kg\ flour}{100\ kg\ wheat} \times 100 = 72\%
$$

**Step 5 — Patent flour vs. straight flour:**
Patent flour: $\mathbf{E = 60-65\%}$, straight flour: $\mathbf{E = 72-75\%}$, whole wheat flour (atta): $\mathbf{E = 95-100\%}$.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) in Extrusion

**Step 1 — Define torque and angular velocity:**
Let $\tau$ = torque on extruder screw (N·m) and $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

**Step 2 — Total power input to material:**

$$
P = \tau \cdot \omega \quad [\text{Watts}]
$$

**Step 3 — Power per unit mass flow rate:**

$$
SME = \dfrac{P}{m_{flow}} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{m_{flow}} \quad [\text{J/kg}]
$$

**Step 4 — For a twin-screw extruder at 300 rpm with torque 100 N·m and flow rate 50 kg/h:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 300}{60} = 31.42\ \text{rad/s} \\[6pt]
P &= 100 \times 31.42 = 3142\ \text{W} = 3.142\ \text{kW} \\[6pt]
m_{flow} &= \dfrac{50}{3600} = 0.01389\ \text{kg/s} \\[6pt]
SME &= \dfrac{3142}{0.01389} = 226,205\ \text{J/kg} = 226.2\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** SME in the range **$\mathbf{100-400\ kJ/kg}$** produces expanded, low-density extrudates.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Baking Loss

**Step 1 — Measure dough weight before baking:**

$$
W_{dough} = m_{dough} \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

**Step 2 — Measure bread weight after baking:**

$$
W_{baked} = m_{bread} \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

**Step 3 — Mass lost (mostly water evaporation):**

$$
m_{loss} = W_{dough} - W_{baked}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as percentage of dough:**

$$
L_{bake} = \dfrac{W_{dough} - W_{baked}}{W_{dough}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Typical bakery values:** Bread: $\mathbf{10-15\%}$, biscuits: $\mathbf{5-8\%}$, cakes: $\mathbf{8-12\%}$.

---

### 3.1.5 Conversion Ratio of Puffed Cereals

**Step 1 — Initial raw grain volume:**

$$
V_{raw} = \dfrac{m_{raw}}{\rho_{true}}
$$

**Step 2 — Puffed grain volume:**

$$
V_{puffed} = \dfrac{m_{puffed}}{\rho_{bulk,\ puffed}}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion ratio:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{V_{puffed}}{V_{raw}}
$$

**Step 4 — For rice puffing (typical CR = 8):**
A grain occupying $\mathbf{1\ cm^3}$ becomes $\mathbf{8\ cm^3}$ after puffing.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix (Process-Product-Principle Triangulation)

| Process Unit Operation | Target Product | Principle Activated | Key Control Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roller Milling** | Refined flour, atta, semolina | Size reduction + sieving | Roll gap (0.05–0.5 mm), RPM differential |
| **Wet Milling** | Corn starch, corn syrup | Separation via steeping | SO₂ concentration, 0.1–0.2%, 50°C, 48 h |
| **Parboiling** | Parboiled rice, poha, murmura | Hydrothermal starch gelatinization | Steam pressure, 1–1.5 kg/cm² |
| **Flaking** | Cornflakes, wheat flakes | Cooking + rolling + toasting | Flake thickness, 0.3–0.5 mm |
| **Puffing** | Popcorn, puffed rice | Sudden decompression | Pressure differential, ≥8 atm |
| **Extrusion** | Noodles, snack pellets | HTST cooking + shear | SME, 100–400 kJ/kg |
| **Baking** | Bread, biscuits, cookies | Maillard + gelatinization | Oven temperature, 180–230°C |
| **Malting** | Malt for brewing | Controlled germination | Steeping, 30°C, 48 h; kilning, 80°C |
| **Fermentation** | Idli, bread, beer | Microbial conversion | pH, temperature, inoculum rate |
| **Drum Drying** | Pre-gelatinized starch | Thin-film cooking | Drum speed, steam pressure |

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A wheat milling industry processes **$5000\ kg$** of cleaned wheat per day and recovers **$3650\ kg$** of refined flour. Calculate the **extraction rate** and **amount of by-products** (bran + germ).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the extraction rate formula:

$$
E = \dfrac{W_{flour}}{W_{wheat}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
E = \dfrac{3650}{5000} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.73 \times 100 \\[4pt]
E &= 73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** By-product mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{by\text{-}product} &= W_{wheat} - W_{flour} \\[4pt]
&= 5000 - 3650 \\[4pt]
&= 1350\ kg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: This is a **straight-grade flour** ($E = 73\%$) commonly used in Indian bakeries. The bran (about $1100\ kg$) is sold as cattle feed at approximately **₹15-20/kg**, adding secondary revenue.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define value-added products from cereals with three suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**
Value-added products from cereals are processed food items derived from cereal grains through primary and secondary processing, which enhance their nutritional quality, shelf life, convenience, and market price beyond the raw grain. Examples:
1. **Breakfast cereals** (cornflakes, oats) — produced by cooking, flaking, and toasting maize or wheat.
2. **Pasta products** (macaroni, spaghetti, vermicelli) — produced by extrusion of durum wheat semolina.
3. **Malted beverages** (e.g., Horlicks, beer) — produced by controlled germination of barley/wheat.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Briefly explain the parboiling of paddy and its advantages."**

**Model Answer:**
Parboiling is a hydrothermal treatment of paddy involving three steps: **soaking** in hot water (60–70 °C for 4–8 h), **steaming** under pressure (1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 15–30 min), and **drying** to safe moisture (~14%).

**Advantages:**
- **Improves nutritional value** by migrating B-vitamins and minerals from bran to endosperm.
- **Reduces breakage** during milling by hardening the kernel.
- **Enhances shelf life** due to deactivation of lipase enzyme.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete manufacturing process of bread** with a neat flow diagram and the role of each ingredient. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **production of cornflakes** with the principles involved in cooking, flaking, and toasting operations. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Bread Manufacturing Process:**

**Ingredients and their roles:**
- **Refined wheat flour (Bread flour, 100 parts):** Provides gluten (gliadin + glutenin) for viscoelastic dough.
- **Water (60–65% of flour weight):** Hydrates gluten and starch.
- **Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, 2–3%):** Ferments sugar into CO₂ and ethanol, causing dough leavening.
- **Salt (1.5–2%):** Controls fermentation rate, strengthens gluten.
- **Sugar (5–8%):** Food for yeast; contributes to browning via Maillard reaction.
- **Fat/Shortening (2–3%):** Tenderizes crumb and extends shelf-life.
- **Optional additives:** Emulsifiers, oxidizing agents, milk powder, preservatives.

**Process steps:**

$$
\text{Ingredient Scaling} \rightarrow \text{Mixing (8-15 min)} \rightarrow \text{Bulk Fermentation (3-4 h)} \rightarrow \text{Dividing} \rightarrow \text{Rounding} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Proof (10-15 min)} \rightarrow \text{Moulding} \rightarrow \text{Final Proof (45-60 min at 35-38 °C, RH 85%)} \rightarrow \text{Baking (220-230 °C for 25-35 min)} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Slicing} \rightarrow \text{Packaging}
$$

**Key reactions during baking:**
- **Oven spring** (initial expansion due to CO₂ release).
- **Starch gelatinization** at 65–80 °C.
- **Protein denaturation** at 75–85 °C.
- **Maillard browning** at >140 °C (crust formation).

---

**(b) Production of Cornflakes:**

**Raw material:** Degermed yellow maize grits or whole maize.

**Process flow:**

$$
\text{Maize Grits} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Cooking (Steam at 95-100 °C for 1.5-2 h)} \rightarrow \text{Tempering (Moisture 30-35%)} \rightarrow \text{Flaking (Roller gap 0.3-0.5 mm)} \rightarrow \text{Toasting (300 °C, 1-2 min)} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Coating (Sugar, Malt, Vitamins)} \rightarrow \text{Packaging}
$$

**Principles:**

1. **Cooking Stage:** Hydrothermal treatment causes **starch gelatinization** (irreversible swelling) and partial **dextrinization**, producing soft, deformable grits.
2. **Flaking Stage:** Compression between twin rollers produces thin flakes with uniform thickness.
3. **Toasting Stage:** Rapid high-temperature heat removes moisture to 1–3% and produces **crispness**, **golden-brown color** (Maillard reaction), and **characteristic aroma**.

**Quality parameters:** Flake density $\mathbf{0.1-0.3\ g/cm^3}$, expansion ratio $\mathbf{6-10:1}$, bulk density $\mathbf{40-80\ g/L}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe in detail the **processing of pasta** including raw material selection, extrusion, and drying. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **malting process** for barley and its application in the brewing industry. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Pasta Processing:**

**Raw material:** Durum wheat (*Triticum durum*) semolina — preferred due to:
- **High yellow pigment** (carotenoids) → desirable yellow color.
- **Strong gluten network** → resists disintegration during cooking.
- **Hard endosperm** → produces coarse semolina granules.

**Process:**

$$
\text{Semolina} \rightarrow \text{Hydration (28-32% water)} \rightarrow \text{Mixing (Vacuum, 15-20 min)} \rightarrow \text{Extrusion (Pressure 80-120 bar, Temperature 40-55 °C)} \rightarrow \text{Cutting} \rightarrow \text{Predrying (50-60 °C, 30 min)} \rightarrow \text{Final Drying (60-80 °C, 4-8 h)} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Packaging}
$$

**Critical factors:**
- **Vacuum extrusion** removes air bubbles → enhances translucency.
- **Low-temperature extrusion** (<60 °C) prevents starch gelatinization → preserves cooking quality (al dente).
- **Slow drying** prevents surface cracking (checking).

---

**(b) Malting of Barley for Brewing:**

**Definition:** Malting is the controlled germination of barley to activate hydrolytic enzymes (α-amylase, β-amylase, proteases, β-glucanases) that degrade starch and proteins into fermentable sugars and amino acids.

**Three stages:**

1. **Steeping (30–50 h at 12–18 °C):** Barley moisture raised from 12% to **42–46%**. Periodic air rests prevent anaerobic damage.

2. **Germination (4–6 days at 15–20 °C):** Acrospire grows to **3/4 of kernel length**. Major biochemical changes:
   - **Starch hydrolysis** by amylases → maltose, maltotriose, dextrins.
   - **Protein hydrolysis** by proteases → free amino nitrogen (FAN).
   - **Cell wall breakdown** by β-glucanases → reduced viscosity.

3. **Kilning (24–36 h, gradient from 50 to 85 °C):** Stops germination. Higher temperatures produce **darker malt** (Munich, chocolate malt); lower temperatures produce **pale malt**.

**Application in Brewing:**

$$
\text{Malt} \xrightarrow{\text{Milling}} \text{Grist} \xrightarrow{\text{Mashing (65-72 °C)}} \text{Wort} \xrightarrow{\text{Boiling + Hops}} \text{Boiled Wort} \xrightarrow{\text{Fermentation with Yeast}} \text{Beer}
$$

**Quality parameter:** **Diastatic Power (DP)** of malt = **30-80 °Lintner units**, indicating enzymatic strength.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in this Topic (KTU Board Examiners):**
>
> 1. **Confusing breakfast cereal types:** Students often write "cornflakes" and "wheat flakes" interchangeably. Cornflakes are from **maize**, while wheat flakes (e.g., Wheaties) are from **whole wheat**. Wrong cereal = **−2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the role of yeast:** In bread questions, many students omit that yeast is a *living microorganism* and write only "fermentation agent." Examiner expects mention of ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** and biochemical reaction:
> $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{Energy}$$
>
> 3. **Missing temperatures and times in process flows:** KTU examiners award marks for **specific process parameters** (e.g., baking at 220–230 °C, extrusion at 120–200 °C). Generic statements like "bake the bread" lose **2-3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture:** Always clarify which basis is used. **Wet basis** is the industry standard for finished product labeling.
>
> 5. **Ignoring gluten quality:** In pasta and bread questions, students often forget to explain the **gliadin-glutenin** network responsible for dough viscoelasticity.
>
> 6. **Incomplete drying curve:** For pasta/biscuit questions, mention all three drying stages: **constant rate**, **falling rate**, and **equilibrium moisture**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Value Added Products from Cereals"**

- **Value addition** = transforming raw grains into processed products with higher economic, nutritional, and convenience value.
- **Primary processing** = cleaning, conditioning, dehusking, milling, malting.
- **Secondary processing** = baking, extrusion, flaking, puffing, fermentation, popping.
- **Gelatinization temperature** of starch ranges from **55–80 °C** depending on cereal type.
- **Safe moisture limit** for cereal storage = **≤14%** (wet basis).
- **Bread process:** Mix → Ferment → Proof → Bake (using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*).
- **Pasta process:** Semolina → Vacuum Extrusion → Slow Drying (durum wheat preferred).
- **Cornflakes:** Cook → Temper → Flake → Toast at **300 °C**.
- **Malting stages:** Steeping (42–46% moisture) → Germination (4–6 days) → Kilning (50–85 °C).
- **Extrusion SME:** **100–400 kJ/kg** produces expanded, low-density products.
- **Parboiling** improves nutritional retention and milling yield of paddy.
- **Functional properties** (WAI, WSI, viscosity) govern end-use of cereal flours.
- **Codex standard for moisture in breakfast cereals:** ≤**12%**.
- **Gluten proteins:** Gliadin (viscosity) + Glutenin (elasticity) → viscoelastic dough.
- **Fermentation reaction:** Glucose → 2 Ethanol + 2 CO₂ + Energy (ATP).
- **Puffed rice conversion ratio:** typically **6–10:1**.
- **Beer is brewed from malted barley; whiskey from malted cereals (barley, rye, maize).**
- **Baking loss for bread:** **10–15%**; for biscuits: **5–8%**.
- **Twin-screw extruders** dominate modern snack food industry.
- **Beta-glucan from oats** is a functional ingredient lowering blood cholesterol.
- **Resistant starch (RS)** in cooled cooked rice acts as prebiotic fiber.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of Legumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Processing of Legumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Legumes** are the dry, edible seeds of plants belonging to the family *Fabaceae* (Leguminosae). They are characterized by a seed structure consisting of three major components: the **seed coat (husk)**, the **cotyledons** (storage tissue rich in protein and starch), and the **embryonic axis (germ)**. The term *processing* in the context of legumes refers to the systematic sequence of mechanical, thermal, enzymatic, and biochemical operations applied to harvested legume seeds to convert them into edible, palatable, shelf-stable, and value-added food products.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, *Processing of Legumes* encompasses the entire post-harvest value chain — from raw grain reception to the manufacture of finished products such as **dals (split pulses), flours, protein isolates, fermented foods, and extruded snacks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The legume processing industry in India is dominated by the conversion of whole pulses (chana, tur, moong, urad, masoor) into **dehusked and split dal**. The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) specification **IS 14715 (1999)** governs the grading of milled pulses.

## 1.2 Classification of Legume Seeds Commonly Processed in India

| Botanical Name | Common Name | Type | Major Use After Processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Cicer arietinum* | Bengal gram (Chana) | Gram | Dal, besan, snacks |
| *Cajanus cajan* | Red gram (Tur/Arhar) | Lentil | Toor dal, sambar |
| *Vigna radiata* | Green gram (Moong) | Bean | Moong dal, sprouts |
| *Vigna mungo* | Black gram (Urad) | Bean | Urad dal, idli/dosa |
| *Lens culinaris* | Lentil (Masoor) | Lentil | Masoor dal |
| *Pisum sativum* | Field pea (Matar) | Pea | Yellow/green peas |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Layer Sandwich" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Lentil Burger:**
> Imagine a legume seed as a tiny **hamburger**. The top bun is the **seed coat (husk)** — tough, fibrous, and indigestible for humans. The patty in the middle is the **cotyledon** — soft, rich in protein and starch, and this is the part we actually want to eat. A thin sesame seed in the center is the **embryo (germ)**. The entire art of legume processing is simply **removing the top bun** (dehulling) and **cutting the patty in half** (splitting) to expose the nutritious interior — while keeping the patty intact and unbroken.

## 1.4 Why Process Legumes? — The Five Pillars

1. **Reduction of Anti-nutritional Factors (ANFs):** Raw legumes contain **trypsin inhibitors, phytic acid, lectins, tannins, and oligosaccharides** (raffinose, stachyose) responsible for flatulence. Processing inactivates them.
2. **Improvement of Digestibility:** Dehulling, cooking, and fermentation increase protein digestibility from **~50% to over 85%**.
3. **Enhancement of Shelf Life:** Moisture reduction to **<12% w.b.** prevents fungal and insect infestation.
4. **Consumer Acceptability:** Color, texture, cooking time, and taste are all improved.
5. **Value Addition & Diversification:** Conversion of cheap raw pulses into high-value protein concentrates, flours, and convenience foods.

## 1.5 Standard Quality Metrics Used in Legume Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Parameters:**
> - **Moisture content of safe storage:** **≤ 12% (wet basis)**
> - **Standard dhal yield (tur, chana):** **70–75%** of cleaned whole grain
> - **Dehulling efficiency (target):** **≥ 90%**
> - **Brokens permissible in Grade I dal:** **≤ 2% (by mass)**
> - **Uric acid (insect/microbial indicator) limit:** **< 100 mg/kg** as per FSSAI
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operations in Primary Processing of Legumes — The Sequential Logic

### 2.1.1 Reception and Pre-cleaning

Whole pulse grains arrive at the processing facility with impurities such as **dust, chaff, stones, mud clods, weed seeds, insect fragments, and other grains**. Reception involves weighing, sampling, and preliminary aspirating.

**Why?** Contaminants damage downstream equipment (especially rollers) and reduce product purity.

### 2.1.2 Cleaning

Performed using a combination of:
- **Vibratory sieves** — separate by size.
- **Air classifiers (aspirators)** — separate by density using terminal velocity $v_t$.
- **Magnetic separators** — remove ferrous fragments.
- **Specific gravity separators (destoners)** — exploit density difference between grain ($\rho \approx 1.3$ g/cm³) and stones ($\rho \approx 2.6$ g/cm³).
- **Disk separators / indented cylinders** — separate by length.
- **Electronic color sorters** — remove discolored/disinfected seeds.

**How?** Each unit exploits a *single physical property difference* between grain and contaminant.

### 2.1.3 Conditioning (Tempering)

This is the **most critical step** in legume processing. It involves controlled addition of moisture (typically **2–5% by weight**), followed by a **tempering (equilibration) period of 2–24 hours** depending on the legume.

**Why?** The seed coat and cotyledon have different moisture absorption rates. Uniform moisture makes the **husk brittle** (easy to remove) while keeping the **cotyledon tough** (resists breakage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The principle behind conditioning is differential moisture absorption — *hygroscopic expansion of the husk vs. the cotyledon creates a microscopic cleavage plane*, enabling clean separation.

### 2.1.4 Dehulling (Dehusking)

Removal of the seed coat. Methods used:
1. **Roller / Cylindrical dehullers** — most common. Grain passes between an abrasive roller and a rubber/screen housing; friction abrades the husk.
2. **Under-runner disc dehullers** — used in traditional Indian dal mills.
3. **Impact dehullers** — centrifugal force flings grains against a stationary surface.
4. **Tangential Abrasive Dehulling Device (TADD)** — lab/pilot scale.
5. **Enzymatic dehulling** — using pectinases/cellulases (emerging technology).

**Dehulling Efficiency (DHE)** is the operative KPI:

$$
\text{DHE} \;(\%) = \frac{\text{Mass of seed coats removed}}{\text{Total mass of seed coats in sample}} \times 100
$$

### 2.1.5 Splitting (Dhal Cutting)

Cleaned, dehusked cotyledons are split along the **natural cleavage plane** using either:
- **Roller splitter (dal cutter)** — slow rotation, sharp blade splits cotyledon.
- **Disc-type splitter** — centrifugal throw against a knife.

> [!NOTE]
> Splitting increases surface area, reduces cooking time by **~40%**, and accelerates water absorption.

### 2.1.6 Polishing / Oiling

The split dal is passed through a **polishing cone** where a fine mist of **edible oil (1–2%)** is sprayed. This gives a glossy appearance, removes fine bran particles, and improves keeping quality by reducing dust adhesion.

### 2.1.7 Grading and Packaging

Polished dal is graded by size using **rotary graders or vibratory sieves** into *Grade I, II, III* as per IS 14715. Packaging in **laminated pouches (PP/PE, 25–50 µm)** with moisture barrier extends shelf life beyond **6 months**.

## 2.2 Secondary Processing — Conversion to Derived Products

| Product | Raw Material | Key Operations | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Besan (gram flour)** | Chana dal | Dry milling → hammer mill → sieving | Pakoras, sweets, thickening |
| **Pulse flour** | Any dal | Roller milling, air classification | Bakery, infant food, pasta |
| **Protein concentrate (PC)** | Defatted pulse flour | Acid/alkali extraction → isoelectric ppt. | Meat extenders, beverages |
| **Protein isolate (PI)** | Pulse flour | Alkali solubilization + acid ppt. | Sport nutrition, infant formula |
| **Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP)** | Defatted flour | High-moisture extrusion | Meat analog, ready-to-eat meals |
| **Fermented batter (Idli/Dosa)** | Urad + rice | Soaking → wet grinding → natural fermentation | Traditional breakfast |

## 2.3 Advanced / Value-Added Processing Technologies

### 2.3.1 Extrusion Cooking

A **single-screw or twin-screw extruder** combines high temperature (**120–180°C**), high pressure (**20–40 bar**), and high shear. The starch gelatinizes, proteins denature, and the sudden pressure drop at the die causes **puffing**.

**Why?** Extrusion simultaneously **cooks, shapes, and texturizes** the product in one continuous step.

### 2.3.2 Germination / Sprouting

Soaking (8–12 h) followed by 24–48 h germination increases:
- **Vitamin C** from trace to **~25 mg/100g**
- **Free amino acids** by 2–3 fold
- **B-vitamin** content

### 2.3.3 Fermentation

Solid-state or submerged fermentation with *Lactobacillus*, *Rhizopus*, or natural microflora:
- Reduces phytic acid by **30–50%**
- Reduces oligosaccharides by **>60%**
- Improves protein digestibility

### 2.3.4 Microwave and Infrared Processing

Emerging technology for **rapid hydration and tempering**. Microwave tempering at **2450 MHz** reduces conditioning time from hours to **5–15 minutes** for some legumes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | All grains |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying calculations |
| 3 | Dhal Yield (DY) | $DY = \dfrac{\text{Mass of finished dhal}}{\text{Mass of cleaned whole grain}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 4 | Dehulling Efficiency (DHE) | $DHE = \dfrac{\text{Husks removed}}{\text{Total husks in sample}} \times 100$ | % | Dehuller performance |
| 5 | Protein Dispersibility Index (PDI) | $PDI = \dfrac{\text{Protein in water soln.}}{\text{Total protein in sample}} \times 100$ | % | Heat damage check |
| 6 | Water Absorption Index (WAI) | $WAI = \dfrac{\text{Weight of sediment gel}}{\text{Original dry weight of sample}}$ | g/g | Starch functionality |
| 7 | Water Solubility Index (WSI) | $WSI = \dfrac{\text{Weight of solutes in supernatant}}{\text{Original dry weight of sample}} \times 100$ | % | Soluble components |
| 8 | Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER) | $PER = \dfrac{\text{Weight gain of rat (g)}}{\text{Protein consumed (g)}}$ | dimensionless | Nutritional quality |
| 9 | Terminal velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | m/s | Aspirator design |
| 10 | Husking coefficient | $H_c = \dfrac{\text{Unbroken cotyledons after dehulling}}{\text{Total kernels passed}} \times 100$ | % | Trade-off KPI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** Always use **SI base units (kg, m, s, °C, Pa)** in derivations. Convert empirical data to SI before substituting.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solvent extraction plants** (n-hexane for defatted pulse flour) use the principle of **Fick's first law of diffusion**: $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ for designing percolation columns.
- **Aspirator design** for dust removal uses **Stokes' Law** for terminal velocity of dust particles.
- **Dal mill economics:** In India, ~**2,000 large-scale dal mills** process **>18 million tonnes** of pulses annually. A 10% improvement in dhal yield translates to savings of **~₹3,000 crore/year** industry-wide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Dhal Yield, Dehulling Efficiency, and Brokens

This is a **frequently tested KTU problem** because it directly quantifies process efficiency.

**Given:**
- Mass of cleaned whole grain fed: $M_0$ (kg)
- Dehulling efficiency: $\eta$ (decimal, e.g., 0.92)
- Mass fraction of seed coat in whole grain: $f_h$ (typically 0.10–0.14 for tur/chana)
- Mass fraction of brokens generated during processing: $f_b$ (typically 0.02–0.05)
- Moisture added during conditioning: $M_c$ (kg water/kg grain)

**To Derive:** Dhal yield $DY$ in percentage.

**Assumption:** All cotyledon mass is preserved as either whole dhal or brokens; only the husk is removed.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on whole grain:**

The cleaned grain consists of husk + cotyledon. Therefore:

$$
M_0 = M_h + M_c^{cot}
$$

where $M_h = f_h \cdot M_0$ is the husk mass and $M_c^{cot} = (1 - f_h) \cdot M_0$ is the cotyledon mass.

**Step 2 — Mass of husk actually removed:**

If dehulling efficiency is $\eta$ (fraction of total husk successfully removed), then:

$$
M_{h, \, \text{removed}} = \eta \cdot M_h = \eta \, f_h \, M_0
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of cotyledon recovered as intact dhal:**
