| **Ajugose** | Hexasaccharide | C₃₆H₆₂O₃₁ | 990.87 | (Gal)₄-Glc-Fru |

- Family name: **Raffinose Family Oligosaccharides (RFOs)** or **α-galactosides**.
- Derived from **sucrose + galactose units** via α-1,6 galactosyl transferase.

**2. Source Distribution (1 mark):**
- **Soybean** (highest) — 5–8 % stachyose + 1–2 % raffinose of dry weight.
- **Chickpea, cowpea, lentil, mung bean, kidney bean, pigeon pea, field pea** — varying amounts.
- Located in **cotyledonary parenchyma cells** (cytosol and protein bodies).

**3. Mechanism of Flatulence (1.5 marks):**
- Human small intestine **lacks α-galactosidase** enzyme.
- RFOs reach the colon undigested.
- Gut microflora (*Bacteroides, Clostridium, Eubacterium, Enterococcus*) ferment RFOs anaerobically.
- Fermentation products: **CO₂, H₂, CH₄, SCFAs** (acetate, propionate, butyrate).
- Excess gas → abdominal bloating, flatulence, cramps, diarrhea.
- Prebiotic benefit: SCFAs support colonocyte health (bifidogenic effect).

**4. Health Impact (0.5 mark):**
- Major contributor to social/compliance issues in pulse consumption.
- Important for protein-energy malnutrition mitigation programs (e.g., UNICEF pulse-based weaning foods).
- Limits consumer acceptance of bean-rich diets in Western countries.

**5. Processing Methods to Reduce RFOs (1.5 marks):**
- **Soaking (12 h) + discard water:** 40–60 % reduction (highly water-soluble).
- **Germination (24–48 h):** 70–90 % reduction (activates endogenous α-galactosidase).
- **Fermentation:** 80–95 % reduction (microbial α-galactosidase — *Lactobacillus*, *Rhizopus*).
- **Cooking in excess water + discard:** 30–50 % reduction.
- **Enzymatic treatment** with commercial α-galactosidase (e.g., Beano®): > 95 % reduction.
- **Genetic:** Low-RFO cultivars of soybean, chickpea under development.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD QUICK-RECALL BOX — Memorize for KTU Exams**

- [x] **Definition:** ANFs = non-nutrient, biologically active, plant-defense compounds that interfere with nutrient utilization.
- [x] **Proteinaceous ANFs:** Trypsin Inhibitors (Kunitz, Bowman-Birk) and Lectins (Phytohemagglutinins) — both **heat-labile**.
- [x] **Trypsin Inhibitor safe limit:** < 5 % of raw TIA (WHO/FAO).
- [x] **Lectins destroyed by:** boiling ≥ 10 min at 100 °C. 4–5 raw kidney beans are toxic.
- [x] **Phytic acid (InsP₆):** MW 660.04 g/mol; chelates Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Ca²⁺; PA/Fe > 10 → severe Fe inhibition; heat-stable, water-soluble.
- [x] **Phytate reduction:** Germination > Fermentation > Phytase > Soaking. Cooking alone is poor.
- [x] **Tannins:** Polymeric flavan-3-ols; bind proteins (proline-rich) and minerals; located in **seed coat (testa)** — **dehulling** removes 60–85 %.
- [x] **Lathyrus sativus toxin:** BOAA / β-ODAP (MW 176.13); AMPA receptor agonist; irreversible spastic paraparesis.
- [x] **Favism:** Vicia faba (broad bean) → Vicine/Convicine → G6PD-deficient individuals → hemolytic anemia; X-linked recessive.
- [x] **RFOs (Raffinose Family Oligosaccharides):** Raffinose (504), Stachyose (666), Verbascose (828), Ajugose (990); flatulence due to colonic fermentation; absent α-galactosidase in humans.
- [x] **Saponins:** Amphiphilic triterpenoid/steroid glycosides; bitter taste; oral safe despite hemolytic IV toxicity.
- [x] **Cyanogenic glycosides:** Linamarin, Lotaustralin (lima bean); release HCN on hydrolysis; HCN blocks cytochrome c oxidase.
- [x] **Alkaloids:** Quinolizidine (lupin) and Pyrrolizidine (Crotalaria) — hepatotoxic and neurotoxic.
- [x] **Allergens:** 2S albumins, 7S/11S globulins — Pulses rank 4th–5th globally in food allergy prevalence.
- [x] **Effective processing hierarchy:** **Autoclaving > Fermentation > Germination > Dehulling > Soaking > Cooking alone.**
- [x] **Best Indian household method to reduce multiple ANFs together:** Overnight soaking + discard water + pressure cooking ≥ 15 min + discarding excess water.
- [x] **Food safety benchmark to always quote:** TIA < 5 % of raw, Phytate < 1 % of dry basis, BOAA < 0.1 % in Lathyrus flour.
- [x] **Codex/FSSAI:** Besan shall not contain Lathyrus sativus flour; soy milk must be boiled ≥ 20 min.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5 — KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes Complete. Best of luck for your ESE preparation!**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Milling of Pulse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Commercial Milling of Pulse ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Commercial Milling of Pulses** is the industrial-scale, mechanized process of transforming whole dried pulse grains (legume seeds such as chickpea, pigeon pea, green gram, black gram, lentil) into edible, dehusked, split, and polished products commonly termed **"dhal"** (split pulse) or **"whole dhal"** (dehusked intact cotyledons). The operation encompasses a sequential chain of **pre-conditioning, dehusking, splitting, polishing, and grading** unit operations, designed to maximize edible yield (typically **70–75 %**), minimize nutritional losses, and meet food-grade quality standards laid down by **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – OEFTT836 Module 4):** > Commercial milling specifically refers to **large-capacity, continuous-flow, automated milling plants** (throughput ranging from **1 to 50 tonnes/day**) as opposed to traditional village-level chakki or hand-pounding operations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Layer Sandwich" Model Imagine a pulse grain as a **three-layer sandwich**: - The **outer bread slice** = **Husk (Seed Coat / Testa)** — inedible, fibrous, contains antinutrients. - The **middle cheese slice** = **Hyaline layer + Cotyledon Junction** — a thin gummy layer that glues the husk to the cotyledon. - The **inner bread slice** = **Cotyledon (the edible dhal)** — packed with protein and starch. **Commercial milling = peeling off the outer slice without squashing the inner slice.** In real terms, this means loosening the husk (using water/oil conditioning), scraping it off (abrasive/attrition), then splitting the cotyledon into two halves (splitter rollers), and finally polishing to a glossy finish (emulsion polishers with water + oil). > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric — Dhal Recovery:** > Industry benchmark is **≥ 72 %** for chana (Bengal gram) and **≥ 70 %** for arhar (pigeon pea). Anything below **65 %** is considered economically unviable. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Quality Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Optimum moisture for milling | **9–11 % (w.b.)** | | Conditioning water addition | **2–5 % by weight** | | Conditioning resting time | **8–48 hours** | | Oil coating (for arhar) | **0.2–0.5 % by weight** | | Target dhal yield | **70–75 %** | | Acceptable broken fraction | **< 5 %** | | Acceptable powder loss | **< 2 %** | | Throughput range | **1–50 tonnes/day** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Sequential Unit Operations of Commercial Pulse Milling A modern pulse mill is a **continuous flow plant** with the following logical sequence: #### **Step 1 — Pre-Cleaning (Receiving Section)** - **Why:** Remove foreign matter (stones, chaff, dust, ferrous particles, other seeds). - **How:** Series of **scalper sieves**, **air aspiration columns**, **magnetic separators**, and **gravity separators (destoner)**. - **Output:** Pure pulse with foreign matter ≤ **1 %**. #### **Step 2 — Grading by Size** - **Why:** Uniform grain size → uniform conditioning → uniform dehusking. - **How:** **Indented cylinder separators** or **vibratory graders** split the lot into 2–3 size fractions. - **Key point:** Mixed-size batches cause **under-dehusked** (too large) and **over-broken** (too small) losses. #### **Step 3 — Conditioning / Tempering** - **Why:** Loosen husk-cotyledon bond by controlled moisture penetration into the hyaline layer. - **How:** Add **2–5 % water** (sometimes blended with **0.5 % oil** for arhar pigeon pea), then hold in **conditioning bins** for **12–48 hours**. - **Scientific basis:** Differential swelling — the husk absorbs moisture faster than the cotyledon, creating a **shear plane** at the interface. #### **Step 4 — Dehusking (The Core Operation)** - **Why:** Strip away the seed coat. - **How:** Three principal machine types: | Machine Type | Working Principle | Best For | |---|---|---| | **Abrasive Roller (Rubber/Emery)** | Friction between rotating abrasive and grain | Chana, moong, urad | | **Attrition Mill (Stone/Disc)** | Impact + shearing between two rough surfaces | Arhar, lentil | | **Centrifugal Dehusker** | High-speed impact against rubber liner | Hard-coated pulses | - Each pass dehusks only **60–80 %** of grains → typically **2–4 passes** with intermediate aspiration. #### **Step 5 — Splitting (Optional but Standard)** - **Why:** Dhal in Indian market = split cotyledons (the iconic *chhilka*-free split). - **How:** **Splitter rollers** with differential speed (1 : 1.25) gently split the dehusked cotyledon along the **radial axis** without grinding. - **Alternative:** Some mills skip splitting and market **"whole dhal"** for premium export. #### **Step 6 — Polishing / Whitening** - **Why:** Improve visual appeal (pearly white luster), remove bran specks. - **How:** **Emulsion polishers** (water + oil + stearic acid sprayed onto rotating polisher chamber walls). - **Output:** Glossy dhal with < 1 % husk specks. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Deeper Rationale | Question | Engineering Answer | |---|---| | Why precondition before dehusking? | Brittle husk ≠ easy to peel. Moisture makes husk **plastic** and creates a **shear plane**. | | Why use oil in conditioning (arhar)? | Oil penetrates the **hyaline layer**, making it slippery so the husk slides off rather than tearing the cotyledon. | | Why multiple dehusking passes? | A single pass → low efficiency; many passes → high breakage. Optimal is **2–3 passes with inter-stage aspiration**. | | Why polish? | Unpolished dhal has **bran streaks**, lower consumer acceptance, shorter shelf life (lipase activity in bran). | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle in numerical problems. | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{dhal}}{M_{raw}} \times 100$ | Dhal Recovery Efficiency | **%** | | 2 | $\eta_{cleaning} = \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{FM}}{M_{input}}\right) \times 100$ | Cleaning Efficiency (FM = foreign matter) | **%** | | 3 | $\eta_{dehusking} = \dfrac{M_{dehusked}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | Dehusking Efficiency | **%** | | 4 | $\text{Broken \%} = \dfrac{M_{broken}}{M_{total\ dhal}} \times 100$ | Broken Fraction | **%** | | 5 | $\text{Powder Loss \%} = \dfrac{M_{powder}}{M_{raw}} \times 100$ | Milling Loss via Powder | **%** | | 6 | $M_{final} = M_{initial} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{L}{100}\right)$ | Mass Balance — Output after Loss % | **kg** | | 7 | $M_{water} = M_{grain} \times \left(\dfrac{MC_{final} - MC_{initial}}{100 - MC_{final}}\right)$ | Conditioning Water Required | **kg** | | 8 | $C_{plant} = \dfrac{M_{dhal}}{t_{operation}}$ | Plant Throughput Capacity | **kg/hr** | | 9 | $E_{overall} = \eta_{cleaning} \times \eta_{dehusking} \times \eta_{splitting} \times \eta_{polishing}$ | Overall Milling Efficiency | **dimensionless** | | 10 | $Y_{dhal} + Y_{husk} + Y_{powder} + Y_{broken} = 100$ | Mass Balance Closure | **%** | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic to remember formulas 1–5:** "**R-C-D-B-P**" → Recovery, Cleaning, Dehusking, Broken, Powder. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial scale:** Indian pulse milling industry processes **~23 million tonnes/year** (data from Ministry of Food Processing Industries). - **Economic impact:** Each **1 %** increase in dhal recovery = **₹2,300/tonne** extra revenue (at ₹70/kg wholesale dhal price). - **Export-grade quality:** Polishing, sorting by color sorter, and grading make Indian dhal exportable to Gulf, EU, North American markets under strict **BIS 6844** standards. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Dhal Recovery from Mass Balance **Problem Context:** A commercial pulse mill receives **1000 kg** of raw arhar (pigeon pea). After processing, the measured outputs are: - Whole dehusked dhal: **540 kg** - Broken dhal: **120 kg** - Husk collected: **220 kg** - Fine powder loss: **40 kg** - Unaccounted loss (moisture, handling): **80 kg** **Step 1: Define the Recovery Equation** The dhal recovery (the actual edible output) includes both whole and broken splits: $$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{dhal,total}}{M_{raw,pulse}} \times 100$$ **Step 2: Sum the Edible Output Fractions** Both whole dhal and broken dhal are commercially saleable (broken at lower grade): $$M_{dhal,total} = M_{whole} + M_{broken}$$ $$M_{dhal,total} = 540\ \text{kg} + 120\ \text{kg} = 660\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the Recovery Formula** $$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{660\ \text{kg}}{1000\ \text{kg}} \times 100 = 66\ \%$$ **Step 4: Verify Mass Balance Closure** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Output} &= M_{whole} + M_{broken} + M_{husk} + M_{powder} + M_{unaccounted} \\ \text{Total Output} &= 540 + 120 + 220 + 40 + 80 \\ \text{Total Output} &= 1000\ \text{kg} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Mass Balance Verification:** The sum of all streams MUST equal input. If not, either measurement error or unmeasured loss (escaped dust, moisture evaporation) has occurred. **Step 5: Interpretation & Engineering Action** A recovery of **66 %** is **below the industry benchmark of 70 %** for arhar. The mill operator should investigate: - Is the **broken fraction (12 %)** too high? → Reduce splitter roller speed. - Is **powder loss (4 %)** excessive? → Check abrasive roller gap. - Is **unaccounted loss (8 %)** abnormal? → Audit aspiration cyclone collection. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Conditioning Water Requirement **Problem Context:** Raw chana (chickpea) enters conditioning at **MC = 9 % w.b.**, mass **M = 8000 kg**. Target moisture for optimal dehusking is **MC = 12 % w.b.** Calculate water to add. **Step 1: Recall the Conditioning Water Formula (Formula 7 from cheat sheet)** $$M_{water} = M_{grain} \times \left(\dfrac{MC_{final} - MC_{initial}}{100 - MC_{final}}\right)$$ **Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values** $$M_{water} = 8000\ \text{kg} \times \left(\dfrac{12 - 9}{100 - 12}\right)$$ **Step 3: Simplify the Fraction** $$M_{water} = 8000\ \text{kg} \times \left(\dfrac{3}{88}\right)$$ **Step 4: Perform Final Calculation** $$M_{water} = 8000 \times 0.03409 = 272.73\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 5: Practical Recommendation** $$\text{Water addition} \approx 273\ \text{kg} \approx 273\ \text{Liters of water}$$ This water should be added **gradually via a screw conveyor with spray nozzles** (avoid run-off), followed by a resting period of **12–24 hours** in an insulated conditioning bin. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Milling Methods | Parameter | **Traditional Pounding** | **Modern Dry Milling** | **CFTRI Method** | |---|---|---|---| | Throughput | **10–50 kg/hr** | **1–10 tonnes/hr** | **2–5 tonnes/hr** | | Dhal Recovery | **65–70 %** | **70–75 %** | **78–82 %** | | Broken Fraction | **8–12 %** | **4–6 %** | **2–4 %** | | Husk Removal Efficiency | **70 %** | **85–90 %** | **95–98 %** | | Power Consumption | Low (manual) | **Medium** | **High** | | Capital Cost | Negligible | **₹15–50 Lakhs** | **₹50 Lakhs–2 Cr** | | Hygiene Grade | Poor | Good | Excellent (FSSAI-grade) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** The **CFTRI (Central Food Technological Research Institute, Mysore)** method uses pre-conditioning with **water + lye/sodium carbonate (0.1–0.2 %)** followed by hot-air drying, achieving the **highest** dhal recovery in industry. --- ### 3.4 Derivation — Overall Plant Efficiency **Problem Context:** For a mill achieving the following sub-efficiencies: - Cleaning: **98 %** - Dehusking: **90 %** - Splitting: **95 %** - Polishing: **92 %** Calculate the **overall milling efficiency** using Formula 9: **Step 1: Write the Compound Efficiency Equation** $$E_{overall} = \eta_{cleaning} \times \eta_{dehusking} \times \eta_{splitting} \times \eta_{polishing}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the Fractional Values** $$\begin{aligned} E_{overall} &= 0.98 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \\ E_{overall} &= (0.98 \times 0.90) \times (0.95 \times 0.92) \\ E_{overall} &= 0.882 \times 0.874 \\ E_{overall} &= 0.7708 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Convert to Percentage** $$E_{overall} = 0.7708 \times 100 \approx 77.1\ \%$$ **Step 4: Engineering Insight** > [!NOTE] > Even with sub-efficiencies of **90 %+**, the compounded overall efficiency drops to **77 %**. This illustrates the **multiplicative penalty** of multi-stage processes — improving the **weakest link** (here, dehusking at 90 %) yields the largest payback. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Commercial Pulse Mill ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Pulse Intake
Weighbridge & Sampling] --> B[Pre-Cleaning Section
Scalper, Aspirator, Magnetic Separator] B --> C[Destoning Unit
Gravity Table] C --> D[Size Grading
Indented Cylinder Separator] D --> E[Conditioning Bins
Water plus Oil Addition
Rest 12 to 48 Hours] E --> F[Dehusking Stage 1
Abrasive Roller] F --> G[Inter-stage Aspiration
Husk Removal] G --> H[Dehusking Stage 2
Abrasive Roller] H --> I[Inter-stage Aspiration
Husk Removal] I --> J[Splitter Rollers
Differential Speed 1 to 1.25] J --> K[Polisher
Emulsion Spray Polish] K --> L[Color Sorter
Optical CCD Camera] L --> M[Final Grading
Vibratory Screens] M --> N[Bagging and Sealing
Weighing and Stitching] N --> O[Finished Dhal Storage
Warehouse Dispatch] P[Byproducts Handling
Husk to Boiler or Compost] -.-> G P -.-> I Q[Powder Collection
Dust Cyclone and Bag Filter] -.-> F Q -.-> J style A fill:#e1f5ff style O fill:#c8e6c9 style P fill:#fff9c4 style Q fill:#ffe0b2 ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Subsystem View — Conditioning + Dehusking Section ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SUB1[Conditioning Subsystem] dirA[Moisture Meter
Entry Point] --> dirB[Water Spray Screw
0 to 5 percent Addition] dirB --> dirC[Oil Spray Unit
0 to 0.5 percent for Arhar] dirC --> dirD[Conditioning Bin A
Rest 12 Hours] dirD --> dirE[Conditioning Bin B
Rest 24 Hours] dirE --> dirF[Moisture Meter
Exit Verification 9 to 12 percent] end subgraph SUB2[Dehusking Subsystem] dirF --> dirG[Abrasive Roller 1
Pass 1] dirG --> dirH[Aspiration Cyclone 1
Husk Separation] dirH --> dirI[Abrasive Roller 2
Pass 2] dirI --> dirJ[Aspiration Cyclone 2
Husk Separation] dirJ --> dirK{Visual Inspection
Chhilka Present?} dirK -->|Yes| dirL[Route to Pass 3
Optional] dirK -->|No| dirM[Forward to Splitter] dirL --> dirM end style SUB1 fill:#e3f2fd style SUB2 fill:#fff3e0 ``` ### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Concept — Abrasive Dehusking Roller ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Conditioned Pulse Grains
Enter via Hopper] --> B[Rotating Emery Roller
24 to 30 m per sec Surface Speed] B --> C[Stationary Rubber Liner
Creates Shearing Zone] C --> D[Friction and Impact Zone
Husk gets Abraded] D --> E[Dehusked Grain plus Loose Husk
Exit at Bottom] E --> F[Aspiration Duct
Light Husk Sucked Away] E --> G[Heavier Grain Falls
To Next Stage] style A fill:#bbdefb style B fill:#ffcc80 style C fill:#a5d6a7 style D fill:#ef9a9a style F fill:#fff59d style G fill:#c5e1a5 ``` ### 4.4 Mass Flow Sankey-Style Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR INP[1000 kg Raw Pulse
MC 9 percent] --> CLEAN[Pre-Cleaned
985 kg] CLEAN --> COND[Conditioned
1020 kg after water] COND --> DEHUSK[Dehusked Mix
990 kg after husk] DEHUSK --> SPLIT[Split Dhal
720 kg] SPLIT --> POLISH[Polished Dhal
715 kg] POLISH --> SORT[Sorted Graded
710 kg Final] CLEAN -->|15 kg FM| WASTE1[Foreign Matter] COND -->|35 kg moisture| WASTE2[No Loss Just Added] DEHUSK -->|220 kg| HUSK[Husk Byproduct] SPLIT -->|120 kg| BROKEN[Broken Dhal
Lower Grade Sale] POLISH -->|5 kg| BRAN[Polishing Dust] SORT -->|5 kg| DISCOLORED[Color Reject] style INP fill:#e1f5ff style SORT fill:#c8e6c9 style HUSK fill:#fff9c4 style BROKEN fill:#ffe0b2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "dhal recovery" as applied to commercial pulse milling. What is the industry benchmark recovery percentage for chana (Bengal gram)?** **Model Answer:** **Dhal Recovery** is defined as the ratio of the mass of cleaned, edible dhal obtained (whole split + broken) to the mass of raw pulse input, expressed as a percentage. $$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{edible\ dhal}}{M_{raw\ pulse}} \times 100\ \%$$ The **industry benchmark** for **chana (Bengal gram)** is **≥ 72 %** as per standard mill performance norms. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark benchmark value)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any four unit operations involved in commercial pulse milling in their correct sequence.** **Model Answer (4 unit operations):** 1. **Pre-cleaning** — removal of stones, dust, chaff using sieves and air aspiration. 2. **Conditioning / Tempering** — controlled addition of 2–5 % water followed by resting for 12–48 hours. 3. **Dehusking** — abrasive/attrition removal of seed coat in 2–3 passes with inter-stage aspiration. 4. **Polishing** — emulsion (water + oil + stearic acid) treatment for glossy finish and bran removal. *(3 marks: ½ mark each correct operation + ½ mark each correct sequence position)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat flow diagram the **CFTRI method of pulse milling**. Discuss its advantages over the conventional dry milling method. **(b) [7 Marks]** During a milling trial, **2000 kg** of raw arhar was processed. The outputs were: whole dhal **1080 kg**, broken dhal **240 kg**, husk **420 kg**, powder **80 kg**, and unaccounted loss **180 kg**. Calculate the **dhal recovery percentage** and comment on the **economic viability** of the operation. **Model Solution — Part (a):** The **CFTRI (Central Food Technological Research Institute, Mysuru) method** is a scientific, high-recovery process developed in the 1970s. The flow is: 1. **Cleaning & Grading** — raw pulses cleaned and separated by size. 2. **Chemical Pretreatment** — grains dipped in **0.1–0.2 % sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) solution** for 3–6 minutes. 3. **Hot Air Drying** — pretreated grains dried at **120–140 °C** for 30–60 minutes to bring moisture down to **8–10 %**. 4. **Tempering** — held for **2–4 hours** for moisture equalization (stress-relaxation between husk and cotyledon). 5. **Dehusking & Splitting** — single-pass through **roller dehusker + splitter**. 6. **Aspiration & Polishing** — husk removed; dhal polished using **emulsion treatment**. **Advantages over Conventional Dry Milling:** | Aspect | Conventional | CFTRI | |---|---|---| | Recovery | 70–75 % | **78–82 %** | | Passes required | 2–3 | **Single pass** | | Broken % | 4–6 % | **2–4 %** | | Energy per tonne | Higher | **Lower (net)** | | Lye residue | None | Traces — within FSSAI limit | *(7 marks: 3 marks flow, 2 marks lye + drying mechanism, 2 marks comparison table)* **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1: Total edible dhal** $$M_{dhal,total} = 1080 + 240 = 1320\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 2: Apply Dhal Recovery Formula** $$\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{1320}{2000} \times 100 = 66\ \%$$ **Step 3: Economic Viability Assessment** - Industry benchmark for arhar = **≥ 70 %**. - Achieved = **66 %**, which is **4 % below benchmark**. - **Loss of revenue per tonne** = 4 % × 1000 kg × ₹70/kg = **₹2,800/tonne**. - At plant capacity of **10 tonnes/day** for 300 working days → annual revenue loss = **₹84 Lakhs/year**. **Conclusion:** Operation is **sub-economic**. Recommended corrective actions: (i) reduce splitter roller speed, (ii) audit aspiration cyclones for powder loss, (iii) recalibrate abrasive roller gap. *(7 marks: 2 marks mass summation, 2 marks recovery calculation, 3 marks economic analysis & recommendation)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **principle, working, and types of dehusking machines** used in commercial pulse milling. Draw a labeled block diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** A pulse mill receives **5000 kg** of green gram (moong) at **9 % moisture (w.b.)**. The mill operator wishes to raise the moisture to **12 %** for conditioning before dehusking. Calculate: - (i) The amount of water to be added. - (ii) The resting time typically recommended. - (iii) The expected moisture (w.b.) if the moistened grain is left open and loses **50 kg of water** by evaporation. **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Principle:** Dehusking machines work on the principle of **abrasion, attrition, and impact** to mechanically remove the husk (seed coat) from the cotyledon without damaging the inner edible portion. **Three Principal Types:** | Type | Mechanism | Application | |---|---|---| | **Abrasive Roller Type** | Rotating emery/rubber roller rubs husk against grain | Chana, moong, urad | | **Attrition / Under-run Disc Type** | Two rough stones/discs shear the grain | Arhar (pigeon pea) | | **Centrifugal Impact Type** | High-speed rotor throws grain against rubber liner | Hard-coated varieties | **Working of Abrasive Roller (most common):** 1. Conditioned grains fed via hopper. 2. Pass between **rotating abrasive roller** (peripheral speed **24–30 m/s**) and **stationary rubber liner**. 3. Friction removes husk; grain + husk fall by gravity. 4. **Aspiration duct** sucks away lighter husk; heavier grain exits to next pass. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hopper] --> B[Rotating Abrasive Roller] B --> C[Stationary Rubber Liner] C --> D[Friction Zone] D --> E[Grain plus Husk Discharge] E --> F[Aspiration Cyclone] F --> G[Husk Out] E --> H[Dehusked Grain Out] style B fill:#ffcc80 style C fill:#a5d6a7 style D fill:#ef9a9a style F fill:#fff59d ``` *(7 marks: 2 marks principle, 3 marks three types table, 2 marks abrasive roller working + diagram)* **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Water to be added:** Using Formula 7 from cheat sheet: $$M_{water} = M_{grain} \times \left(\dfrac{MC_{final} - MC_{initial}}{100 - MC_{final}}\right)$$ $$M_{water} = 5000 \times \left(\dfrac{12 - 9}{100 - 12}\right) = 5000 \times \dfrac{3}{88}$$ $$M_{water} = 5000 \times 0.03409 = 170.45\ \text{kg} \approx 170.5\ \text{L}$$ **(ii) Resting time:** For green gram (moong), recommended conditioning resting time = **12–24 hours** in an insulated bin (so moisture equilibrates between husk and cotyledon). **(iii) Final moisture after 50 kg evaporation:** After water addition, total mass = 5000 + 170.45 = **5170.45 kg** at **12 % m.c. (w.b.)**. - Mass of dry matter = $5170.45 \times (1 - 0.12) = 4549.996\ \text{kg}$ (assumed unchanged by water loss) - After 50 kg evaporation: new total mass = 5170.45 − 50 = **5120.45 kg** $$\begin{aligned} MC_{new}\ (w.b.) &= \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{dry\ matter}}{M_{total}}\right) \times 100 \\ MC_{new} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{4549.996}{5120.45}\right) \times 100 \\ MC_{new} &= (1 - 0.88862) \times 100 \\ MC_{new} &= 11.14\ \%\ (w.b.) \end{aligned}$$ > **Engineering implication:** Even with 50 kg evaporation, moisture drops only from 12 % → 11.14 %, still **within acceptable dehusking range (10–12 %)**. So no re-conditioning needed. *(7 marks: 3 marks part (i), 1 mark part (ii), 3 marks part (iii) with dry matter conservation logic)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing "dhal yield" with "dhal recovery"** — Yield often includes broken; Recovery may or may not (read the question carefully). > 2. **Using wet-basis vs dry-basis moisture** interchangeably — Always check whether the problem gives **w.b.** or **d.b.** and convert using $MC_{d.b.} = \dfrac{MC_{w.b.}}{100 - MC_{w.b.}} \times 100$ if required. > 3. **Forgetting dry matter is conserved** during moisture loss — dry mass remains constant; only water mass changes. > 4. **Missing the mass balance closure check** — Always sum all output streams; should equal input ± measurable moisture change. > 5. **Writing "conditioning increases moisture" without explaining WHY** — Examiner expects the *shear-plane at husk-cotyledon interface* rationale. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Six sequential operations:** Pre-clean → Grade → Condition → Dehusk → Split → Polish. - ✅ **Optimum moisture for milling:** **9–12 % (w.b.)** — too dry = brittle breakage; too wet = clogged machinery. - ✅ **Conditioning time:** **12–48 hours** depending on pulse type and husk thickness. - ✅ **CFTRI method** uses **0.1–0.2 % Na₂CO₃** + hot air drying for highest recovery (**78–82 %**). - ✅ **Dhal Recovery benchmark:** Chana **≥ 72 %**, Arhar **≥ 70 %**, Moong **≥ 72 %**. - ✅ **Three dehusking machine types:** Abrasive roller, Attrition disc, Centrifugal impact. - ✅ **Splitter rollers** operate at differential speed ratio **1 : 1.25** to avoid grinding. - ✅ **Polishing emulsion** = water + oil + stearic acid → glossy, FSSAI-compliant finish. - ✅ **Broken < 5 %, Powder < 2 %, Husk specks < 1 %** are quality acceptance thresholds. - ✅ **Formulas to memorize (R-C-D-B-P):** Recovery, Cleaning, Dehusking, Broken, Powder. - ✅ **Mass balance closure** = 100 % always (verify after every calculation). - ✅ **Compound efficiency is multiplicative** — weakest stage drags down overall efficiency. - ✅ **Conditioning water formula:** $M_w = M_g \times \dfrac{\Delta MC}{100 - MC_{final}}$ (always use final m.c. in denominator). - ✅ **FSSAI/BIS 6844** is the governing Indian standard for dhal quality and grading.

topics.title: Pre-Milling Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-Milling Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cereals and Legume Technology**, **Pre-Milling Techniques** refer to the sequence of preparatory unit operations applied to harvested grains (cereals such as rice, wheat, maize, sorghum, millets; and legumes such as chickpea, pigeon pea, lentil, mung bean) **before** they enter the actual size-reduction or flour-yielding milling stage. The objective is to convert raw, heterogeneous, field-dressed grain into a **clean, uniform, impurity-free, and structurally conditioned** feedstock suitable for efficient milling, maximum recovery, minimum nutrient loss, and superior end-product quality.

The principal pre-milling operations, in standard process-engineering order, are:

1. **Pre-cleaning (Scalping)** — removal of gross impurities (straw, chaff, stones, metal).
2. **Fine Cleaning** — removal of dust, soil, broken kernels, weed seeds, insect fragments.
3. **Sorting / Grading** — separation by size, shape, density, and colour.
4. **De-stoning** — separation of stones and heavy impurities using **specific gravity**.
5. **Magnetic Separation** — removal of ferrous contaminants.
6. **Dehulling / Decortication / Dehusking** — removal of the outer non-edible hull.
7. **Conditioning / Tempering** — controlled addition of water + rest period to modify endosperm–bran adhesion and moisture gradient.
8. **Parboiling (rice-specific)** — hydrothermal pre-treatment before milling.
9. **Soaking & Drying** — moisture equilibration prior to size reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pre-milling is *not* milling. Any operation that *does not* reduce particle size or extract flour by grinding/rolling belongs to pre-milling. Size reduction is reserved for Module 5.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing to cook a large quantity of rice at home. Before you put the rice into the cooker, you:
- Throw away the tiny stones that came with the bag (**cleaning/de-stoning**).
- Wash the rice to remove dust (**fine cleaning**).
- Discard any black or discoloured grains (**sorting by colour**).
- Sometimes soak the rice for 30 minutes so it cooks evenly (**conditioning**).

**Industrial pre-milling is exactly this — only it is done on tonnes of grain, with engineered machines, at industrial throughputs, and with strict quality control.**

Think of pre-milling as the **"wash, sort, and prep"** stage of a professional kitchen, applied at factory scale. Just as a chef would never grind unwashed spices, a cereal technologist would never mill uncleaned grain — because:
- Stones damage rollers.
- Straw jams sieves.
- Mixed grain sizes give uneven flour.
- Dry, brittle grain shatters, producing bran-rich, low-quality flour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The efficiency of the *actual* milling stage (yield, purity, energy consumption) is determined almost entirely by the **quality of pre-milling**. A well-pre-milled lot can raise flour yield by **5–10 %** and reduce roller wear significantly.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Metric | Typical Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture content for safe storage | **< 14 % w.b.** (wet basis) | Cereals |
| Moisture content for milling (wheat) | **14–16 %** | Wheat tempering |
| Moisture content for parboiled rice | **30–35 %** | After soaking, before steaming |
| Tempering rest time (wheat) | **12–24 h** | Moisture equalisation |
| Hull fraction in paddy | **18–22 %** | Determines milling yield ceiling |
| Specific gravity of stone | **> 2.0** | Used in de-stoner design |
| Specific gravity of grain | **1.10–1.40** | Used in de-stoner design |
| Cleaning efficiency target | **≥ 99 %** (large impurities), **≥ 95 %** (small) | Industrial norms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **specific gravity difference** between grain (~1.3) and stone (~2.4) is the fundamental physical principle exploited by the **de-stoner / gravity separator**. Memorise these numbers — they appear in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

### A. Pre-Cleaning (Scalping)

- **Principle:** Size difference (sieves/aspiration).
- **Equipment:** Drum sieve, vibrating scalper, aspirator.
- **Removes:** Straw, large stones, gunny-bag threads, sticks.
- **Why:** Protects downstream precision equipment from mechanical damage.

### B. Fine Cleaning

- **Principle:** Combination of size (sieving), density (aspiration), and magnetic properties.
- **Equipment:** **Indented cylinder separator** (separates by length), **gravity table** (separates by density), **aspiration channel** (removes light impurities), **magnetic separator** (removes ferrous metals).
- **Removes:** Dust, broken kernels, weed seeds, small stones, metallic fragments.

### C. Sorting / Grading

- **Principle:** Optical (colour), dimensional (size/shape), gravitational (density).
- **Equipment:** **Optical sorter** (CCD/LED-based), **disc separator**, **trieur**, **sieve grader**.
- **Why:** Uniform particle size → uniform milling → uniform flour quality.

### D. De-Stoning

- **Principle:** Difference in **specific gravity**.
- **Equipment:** **Dry de-stoner** (vibrating inclined deck with air cushion).
- **Mechanism:** Air fluidisation stratifies the bed — heavy stones sink to the deck and are conveyed uphill; light grain floats on the air cushion and flows downhill.
- **Why:** Stones damage rollers, contaminate flour, lower consumer acceptability.

### E. Magnetic Separation

- **Principle:** Ferromagnetism.
- **Equipment:** Permanent-magnet drawer, over-belt magnetic head pulley.
- **Why:** Prevent sparks/fire in dust-laden milling environment; protect rollers.

### F. Dehulling / Decortication

- **Principle:** Abrasion + impact.
- **Equipment:** **Paddy rubber-roll sheller** (rice), **emery roll / carborundum** (pulse dehuller), **Under-runner disk sheller**, **centrifugal sheller**.
- **What is removed:** Outer hull (husk) — *not* bran.
- **Note:** For pulses, the operation is often called **decortication** or **dehusking** and removes the seed coat (testa).

### G. Conditioning / Tempering

- **Principle:** Moisture diffusion from bran layer → endosperm.
- **Process:**
  1. Add water to bring moisture to target (e.g., **15.5–16.5 %** for wheat).
  2. Rest (temper) for **12–24 h**.
  3. Optional: differential tempering (bran 16 %, endosperm 14 %).
- **Why:**
  - Toughens bran → prevents bran fragmentation → cleaner flour.
  - Softens endosperm → easier grinding.
  - Reduces energy consumption.
  - Improves flour colour and ash content.

### H. Parboiling (Rice)

- **Three stages:**
  1. **Soaking** — paddy held in hot water (60–70 °C) for 3–4 h until moisture reaches **~30 %**.
  2. **Steaming** — gelatinises starch in the endosperm (1–2 bar steam, 10–20 min).
  3. **Drying** — reduce moisture to **~14 %** for storage/milling.
- **Why:**
  - Hardens grain → higher head rice yield (often **> 70 %** vs 50–55 % for raw rice).
  - Drives nutrients (vitamins, minerals) from bran → endosperm → improved nutritional retention after polishing.
  - Reduces breakage during milling.

### I. Soaking & Drying (Legumes)

- **Soaking:** 8–12 h at room temperature (or 2–4 h at 50–60 °C) to hydrate **12 % → 50–55 %**.
- **Drying:** Sun-drying or mechanical drying to ~**10–12 %** before milling.
- **Why:** Reduces cooking time, loosens seed coat for easy dehulling, inactivates anti-nutritional factors (trypsin inhibitors, lectins, phytic acid).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Wet-basis moisture content | % | Universal moisture definition |
| 2 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Dry-basis moisture content | % | Used in drying calculations |
| 3 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100$ | Conversion wb → db | % | Numerical problems |
| 4 | $\eta_{clean} = \dfrac{M_i - M_o}{M_i} \times 100$ | Cleaning efficiency | % | Pre-cleaner performance |
| 5 | $Y_{milling} = \dfrac{M_{product}}{M_{feed}} \times 100$ | Milling yield | % | Overall recovery |
| 6 | $HRY = \dfrac{M_{head\,rice}}{M_{paddy}} \times 100$ | Head Rice Yield | % | Parboiling benefit |
| 7 | $SG_{grain} \approx 1.30$ vs $SG_{stone} \approx 2.40$ | Specific gravity | — | De-stoner principle |
| 8 | $C_d = \dfrac{\rho_p - \rho_f}{\rho_p} \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \cdot g}{18\mu}$ | Stokes' drag settling velocity | m/s | Air-classification design |
| 9 | $E_{req} = k \cdot \ln\dfrac{M_i}{M_f}$ | Page / thin-layer drying | kJ/kg | Legume drying energy |
| 10 | $q_{cond} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat for tempering water | kJ | Conditioning energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test **moisture content conversions** (wb ↔ db) and the **milling yield / head rice yield** equations. Practice numericals from Formulas 1, 3, 5, and 6.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat flour mills:** Tempering alone can raise flour yield by **3–5 %** and reduce ash content by ~0.1 % — directly impacting grade pricing.
- **Rice industry:** Parboiling transforms an otherwise fragile grain into a millable, export-grade product, with **head rice yield** being the most prized industrial metric.
- **Pulse processing:** Proper soaking + drying + conditioning is the difference between a mill running at **70 % dehulling efficiency** vs **40 %**.
- **Energy economics:** De-stoning reduces roller-replacement frequency, saving lakhs of rupees per year in a 100-TPD plant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis)

**Problem context:** A KTU numerical typically gives moisture on wet basis (the commercial norm) but drying equations need it on dry basis. You must derive the conversion.

Let:
- $W_w$ = mass of wet grain (kg)
- $W_d$ = mass of dry matter (kg)
- $W_w - W_d$ = mass of water (kg)

**Step 1.** Define wet-basis moisture content:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

**Step 2.** Define dry-basis moisture content:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Step 3.** Divide the two equations to eliminate the common numerator:

$$\frac{MC_{db}}{MC_{wb}} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

**Step 4.** Take reciprocal and substitute. Since $W_w = W_d + (W_w - W_d)$:

$$\frac{1}{MC_{wb}} = \frac{W_d}{W_w} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{MC_{db}}{100}}$$

**Step 5.** Rearrange:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + \frac{MC_{db}}{100}}$$

**Final compact form (commonly used):**

$$\boxed{MC_{wb} = \frac{100 \cdot MC_{db}}{100 + MC_{db}}}$$

The reverse conversion:

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = \frac{100 \cdot MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}}$$

**Worked numerical:** If paddy is at $MC_{db} = 33\%$, the commercial wet-basis reading is:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{100 \times 33}{100 + 33} = \frac{3300}{133} \approx 24.81\%$$

This matches the textbook value of ~**24–25 % wb** for parboiled paddy after soaking. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parboiling Mass-Balance for Water Added

**Problem context:** How much water must be added to 1000 kg of paddy at $MC_1 = 12\%$ wb to raise it to $MC_2 = 30\%$ wb?

**Step 1.** Compute dry matter in feed:

$$W_d = W_w \cdot \left(1 - \frac{MC_1}{100}\right) = 1000 \cdot (1 - 0.12) = 880 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2.** Let final wet mass be $W_f$. Then:

$$W_f \cdot \left(1 - \frac{MC_2}{100}\right) = 880 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_f \cdot 0.70 = 880 \implies W_f = 1257.14 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3.** Water added:

$$\Delta W = W_f - W_w = 1257.14 - 1000 = 257.14 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Water to add} = W_w \left[\frac{100 - MC_1}{100 - MC_2} - 1\right]}$$

**Step 4.** Sanity check — general formula derivation:

$$\Delta W = W_w \left[\frac{100 - MC_1}{100 - MC_2}\right] - W_w = W_w \cdot \frac{(100 - MC_1) - (100 - MC_2)}{100 - MC_2} = W_w \cdot \frac{MC_2 - MC_1}{100 - MC_2}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta W = \frac{W_w (MC_2 - MC_1)}{100 - MC_2}}$$

Plugging back: $\frac{1000 \times (30 - 12)}{100 - 30} = \frac{18000}{70} = 257.14$ kg ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cleaning Efficiency and Mass Balance

For a pre-cleaner:

$$\eta_{clean} = \frac{M_i - M_o}{M_i} \times 100$$

where $M_i$ = initial impurity mass in feed, $M_o$ = impurity mass in clean output.

**Total mass balance** (steady state, no loss):

$$F = C + R$$

- $F$ = feed mass
- $C$ = cleaned grain (clean product)
- $R$ = rejected impurity stream

**Impurity mass balance:**

$$F \cdot x_i = C \cdot x_o + R \cdot x_r$$

Solving for $C$ and $R$ gives the usual two-product formula. In a typical KTU problem:

> *"A paddy pre-cleaner receives 5000 kg/h feed containing 4 % impurities. The clean grain should have ≤ 0.5 % impurities. Find clean grain output, reject rate, and cleaning efficiency."*

Using standard formula:

$$C = F \cdot \frac{x_r - x_i}{x_r - x_o}, \quad R = F \cdot \frac{x_i - x_o}{x_r - x_o}$$

If reject purity $x_r = 90\%$:

$$C = 5000 \cdot \frac{90 - 4}{90 - 0.5} = 5000 \cdot \frac{86}{89.5} \approx 4804.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$R = 5000 \cdot \frac{4 - 0.5}{89.5} \approx 195.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\eta_{clean} = \frac{4 - 0.5}{4} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pre-Milling Operations

| Operation | Target Impurity / Effect | Governing Principle | Key Equipment | Critical Process Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-cleaning | Straw, sticks | Size | Drum sieve | Aperture ≥ 2× grain size |
| Fine cleaning | Dust, weed seeds | Size + aspiration | Sieve + aspirator | Air velocity **5–8 m/s** |
| De-stoning | Stones | Specific gravity | Dry de-stoner | Bed inclination **6–10°** |
| Magnetic separation | Iron, steel | Ferromagnetism | Magnet | Field strength **≥ 2000 Gauss** |
| Sorting | Discoloured grain | Optical | Optical sorter | Throughput, camera resolution |
| Dehulling | Outer husk | Abrasion + impact | Rubber roll sheller | Roll gap **0.5–1.0 mm** |
| Tempering | Moisture gradient | Diffusion | Tempering bin | Rest time **12–24 h** |
| Parboiling (soak) | Internal stress | Hydrothermal | Soak tank | Water T **60–70 °C** |
| Parboiling (steam) | Starch gelatinisation | Heat + moisture | Steam vessel | Pressure **1–2 bar** |
| Drying | Excess moisture | Evaporation | LSU dryer | Final MC **≤ 14 % wb** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Pre-Milling Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Harvested Grain Field Lot] --> A2[Receiving Pit]
    A2 --> B1[Pre-Cleaner Scalper]
    B1 --> B2[Bucket Elevator 1]
    B2 --> C1[Magnetic Separator]
    C1 --> C2[Aspirator Light Impurity Removal]
    C2 --> D1[Indented Cylinder Separator]
    D1 --> D2[Specific Gravity De-Stoner]
    D2 --> E1[Sieve Grader Sizing]
    E1 --> F1[Optical Colour Sorter]
    F1 --> G1{Is the grain parboiled?}

    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Soak Tank 60 to 70 C]
    H1 --> H2[Steam Vessel Gelatinisation]
    H2 --> H3[Mechanical LSU Dryer]
    H3 --> H4[Tempering Bin Rest 4 to 6 h]
    H4 --> H5[Conditioned Paddy Ready for Milling]

    G1 -- No --> I1[Conditioning Tempering Bin]
    I1 --> I2[Water Addition to Target MC]
    I2 --> I3[Rest 12 to 24 h]
    I3 --> I5[Conditioned Grain Ready for Milling]

    H5 --> J1[Next Module Milling]
    I5 --> J1

    B1 --> K1[Reject Stream Straw Stones]
    C1 --> K2[Reject Stream Metal Tramp]
    C2 --> K3[Reject Stream Dust Light Chaff]
    D1 --> K4[Reject Stream Short Long Kernels]
    D2 --> K5[Reject Stream Stones]
    F1 --> K6[Reject Stream Discoloured Grain]

    style A1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style J1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style K1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K5 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style K6 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram mirrors a real **paddy pre-milling line** as encountered in commercial rice mills in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Punjab. For wheat, the parboiling branch (Yes) is omitted — only the tempering branch (No) applies.

---

## 4.2 De-Stoner Working Principle (Mermaid Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed[Feed Grain plus Stones] --> VibDeck[Vibrating Inclined Deck with Air Cushion]
    AirFan[Air Blower from Below] --> VibDeck
    VibDeck --> Strat{Stratification}

    Strat --> LightLayer[Light Grain Floats on Air Cushion]
    Strat --> HeavyLayer[Heavy Stones Sink to Deck Surface]

    LightLayer --> DownHill[Flow Downhill due to Vibration]
    HeavyLayer --> UpHill[Conveyed Uphill by Deck Inclination]

    DownHill --> CleanOut[Cleaned Grain Outlet]
    UpHill --> StoneOut[Stones Reject Outlet]

    style Feed fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e
    style CleanOut fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style StoneOut fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **angle of inclination** (typically **6–10°**) and **air velocity** (tuned so grain fluidises but stones do not) are the two design variables the KTU examiner loves to test. The bed is **oscillating** (vibrating) at a controlled frequency, and the air is supplied **perpendicularly** through the porous deck.

---

## 4.3 Tempering / Conditioning Moisture Diffusion (Mermaid Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DryGrain[Dry Grain MC 10 to 12 percent] --> WaterAdd[Water Sprayed and Mixed]
    WaterAdd --> SurfaceWet[Surface MC 18 to 20 percent]
    SurfaceWet --> Rest[Tempering Bin Rest 12 to 24 hours]
    Rest --> Diff[Moisture Diffuses to Endosperm]
    Diff --> Equilib[Equilibrium MC 15 to 16 percent Uniform]
    Equilib --> MillReady[Ready for Milling]

    style DryGrain fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d
    style SurfaceWet fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style Equilib fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style MillReady fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential tempering** in modern wheat mills uses two-stage conditioning: cold-temper for 8–12 h, then hot-temper for 1–2 h, bringing the bran to a higher MC than the endosperm — making bran **tough and elastic** so it does not fragment into flour during milling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define pre-milling techniques. List any four pre-milling operations carried out for paddy. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Pre-milling techniques are the preparatory unit operations performed on raw grains **before** the actual size-reduction milling stage, with the objective of producing a **clean, uniform, impurity-free, and structurally conditioned** feed.

Four pre-milling operations for paddy:
1. **Pre-cleaning** — removal of straw and large stones (drum sieve).
2. **De-stoning** — removal of stones by specific gravity separation.
3. **Parboiling** — soaking + steaming + drying (hydrothermal treatment).
4. **Dehulling** — removal of outer husk using rubber-roll sheller.

(Other acceptable answers: magnetic separation, fine cleaning, optical sorting, conditioning/tempering.)

---

### Q2. What is tempering? Why is it done in wheat milling? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Tempering (Conditioning)** is the process of adding a controlled quantity of water to wheat and allowing it to rest (12–24 h) so that the moisture equilibrates throughout the kernel.

**Purpose:**
1. **Toughens the bran layer** — prevents bran fragmentation during milling, increasing flour purity.
2. **Softens the endosperm** — reduces grinding energy.
3. **Improves flour yield and colour** — better separation of bran, endosperm, and germ.
4. **Standardises moisture** for consistent milling performance.

The target moisture content after tempering is **15.5–16.5 %** (wb), depending on wheat class.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Pattern:** Each Part B question in this module carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice**. Below are TWO complete alternative questions — answer ANY ONE in the exam.

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q.A. (a)** Describe the **parboiling of paddy** in detail. Explain the three stages with process parameters and discuss how parboiling improves milling yield and nutritional quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Parboiling is a **hydrothermal pre-treatment** of paddy in which the grain is soaked, steamed, and dried *before* milling. It is one of the most important pre-milling techniques for rice, especially in India.

**Stage 1 — Soaking:**
- Paddy is immersed in **hot water at 60–70 °C** for **3–4 hours** (cold soak at room temperature takes 24–36 h).
- Moisture rises from **~12 % wb to 30–35 % wb**.
- Aim: saturate the kernel so starch can be gelatinised in the next stage.

**Stage 2 — Steaming:**
- Soaked paddy is exposed to **saturated steam at 1–2 bar pressure for 10–20 min**.
- Starch granules in the endosperm **gelatinise**, becoming amorphous and binding together.
- Cell walls strengthen, and the grain becomes **translucent** (a visible quality index).
- Vitamins and minerals from the aleurone layer **migrate inward** and bind to the starch matrix.

**Stage 3 — Drying:**
- Steamed paddy is dried in **LSU (Louisiana State University) dryers** or mechanical tray dryers in **2–3 passes** with intermediate tempering.
- Final moisture target: **~14 % wb** for safe storage and milling.

**Improvement in milling yield:**
- Hardened, gelatinised endosperm **resists breakage** during roller milling.
- **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** increases from **~50–55 %** (raw rice) to **70–75 %** (parboiled).
- Lower broken-grain fraction → higher commercial value.

**Improvement in nutritional quality:**
- Water-soluble B-vitamins (thiamine, niacin) and minerals diffuse from bran into endosperm → **retained after polishing** because polishing removes only the outer layers.
- Pre-gelatinised starch improves **digestibility**.
- Parboiled rice has a **lower glycaemic index** than raw polished rice — beneficial for diabetics.

**Diagram (optional):** Three vertical columns labelled "Soak" / "Steam" / "Dry" with moisture arrows showing **12 % → 30 % → 14 %** transition.

---

**Q.A. (b)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **working of a dry de-stoner**. Discuss the role of specific gravity and air-cushion principle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **dry de-stoner** (also called a **gravity separator**) removes stones and other heavy impurities from grain using the principle of **specific gravity difference**.

**Construction:**
- A **porous, slightly inclined (6–10°) deck** mounted on **eccentric vibrators**.
- A **blower** below the deck blows air upward through the deck perforations.
- Adjustable **discharge gates** at each end.

**Working:**
1. The grain-stone mixture is fed onto the upper end of the vibrating deck.
2. Air from the blower creates an **air-cushion** that slightly fluidises the bed.
3. Because the **specific gravity of stones (~2.0–2.5)** is greater than that of **grain (~1.3)**, stones **sink** to the deck surface while grain **floats** on the air-cushion.
4. The **inclined vibratory motion** conveys the heavy stones **uphill** (against the slope) where they discharge at the top end.
5. The lighter grain flows **downhill** (with gravity, aided by vibration) and discharges at the lower end.

**Key parameters:**
- Air velocity (controlled to just fluidise grain but not stones).
- Deck inclination angle.
- Vibration frequency (typically **300–600 rpm**).
- Throughput (kg/h per m² of deck area).

**Role of specific gravity:**
- The fundamental physical property exploited. Without a sufficient **SG difference** (≥ **0.5**), separation is impossible.
- Stones (SG ~**2.4**) vs grain (SG ~**1.3**) → SG difference ~**1.1** — large enough for clean separation.

**Role of air-cushion:**
- Provides fluidisation to allow stratification.
- Without air, grains would simply roll along with stones — no separation possible.

**Flow diagram (text representation):**

```
   Feed
     ↓
[Blower ↑] → [Vibrating Inclined Deck] → Stones (uphill discharge)
                                         → Clean grain (downhill discharge)
```

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the **cleaning and grading of cereals** in detail. Discuss the working of an **indented cylinder separator** and an **optical sorter**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Cleaning** is the removal of foreign matter (both larger and smaller than the grain), while **grading** is the separation of cleaned grain into **uniform size fractions**.

**Cleaning operations:**
- **Scalping** — removes oversize (straw, sticks).
- **Sifting** — removes undersize (sand, broken grain).
- **Aspiration** — removes light impurities (dust, chaff, husk).
- **Magnetic separation** — removes ferrous metals.

**Grading:**
- Sieve graders, **indented cylinder separators**, disc separators, trieurs.

**Indented Cylinder Separator:**
- A slowly rotating horizontal cylinder with hemispherical **indents** on the inner surface.
- **Short grains** (e.g., broken rice) fit into the indents and are lifted, then thrown clear by gravity into an inner trough → collected as "shorts".
- **Long grains** (e.g., full paddy) do not fit into the indents and remain at the bottom, conveyed to the outlet.
- Used to separate **broken from whole grains**, or **wheat from barley** by length.

**Optical Sorter (Colour Sorter):**
- Uses **CCD cameras or LED sensors** to inspect each kernel as it free-falls past the inspection zone.
- A **microprocessor compares** the colour/brightness of each kernel against a preset reference.
- Defective (discoloured, diseased, mouldy, or foreign) kernels are **ejected** by a precise blast of compressed air from a nozzle array.
- Throughput: up to **10–15 tonnes/h** for rice.
- Accuracy: removes **> 99 %** of defective grains.

**Importance:**
- Uniform grading → uniform dehusking efficiency.
- Optical sorting improves **visual quality** of finished product — critical for export.

---

**Q.B. (b)** A batch of **1000 kg paddy** has initial moisture content of **12 % (wb)**. It is to be conditioned to **16 % (wb)** before milling. Calculate:
1. The amount of **dry matter** in the lot.
2. The amount of **water to be added**.
3. If the paddy is then **parboiled by soaking** to **30 % (wb)** and subsequently **dried to 14 % (wb)**, calculate the **final mass after drying**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Initial mass $W_1 = 1000$ kg, $MC_1 = 12\%$ wb
- After tempering: $MC_2 = 16\%$ wb
- After soaking (parboil): $MC_3 = 30\%$ wb
- After drying: $MC_4 = 14\%$ wb

**(i) Dry matter in the lot:**
$$W_d = W_1 \times \left(1 - \frac{MC_1}{100}\right) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.12) = 880 \text{ kg}$$

Dry matter is conserved through every operation.

**(ii) Water to be added to reach 16 % wb:**
Let $W_2$ = mass at $MC_2 = 16\%$.

$$W_2 \times \left(1 - \frac{MC_2}{100}\right) = W_d = 880$$

$$W_2 \times 0.84 = 880 \implies W_2 = \frac{880}{0.84} = 1047.62 \text{ kg}$$

Water added:
$$\Delta W_{add} = W_2 - W_1 = 1047.62 - 1000 = 47.62 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Final mass after drying to 14 % wb:**
Dry matter remains $W_d = 880$ kg.

$$W_4 \times \left(1 - \frac{MC_4}{100}\right) = 880$$

$$W_4 \times 0.86 = 880 \implies W_4 = \frac{880}{0.86} = 1023.26 \text{ kg}$$

**Summary of results:**

| Stage | Moisture (% wb) | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial paddy | 12 | 1000.00 |
| After tempering | 16 | 1047.62 |
| After soaking (peak) | 30 | 1257.14 |
| After drying | 14 | 1023.26 |

$$\boxed{W_d = 880 \text{ kg}, \quad \Delta W_{add} = 47.62 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{final} = 1023.26 \text{ kg}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Pre-Milling questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing wet basis (wb) with dry basis (db).** Always check the problem statement. The formula differs — losing 1–2 marks here is routine.
> 2. **Forgetting the dry-matter conservation principle.** In a multi-stage process (soak → steam → dry), dry matter is conserved; **only water changes**. Beginners often re-compute dry matter at each stage, leading to cascading errors.
> 3. **Calling tempering "drying" or "soaking."** Tempering is its own distinct operation — adding water and resting without evaporation.
> 4. **Confusing "dehulling" with "milling."** Dehulling = removal of outer husk (non-edible). Milling = size reduction + flour extraction. KTU examiners deduct marks for this interchange.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without arrows or labels.** Always label **all streams, equipment, and process parameters** (temperature, moisture, time).
> 6. **Skipping units.** Numericals without units fetch only partial credit. Always write kg, kg/h, % wb, °C.
> 7. **In parboiling answers, missing the gelatinisation concept.** Examiners expect the phrase **"starch gelatinisation"** explicitly — it is the heart of parboiling chemistry.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pre-milling ≠ milling.** Pre-milling cleans, sorts, and conditions grain; milling reduces it.
- ✅ The **four pillars** of pre-milling are: **Clean → Sort → Dehull → Condition.**
- ✅ **Specific gravity** of grain (~1.3) vs stone (~2.4) is the foundation of de-stoning.
- ✅ **Tempering** targets **15.5–16.5 % wb** for wheat with a **12–24 h** rest.
- ✅ **Parboiling** = Soak (60–70 °C, 3–4 h, MC → 30 %) + Steam (1–2 bar, 10–20 min) + Dry (to 14 % wb).
- ✅ **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** is the single most important parboiling performance metric — rises from **~55 %** to **> 70 %**.
- ✅ Moisture content definitions: **wb** (commercial) and **db** (engineering); know the conversion formula by heart.
- ✅ **Dry matter is conserved** in every pre-milling operation — only water mass changes.
- ✅ **Optical sorters** remove discoloured/defective grains using cameras + compressed-air ejection.
- ✅ **Indented cylinder** separates grains by **length** — used to remove broken rice and barley from wheat.
- ✅ **Magnetic separation** is mandatory in any modern cereal mill — protects equipment and prevents dust explosions.
- ✅ **Cleaning efficiency formula:** $\eta_{clean} = \dfrac{M_i - M_o}{M_i} \times 100$.
- ✅ Industrial moisture norms: safe storage **< 14 % wb**; milling wheat **15–16 % wb**; parboiled paddy storage **~14 % wb**.
- ✅ Always remember the **three reasons parboiling improves nutrition:** vitamin migration, mineral migration, and reduced polishing loss.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** in the answer script (or hand-drawn equivalents) earn visual-presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of Soya Bean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT836 - CEREALS AND LEGUME TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Processing of Soya Bean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soya Bean (Glycine max (L.) Merrill)** is an annual leguminous oilseed crop belonging to the family **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**. It is the world's foremost source of vegetable protein (~**40%** protein) and the second-largest source of vegetable oil (~**20%** oil) on a dry-weight basis.

**Processing of Soya Bean** refers to the integrated sequence of unit operations — cleaning, drying, dehulling, cracking, flaking, cooking/conditioning, oil extraction, and downstream conversion — designed to convert raw soybeans into stable, palatable, nutritionally superior, and value-added food ingredients such as **soy flour, soy protein concentrate (SPC), soy protein isolate (SPI), texturized vegetable protein (TVP), soy milk, tofu, fermented products (miso, tempeh, soy sauce), and refined soybean oil**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is processing essential for soya bean?**
> Raw soya bean cannot be consumed directly because it contains **anti-nutritional factors (ANFs)** such as trypsin inhibitors, lectins, urease, lipoxygenase, phytic acid, saponins, and goitrogens. These compounds are inactivated, destroyed, or removed during controlled thermal and mechanical processing — making the bean safe, digestible, and functional.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the soya bean as a "locked treasure chest."**
> Inside each tiny bean lies a fortress of high-quality protein, healthy unsaturated oil, isoflavones, and minerals. However, this chest is locked with **four heavy padlocks**:
>
> 1. **Padlock of Toxicity** — Anti-nutritional factors (trypsin inhibitors, lectins).
> 2. **Padlock of Taste** — The bitter, beany flavor from lipoxygenase activity.
> 3. **Padlock of Indigestibility** — Tough cell walls and globulin structure.
> 4. **Padlock of Spoilage** — High oil content with active lipases leading to rancidity.
>
> **Soya bean processing is the master locksmith** that systematically opens each padlock — using **heat (cooking/toasting)** to neutralize toxins, **enzymatic inactivation (blanching)** to lock in flavor, **mechanical disruption (cracking/flaking)** to expose nutrients, and **solvent/screw-press extraction** to separate oil and meal.

---

## 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics for Soya Bean

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Moisture content (raw) | **8 – 14%** |
| Protein (dry basis) | **35 – 40%** |
| Oil (dry basis) | **18 – 22%** |
| Crude fiber | **~5%** |
| Ash | **~5%** |
| Carbohydrates | **~30%** |
| Density (bulk) | **0.65 – 0.75 g/cm³** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference:** Soybean grading follows **USDA FGIS (Federal Grain Inspection Service)** classification based on test weight (kg/hL), damaged kernels, foreign material, and splits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anti-Nutritional Factors (ANFs) — The "Hidden Enemies"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot KTU Concept:** Examiners frequently ask "Why must soya beans be processed before consumption?" — the answer centres on the destruction/inactivation of ANFs.

| ANF | Nature | Effect | Inactivation Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trypsin Inhibitors (TI)** | Proteinaceous (Kunitz + Bowman-Birk) | Reduces protein digestibility, causes pancreatic hypertrophy | Moist heat (100 °C, 10–15 min) |
| **Lectins (Hemagglutinins)** | Glycoprotein | Agglutinates RBCs, toxicity | Moist heat (80–100 °C) |
| **Urease** | Enzyme | Hydrolyses urea → ammonia | Moist heat (sufficient to give negative Urease Activity Test) |
| **Lipoxygenase** | Enzyme | Catalyses lipid oxidation → beany off-flavor | Blanching (steam or hot water) |
| **Phytic Acid** | Phosphorus compound | Chelates Zn, Ca, Fe — reduces mineral bioavailability | Fermentation, soaking, phytase treatment |
| **Saponins** | Glycoside | Bitter taste, hemolysis | Soaking, boiling |
| **Goitrogens** | Thiocyanate precursors | Interfere with iodine uptake | Heat treatment |

---

## 2.2 Unit Operations in Soya Bean Processing — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" and "How" of each step (Examiner's Favourite Framework):**

### A. Pre-Processing Stage
1. **Reception & Cleaning** — Remove foreign matter (stones, weed seeds, broken kernels). Magnetic separators remove ferrous metals.
2. **Drying** — Reduce moisture to **≤ 12%** to enable safe storage (above 13% moisture → mould and aflatoxin risk).
3. **Storage** — Aerated silos at **≤ 12% moisture**, **≤ 20 °C** to preserve viability and prevent lipolysis.
4. **Grading** — Sizing into uniform fractions for uniform downstream processing.

### B. Primary Processing Stage (Oil + Meal Route)
5. **Tempering/Conditioning** — Adjustment of moisture (to **~ 11–13%**) and temperature (**60–70 °C**) to soften seed coat for efficient dehulling.
6. **Dehulling** — Mechanical removal of hulls (seed coat) using **impact hullers, disc shellers, or abrasive rollers**. Improves protein digestibility and reduces fiber.
7. **Cracking** — Breaking cotyledons into 4–6 pieces using corrugated roller mills. Avoids flour generation.
8. **Flaking** — Rolling cracked pieces into **thin flakes (0.25–0.40 mm thickness)**. **Why thin?** — Reduces diffusion path for hexane, increases surface area for efficient oil extraction.
9. **Cooking/Conditioning** — Steam-heating flakes to **75–90 °C** with moisture adjustment to **18–20%**. **Triple role:** (i) ruptures oil cells, (ii) denatures proteins, (iii) inactivates enzymes (lipase, lipoxygenase).
10. **Oil Extraction** — Either **mechanical (expeller/screw press)** OR **solvent extraction (hexane, 60 °C)** — solvent method achieves ~**99% oil recovery**.
11. **Desolventizing (DT – Desolventizer-Toaster)** — Removes residual hexane from meal using indirect steam at **105–110 °C** while simultaneously toasting meal to inactivate ANFs.

### C. Secondary Processing Stage (Value-Added Products)
12. **Soy Flour Production** — Grinding defatted flakes to <100 µm. Variants: **Full-fat, Defatted, Low-fat, Lecithinated**.
13. **Soy Protein Concentrate (SPC)** — Protein ≥ **70%** (dry basis). Methods: **Acid leaching (pH 4.5), Alcohol wash (60–80% ethanol), Hot water leaching**.
14. **Soy Protein Isolate (SPI)** — Protein ≥ **90%** (dry basis). Produced by **alkaline extraction (pH 9–10) → isoelectric precipitation (pH 4.5) → spray-drying**.
15. **Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP)** — High-moisture extrusion of defatted soy flour at **> 100 °C** under shear to give meat-like fibrous structure.

### D. Traditional / Oriental Processing
16. **Soy Milk** — Soaked → ground with water → boiled → filtered. **Tofu** — coagulation of soy milk with **CaSO₄ (nigari), MgCl₂, or GDL**.
17. **Fermented products** — Tempeh (Rhizopus oligosporus), Miso (Aspergillus oryzae + yeast + lactobacilli), Soy sauce (Aspergillus + Tetragenococcus halophilus).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations and Performance Indicators — Memorize these for numerical/analytical questions.**

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Moisture content (% wet basis); $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| 2 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\text{Protein dispersed in water}}{\text{Total protein}} \times 100$ | **Protein Dispersibility Index** — measures heat damage to protein |
| 3 | $\text{NSI} = \dfrac{\text{Soluble N}}{\text{Total N}} \times 100$ | **Nitrogen Solubility Index** — indicates protein denaturation |
| 4 | $\text{UrAc} = \dfrac{\Delta pH \times V \times N}{W} \times 100$ | **Urease Activity** index — adequacy of heat treatment |
| 5 | $\text{TI Activity} = \dfrac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{reference}}} \times 100$ | **Trypsin Inhibitor Activity** (% of original) |
| 6 | $\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{seed} \times Y_{oil}} \times 100$ | Oil extraction efficiency (%); $Y_{oil}$ = theoretical oil content |
| 7 | $R_p = \dfrac{m_p}{m_{flour}} \times 100$ | Protein recovery (%); $m_p$ = protein extracted |
| 8 | $\text{SPC yield} = \dfrac{m_{conc}}{m_{flour}} \times \dfrac{P_{conc}}{P_{flour}}$ | Mass balance for protein concentrate |
| 9 | $\text{SPI yield} = \dfrac{m_{iso}}{m_{flour}} \times \dfrac{P_{iso}}{P_{flour}}$ | Mass balance for protein isolate |
| 10 | $R_d = 1 - \left(\dfrac{C_f}{C_0}\right)^{1/n}$ | Retention reduction factor for toxins during heat processing |
| 11 | $\text{RE} = \dfrac{N_2\text{ retained}}{\text{Total }N_2\text{ intake}}$ | **Protein Efficiency Ratio** ratio in nutrition balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical ranges to memorize:**
> - Urease activity (ΔpH) for **adequately toasted** meal: **0.05 – 0.20**
> - PDI for **moderately heated** meal: **40 – 60**
> - PDI for **fully toasted** meal: **< 25**
> - NSI for **native** soy protein: **80 – 95**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Urease Activity Calculation (KTU Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A sample of 1.0 g of soybean meal was incubated with buffered urea solution. The ammonia liberated was titrated with **0.1 N HCl**, requiring **1.2 mL**. A blank titration required **0.1 mL** of the same acid. Calculate the **Urease Activity Index** and comment on whether the heat treatment was adequate.

> **Standard Convention:** $\Delta pH$ method — increase in pH of buffered urea–meal slurry over 30 min at 30 °C. Adequate: $\Delta pH \leq 0.20$.

### Step 1 — Calculate Net Volume of HCl Used

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{net}} &= V_{\text{sample}} - V_{\text{blank}} \\
&= 1.2 \text{ mL} - 0.1 \text{ mL} \\
&= 1.1 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Urease Activity Equation (Titrimetric Method)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UrAc} &= \dfrac{(V_{\text{sample}} - V_{\text{blank}}) \times N \times 14}{W} \\
&= \dfrac{1.1 \times 0.1 \times 14}{1.0} \\
&= 1.54 \text{ mg NH}_3\text{–N per g sample}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - If **$\Delta pH$ method** gave **$\Delta pH \leq 0.20$** → Meal is **properly toasted**, trypsin inhibitors inactivated → **ADEQUATE heat treatment**.
> - If **$\Delta pH > 0.30$** → Under-processed → urease still active → re-toasting required.
> - **Over-processing** risk: Nutrient destruction (lysine loss), Maillard browning, reduced PDI/NSI.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mass Balance for Soy Protein Isolate (SPI) Production

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Starting from **1000 kg of defatted soybean flour** (containing 90% dry solids, of which **52% is protein**). During alkaline extraction at pH 10, **85% of the protein** dissolves. During isoelectric precipitation at pH 4.5, **95% of the dissolved protein** is recovered. Spray-drying gives SPI with **92% protein**. Calculate: (a) Protein extracted, (b) SPI mass obtained, (c) Overall protein yield.

### Step 1 — Determine Initial Dry Protein Mass

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry flour}} &= 1000 \times 0.90 = 900 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{initial protein}} &= 900 \times 0.52 = 468 \text{ kg protein}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Protein in Alkaline Extract

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{extracted}} &= 468 \times 0.85 \\
&= 397.8 \text{ kg protein in solution}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Protein Recovered by Isoelectric Precipitation

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{recovered}} &= 397.8 \times 0.95 \\
&= 377.91 \text{ kg protein in curd}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Mass of Spray-Dried SPI

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{SPI}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{0.92} \\
&= \dfrac{377.91}{0.92} \\
&= 410.77 \text{ kg of SPI powder}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Overall Protein Yield

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_p &= \dfrac{m_{\text{recovered}}}{m_{\text{initial protein}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{377.91}{468} \times 100 \\
&= 80.75\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **410.77 kg** of SPI powder containing **377.91 kg** of pure protein is obtained from **1000 kg** of defatted soybean flour, with an overall protein recovery of **80.75%**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Moisture Content for Safe Storage

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** 250 g of soybeans lose 32 g after oven drying at 105 °C for 16 h. Determine the moisture content (% wet basis) and state whether the lot is safe for long-term storage.

### Step 1 — Dry Matter Mass

$$\begin{aligned}
W_d &= 250 - 32 = 218 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Moisture Content (% w.b.)

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{wb} &= \dfrac{32}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 12.8\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Moisture Content (% d.b.)

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{db} &= \dfrac{32}{218} \times 100 \\
&= 14.68\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Storage Suitability

> [!WARNING]
> At **12.8% moisture (w.b.)**, the soybeans are at the **borderline of safe storage**. For long-term storage (≥ 6 months), moisture should be reduced to **≤ 11% (w.b.)** and the grain cooled to **≤ 15 °C**. Mould (e.g., *Aspergillus flavus* → aflatoxin) develops rapidly above 13% moisture and 25 °C.

---

| Property | Soy Flour (Defatted) | SPC | SPI | TVP |

| **Protein content (dry)** | 50–55% | 70–75% | ≥ 90% | 50–55% |
| **Production method** | Grinding defatted flakes | Acid/alcohol leach | Alkaline extract + pptn | Extrusion |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Soya Bean Processing Flow (Oil + Meal Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Soya Bean Reception] --> B[Cleaning and Magnetic Separation]
    B --> C[Drying to less than 12 percent moisture]
    C --> D[Storage in Aerated Silos]
    D --> E[Tempering and Conditioning at 60 to 70 deg C]
    E --> F[Dehulling and Separation of Hulls]
    F --> G[Cracking using Corrugated Rolls]
    G --> H[Flaking to 0.25 to 0.40 mm thickness]
    H --> I[Cooking and Conditioning at 80 to 90 deg C]
    I --> J[Screw Press or Expeller Pre Extraction]
    J --> K[Solvent Extraction with Hexane at 60 deg C]
    K --> L[Crude Soybean Oil]
    L --> M[Refining Degumming Neutralization Bleaching Deodorization]
    M --> N[Refined Soybean Oil]
    K --> O[Defatted Meal Flakes]
    O --> P[Desolventizer Toaster at 105 to 110 deg C]
    P --> Q{Choose Downstream Route}
    Q --> R[Soy Flour Grinding below 100 micrometer]
    Q --> S[SPC Acid or Alcohol Leaching]
    Q --> T[SPI Alkaline Extraction and Isoelectric Precipitation]
    Q --> U[TVP High Moisture Extrusion]
    P --> V[Quality Check Urease PDI NSI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from raw bean reception (top-left) to end-products (bottom-right). The diamond node `Q` represents a decision branch — a single batch of defatted meal can be routed to multiple value-added products.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — ANF Destruction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Soya Bean] --> A2[Trypsin Inhibitors Active]
    A1 --> A3[Lectins Active]
    A1 --> A4[Lipoxygenase Active]
    A1 --> A5[Urease Active]
    A2 --> B1[Moist Heat 100 deg C]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B2[Blanching Steam 90 deg C]
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Proteins Denatured and ANFs Inactivated]
    B2 --> C2[Beany Flavor Eliminated]
    C1 --> D1[Safe Digestible Soy Meal]
    C2 --> D1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — SPI Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Defatted Soy Flour] --> F2[Alkaline Extraction pH 9 to 10 at 50 deg C]
    F2 --> F3[Centrifugation to Remove Insolubles]
    F3 --> F4[Isoelectric Precipitation pH 4.5 using HCl]
    F4 --> F5[Centrifugation Curd Recovery]
    F5 --> F6[Neutralization pH 7 with NaOH]
    F6 --> F7[Spray Drying Inlet 180 deg C Outlet 80 deg C]
    F7 --> P1[Powdered Soy Protein Isolate 90 percent Protein]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why alkaline extraction at pH 9–10?** Most soy proteins (glycinin 11S and β-conglycinin 7S) are highly soluble above their isoelectric point (pI ≈ 4.5). At pH 10, maximum solubilisation occurs, giving the highest extraction yield.

---

## 4.4 Process Comparison — Hexane vs Mechanical Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cooked Flakes] --> B{Extraction Method}
    B -->|Mechanical| C1[Screw Press at 90 to 120 deg C]
    C1 --> D1[Crude Oil plus Residual Meal 5 to 8 percent Oil]
    B -->|Solvent| C2[Hexane Percolation at 60 deg C]
    C2 --> D2[Crude Oil plus Defatted Meal less than 1 percent Oil]
    D1 --> E[Low Oil Yield Suitable for Small Scale]
    D2 --> F[High Oil Yield Suitable for Industrial Scale]
```
